# Reasoning
The original task is a fairly standard “find the maximum” exercise.  
To make it more challenging we can:

• Ask for additional, harder-to-prove facts (e.g. an index of the maximum, or the number of occurrences).  
• Introduce an auxiliary predicate that is slightly more involved than “is _max”.  
• Require several distinct properties to hold simultaneously, so that a proof now needs a few more lemmas or inductive arguments instead of a single loop invariant.  
• Keep everything inside the unbounded `int` domain so that overflow is still irrelevant, but insist on determinism (e.g. *first* index, *smallest* element fulfilling a predicate, etc.).

Below are three stand-alone, higher-difficulty specifications that follow those ideas.  
Each block inside <<< … >>> is a complete problem statement that a spec generator could consume on its own.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function together with two supporting specification predicates.

1. Predicates (ghost-only)
   a)  spec fn is_max(m: int, numbers: Seq<int>) -> bool
       Purpose: express that m is a maximum element of the sequence, exactly as in the original problem.

   b)  spec fn first_index_of(v: int, numbers: Seq<int>) -> nat
       Purpose: returns the smallest index k such that numbers[k] == v.
       Definition requirements:
         • 0 ≤ k < numbers.len()
         • numbers[k] == v
         • for all j with 0 ≤ j < k we have numbers[j] ≠ v

2. Executable function
   fn max_with_first_index(numbers: &[int]) -> (max_val: int, idx: usize)

   Inputs:
     • numbers – immutable slice of mathematical integers.

   Outputs:
     • max_val – a value which is a maximum of the slice,
     • idx     – the first position at which that maximum occurs.

   Preconditions (requires):
     • numbers.len() > 0

   Postconditions (ensures):
     • is_max(max_val, numbers@)
     • idx == first_index_of(max_val, numbers@)   // idx is the *smallest* index with that value
     • idx < numbers.len() ∧ numbers@[idx] == max_val

   Notes for the implementer:
     • You will need a loop invariant that keeps track of the current maximum and the
       earliest position at which it was seen.
     • The verifier must be convinced that idx never exceeds numbers.len() – 1 and
       that no earlier occurrence of max_val exists.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function together with two supporting specification predicates.

1. Predicates (ghost-only)
   a)  spec fn is_max(m: int, numbers: Seq<int>) -> bool
       (same semantics as usual)

   b)  spec fn count_occurrences(v: int, numbers: Seq<int>) -> nat
       Purpose: counts how many elements of `numbers` equal `v`.
       Definition: returns |{ i | 0 ≤ i < numbers.len() ∧ numbers[i] == v }|.

2. Executable function
   fn max_and_frequency(numbers: &[int]) -> (max_val: int, freq: usize)

   Inputs:
     • numbers – an immutable slice of ints (non-empty).

   Outputs:
     • max_val – the maximum value found,
     • freq    – the exact number of times that maximum appears.

   Preconditions (requires):
     • numbers.len() > 0

   Postconditions (ensures):
     • is_max(max_val, numbers@)
     • freq == count_occurrences(max_val, numbers@)
     • freq > 0                                 // sanity: the max occurs at least once
     • freq as int <= numbers.len() as int      // optional sanity bound

   Implementation hints:
     • A single linear scan that keeps both the running maximum and its current
       count must be verified.
     • When a new, larger maximum is seen, reset the counter to 1; when a value
       equal to the current max is seen, increment the counter.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function together with one supporting specification predicate.

1. Predicate (ghost-only)
   spec fn is_second_max(m2: int, numbers: Seq<int>) -> bool
   Purpose: express that m2 is the *largest value that is strictly less than* the
            maximum of the sequence (sometimes called the “second maximum”).
   Definition: returns true iff
     • numbers.len() ≥ 2, and
     • let M = max(numbers);                        // the (ordinary) maximum
       – there exists i with 0 ≤ i < numbers.len() such that numbers[i] == m2,
       – m2 < M,                                    // it is strictly below the maximum
       – for every j, if numbers[j] < M then numbers[j] ≤ m2.  // nothing in between

2. Executable function
   fn second_max(numbers: &[int]) -> (result: int)

   Inputs:
     • numbers – slice of ints having at least two *distinct* values.

   Outputs:
     • result – the second largest distinct element.

   Preconditions (requires):
     • numbers.len() ≥ 2
     • ∃ i, j < numbers.len(), numbers[i] ≠ numbers[j]   // ensures at least two different values

   Postconditions (ensures):
     • is_second_max(result, numbers@)

   Additional requirement:
     • The algorithm must run in a single pass (O(n)) without auxiliary heap allocation.
       This should be evident from the structure of the code that the verifier sees.

   Notes:
     • One customary pattern is to maintain two ghost variables `cur_max` and
       `cur_second`, updating them as the scan progresses.  Proving that
       `cur_second` indeed tracks the largest value below `cur_max` throughout
       the loop is the main verification challenge.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise merely asks the learner to detect the *existence* of an even number in a slice.  
That property is (a) monotone, (b) easy to express with a single existential quantifier, and (c) implementable by a trivial linear scan whose proof needs only one loop invariant.

To raise the difficulty we can:

• Keep the helper `is_even` (so the student already knows one spec), but demand a *rarer* numeric predicate such as “exactly two evens”, “prefix-sum parity alternation”, or “minimal index with a property”.

• Add quantifier alternations (`∀`–`∃` or minimality) or arithmetic reasoning (prefix sums, counting) so that the proof of correctness needs a more elaborate invariant.

• Occasionally constrain complexity (e.g. require one pass, O(1) extra space) so that the implementation cannot just allocate helper vectors.

Below are three harder, self-contained problem statements that fulfil those goals.  Each is written so that a spec generator could construct the Verus function contracts from the English alone.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus/Rust function together with one helper specification function.

1. Helper spec (pure, ghost-only code)  
   spec fn is_even(n: int) -> bool  
   Definition: returns true exactly when n % 2 == 0.

2. Primary executable function  
   fn has_exactly_two_evens(a: &[u32]) -> (result: bool)

   • Input  
     – a: an immutable slice of 32-bit unsigned integers.

   • Output  
     – result: bool.

   • Preconditions (requires)  
     true   // accepts every slice, even empty

   • Postcondition (ensures)  
     result  ⇔  
        (∃ i:int, j:int •  
            0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < a.len() ∧ i ≠ j ∧  
            is_even(a[i] as int) ∧ is_even(a[j] as int) ∧  
            (∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ k ≠ i ∧ k ≠ j ⇒ ¬is_even(a[k] as int)))

     In words: result is true exactly when the slice contains *precisely two* even elements, at two different indices i and j, and every other element is odd.

   • Additional requirement  
     – The implementation must run in strictly one linear pass over the slice and use only O(1) additional executable memory (ghost variables are unrestricted).

No additional helpers or lemmas are allowed.  A typical implementation counts evens while scanning, aborting early when a third even is found, and the proof must show that the counter value correctly characterises the postcondition.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus/Rust function together with one helper specification function.

1. Helper spec (pure, ghost-only code)  
   spec fn is_even(n: int) -> bool  
   Definition: returns true exactly when n % 2 == 0.

2. Primary executable function  
   fn first_strictly_larger_than_all_previous(a: &[u32]) -> (idx: Option<usize>)

   • Input  
     – a: an immutable slice of 32-bit unsigned integers.

   • Output  
     – idx: Option<usize>.  `Some(i)` denotes an index; `None` denotes the absence of such an index.

   • Preconditions (requires)  
     true

   • Postconditions (ensures)  

     • If idx is Some(i):  
       – 0 ≤ i < a.len()  
       – (∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < i ⇒ a[j] < a[i])          // a[i] is larger than *every* previous element  
       – (∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < i ⇒ true) // vacuous helper, keeps pattern consistent  
       – (∀ m:int • 0 ≤ m < a.len() ∧ (∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < m ⇒ a[j] < a[m]) ⇒ m ≥ i)  
         (i is the *smallest* index with the property, i.e. minimality)

     • If idx is None:  
       – (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒  
            (∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < i ∧ a[j] ≥ a[i]))  
         // every element has some previous element that is ≥ it

   • Complexity constraint  
     The function must examine every element at most once and maintain only O(1) executable state.

A verified solution typically walks through the slice while tracking the current maximum; when it sees an element strictly larger than this maximum it reports its position and terminates.  The proof now has to establish the minimality property, which introduces universal–existential reasoning not present in the original exercise.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus/Rust function together with one helper specification function.

1. Helper spec (pure, ghost-only code)  
   spec fn is_even(n: int) -> bool  
   Definition: returns true precisely when n % 2 == 0.

2. Primary executable function  
   fn alternating_parity_prefix(a: &[u32]) -> (result: bool)

   • Input  
     – a: immutable slice of u32.

   • Output  
     – result: bool.

   • Preconditions (requires)  
     true

   • Postcondition (ensures)  
     result  ⇔  
       (∃ p:int • 0 ≤ p ≤ a.len() ∧  
          (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < p ⇒                    // prefix [0, p)
              (i % 2 == 0 ⇒ is_even(a[i] as int)) ∧
              (i % 2 == 1 ⇒ ¬is_even(a[i] as int))) ∧
          (∀ j:int • p ≤ j < a.len() ⇒              // suffix [p, len)
              (j % 2 == 0 ⇒ ¬is_even(a[j] as int)) ∧
              (j % 2 == 1 ⇒ is_even(a[j] as int))))

     In words: result is true iff there exists a split point p such that the slice’s *prefix* alternates “even, odd, even, …” starting with even at index 0, while the *suffix* alternates the *opposite* way “odd, even, odd, …” starting with odd at index p.

   • Implementation hint / constraint  
     The algorithm must run in linear time and may not allocate any vectors; two integer counters and a Boolean flag should suffice.

To verify this specification the student must craft a loop invariant that separately tracks whether the examined part of the slice still satisfies the alternating-parity condition with respect to the (yet-unknown) split position `p`, and must reason about both universal quantifiers in the postcondition.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Specification function to check if a string is a decimal with two precision
spec fn is_decimal_with_two_precision(s: Seq<u8>) -> bool {
    exists|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() && s[i] == '.' && s.len() - i - 1 == 2
}

/// Function to check if a string is a decimal with two precision
fn is_decimal_with_two_precision_func(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)
    ensures
        result ==> is_decimal_with_two_precision(s@),
        !result ==> !is_decimal_with_two_precision(s@),
{
```

# Reasoning
1. Main idea  
   The file contains a pure specification predicate `is_decimal_with_two_precision` and an executable wrapper `is_decimal_with_two_precision_func`.  
   Together they formalise the notion “the byte slice represents a *decimal number with exactly two characters after a dot*”.

2. Helper spec function  
   spec fn is_decimal_with_two_precision(s: Seq<u8>) -> bool  
   • Argument `s` is a mathematical sequence of bytes (`u8`).  
   • It is *true* when there exists an index `i` that satisfies three constraints:  
     – `0 ≤ i < s.len()` (the index is in bounds)  
     – `s[i] == '.'`   (the byte at that position is the ASCII period 0x2E)  
     – `s.len() - i - 1 == 2` (exactly two bytes follow that dot).  
   • Note that the predicate does *not* require the characters before or after the dot to be decimal digits; only the length condition is checked.  
   • The semantics therefore accept any string whose length is at least three and that has at least one ‘.’ positioned so that exactly two bytes remain after it (e.g. "12.34", ".ab", "xx.yy", etc.).

3. Executable function  
   fn is_decimal_with_two_precision_func(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)  
   • Input  
     – `s`: an immutable byte slice (`&[u8]`).  
   • Output  
     – `result`: a boolean.  
   • Preconditions: none — the slice may be empty, arbitrarily long, and contain any bytes.  
   • Postconditions (ensures)  
     – `result ==> is_decimal_with_two_precision(s@)`  
     – `!result ==> !is_decimal_with_two_precision(s@)`  
     Taken together these two implications are logically equivalent to  
       `result == is_decimal_with_two_precision(s@)`  
     where `s@` is Verus’ ghost view that turns the runtime slice into a specification-time `Seq<u8>`.  
   • Consequently the function must compute a Boolean that precisely matches the predicate above.  A correct body can, for instance, scan the slice for a period and check whether exactly two elements remain afterwards.

4. Subtleties / edge cases  
   • Multiple dots: as long as *one* of them has exactly two trailing bytes, the predicate is true.  
   • Length shorter than 3, or dot at last two positions: predicate is false.  
   • Characters need not be ASCII digits; validating digit characters is out of scope for this spec.  
   • No overflow or arithmetic problems arise because only slice lengths and differences are used, all of which are non-negative integers.

5. Re-implementing the spec  
   To reconstruct the specification from scratch one must provide  
   – the helper predicate `is_decimal_with_two_precision` with the exact `exists` formula above, and  
   – the executable function that takes a `&[u8]`, returns a `bool`, and enforces `result == is_decimal_with_two_precision(s@)` via ensures-clauses.

# Final Description

      ∧ s[i] == '.'                   // the byte at i is the ASCII dot (0x2E)
      ∧ s.len() − i − 1 == 2          // exactly two bytes follow that dot

(No assumptions are made about the content of the other bytes.)

2. Executable, verified wrapper

Output:

Preconditions:

Postconditions:

where `s@` is Verus’ ghost view converting the slice to a `Seq<u8>`.

# Reasoning
The original exercise is quite small: validate that a byte‐slice is non-empty and every byte is an ASCII digit.  
To raise the difficulty we can

1.  Add an extra syntactic rule such as “no leading zeros unless the number is exactly 0”.  
2.  Permit an optional sign, so the first byte might be '+' or '-', which means the loop body must skip one extra element and the spec has to mention the signed case explicitly.  
3.  Require computing (or partially computing) a semantic property, e.g. returning the numeric value when the string is valid hexadecimal, so post-conditions must relate the Option content to a mathematically defined value; this forces an inductive proof about accumulation / overflow.  

Below are three standalone, higher-difficulty variants implementing these ideas.  Each description is fully self-contained so that a spec generator can create the Verus specification from it alone.

# Output
<<<
Implement three items in Verus-Rust.

1.  A pure specification helper  
    spec fn is_digit(c: u8) -> bool  
    Returns true exactly when 48 ≤ c ≤ 57 (ASCII ‘0’–‘9’).

2.  A pure specification helper  
    spec fn is_nonzero_digit(c: u8) -> bool  
    Returns true exactly when 49 ≤ c ≤ 57 (ASCII ‘1’–‘9’).

3.  A verified, executable function  
    fn is_integer_no_leading_zeros(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Inputs  
 • s: immutable slice of bytes interpreted as an ASCII string.

Output  
 • result: bool.

Pre-conditions  
 • none.

Post-condition  
 result is logically equivalent to:  
   – s.len() > 0,                                                and  
   – ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ is_digit(s[i]),                  and  
   – (s.len() == 1) ∨ is_nonzero_digit(s[0]).                    // forbid “00…”, allow solitary “0”

Formally  
   result <==>  
       s.len() > 0  
       && (forall|i:int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> is_digit(s[i]))  
       && (s.len() == 1 || is_nonzero_digit(s[0]))

In words, the function must return true exactly when the slice is a non-empty sequence of decimal digits that does not start with ‘0’ unless the entire string is just “0”.  No additional lemmas or assumptions are required; any body that satisfies the spec is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Implement two items in Verus-Rust.

1.  A pure specification helper  
    spec fn is_signed_digit(c: u8) -> bool  
    Returns true when c is ‘+’ (43), ‘-’ (45), or an ASCII digit ‘0’–‘9’ (48–57).

2.  A verified executable function  
    fn is_signed_integer(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Inputs  
 • s: immutable slice of bytes interpreted as ASCII.

Output  
 • result: bool.

Pre-conditions  
 • none.

Post-condition  
 result is logically equivalent to the conjunction  
   (a) s.len() > 0,                                                       and  
   (b) if s[0] is ‘+’ or ‘-’ then s.len() > 1 (at least one digit follows), and  
   (c) ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ (i == 0 ? is_signed_digit(s[i]) : 48 ≤ s[i] ≤ 57)

Formally  
   result <==>  
       s.len() > 0  
       && (   (s[0] == 43 || s[0] == 45) ==> s.len() > 1)  
       && (forall|i:int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==>  
              (if i == 0 { is_signed_digit(s[i]) } else { 48 <= s[i] && s[i] <= 57 }))

The function must return true exactly when the slice encodes an optional ‘+’/‘-’ sign followed by at least one decimal digit, with no other characters.  No helper lemmas may be assumed.
>>>

<<<
You are to implement three things in Verus-Rust.

1.  Pure specification helper  
    spec fn is_hex_digit(b: u8) -> bool  
    Returns true when b is an ASCII hexadecimal digit:  
        48–57 (‘0’–‘9’), 65–70 (‘A’–‘F’), or 97–102 (‘a’–‘f’).

2.  Pure specification function converting a single hex digit to its value  
    spec fn hex_val(b: u8) -> nat  
    Requires is_hex_digit(b).  
    Ensures 0 ≤ hex_val(b) ≤ 15 and matches the conventional numeric value.

3.  Verified executable parser  
    fn parse_hex_u64(s: &[u8]) -> (out: Option<u64>)

Inputs  
 • s: immutable slice of ASCII bytes.

Output  
 • out: Option<u64>.

Pre-conditions  
 • none.

Post-conditions  
 • If out is Some(v):  
     – 1 ≤ s.len() ≤ 16,                                     // at most 64 bits  
     – ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ is_hex_digit(s[i]),        // every char hex  
     – v == Σ_{i=0}^{s.len()-1} hex_val(s[i]) * 16^{s.len()-1-i}. // numeric value  
 • If out == None, the slice fails at least one of the above three bullets.  
 • The function never panics.

No additional lemmas beyond the ones declared above may be assumed; prove that the accumulation does not overflow 64 bits when the length check passes.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn subtract_sequences(a: Vec<i32>, b: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() == b.len(),
        a.len() < 1000,
        forall|k: nat| k < a.len() as nat ==> (#[trigger] a[k as int] - #[trigger] b[k as int] >= i32::MIN && #[trigger] a[k as int] - #[trigger] b[k as int] <= i32::MAX),
    ensures
        result.len() == a.len(),
        forall|i: nat| i < result.len() as nat ==> result[i as int] == a[i as int] - b[i as int],
{
```

# Reasoning
We must understand every clause:

1. Function signature  
   subtract_sequences takes ownership of two heap vectors `a` and `b` (type `Vec<i32>`). It returns a new vector `result` (also `Vec<i32>`). The vectors are not borrowed—they are moved into the function.

2. Preconditions (requires)
   • `a.len() == b.len()` – both inputs must have identical length so a point-wise subtraction is well-defined.  
   • `a.len() < 1000` – the vector length is bounded by 999; a mild finiteness constraint useful for verification termination or for later proofs.  
   • `∀ k:nat . k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] − b[k] is an i32` – for every index within range, the subtraction of the two elements stays inside the closed range `[i32::MIN , i32::MAX]`.  Because the spec works with mathematical `int`s (unbounded) this clause guarantees that performing the subtraction in 32-bit arithmetic cannot overflow in the executable body.

3. Postconditions (ensures)
   • `result.len() == a.len()` – the output vector has exactly the same length as the inputs.  
   • `∀ i:nat . i < result.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[i] − b[i]` – element-wise: the i-th entry of the result equals the difference of the corresponding entries of `a` and `b`.

4. No additional helper spec functions are referenced.

5. Consequences for the implementation
   • A correct solution must allocate or otherwise construct a vector `result` of the same size and compute each `result[i]` as `a[i] - b[i]`.  
   • Because the precondition rules out overflow, the body can safely perform the subtraction using regular `i32` arithmetic.  
   • Ownership of the input vectors is consumed; they cannot be used by the caller afterwards.

6. Indices
   • Quantifiers use `nat` (natural numbers ≥0).  When indexing a vector in the spec the index is cast to `int` with `as int`, which is required by Verus’s vector‐indexing rules.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function named `subtract_sequences`.

Inputs (moved, not borrowed):
  • `a: Vec<i32>` — a vector of 32-bit signed integers  
  • `b: Vec<i32>` — another vector of the same length

Output:
  • `result: Vec<i32>` — a vector of 32-bit signed integers containing the element-wise difference `a[i] - b[i]`.

Preconditions (`requires`):
 1. `a.len() == b.len()` — the two input vectors must have identical length.  
 2. `a.len() < 1000` — the common length is strictly less than 1000.  
 3. For every natural number `k` with `k < a.len()`, the subtraction of the two elements stays inside the i32 range:  
      `i32::MIN ≤ a[k] - b[k] ≤ i32::MAX`.  
    (This guarantees that performing the subtraction at run-time cannot overflow.)

Postconditions (`ensures`):
 1. `result.len() == a.len()` — the output vector length equals the input length.  
 2. For every natural number `i` with `i < result.len()`, the i-th element of the result equals the i-th element of `a` minus the i-th element of `b`:  
      `result[i] == a[i] - b[i]`.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required. Any body that constructs the vector satisfying these contracts—typically by iterating from 0 to `a.len() - 1`, subtracting corresponding elements, and pushing the differences into a fresh `Vec<i32>`—will meet the specification.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is trivial because its sole precondition (`n < 1`) collapses the entire domain to the single value `0`.  
To “raise the bar” we keep the spirit of *small, self-contained* functions but require non-trivial arithmetic facts or more intricate predicates that force the learner to use quantified reasoning, bit-level arithmetic, or algebraic identities.  

Below are three strictly harder, yet still well-scoped, alternatives:

1. Bit-level reasoning about the position of the highest `1`-bit.  
2. Number-theoretic reasoning about a modular inverse.  
3. Slice reasoning that combines arithmetic facts with frame conditions.

Each description is self-contained and detailed enough for a spec generator to create a Verus contract without additional context.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the exact contract below.

Function signature  
fn highest_set_bit(n: u64) -> (k: u8)

• Parameters  
  – n: u64 – an unsigned 64-bit integer, not zero.

• Return value  
  – k: u8 – the index of the most significant `1`-bit of `n`, counting from 0 for the least significant bit.

• Preconditions (requires)  
  1. n > 0                                          // value must be non-zero  
  2. n < (1u128 << 64) as u64                      // redundant but fixes domain explicitly  
  3. (n & (n - 1)) != 0 ==> k < 63                 // separate uncommon constraint in post

• Postconditions (ensures)  
  1. 0u8 <= k < 64u8                               // result is a valid bit index  
  2. (1u64 << k) <= n                              // the bit *k* itself is set  
  3. n < (1u64 << (k + 1))                         // no more significant bits are set  
  4. forall j: u8 :: 0u8 <= j < k ==> (n & (1u64 << (j as u64))) == 0  
     // every less significant index *below* k may be zero, but none above are one  
  5. (1u64 << k) & n == (1u64 << k)                // redundantly states exact equality

No auxiliary public functions are allowed; private ghost helpers and lemmas are permitted.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the exact contract below.

Function signature  
fn modular_inverse(a: u128, m: u128) -> (inv: u128)

• Parameters  
  – a: u128 – the residue whose inverse we seek.  
  – m: u128 – the modulus; limited to a 64-bit range but stored in 128 bits.

• Return value  
  – inv: u128 – the least-non-zero residue such that (a × inv) ≡ 1 (mod m).

• Preconditions (requires)  
  1. 2 ≤ m ≤ (1u128 << 63) − 1            // large yet practical modulus  
  2. 0 < a < m                             // a is a proper residue  
  3. gcd(a, m) == 1                        // a is *coprime* to m (uncommon explicit).  
                                           // A helper spec-fn `gcd` may be declared.  

• Postconditions (ensures)  
  1. 1 ≤ inv < m                           // canonical representative, never zero  
  2. (a * inv) % m == 1                    // multiplicative inverse property  
  3. forall x: u128 :: 1 ≤ x < m && (a * x) % m == 1 ==> x == inv  
     // uniqueness of the inverse in the reduced residue system  
  4. (m - inv) * a % m == m - 1            // complementary fact useful for tests

The implementation may invoke ghost-level extended-Euclid lemmas but must keep executable
code free of panics.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the exact contract below.

Function signature  
fn even_sum(xs: &mut [u32]) -> (s: u64)

• Parameters  
  – xs: &mut [u32] – a mutable slice whose length is strictly positive and at most 100.

• Return value  
  – s: u64 – the arithmetic sum of all elements, promoted to 64 bits.

• Preconditions (requires)  
  1. xs.len() > 0 && xs.len() ≤ 100  
  2. forall i: usize :: i < xs.len() ==> xs[i] % 2 == 0    // every element is even  
  3. xs.len() % 2 == 0                                     // less common: length is *even*  

• Postconditions (ensures)  
  1. s == sum(xs@)                                         // `xs@` denotes the final slice value  
  2. s % 2 == 0                                            // result remains even  
  3. xs == old(xs)                                         // function is *pure* w.r.t. slice state  
  4. s ≤ 100 * (u32::MAX as u64)                           // avoids overflow in proof space  
  5. forall i: usize :: i < xs.len() ==> xs[i] ≤ old(xs[i]) // non-expansive frame check

An auxiliary pure spec-fn `sum` over sequences may be declared.  
Loops must have a ghost variant and preserve the above invariants.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task (“return the maximum element of a non-empty vector”) is solved by a single scan and only two classical post-conditions (existence and maximality).  
To raise the difficulty, I prepared three alternative specifications.  Each keeps the basic flavour (scanning a vector) but adds one or more of the following:

• extra outputs that must satisfy subtle tie-breaking rules (earliest index),  
• requirements that relate *two* extreme values at *two different* indices,  
• global counting constraints that force the implementer to reason about frequencies (majority element).  

All three versions retain simple size bounds (< 1000) so that the spec writer need not worry about machine overflow, yet they each require additional logical steps beyond the “maximum of a list” example.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function with the following specification.

Signature  
    fn arg_max(values: &Vec<int>) -> (max: int, idx: int)

Inputs  
    • values: immutable reference to a vector of mathematical integers.

Outputs  
    • max: the element’s value that is maximal in the vector.  
    • idx: the *smallest* index at which that maximal value occurs.

Preconditions  
    1. Non-emptiness: values.len() > 0.

    fn top_two(values: &Vec<int>) -> (first_max: int, second_max: int)

Outputs

       and i may equal j only if the values differ (impossible here).  
    3. Maximality of first_max: ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < values.len() ⇒ values[k] ≤ first_max.  
    4. “Second-place” property:  
       ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < values.len() ∧ values[k] < first_max ⇒ values[k] ≤ second_max.

Design and verify a Verus-Rust executable function with the exact contract below.

Function signature  
fn to_seq(a: &[int], i: usize) -> (res: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a – an immutable slice of Verus mathematical integers (`int`).  
• i – a starting index inside the slice (type `usize`).

Return value  
• res – a vector of `int` that will contain the suffix of `a` beginning at position `i`.

Preconditions  
1. i ≤ a.len() (the starting index is at or before the end of the slice).  
2. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2 (the slice is strictly shorter than half of the largest representable `usize`, preventing any subsequent length arithmetic from overflowing).

Postconditions  
1. res.len() == a.len() − i.  
   Thus the result’s length equals the number of elements from index i to the end of the slice.  
2. ∀ j : nat • j < res.len() ⇒ res[j] == a[i + j].  
   Every element of the result is exactly the element of the original slice that is j positions after i, preserving order.

Together, these postconditions mean that res is the exact suffix of a that starts at index i (empty when i == a.len()).

Termination metric  
decreases a.len() − i.  
This metric allows the verifier to justify any internal loop or recursion that progressively shortens the “remaining suffix.”

Implementation notes for the solver  
• Allocate a new Vec<int> whose capacity equals a.len() − i.  
• Copy or move the elements a[i], a[i+1], …, a[a.len()−1] into it.  
• The pre-condition on a.len() ensures that computations such as a.len() − i and i + j stay within the `usize` range, so no explicit overflow checks are needed.  
• When i == a.len(), simply return an empty vector.

Re-implementing from scratch using only the above description will reproduce the original specification exactly.

```rust
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;

verus! {

/// Specification function for checking if a sequence contains 'z' or 'Z'
pub open spec fn contains_z(s: Seq<char>) -> bool {
    exists|i: int| 0 <= i && i < s.len() && (s[i] == 'z' || s[i] == 'Z')
}

/// Function to check if a string contains 'z' or 'Z'
fn contains_z_func(s: &[char]) -> (result: bool)
    requires
        s.len() > 0,
    ensures
        result <==> contains_z(s@),
{
```

# Reasoning
We must distill the specification:

1. Helper spec function contains_z:
   - Arguments: s: Seq<char> (ghost sequence of characters).
   - Returns bool.
   - Definition: returns true iff there exists an integer index i within bounds (0 ≤ i < s.len()) such that the character at that index is either lowercase 'z' or uppercase 'Z'.
   - Public (`pub`) and `open` so can be used outside the module and unfolded in proofs.

2. Executable function contains_z_func:
   - Inputs:
       - s: &[char] — an immutable slice of characters (in Rust, a view onto an array or part of a Vec).
   - Output:
       - result: bool.
   - Preconditions (`requires`):
       - s.len() > 0 — the slice must be non-empty. (The spec deliberately disallows empty slices.)
   - Postconditions (`ensures`):
       - result <==> contains_z(s@)
         (<==> is logical equivalence): After the function returns, result must be true exactly when the ghost view of the slice `s@` satisfies contains_z. In other words, result is true iff the slice contains at least one 'z' or 'Z', and false otherwise.
   - No other constraints.

Important details:
• `s@` is Verus notation turning the concrete slice into a pure sequence (Seq<char>) used only in specifications.
• The spec function uses `exists` quantifier; implementation may be any algorithm that produces a Boolean consistent with this logical condition.
• Since the slice cannot be empty (precondition), implementer can safely dereference elements if desired, but emptiness must still be handled logically.

# Final Description
<<<
We need two items: a pure specification helper and an executable verified function.

1. Specification helper

   pub open spec fn contains_z(s: Seq<char>) -> bool

   Purpose: Determine whether a given ghost sequence of characters contains at least one ‘z’ or ‘Z’.

   Formal definition:
       contains_z(s)  ≔  ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ (s[i] = ‘z’ ∨ s[i] = ‘Z’)

   That is, it returns true exactly when some index inside the bounds of the sequence holds either the lowercase or uppercase letter z. It is marked `pub open` so callers may both use and unfold it in proofs.

2. Executable verified function

   fn contains_z_func(s: &[char]) -> (result: bool)

   Inputs
       • s : immutable slice of `char`.  
   Output
       • result : bool – should state whether the slice contains at least one ‘z’ or ‘Z’.

   Preconditions (requires)
       • s.len() > 0   — the slice must contain at least one character.

   Postconditions (ensures)
       • result  ⇔  contains_z(s@)

         (`s@` is the ghost view of the slice as Seq<char>; ⇔ is logical equivalence.)  
         After execution, `result` must be true if and only if the slice’s mathematical sequence contains either ‘z’ or ‘Z’, and false otherwise.

   No other constraints exist; any implementation strategy (linear scan, etc.) that satisfies the contract is acceptable.

Implementers must therefore:
   • Respect the non-empty precondition.
   • Compute a Boolean that mirrors the helper spec precisely.
   • Provide a proof (implicit or explicit) that the implementation meets the equivalence postcondition.

This description contains everything necessary to recreate the specification exactly.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is quite straightforward: produce the suffix of a slice and prove two direct post-conditions.  
To make the task a little harder we can:

• Keep the overall flavour (“work on a slice, starting at index i”), yet do something more interesting with the data.  
• Introduce an extra argument or a helper specification (e.g. a predicate) so that the student has to quantify over more cases.  
• Require the function to establish properties that are slightly trickier to prove (reversal, subsequence filtering, prefix sums).  
• Preserve soundness by repeating the essential overflow guard on `a.len()`.

Below are three standalone, strictly‐worded specifications that satisfy these goals.  Each one is longer by ~10 words, adds at least one new reasoning step, and replaces the very common “copy all elements verbatim” requirement with a more specific behaviour.


# Output
<<<
Design and formally verify, in Verus (Rust), the executable function described below.

Function signature  
fn reversed_suffix(a: &[int], i: usize) -> (res: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a – an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
• i – a starting index inside the slice (`usize`).

Return value  
• res – a vector containing the elements of `a` from position i to the end, but in reverse order.

Preconditions  
1. i ≤ a.len().  
2. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2.

Postconditions  
1. res.len() == a.len() − i.  
2. ∀ j : nat • j < res.len() ⇒ res[j] == a[a.len() − 1 − j].  
   (Every element of the result equals the corresponding element of the original suffix, counted from the end, establishing perfect reversal.)
3. When i == a.len(), res is the empty vector.

Termination metric  
decreases a.len() − i.

Implementation hints  
• Allocate a new vector with capacity a.len() − i.  
• Iterate backward from a.len()−1 down to i, pushing each element onto res.  
• No overflow checks are needed thanks to the precondition on a.len().
>>>

<<<
Create and verify a Verus-Rust function that filters a suffix according to an arbitrary predicate.

Function signature  
fn filter_suffix(
    a: &[int],
    i: usize,
    pred: spec fn (int) -> bool
) -> (res: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a – slice of mathematical integers.  
• i – starting index of the suffix.  
• pred – a pure specification-time predicate over `int`.

Return value  
• res – the subsequence of a[i ..] that contains only those elements satisfying pred, maintaining their original order.

Preconditions  
1. i ≤ a.len().  
2. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2.

Postconditions  
1. ∀ j : nat • j < res.len() ⇒ pred(res[j]).  
2. ∀ k : nat •
       i ≤ k < a.len() ∧ pred(a[k]) ⇒
       ∃ j : nat • j < res.len() ∧ res[j] == a[k] ∧
            (∀ m : nat • m < j ⇒ index_of(a, res[m]) < k).  
   (Every qualifying element of the original suffix appears exactly once in res, and the relative order is preserved.)
3. res.len() ≤ a.len() − i.  
4. When no element of a[i ..] satisfies pred, res is empty.

Termination metric  
decreases a.len() − i.

Implementation notes  
• A simple two-pass algorithm is acceptable (first count, then allocate), but a single forward pass with `push` is fine as well.
>>>

<<<
Specify and implement a function that computes prefix-sums over a slice’s suffix.

Function signature  
fn suffix_prefix_sums(a: &[int], i: usize) -> (res: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a – slice of mathematical integers.  
• i – starting index (`usize`).

Return value  
• res – a vector whose j-th entry equals the sum of a[i .. i+j].

Preconditions  
1. i ≤ a.len().  
2. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2.

Postconditions  
1. res.len() == a.len() − i.  
2. ∀ j : nat • j < res.len() ⇒
       res[j] == ∑_{k=0}^{j} a[i + k].  
   (Thus res[0] == a[i], res[1] == a[i]+a[i+1], ….)  
3. If ∀ k : nat • i ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≥ 0 then
       ∀ j : nat • j+1 < res.len() ⇒ res[j] ≤ res[j+1].  
   (Monotonicity bonus property when the slice is non-negative.)

Termination metric  
decreases a.len() − i.

Implementation tips  
• Maintain a running `sum` while iterating from i upward, pushing the updated sum into res each step.  
• Because `int` is unbounded in Verus, no arithmetic overflow checks are needed, yet providing the monotonicity lemma may still require an extra ghost proof.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;
use vstd::seq::*;

verus! {

/// Specification function
pub open spec fn all_elements_equal(a: Seq<int>, n: int) -> bool
    recommends
        a.len() > 0,
{
    forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> a[i] == n
}

/// Proof function
fn all_elements_equal_func(a: &[int], n: int) -> (result: bool)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        result ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> a[i] == n,
        !result ==> exists|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() && a[i] != n,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We need to describe:

1. Spec function `all_elements_equal`.
   - Inputs: sequence a of ints, integer n.
   - Recommends a.len() > 0; meaning caller should only use spec function when sequence not empty.
   - Returns bool: true if every element equals n. Implementation is universal quantification.

2. Proof function `all_elements_equal_func`.
   - Inputs: slice a of ints (runtime array of mathematical ints), integer n.
   - Precondition requires a.len() > 0.
   - Output result: bool.
   - Postconditions:
     - If result is true, then for all indices i within bounds, a[i] == n.
     - If result is false, then there exists an index i within bounds such that a[i] != n.
   => So result is "are all elements equal to n?".

Important details: 'int' is mathematical unbounded integer. Vector length positive.

No postcondition requiring equivalence; only implication both ways capturing semantics: equivalent but parted.

Implementation: compute bool; verifying post.

Now produce final description.

# Reasoning
The original exercise is straightforward: two non-negative integers in, the larger out.  
To make the assignment a little harder while still remaining focused on elementary integer reasoning, I propose three alternative tasks, each of which:

• Keeps the spirit of “compute a maximum–like quantity”, so the relationship to the original is visible.  
• Adds one or two extra post-conditions that require an additional proof step (e.g., extra algebraic property, or interaction with another helper), or broadens the domain beyond non-negative integers.  
• Stays small enough that a learner can finish in a reasonable time, yet is rich enough to force them to think about specification design and a slightly more delicate verification proof.

The three variants are:

1. “three_max” – return the largest of three arbitrary mathematical integers, plus prove that the returned value is unaffected by permuting the arguments.  
2. “bounded_max” – return the maximum of two integers but with an upper bound; prove that the result never exceeds the bound and behaves as ordinary max when both inputs are below the bound.  
3. “list_arg_max” – given a non-empty slice of non-negative integers, return an index whose element is at least as large as every element in the slice (ties allowed).  This introduces quantifiers over the slice and forces the user to combine index-based reasoning with integer inequalities.

Each problem statement is fully self-contained, written so that a code-generation system can create a Verus spec directly from it.

# Output
<<<
Create two items in Verus-Rust.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn three_max(a: int, b: int, c: int) -> int  
   • Purpose: return the largest of the three given mathematical integers.  
   • Definition (reference, not necessarily an in-code definition):  
         if a ≥ b ∧ a ≥ c { a }  
         else if b ≥ a ∧ b ≥ c { b }  
         else { c }

2. Executable, verified function  
   fn three_max_func(a: int, b: int, c: int) -> (r: int)

   Inputs  
     - a: int  
     - b: int  
     - c: int  

   Preconditions (requires)  
     (none – the arguments may be any mathematical integers, positive or negative).

   Output  
     - r: int  

   Postconditions (ensures)  
     1. r ≥ a ∧ r ≥ b ∧ r ≥ c                      (r is at least each argument)  
     2. r == a ∨ r == b ∨ r == c                    (r equals one of the inputs)  
     3. For every permutation p of (a,b,c),  
        three_max_func(p.0, p.1, p.2) == r          (result independent of argument order)

   Hints  
   • Property 3 can be discharged by proving that r == three_max(a,b,c) and that three_max is symmetric in its arguments.  
   • No recursion is needed, but the body will need a few nested `if` expressions, plus at least one short lemma about symmetry.  
>>>

<<<
Create two items in Verus-Rust.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn bounded_max(x: int, y: int, upper: int) -> int  
   • Purpose: return min(max(x, y), upper); i.e. the larger of x and y, but not exceeding `upper`.

2. Executable, verified function  
   fn bounded_max_func(x: int, y: int, upper: int) -> (r: int)

   Inputs  
     - x: int  
     - y: int  
     - upper: int  

   Preconditions (requires)  
     - upper ≥ 0                                  (we bound with a non-negative ceiling)

   Output  
     - r: int  

   Postconditions (ensures)  
     1. r ≤ upper                                 (never exceeds the ceiling)  
     2. r ≥ x ∧ r ≥ y ∧ r == x ∨ r == y ∨ r == upper  
                                                 (r is either one of the inputs or the ceiling)  
     3. If x ≤ upper ∧ y ≤ upper  ⇒  r == max(x,y)  
                                                 (behaves like ordinary max when both below bound)  
     4. If upper ≤ x  ⇒  r == x   and  if upper ≤ y  ⇒  r == y  
                                                 (when an argument already reaches the ceiling)  

   Together these clauses fully characterise the clamped maximum.  A straightforward chain of `if` / `else` checks suffices, but the proof must show the conditional commitments of clauses 3 and 4.
>>>

<<<
Create three items in Verus-Rust.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn slice_max(s: &Seq<int>) -> int  
   • Purpose: mathematically, the maximum element of a non-empty sequence of integers.  
   • Definition (informal): the unique m ∈ s such that ∀e ∈ s. e ≤ m.

2. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn is_arg_max_index(s: &Seq<int>, i: int) -> bool  
   • Purpose: true exactly when 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ ∀k. 0 ≤ k < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≥ s[k].  

3. Executable, verified function  
   fn arg_max_index(s: &Vec<int>) -> (idx: int)

   Inputs  
     - s: &Vec<int>              (the function borrows but does not mutate the vector)

   Preconditions (requires)  
     - s.len() > 0               (slice must be non-empty)  
     - ∀k. 0 ≤ k < s.len() ⇒ s[k] ≥ 0        (all elements are non-negative)

   Output  
     - idx: int                  (an index into the vector)

   Postconditions (ensures)  
     1. 0 ≤ idx < s.len()                                   (idx is in range)  
     2. is_arg_max_index(s, idx)                            (element at idx is ≥ every element)  
     3. s[idx] == slice_max(s)                              (returned element equals mathematical max)  

   Complexity requirement  
     - The implementation must run in O(s.len()) time and O(1) extra space; i.e. one linear scan without allocation.

   Additional note  
   • Ties are allowed: if several indices hold the maximum value, any one of them may be returned, as long as all obligations above are satisfied.
>>>

Implement a formally-verified method `binary_search` for the struct `Benchmark2`.

Signature  
    fn binary_search(&self, a: &[int], key: int) -> (result: i32)

Behaviour  
    • The function performs a binary search for `key` inside the slice `a`, which is known to be sorted in non-decreasing order.  
    • If `key` is found, it must return an index at which the key is stored (as a 32-bit signed integer).  
    • If `key` is not present, it must return –1.

Inputs  
    – `a`: immutable slice of unbounded integers, length ≥ 1 and < i32::MAX.  
    – `key`: the integer value to look for.  
    – `&self` is not used by the algorithm; it is included only because the function is defined inside an `impl`.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. `a.len() > 0` – the slice is non-empty.  
    2. `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize` – its length fits into a signed 32-bit integer.  
    3. Sortedness: `∀ i, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j]`.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. The return value respects the range: `–1 ≤ result < a.len() as i32`.  
    2. If `result ≥ 0`, then `a[result] == key`; i.e., the key appears exactly at the returned index.  
    3. If `result == –1`, then the key is absent: `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ key`.

Notes for the implementer  
    • Returning any valid index that contains the key satisfies the contract; the first or last occurrence is not required.  
    • The caller is guaranteed that converting the index between `i32` and Verus’ `int` is safe because of the length bound.  
    • Any algorithmic strategy (iterative or recursive) that meets the specification and terminates is acceptable; a classic logarithmic-time binary search loop is the natural choice.  
    • No ghost state or additional lemmas are necessary beyond what is stated.

# Reasoning
The original assignment only asks learners to prove three “simple” correctness properties of an ordinary binary‐search:  
1) returned index is in range, 2) if the index is non-negative it really holds the key, and 3) if the result is –1 the key is absent.  
All three can be discharged with a single standard loop invariant that shrinks an interval.  
To make the exercise moderately harder we can:

• Strengthen the post-conditions so that they speak not only about *some* occurrence of the key, but e.g. about the *left-most* one, or about the precise “insertion point” that would keep the slice sorted.  
• Force the spec to account for duplicates, which means the proof must go beyond plain “there exists” arguments and has to reason with ∀/∃ minimality.  
• Add less common behaviour such as “Java-like” negative return values encoding the insertion point, or searching in a *rotated* sorted slice.  
• Keep the signature essentially the same so that the learner still writes one pure function with a proof, but now needs at least one additional ghost variable or lemma and a slightly more involved invariant.

Below are three stand-alone, self-contained problem statements that incorporate those ideas while staying close enough to the original to be recognisably related.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally-verified method `binary_search_leftmost` for the struct `Benchmark2`.

Signature  
    fn binary_search_leftmost(&self, a: &[int], key: int) -> (result: i32)

Behaviour  
    • The slice `a` is sorted in non-decreasing order and may contain duplicate values.  
    • The method must perform a binary search for `key`.  
    • If the key is present, it must return the *smallest* index at which it occurs.  
    • If the key is absent, it must return –1.

Inputs  
    – `a`: immutable slice of mathematical integers, length ≥ 1 and < i32::MAX.  
    – `key`: the integer value to locate.  
    – `&self` is unused but required by the surrounding `impl`.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. 0 < a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  
    2. Sortedness: ∀ i, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. –1 ≤ result < a.len() as i32.  
    2. If result ≥ 0 then  
       a) a[result] == key, and  
       b) ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < result ⇒ a[k] < key   (i.e. `result` is the leftmost position of `key`).  
    3. If result == –1 then ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ key.

Notes  
    • The proof now needs to establish the minimality clause 2b in addition to the usual correctness facts; expect to maintain two coordinated loop invariants.  
    • Returning any index ≥ 0 that satisfies the minimality clause is accepted, even if duplicates exist.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified method `rotated_binary_search` for the struct `Benchmark2`.

Signature  
    fn rotated_binary_search(&self, a: &[int], key: int) -> (result: i32)

Behaviour  
    • The slice `a` is produced by taking a strictly increasing array and *rotating* it once:  
      ∃ p:int • 0 ≤ p ≤ a.len() ∧  
      (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < p–1 ⇒ a[i] < a[i+1]) ∧  
      (∀ i:int • p ≤ i < a.len()–1 ⇒ a[i] < a[i+1]) ∧  
      a.last() < a.first()   when 0 < p < a.len().  
      (If p == 0 the slice is already strictly increasing.)  
    • The function must find `key` in this rotated sorted slice.  
    • Return any index containing the key, or –1 if the key is absent.

Inputs  
    – `a`: immutable slice of mathematical integers, length ≥ 1 and < i32::MAX.  
    – `key`: the integer being sought.  
    – `&self` is unused.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. 0 < a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  
    2. Rotated-sortedness as described above (exactly one ascending “wrap-around” point).

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. –1 ≤ result < a.len() as i32.  
    2. If result ≥ 0 then a[result] == key.  
    3. If result == –1 then ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ key.

Hints  
    • You will likely need an auxiliary lemma showing that one of the two monotone segments must contain the key.  
    • A classic O(log n) algorithm that first locates the pivot and then does a standard binary search on the appropriate half is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified method `binary_search_with_insertion` for the struct `Benchmark2`.

Signature  
    fn binary_search_with_insertion(&self, a: &[int], key: int) -> (result: i32)

Behaviour  
    • The slice `a` is sorted in non-decreasing order (duplicates allowed).  
    • If the key is present, return any index holding the key (non-negative).  
    • If the key is absent, return a *negative* value that encodes the insertion point via  
          insertion_index = –result – 1.  
      After inserting `key` at that index the slice would still be sorted.

Inputs  
    – `a`: immutable slice of mathematical integers, 0 ≤ len < i32::MAX.  
    – `key`: integer to search.  
    – `&self` is unused.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  
    2. ∀ i, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. If result ≥ 0 then  
         a[result] == key ∧ 0 ≤ result < a.len() as i32.  
    2. If result < 0 then let ins:int = –result – 1 in  
         0 ≤ ins ≤ a.len() ∧  
         (∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < ins ⇒ a[k] < key) ∧  
         (∀ k:int • ins ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ key < a[k]).  
      (The returned insertion point keeps the slice strictly sorted.)  
    3. The function terminates.

Remarks  
    • This specification mirrors the contract of Java’s `Arrays.binarySearch` and Rust’s `slice::binary_search`.  
    • Proving property 2 requires reasoning about indices that can equal `a.len()` (tail insertion).
>>>

    fn pairwise_addition(a: &Vec<int>) -> (result: Vec<int>)

Inputs

# Reasoning
The specification describes a pure, deterministic function called `add_arrays` that builds a new vector by element-wise addition of two input slices.

1. Inputs  
   • `a`: `&[int]` — an immutable slice containing mathematical integers (Verus `int`).  
   • `b`: `&[int]` — another immutable slice of the same element type.  
   Both are executable, run-time slices.

2. Output  
   • `c`: `Vec<int>` — a newly allocated, owned vector of mathematical integers that will store the pairwise sums.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a. `a.len() == b.len()` — the two slices must have identical length so that elements can be added one-to-one.  
   b. `a.len() < 1000` — for practicality the length is bounded to fewer than 1 000 elements; this can help the verifier with loop reasoning but is logically optional.  
   c. `∀ k:int . 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ a[k] + b[k] < i32::MAX` — for every valid index the sum of the two corresponding elements is non-negative and strictly less than `i32::MAX`.  
      • This guarantees every individual result fits into the positive part of the signed-32 range; although the result type is `int`, the condition prevents overflow in any realistic implementation that would use 32-bit machine arithmetic.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   a. `c.len() == a.len()` — the length of the new vector exactly matches the common length of the inputs.  
   b. `∀ i:int . 0 ≤ i < c.len() ⇒ c[i] == a[i] + b[i]` — each element of `c` equals the sum of the corresponding elements of `a` and `b`.

5. Functional meaning  
   The function must allocate a fresh vector of the same size as the two input slices and fill it so that each position `i` holds `a[i] + b[i]`.  No other side effects are allowed.  The pre-condition about the sums fitting into the 32-bit positive range eliminates any need for wrap-around or saturation semantics; the mathematical addition is well-defined and bounded.

6. Subtleties  
   • The element type is `int`, Verus’s unbounded mathematical integers; however the range pre-condition talks about `i32::MAX`, so an implementation may legitimately use 32-bit arithmetic.  
   • There is no requirement about mutation of the input slices; they must remain unchanged.  
   • The `i32::MAX` bound is strict (“<”), so the largest permitted sum is `i32::MAX – 1`.  
   • The function body can be a simple for-loop, but in Verus the loop will have to be annotated or use the standard library’s `vec_map` combinator in ghost form; any approach that satisfies the contracts is acceptable.

# Final Description

Function name  
    add_arrays

    fn add_arrays(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    • a — an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
    • b — another immutable slice of integers.

Output  
    • c — a freshly allocated `Vec<int>` whose elements are the pairwise sums of `a` and `b`.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() == b.len()              // both slices have identical length  
    2. a.len() < 1000                  // length is strictly less than 1 000  
    3. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ a[k] + b[k] < i32::MAX  
       // for every index the sum of the two input elements is non-negative and fits
       // strictly below the maximum signed-32 value, ruling out overflow.

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. c.len() == a.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < c.len() ⇒ c[i] == a[i] + b[i]  
       // every element of the result equals the corresponding element-wise sum.

Semantics  
    The function must return a vector whose length equals that of the input slices; at each position i (0 ≤ i < length), the element stored in c is exactly a[i] + b[i].  No other values may appear, and the order must be preserved.  The implementation may rely on the pre-condition that every such sum is between 0 (inclusive) and i32::MAX − 1 (inclusive), ensuring that using 32-bit arithmetic is safe.

No helper spec functions or additional lemmas are required; a straightforward loop or iterator-based construction is sufficient provided it is verified to meet the above contracts.

# Reasoning
The original task (“add_arrays”) is essentially linear, element-wise, and uses only two easy-to-state invariants (equal length & no overflow).  
To harden things, I propose three new, stand-alone specifications that each require (1) strictly more logical reasoning steps, (2) slightly rarer constraints, and (3) at least one potential source of confusion (including one deliberately wrong code fragment).  

Problem 1 – “compute_weighted_prefix_sums”  
• Introduces a second slice of odd, positive weights.  
• Requires construction of a *prefix* array, not a pointwise one.  
• Adds a universal quantifier in the post-condition that ranges over **two** indices (to express the summation), thereby forcing an auxiliary lemma or loop invariant.  

Problem 2 – “merge_sorted_unique”  
• Works with sets rather than numeric operations, demanding reasoning about *ordering* and *duplicate removal.*  
• The post-condition couples a permutation-like property (membership equivalence) with a *sorted* guarantee—two orthogonal conditions that cannot be proved with a single trivial invariant.  
• A space upper bound is added to require explicit allocation size reasoning.  

Problem 3 – “rotate_left_in_place”  
• Operates *in-place* on a mutable slice, which is harder in Verus because old vs. new array values must be tracked with `old(...)`.  
• Entails reasoning about *cyclic permutations*—usually implemented with 3-segment reversal logic—which demands at least two loop invariants.  
• Supplies a misleading, incorrect Rust snippet that swaps two halves but forgets the final reversal, luring the learner into subtle off-by-k errors.

Each specification is fully detailed so a spec generator can translate it directly into Verus code.

# Output
<<<
Function name  
    compute_weighted_prefix_sums

Signature  
    fn compute_weighted_prefix_sums(a: &[int], w: &[int]) -> (pref: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    • a — immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
    • w — immutable slice of *odd*, strictly-positive integers used as weights.

Output  
    • pref — freshly allocated `Vec<int>` whose length is a.len() + 1 and where  
             pref[0] = 0, pref[i + 1] = pref[i] + a[i] * w[i] for every valid i.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() == w.len()                                                  // same length  
    2. 0 < a.len() ≤ 1_000                                                 // non-empty & bounded  
    3. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < w.len() ⇒ w[k] > 0 ∧ w[k] % 2 == 1                // weights are odd  
    4. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ |a[k] * w[k]| < i64::MAX / 2            // no 64-bit overflow

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. pref.len() == a.len() + 1                                           // one extra slot  
    2. pref[0] == 0                                                        // base case  
    3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i ≤ a.len() ⇒                                         // cumulative sum
         pref[i] == Σ { a[k] * w[k] | 0 ≤ k < i }                          // (empty sum is 0)
    4. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < pref.len() - 1 ⇒ pref[i] ≤ pref[i + 1]            // monotone non-decreasing

Semantics  
    The function must allocate a vector of length n + 1 (where n = a.len()).  
    The first element is 0. Every subsequent element stores the running total of the
    weighted products up to, *but not including*, that index.  
    Because each weight is odd and positive, the sequence is guaranteed to be
    monotonically non-decreasing (property 4). 64-bit arithmetic must be used to
    respect the overflow condition given in precondition 4.

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original exercise is extremely small: compute the maximum of two mathematical integers with no pre- or post-conditions other than the usual “is at least as large and equals one of the arguments”.  
To make the task more challenging yet still suitable for a beginning Verus learner, we can add some of the following twists:

• Increase the number of inputs and therefore the number of logical comparisons that must be proved.  
• Require the function to return auxiliary information (e.g. an index) whose correctness must also be verified, adding additional quantified conditions.  
• Impose tie-breaking rules so that several implications have to be proved rather than a single disjunction.  
• Move from fixed arity to a variable-length sequence; this forces the learner to reason about quantified properties over an entire `Seq<int>` and handle the empty case with an explicit pre-condition.  
• Require two results (max and second-max), forcing reasoning about ordering between three different values (`max₁ ≥ max₂ ≥ all others`).  
• Add an extra input “upper bound” and define the result as `min(max(a,b), upper)`, which introduces nested conditionals and case splitting in the proof.  

Below are three stand-alone, strictly harder versions that incorporate these ideas.  Each variant is approximately ten or more words longer than the original and replaces the very common “just return the larger value” rule with a more specialised contract.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function named `arg_max3`.

Signature  
    fn arg_max3(a: int, b: int, c: int) -> (m: int, idx: u8)

Inputs  
    • a — a mathematical integer (`int`).  
    • b — a mathematical integer.  
    • c — a mathematical integer.

Outputs  
    • m   — the maximum value among the three inputs.  
    • idx — an unsigned 8-bit integer that indicates which argument was chosen  
            (0 means `a`, 1 means `b`, 2 means `c`).

Preconditions  
    • None.

Postconditions  
    1. 0 ≤ idx < 3  
    2. (idx == 0 ⇒ m == a) ∧ (idx == 1 ⇒ m == b) ∧ (idx == 2 ⇒ m == c)  
    3. m ≥ a ∧ m ≥ b ∧ m ≥ c  
    4. Earliest-occurrence rule: for every j < idx, the j-th input value is  
       strictly smaller than m.  (Consequently, when several arguments share  
       the maximum value the function must pick the one with the lowest index.)  

Interpretation: `m` must equal one of the three arguments and be no smaller
than any of them.  If there is a tie, the first (lowest-index) maximal element
must be selected, and `idx` must faithfully encode that choice.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function named `two_largest`.

Signature  
    fn two_largest(xs: Seq<int>) -> (first: int, second: int)

Input  
    • xs — a finite sequence of mathematical integers.

Outputs  
    • first  — the greatest element of `xs`.  
    • second — the greatest element of `xs` that is strictly smaller than
               `first`; if every element equals `first`, then `second == first`.

Preconditions  
    • xs.len() ≥ 2   (the sequence is non-empty and has at least two elements).

Postconditions  
    1. ∀ i: int :: 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ⇒ xs[i] ≤ first  
    2. ∃ i: int :: 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ∧ xs[i] == first          (maximum present)  
    3. second ≤ first  
    4. ∀ i: int :: 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ∧ xs[i] < first ⇒ xs[i] ≤ second  
    5. ∃ j: int :: 0 ≤ j < xs.len() ∧ xs[j] == second         (second max present)  
    6. second == first  ⇔  (∀ k: int :: 0 ≤ k < xs.len() ⇒ xs[k] == first)

Interpretation: `first` is the usual maximum of the sequence, while `second`
is the maximum among the elements that are strictly smaller than `first`;
when every element is identical, both outputs coincide.  All six clauses must
be proved from the implementation.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function named `clamped_max`.

Signature  
    fn clamped_max(a: int, b: int, upper: int) -> (c: int)

Inputs  
    • a     — a mathematical integer.  
    • b     — another mathematical integer.  
    • upper — an upper-bound integer that acts as a clamp.

Output  
    • c — the value obtained by first taking the larger of `a` and `b`, then
           limiting that result so it never exceeds `upper`.

Preconditions  
    • None.  (In particular, `upper` may be smaller than both inputs.)

Postconditions  
    1. c ≤ upper                                   (never exceeds the clamp)  
    2. c == a ∨ c == b ∨ c == upper                (result source enumerated)  
    3. If max(a, b) ≤ upper then c == max(a, b).  
    4. If max(a, b)  > upper then c == upper.

Interpretation: `clamped_max` implements `min(max(a, b), upper)` in a single
verified step, requiring proof of the two mutually exclusive cases 3 and 4 in
addition to the basic inequalities of clause 1.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Find the minimum element in an array.
fn minimum(a: &[int]) -> (m: int)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        exists|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() && m == a[i],
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> m <= a[i],
{
```

# Reasoning
The function named `minimum` is an executable (non-`spec`) Verus/Rust routine whose purpose is to compute the smallest element of a run-time slice.

1. Arguments  
   • `a: &[int]` – an immutable reference to a slice of `int`.  
     Verus’s `int` is the unbounded mathematical integer type, so arithmetic performed on its values is assumed never to overflow at the specification level.  
   The only precondition imposed on `a` is:
   • `a.len() > 0` – the slice must be non-empty.

2. Return value  
   • `(m: int)` – the function returns a single `int` named `m`.

3. Postconditions  
   The returned `m` must satisfy two logical obligations:
   a) Existence:  
      `exists|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() && m == a[i]`  
      meaning that `m` is actually one of the elements stored in the slice.
   b) Minimality:  
      `forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> m <= a[i]`  
      meaning that `m` is less than or equal to every element of `a`.

   Together, these properties state precisely that `m` is a minimum value of the slice (ties are allowed; any value equal to the minimum is acceptable).

4. No extra helper functions, lemmas, or decreases clauses are required.  
   Any body that provably returns such an `m` (e.g., a simple linear scan) fulfills the specification.

5. Subtleties  
   • Because `int` is unbounded, there are no overflow concerns.  
   • The spec does not require uniqueness—if several elements share the same minimal value, returning any one of them is correct.

# Final Description (put between tags)
<<<
Implement a formally-verified function named `minimum` in Verus-Rust.

Signature:
    fn minimum(a: &[int]) -> (m: int)

Inputs:
    - `a`: immutable slice containing values of type `int` (unbounded mathematical integers).

Precondition (requires):
    - `a.len() > 0` — the slice must contain at least one element.

Output:
    - `m`: an `int` representing the minimum value found in `a`.

Postconditions (ensures):
    1. There exists an index `i` with `0 ≤ i < a.len()` such that `m` equals the element at that index; in other words, `m` is drawn from the slice:
         ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ m == a[i]
    2. For every valid index `i`, the returned value is less than or equal to the element at that index; hence no element is smaller than `m`:
         ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ m ≤ a[i]

Taken together, these conditions guarantee that `m` is a (not necessarily unique) minimum element of the slice. No additional helper specs or lemmas are needed, and arithmetic overflow is impossible because `int` is an unbounded type. Any verified implementation—such as a simple loop that tracks the smallest seen value—will satisfy the specification.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning  
The original exercise only asks for a classical “take the maximum of a non-empty slice” proof, which needs one loop invariant and a simple existential argument.  To make it moderately harder, we can:

1. Ask for additional information together with the maximum (e.g. its first index or its multiplicity).  
   –  This forces the student to track two state variables and to establish a stronger loop invariant.  

2. Add a *uniqueness / minimal-index* requirement rather than the usual “any max will do”.  
   –  This is a less common but still very natural specification.  

3. Require counting how many times the maximum occurs, combining accumulation and comparison.  
   –  This adds a second accumulator and quantifier reasoning for the post-condition.  

4. Include a more involved optimisation criterion (e.g. “largest sum of a non-adjacent subsequence”).  
   –  This introduces dynamic programming style reasoning with two ghost variables per iteration.  

All three proposed variations keep the slice input and the `u64` element type, so they remain recognisable extensions of the original task.  Each is still doable in Verus with a single linear scan, but every one of them needs a noticeably richer specification and a handful of extra proof obligations.

# Output
<<<

Contracts

• Postconditions  
    1. 0 ≤ idx < a.len()                                                   (index is in range).  
    2. m == a[idx]                                                         (returned index really holds the value).  
    3. ∀ i:nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ m                                 (m is ≥ every element).  
    4. ∀ i:nat. 0 ≤ i < idx ⇒ a[i] < m                                     (idx is the *first* position of a maximum).  

No auxiliary spec functions need to be exposed.  
An implementation that performs one linear scan is acceptable, but the verifier must be convinced that all four post-conditions hold for every admissible input.

Signature  
    fn max_and_count(a: &[u64]) -> (m: u64, c: u64)

Contracts  
• Preconditions  
    – The slice is non-empty: `a.len() > 0`.

• Postconditions  
    1. ∀ i:nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ m                                 (m is ≥ every element).  
    2. ∃ i:nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ m == a[i]                                (m actually occurs).  
    3. c == |{ i:nat | 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] == m }|                      (c equals the multiplicity of m).

<<<
Write and prove correct a Verus-Rust function `max_sum_non_adjacent` that computes the maximum possible sum of elements in a slice of `u64` **subject to the constraint that no two chosen elements are adjacent in the original slice** (classical “house robber” problem).  The slice may be empty.

Signature  
    fn max_sum_non_adjacent(a: &[u64]) -> (best: u64)

Contracts  
• Preconditions  
    – None (even an empty slice is allowed).

• Postconditions  
    1. best == max  { ∑_{i∈S} a[i] | S ⊆ {0,…,a.len()−1} ∧ ∀ i,j∈S. i+1 ≠ j }  
       (best equals the largest sum of any subset of indices with no two consecutive indices).  
    2. best ≥ 0  (trivial for `u64`, but must still be asserted).  

Hint for implementers (not part of the spec): a linear dynamic-programming scan keeping two ghost variables—the best sum *including* the previous element and the best sum *excluding* it—will satisfy the proof obligations without requiring unbounded quantifier reasoning at each step.

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: just find *some* longest inner vector and return it.  
To make things a bit harder, I create three variants that introduce extra specifications which
force more subtle reasoning:

1. “Earliest‐longest” – the implementation must return **the first** longest list and also expose
   its index.  The minimal-index tie-breaking property adds a non-trivial second post-condition.

2. “All-longest indices” – the implementation must compute **every** index whose list attains the
   maximum length, return them in a strictly increasing vector, and prove both completeness and
   uniqueness.  Handling a result that is *itself* a vector of indices requires more quantified
   reasoning.

3. “Longest list plus count” – in addition to one longest list the function must output the exact
   count of *all* longest lists.  The implementation therefore needs to compute and verify a global
   aggregate, not just pick an element.

Each variant keeps the same general flavour as the original problem, but the added contractual
clauses (≈10–20 extra words) force extra loops, conditionals, and quantified proofs in Verus.

Design and verify the following in Verus-Rust.

Function signature  
    fn first_max_length_list(lists: Vec<Vec<int>>) -> (idx: nat, max_list: Vec<int>)

Purpose  
    Return the *earliest* (lowest-index) list whose length is at least as large as every other
    inner list’s length, and also return that index.

Inputs  
    • lists – a run-time Vec containing at least one (possibly empty) Vec<int>.

Outputs  
    • idx       – the index inside `lists` at which an earliest maximally long list is found.  
    • max_list  – exactly the vector stored at `lists[idx]`.

Formal contract  
Precondition  
    1. lists.len() > 0                                      — outer vector is non-empty.

Postconditions  
    1. 0 <= idx < lists.len()                               — returned index is in-bounds.  
    2. max_list == lists[idx]                               — returned list is that element.  
    3. ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < lists.len() ⇒ lists[j].len() ≤ max_list.len()  
       (max_list really has maximal length).  
    4. ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < idx  ⇒ lists[j].len() <  max_list.len()  
       (no strictly earlier list is *as long*; idx is minimal among ties).

Notes  
    • If there is only one longest list, its index must be returned.  
    • Because the element type is mathematical `int`, values never affect correctness—only lengths
      and indices matter.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify the following in Verus-Rust.

Function signature  
    fn collect_max_length_indices(lists: Vec<Vec<int>>) -> (indices: Vec<nat>)

Purpose  
    Return *all* indices whose corresponding inner list’s length equals the maximum length
    appearing in `lists`; the returned indices must be in strictly increasing order.

Inputs  
    • lists – a run-time Vec containing at least one (possibly empty) Vec<int>.

Output  
    • indices – a non-empty Vec<nat> satisfying the formal contract below.

Formal contract  
Precondition  
    1. lists.len() > 0

Postconditions  
    1. indices.len() > 0                                            — at least one longest list exists.  
    2. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < indices.len() ⇒  
         (indices[k] < lists.len() ∧ lists[indices[k]].len() == max_len)  
       where max_len == max_{0 ≤ m < lists.len()} lists[m].len().  
    3. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k + 1 < indices.len() ⇒ indices[k] < indices[k+1]  
       (indices are strictly increasing, hence unique).  
    4. ∀ m:int. 0 ≤ m < lists.len() ∧ lists[m].len() == max_len ⇒  
         (∃ p:int. 0 ≤ p < indices.len() ∧ indices[p] == m)  
       (completeness: every longest list’s index is included).

Notes  
    • Only the order of indices (ascending) is constrained; the caller may freely inspect the
      actual lists using the returned positions.  
    • No additional helper parameters are permitted.
>>>

<<<

        -> (max_list: Vec<int>, num_max: nat)

Purpose

Inputs

You must implement and verify two things in Verus-Rust.

1. Pure specification helper

    spec fn sorted(a: Seq<int>) -> bool

    Purpose: define what it means for a sequence of integers to be sorted in non-decreasing order.

    • Parameter  
        a : Seq<int> — an immutable mathematical sequence.

    • Pre-condition (recommends):  
        a ≠ Seq::<int>::empty() — the sequence must be non-empty whenever the predicate is used.

    • Definition:  
        The predicate holds exactly when  
        ∀ j,k:int • 0 ≤ j < k < a.len() ⇒ a[j] ≤ a[k]

    That is, every earlier element is less than or equal to every later element.


2. Executable, verified function

    fn binary_search(a: &[int], value: int) -> (index: i32)

    • Inputs  
        – a : &[int] — an immutable slice of integers that is known to be sorted (see pre-conditions).  
        – value : int — the target value we wish to locate.

    • Output  
        – index : i32

    • Preconditions (requires)  
        1. a.len() > 0                                  (the slice is non-empty)  
        2. sorted(a@)                                   (its ghost view is sorted as defined above)  
        3. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize                  (every valid position fits into an i32)

    • Postconditions (ensures)

        Success case (index ≥ 0):  
            index < a.len() as i32                             
            and a[index as int] == value

        Failure case (index < 0):  
            ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≠ value

        In other words, the function must return a non-negative index that points to an occurrence of value when the value is present, and a negative number (any negative number) when the value is absent.

    • Additional notes  
        – The specification does not dictate which occurrence to return if duplicates exist; any valid occurrence satisfies the contract.  
        – When the value is absent, the single requirement is that the returned index be negative; the exact negative value is left to the implementation.  
        – The pre-condition that the slice length be smaller than i32::MAX ensures the “success” index and the length comparison are representable inside the i32 range.  
        – Implementers will typically write a classical O(log n) binary search loop, but any algorithm that satisfies the contracts is accepted.

Re-implementing the above description verbatim will reproduce the original function specification.

```rust
    requires
        a.len() >= 1,
    ensures

        exists|k: int| 0 <= k < a.len() as int && m == a[k],
{
```
