# Reasoning (you output)
The function specification is straightforward—it describes a routine that returns the maximum value contained in an immutable run-time slice of Verus `int`s (mathematical, unbounded integers).

1. Inputs  
   • `a: &[int]` – an immutable borrow of a slice whose elements live in the mathematical `int` domain.  
   Slices carry a run-time length `a.len()` of type `usize`, but within specifications we convert that to `int` for arithmetic/quantifier ranges (`a.len() as int`).

2. Output  
   • `m: int` – a single mathematical integer.

3. Pre-condition (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() >= 1` – the slice must be non-empty so a maximum is well defined. No other hypothesis is imposed (values can be negative, duplicates allowed, etc.).

4. Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
   • Universal upper-bound: `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ m ≥ a[k]`.  
     This says the returned value is at least as large as every element in the slice.  
   • Witness of attainment: `∃ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ m = a[k]`.  
     There exists an index whose element equals the result. Together with the first clause, this implies `m` is indeed *a* maximum element of the slice (not necessarily unique, but one of the largest values present).

5. Auxiliary spec functions / lemmas  
   None are referenced; the spec is self-contained.

6. Subtleties  
   • The specification reasons over mathematical integers (`int`), so there is no overflow concern.  
   • Because only a slice reference is supplied, the implementation cannot modify the data; it must only read it.  
   • The function must be executable (not a `spec fn`), meaning its body will run at run time while still being verified against the logical conditions above.

A correct implementation will iterate (or recurse) through the slice, compute a running maximum, and return it while proving that the result satisfies the two post-conditions.

  2. Witness of membership: there exists a (possibly non-unique) index whose element equals the result:

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original task asks the learner to
  • define a simple `sorted` predicate, and  
  • implement one flavour of binary-search that merely distinguishes “found” vs “not found”.

That contract is easy because
  – duplicates may be ignored,  
  – no information about the *position* of a missing element is required, and  
  – no bound on the number of loop iterations must be proved.

To make the exercise more challenging we can

1. Demand a *stable* result: when the value exists the function must return its **first** index, forcing the learner to reason about minimality.
2. When the value is absent, require the function to encode the canonical *insertion position* (`-(pos + 1)`) rather than an arbitrary negative number.
3. Offer a variant that returns the whole `[start, end)` range of equal values, combining two binary searches in one verified routine.
4. Add a ghost “step counter” and oblige the implementer to prove an upper bound of `⌈log₂(n)⌉ + 1` iterations, introducing a quantitative reasoning component.

Each of the following three standalone problem statements incorporates at least one of these extra subtleties, uses ≈10–40 extra words, and is written with enough precision that a spec generator can translate it directly into Verus.  

# Output
<<<
You must implement and verify two items in Verus-Rust.

1. Pure specification helper
   spec fn sorted(a: Seq<int>) -> bool

   • Parameter  
       a : Seq<int> — an immutable mathematical sequence.

   • Recommends  
       a ≠ Seq::<int>::empty()

   • Definition  
       sorted(a) holds exactly when  
           ∀ j,k:int • 0 ≤ j < k < a.len() ⇒ a[j] ≤ a[k]

2. Executable, fully verified function
   fn binary_search_first(a: &[int], value: int) -> (index: i32)

   • Inputs  
       – a : &[int]  — immutable slice, mathematically modelled by a@ : Seq<int>  
       – value : int — target value

   • Preconditions (requires)  
       1. a.len() > 0                                    (slice non-empty)  
       2. sorted(a@)                                     (ghost view sorted)  
       3. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize                    (indices fit into i32)

   • Postconditions (ensures)

       Case “value present”  (index ≥ 0):  
         – index < a.len() as i32                                   
         – a[index as int] == value                                 
         – ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < index ⇒ a[k] < value                   // first occurrence

       Case “value absent”   (index < 0):  
         Let insertion_point:int = -(index as int) - 1; then  
         – 0 ≤ insertion_point ≤ a.len()                            
         – ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < insertion_point ⇒ a[k] < value         
         – ∀ k:int • insertion_point ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ value < a[k]   
         (The returned negative number therefore encodes where the value would be inserted.)

   • Additional notes  
       – Any asymptotically logarithmic implementation is acceptable; proof of complexity is **not** required here.  
       – Because minimality is demanded, the learner must reason about strict inequalities on the left side of the array when duplicates exist.
>>>

2. Auxiliary pure predicate

        = ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < idx ⇒ a[k] < v

3. Executable, verified routine
   fn equal_range(a: &[int], value: int) -> (start: i32, end_: i32)

   • Inputs  
       – a : &[int]  
       – value : int

   • Preconditions  
       1. sorted(a@)  
       2. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize

   • Postconditions  
       – 0 ≤ start ≤ end_ ≤ a.len() as i32                               
       – strictly_less_prefix(a@, start as int, value)                   // left strict part  
       – ∀ k:int • start ≤ k < end_ ⇒ a[k] == value                      // equality segment  
       – ∀ k:int • end_ ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ value < a[k]                     // right strict part

   • Informal interpretation  
       The function returns the half-open interval `[start, end_)` that contains **all** occurrences of `value`.  
       If `value` is absent then `start == end_` and the pair designates the correct insertion position.

   • Implementation hint  
       A standard approach is to call two binary searches (lower-bound and upper-bound) or to implement them inline; both must be verified.

          let n:int = a.len();  
          steps ≤ (if n == 0 { 0u32 } else { (integer_log2(n) + 1) as u32 })  
          where integer_log2(n) is the mathematical floor of log₂(n).

       – The routine must maintain a ghost counter tracking the number of loop iterations (or recursive calls) and return it in `steps`.

       – The proof obligation forces the learner to combine functional correctness with a mild, formal performance bound.

nl_desc_oai:
Implement a formally-verified Verus function having the exact signature

    fn search_recursive(a: &Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize, x: i32) -> (k: i32)

that performs a recursive search for the *right-most* occurrence of the value `x` inside the slice of `a` that ranges from index `i` (inclusive) to index `j` (exclusive).

Detailed contract

Inputs
• a — immutable reference to a vector of i32 values.  
• i — lower bound of the search interval (inclusive).  
• j — upper bound of the search interval (exclusive).  
• x — the element to look for.

Preconditions (requires)
1. 0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ a.len()  
2. j − i < 100 000  
3. j ≤ i32::MAX as usize          (so that every legal index fits into a 32-bit signed integer)

Output
• k : i32 — the search result.

Postconditions (ensures)
1. (i ≤ k < j) ∨ (k == −1) — the function either returns a valid index inside the interval or the sentinel −1 signalling failure.  
2. k ≠ −1 ⇒ a[k] == x — if an index is returned, that position indeed stores the value x.  
3. k ≠ −1 ⇒ ∀ r:nat. k < r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ x — when a match is found, there is no later occurrence of x in the interval; thus k is the right-most match within [i, j).  
4. k == −1 ⇒ ∀ r:nat. i ≤ r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ x — if −1 is returned, x does not appear anywhere between i (inclusive) and j (exclusive).

Termination
The recursive algorithm must strictly reduce the interval length; this is captured by the measure `decreases j - i`.

No additional helper specifications are required.  Any implementation that satisfies the above contract will verify, but the simplest is a tail-recursive routine that inspects position `j − 1` and, if necessary, recurses on the shortened interval.

# Reasoning
The original task only asks the learner to locate the right-most occurrence of a given value in the interval `[i, j)`.  
To raise the difficulty we can

•  Return additional information (e.g. the exact number of occurrences) and prove its correctness.  
•  Replace the “plain search” requirement with a less common variant, e.g. “right-most element **≤** `x` in a sorted slice”.  
•  Force the implementer to combine two separate properties (max-element search + right-most choice) so that extra reasoning steps are necessary.  

Below are three independent, stand-alone problem statements that incorporate these ideas.  Each keeps the same flavour (recursive search on an interval) but introduces stricter contracts and/or more facts that must be established, thus demanding more intricate ghost reasoning in Verus.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus function having the exact signature

    fn search_and_count(a: &Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize, x: i32) -> (k: i32, cnt: nat)

that performs a **recursive right-to-left scan** of the slice of `a` ranging from `i` (inclusive) to `j` (exclusive).

Inputs  
• `a` — immutable reference to a vector of 32-bit signed integers.  
• `i` — lower bound of the search interval (inclusive).  
• `j` — upper bound of the search interval (exclusive).  
• `x` — the value to look for.

Preconditions (`requires`)  
1. 0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ a.len()  
2. j − i < 100 000                            // keeps recursion depth modest  
3. j ≤ i32::MAX as usize                     // every legal index fits into `i32`  

Outputs  
• `k` : i32    — right-most index of `x` in `[i, j)`, or −1 if absent.  
• `cnt`: nat   — total number of occurrences of `x` in `[i, j)`.

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
1. (i ≤ k < j) ∨ (k == −1)  
2. k ≠ −1 ⇒ a[k] == x  
3. k ≠ −1 ⇒ ∀ r:nat. k < r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ x    // `k` is the right-most hit  
4. k == −1 ⇔ cnt == 0  
5. cnt == |{ r:nat | i ≤ r < j ∧ a[r] == x }|   // exact occurrence count  
6. cnt > 0 ⇒ a[k] == x                          // consistency between both results

Termination  
Use the measure `decreases j - i`.  No auxiliary specifications are mandatory.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus function having the exact signature

    fn rightmost_le_in_sorted(a: &Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize, x: i32) -> (k: i32)

that searches **a non-decreasing slice** for the right-most element **≤ `x`**.

Inputs  
• `a` — immutable reference to a vector of 32-bit signed integers.  
• `i` — lower bound of the search interval (inclusive).  
• `j` — upper bound of the search interval (exclusive).  
• `x` — comparison pivot.

Preconditions  
1. 0 ≤ i < j ≤ a.len()                        // interval must be non-empty  
2. ∀ r s:nat. i ≤ r < s < j ⇒ a[r] ≤ a[s]    // slice is sorted (non-decreasing)  
3. j − i ≤ 131 072                           // logarithmic search depth bound  
4. j ≤ i32::MAX as usize

Output  
• `k` : i32 — index of the last element ≤ `x`, or −1 if all slice elements are > `x`.

Postconditions  
1. (i ≤ k < j) ∨ (k == −1)  
2. k ≠ −1 ⇒ a[k] ≤ x  
3. k ≠ −1 ⇒ ∀ r:nat. k < r < j ⇒ a[r] > x       // right-most such element  
4. k == −1 ⇒ ∀ r:nat. i ≤ r < j ⇒ a[r] > x      // no qualifying element  
5. The implementation must run in **O(log (j − i))** recursion depth (hint: binary search) and prove this with an appropriate `decreases` tuple.

No helper specifications are required, but an auxiliary ghost lemma asserting the monotonic nature of the slice may be convenient.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus function having the exact signature

    fn rightmost_max(a: &Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize) -> (k: i32)

that returns the **right-most position holding a maximum value** within the slice `[i, j)`.

Inputs  
• `a` — immutable reference to a vector of 32-bit signed integers.  
• `i` — lower bound of the search interval (inclusive).  
• `j` — upper bound of the search interval (exclusive).

Preconditions  
1. 0 ≤ i < j ≤ a.len()        // non-empty interval  
2. j − i < 131 072            // depth restriction  
3. j ≤ i32::MAX as usize

Output  
• `k` : i32 — index of the (right-most) maximum element in `[i, j)`.

Postconditions  
1. i ≤ k < j  
2. ∀ r:nat. i ≤ r < j ⇒ a[r] ≤ a[k]           // `a[k]` is a maximum value  
3. ∀ r:nat. k < r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ a[k]           // choose the right-most among ties  
4. ∀ r:nat. i ≤ r < j ⇒ (a[r] == a[k]) ⇒ r ≤ k   // equivalent formulation of 3  

Termination  
Use the measure `decreases j - i`.  A simple divide-and-conquer recursion that splits the interval in half and then compares the two candidate maxima is sufficient, but any verified implementation is acceptable.
>>>

The function signature and contracts are extremely small, so it is important to list every item explicitly.

   • delimiter: int  
     - A single int value that is intended to be placed between the original elements.  
     - The specification imposes no bounds or other constraints on this value.

3. Output

   • numbers.len() < i32::MAX – the slice must be smaller than the maximum positive 32-bit signed integer.

5. Post-conditions (ensures)  
   • interspersed.len() == if numbers.len() > 0 { 2 * numbers.len() - 1 } else { 0 }  
     - Because the first pre-condition already says numbers is non-empty, the if-branch that actually matters is the true branch:  
       interspersed.len() == 2 * numbers.len() - 1  
     - In other words, the resulting vector must contain exactly twice as many positions as the input slice, minus one.  
     - No constraints are placed on the actual values stored inside interspersed; only its length is specified.

6. Absence of any further guarantees  
   There are no post-conditions describing how the elements of numbers and delimiter are arranged in the output.  The usual semantics of an “intersperse” routine would be to place the delimiter between successive elements, but that behaviour is NOT enforced by the specification.  Any vector that satisfies the length requirement is acceptable.

7. Implementation considerations for a solver  
   • Because all constraints relate only to the length, the simplest correct implementation is to allocate a Vec<int> of the required size and fill it with any arbitrary int values (for example, zeros) or even with repeated copies of delimiter.

# Reasoning
The original task only requires returning one Boolean that agrees with `positive(v@)`.  
To make the verification exercise more interesting we can:

1. Ask for an additional *dependent* value that must itself satisfy a mathematical property (e.g., the **minimum** element, the **sum** in a larger integer domain, or the **index of the first negative**).  
   ‑ This forces the learner to write ghost code that computes and exposes that value and to connect it with the run-time result via additional post-conditions.

2. Replace the common “all elements are non-negative” check by a slightly less common but still easy-to-understand property (e.g., **strictly increasing and non-negative**, or **prefix monotonicity**).

3. Introduce an `Option` return to cover both “all good” and “found a counterexample”, making the control-flow proof slightly trickier.

4. Keep the run-time complexity basically the same (linear), but require a second pass in the spec to describe minimum, sum, or index, thus adding logical—not computational—difficulty.

Below are three standalone, more demanding specifications that follow these ideas and can each be implemented and proved correct in Verus.

# Output

together with the already provided specification helper `positive`
and a new helper `seq_min`.

------------------------------

   – As before: every element of `s` is ≥ 0.

2. spec fn seq_min(s: Seq<i32>) -> i32
   – Requires: s.len() > 0.
   – Returns: the smallest value occurring in `s`.
     Formally:  (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ seq_min(s) ≤ s[i])  ∧
                (∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < s.len() ∧ seq_min(s) == s[j])

Primary executable function
---------------------------
fn mpositive_min(v: &[i32]) -> (b: bool, m: i32)

Inputs
  • v: immutable slice (`&[i32]`) of 32-bit signed integers.

Outputs
  • b: `true` exactly when every element of `v` is non-negative.
  • m: the minimum element of `v` (returned even if b == false).

Preconditions (requires)
  1. v.len() > 0                                                           // non-empty
  2. v.len() < i32::MAX as usize                                           // length fits in `int`
  3. (*additional*) ∀ k:usize • k < v.len() ⇒ v[k] <= i32::MAX − 1         // avoid INT_MIN corner

Postconditions (ensures)
  1. b == positive(v@)
  2. m == seq_min(v@)

Any sound implementation (e.g., single pass that tracks current minimum)
is acceptable, but it must be proved to satisfy all contracts.

Define an executable, formally-verified function in Verus named binary_search that implements the “lower-bound” variant of binary search.

Signature  
    fn binary_search(a: &Vec<int>, key: int) -> (n: usize)

Parameters  
  • a — immutable reference to a vector whose elements are mathematical integers (`int`).  
  • key — an `int` value to be located or positioned in the ordering.

Return value  
  • n — an index of type `usize`.

Pre-condition  
  • The vector is sorted in non-decreasing order:
        ∀ i,j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j]

  No other assumptions (the vector may be empty and key is unconstrained).

Post-conditions  
  1. Index bounds: 0 ≤ n ≤ a.len().  
  2. All elements strictly before n are smaller than the key:
        ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < n ⇒ a[i] < key  
  3. Every element from n onward is at least the key:
        ∀ i:int • n ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ key ≤ a[i]

Intuitive meaning  
  n is the first position at which `key` could be inserted without violating the vector’s sorted order.
  • If `key` appears in the vector, n equals the index of its first occurrence.  
  • If `key` is less than every element, n is 0.  
  • If `key` is greater than every element, n is a.len().

An implementation that performs a standard logarithmic-time binary search and produces such an index will satisfy the specification.

# Reasoning
The original exercise is an elementary, single-result, lower-bound binary search over an `int` vector.  
To raise the level of difficulty we can:

• Add an extra observable (e.g. the number of comparisons) and require a bound on it.  
• Move from concrete `int` to an arbitrary type `T` together with a user-supplied comparator that must be proven to be a total preorder; the student now has to thread the comparator through the algorithm and carry its algebraic properties as ghost assumptions.  
• Ask for a *range* of equal keys (both the first and the first-greater position), forcing the student to reason about two correlated indices.  
• Transform the search into an *in-place* insertion that mutates the vector, making the post-state need to satisfy new shape and ordering properties.

Below are four standalone, progressively harder specifications employing these ideas.  
Each block is completely self-contained so that a spec generator sees only that text.

# Output
<<<
Define an executable, formally-verified function in Verus named binary_search_with_count that implements the “lower-bound” binary search and additionally reports how many comparisons were performed.

Signature  
    fn binary_search_with_count(a: &Vec<int>, key: int) -> (n: usize, comparisons: nat)

Parameters  
  • a — immutable reference to a vector of mathematical integers.  
  • key — the search value.

Return values  
  • n — the first index whose element is ≥ key (as in a standard lower-bound).  
  • comparisons — the number of element-to-key comparisons performed by the concrete implementation.

Pre-conditions  
  • Sortedness: ∀ i, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j]

Post-conditions  
  1. Index bounds: 0 ≤ n ≤ a.len()  
  2. Strict-before property: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < n ⇒ a[i] < key  
  3. From-n-onward property: ∀ i:int • n ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ key ≤ a[i]  
  4. Comparison bound: comparisons ≤ ⌈log₂(a.len() + 1)⌉  
     (Use the usual base-2 logarithm on natural numbers; treat log₂(0) as 0.)  
  5. Soundness of the counter: the algorithm’s observable variable that tracks comparisons must equal the returned value.

Intuitive meaning  
    The function still behaves like a normal lower-bound search but must also expose and formally justify a small-O(log n) comparison count, giving the verifier a concrete upper bound.
>>>

<<<
Implement a generic, formally-verified function in Verus named generic_lower_bound that works for any element type together with an explicit comparator.

Signature  
    fn generic_lower_bound<T>(
        a: &Vec<T>,
        key: T,
        cmp: spec fn (T, T) -> int,
    ) -> (n: usize)
        where T: Clone

Semantic meaning of cmp  
    cmp(x, y) < 0  ⇒  x ≺ y  
    cmp(x, y) = 0  ⇒  x ≡ y  
    cmp(x, y) > 0  ⇒  x ≻ y

Ghost requirements on cmp  
  • Total preorder:
        – Reflexive:     ∀x. cmp(x,x) = 0  
        – Transitive:    ∀x,y,z. cmp(x,y) ≤ 0 ∧ cmp(y,z) ≤ 0 ⇒ cmp(x,z) ≤ 0  
        – Total:         ∀x,y. cmp(x,y) ≤ 0 ∨ cmp(y,x) ≤ 0

Parameters  
  • a — immutable vector of `T`.  
  • key — value to locate.  
  • cmp — comparison function used both in spec and code.

Pre-condition  
  • Sorted w.r.t. cmp: ∀ i,j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ cmp(a[i], a[j]) ≤ 0.

Post-conditions  
  1. 0 ≤ n ≤ a.len()  
  2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < n ⇒ cmp(a[i], key) < 0  
  3. ∀ i:int • n ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ cmp(key, a[i]) ≤ 0

Extra requirement  
  • No element of `a` is moved or cloned more than O(log a.len()) times in the executable code (track with a ghost counter).  (This avoids naïve linear-time generic solutions.)

The function must be executable, perform an ordinary logarithmic-time binary search using `cmp`, and satisfy the above logical obligations.
>>>

<<<
Define an executable, formally-verified function in Verus named equal_range that returns the half-open index range of all occurrences of a key inside a sorted vector.

Signature  
    fn equal_range(a: &Vec<int>, key: int) -> (start: usize, end: usize)

Pre-condition  
  • Sorted non-decreasing: ∀ i,j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j]

Post-conditions  
  1. 0 ≤ start ≤ end ≤ a.len()  
  2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < start ⇒ a[i] <  key  
  3. ∀ i:int • start ≤ i < end ⇒ a[i] == key  
  4. ∀ i:int • end ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ key <  a[i]  
  5. Minimality:  
        – If start > 0 then a[start-1] <  key  
        – If end < a.len() then key <  a[end]

Intuitive meaning  
    The slice a[start .. end] contains exactly the keys equal to `key`; if `key` is absent then start == end is the lower-bound position where it could be inserted.

The implementation should perform two logarithmic-time binary searches (or an equivalent fused search) and must prove that the two returned indices satisfy all stated properties, particularly their mutual consistency.
>>>

<<<
Write a function in Verus named sorted_insert that performs an in-place, stable insertion of a value into a sorted vector while maintaining order.

Signature  
    fn sorted_insert(a: &mut Vec<int>, key: int) -> (idx: usize)

Pre-condition  
  • Sortedness: ∀ i,j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j]

Post-conditions  
  1. a.len() == old(a.len()) + 1  
  2. Sortedness preserved: ∀ i,j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j]  
  3. idx is the position into which `key` was inserted:
        – 0 ≤ idx < a.len()  
        – a[idx] == key  
        – ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < idx ⇒ a[i] ≤ key  
        – ∀ i:int • idx < i < a.len() ⇒ key ≤ a[i]
  4. Stability: All elements equal to `key` that were already present now appear before the newly inserted `key`.  
     Formally, if old_a represents the vector before insertion, then
        ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < old_a.len() ∧ old_a[i] == key ⇒
            (∃ j:int • j < idx ∧ a[j] == key ∧ a[j] == old_a[i])

The algorithm must be asymptotically O(log n) comparisons plus O(n) element moves (the usual insert-into-sorted-array strategy) and prove all above properties, including the subtle stability guarantee.
>>>

```rust
verus! {

/// Reverses an array of characters.
fn reverse(a: &[char]) -> (b: Vec<char>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        a.len() == b.len(),
        forall|x: nat| 0 <= x < a.len() ==> b[x as int] == a[a.len() - 1 - x as int],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The function `reverse` describes the classical “reverse a collection” operation, formalised so that Verus can prove it correct.

1. Inputs  
   • `a: &[char]` — an immutable slice of Rust `char`s (a `char` is a 32-bit Unicode scalar value).  
   The slice is read-only inside the function; no mutation of `a` is allowed.

2. Output  
   • `b: Vec<char>` — a fresh vector that will contain the reversed characters.

3. Return variable name  
   • The returned vector is bound to the identifier `b`.

4. Pre-condition (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` — the slice must contain at least one element.  
     This guarantees that the arithmetic `a.len() - 1` appearing in the post-condition is defined.

5. Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
   • `a.len() == b.len()` — the reversed vector must have exactly the same length as the input slice.  
   • `forall |x: nat| 0 <= x < a.len() ==> b[x as int] == a[a.len() - 1 - x as int]`  
     Translation: for every natural number index `x` in the legal range `[0, a.len())`, the element at position `x` in `b` must equal the element at the mirror-image position in `a`, namely `a.len() - 1 - x`.  
     The explicit `as int` casts are required by Verus to turn the `nat` variable into an `int` usable for indexing.

6. Supporting spec functions / lemmas  
   • None are referenced; the specification is entirely contained in the `requires`/`ensures` clauses.

7. Behavioural meaning  
   Together the post-conditions precisely state that `b` is the character-wise reversal of `a`.  
   If `a = ['h','e','l','l','o']`, then `b` must be `['o','l','l','e','h']`.  
   The contract imposes no constraints on performance or algorithm; any implementation that builds a vector satisfying these two conditions is acceptable.

8. Edge cases  
   • Because of the `a.len() > 0` pre-condition, the empty slice never occurs.  
   • Length 1 slices are allowed; the mirror mapping then maps 0 to 0, so `b[0]` must equal `a[0]`.

9. How to reconstruct the spec  
   – Function name: `reverse`  
   – Signature: `fn reverse(a: &[char]) -> (b: Vec<char>)`  
   – Pre-condition: `a.len() > 0`  
   – Post-conditions:  
       1. lengths equal,  
       2. universal quantifier equating each `b[x]` with `a[len-1-x]`.  
   No other clauses or helpers are present.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Implement a formally verified function `reverse` that produces the character-wise reversal of a non-empty slice.

Function signature  
    fn reverse(a: &[char]) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
    a : an immutable slice of `char` (32-bit Unicode scalar values).

Output  
    b : a `Vec<char>` containing the same characters as `a` but in reverse order.

Specification

Pre-condition (requires)  
    • a.len() > 0 — the caller must supply a slice that contains at least one element.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. a.len() == b.len() — the resulting vector has exactly the same length as the input slice.  
    2. ∀ x:nat • 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ b[x] == a[a.len() − 1 − x] — for every valid index, the element placed at position x in the output equals the element that was at the mirror position (len−1−x) in the input.  
       (In Verus syntax this is written  
          forall|x: nat| 0 <= x < a.len() ==>  
              b[x as int] == a[a.len() - 1 - x as int] )

No additional helper spec functions or lemmas are needed.

Informally: `reverse` must return a vector that is exactly the reverse of the provided slice; if the slice is ['a','b','c'], the function must return ['c','b','a'].

Any body that satisfies the above pre- and post-conditions will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

The original exercise only requires a one-line arithmetic result and two very simple proof obligations.  
To make the task a little harder while still being feasible for someone who has already met Verus, I propose three variants:

1.  Ask for the *tetrahedral* number instead of the triangular number.  The closed-form now involves three successive integers and a division by six, so overflow reasoning and algebraic equalities become slightly more involved.

2.  Ask for an arbitrary binomial coefficient `C(n,k)` together with an *additional* post-condition that demonstrates its symmetry (`C(n,k)=C(n,n-k)`).  This forces the learner to introduce a helper specification of the binomial function, prove the symmetry, and deal with two input parameters.

3.  Ask for the *n-th Fibonacci number* with an explicit requirement that the implementation use the fast-doubling algorithm (or any `O(log n)` method) and with stronger post-conditions: the usual recursive definition *and* a simple monotonicity lemma.  Because Verus cannot measure time directly, the specification words the efficiency requirement informally (“should not be worse than …”); the prover will still need loop or recursive invariants that are more sophisticated than a single arithmetic equality.

Each description below is self-contained, detailed enough for a spec generator, and roughly ten words longer than the original statement.

# Reasoning
The function `find_max` is intended to scan a non-empty slice of mathematical integers (`int`) and return an index that points to a biggest element.

1. Inputs  
   • `a: &[int]` — an immutable slice whose items are of type `int` (Verus’s unbounded mathematical integer, not a bounded machine type).  
   The slice can have any positive length.

2. Output  
   • `i: usize` — a machine unsigned integer that will serve as an index into the slice.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` — the caller must guarantee the slice is non-empty so that a maximum element exists and an index can be returned.

4. Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
   • `0 <= i && i < a.len()` — the returned index is within bounds of the slice.  
   • `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i]` — every element of the slice is less than or equal to the element located at index `i`.  
     Consequently, `a[i]` is a (not necessarily unique) maximum value of the slice.

5. Absence of other helpers  
   The specification references no auxiliary lemma, spec function or ghost state.

6. Freedom of implementation  
   Any verified body that provably meets the above pre- and post-conditions is acceptable (one-pass scan, divide-and-conquer, etc.). Because the element type is `int`, overflow is impossible in the specification domain.

7. Subtleties  
   • If the maximum value appears several times, the spec allows returning any one of those indices; no stability requirement is imposed.  
   • The `a[k] ≤ a[i]` relation is written using `int`, therefore it is purely mathematical and not subject to machine-integer overflow or wrap-around.  
   • The returned `usize` must be cast to `int` (`i as int`) when compared inside the universal quantifier, because the quantifier ranges over `int`.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally-verified function in Verus named `find_max` that returns the index of a maximum element in a slice.

Signature  
    fn find_max(a: &[int]) -> (i: usize)

Inputs  
    • a : immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).

Output  
    • i : `usize` — an index into `a` pointing to a maximal element.

Precondition  
    • a.len() > 0 — the slice must contain at least one element.

Postconditions  
    1. 0 ≤ i < a.len() — the output index is within bounds.  
    2. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i] — every element of the slice is less than or equal to the element located at index i.  
       Therefore a[i] is a maximum value of the slice (ties allowed).

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required. Any algorithm that satisfies the contract (e.g., a single linear scan keeping track of the current best index) is acceptable. If the maximum value occurs multiple times, the function may return any of the corresponding indices.
>>>

# Reasoning
Function summary:
- Name: to_char_array.
- Inputs:
  • s: &Vec<char> – an immutable borrow of a runtime vector containing Rust `char` values (Unicode scalar values, 32-bit each).
- Output:
  • a: Vec<char> – a newly-constructed vector that will be returned by the function.
- Preconditions (requires):
  • s.len() ≥ 0.  
    This is vacuously true for every vector in Rust because the length is always non-negative; therefore there are effectively no meaningful preconditions.
- Postconditions (ensures):
 1. a.len() == s.len() – the returned vector must have exactly the same length as the input vector.
 2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ a[i] == s[i] – element-wise equality: every index that is valid for `s` must contain exactly the same character in `a`.

Interpretation:
The specification says that `to_char_array` must return a vector that is an exact copy (clone) of the input vector. No other behaviour is constrained; in particular, the returned vector may be freshly allocated or share memory through reference counting, but from the caller’s point of view it must behave as a distinct vector with identical contents.

Implementation hints:
A straightforward verified implementation can:
  • Allocate a new `Vec<char>` with capacity `s.len()`.
  • Iterate over `s`, pushing each character into the new vector.
Any method that satisfies the element-wise equality is acceptable.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are referenced; the specification is entirely self-contained.

# Final Description
<<<
Design a formally verified function in Verus-Rust with the signature

fn to_char_array(s: &Vec<char>) -> (a: Vec<char>)

Behavioural specification

Inputs
    - s: an immutable reference to a vector of Rust `char` values.

Outputs
    - a: a vector of `char` that is returned to the caller.

Preconditions (requires)
    - None of substance; the only stated requirement is s.len() ≥ 0, which is automatically true for every Rust vector. The function must therefore accept any vector, including the empty one.

Postconditions (ensures)
    1. a.len() == s.len()  
       The returned vector has exactly the same length as the input vector.
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ a[i] == s[i]  
       For every valid index of the input vector, the character stored at that position in the output vector equals the character stored at the same position in the input vector.

In plain words, `to_char_array` must create and return a new vector that is an element-for-element copy of its argument. The caller must observe that the two vectors are of equal length and contain identical characters at every index.

No helper spec functions or lemmas are involved.

Any executable body that satisfies the above contract—such as allocating a fresh vector and cloning each character—will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is trivial because the pre-condition forces the only legal input to be `0`; the implementation can therefore be a single `return 0;`.  
To make the exercise more interesting, each new version

• removes the “always-0” loophole,  
• adds a few uncommon or multi-step constraints, and  
• still stays small enough for one function, so that it can be translated directly into a Verus specification by an automatic tool.

Three stand-alone variants are proposed:

1. `safe_square` – asks for the square of an *even* number that is still small enough to avoid overflow, and additionally requires/proves divisibility properties of the result.

2. `sum_of_squares` – works over a whole sequence; the implementer must supply a loop (or recursion) together with invariants to prove that the running total never overflows and matches the mathematical sum.

3. `next_power_of_two` – bit-manipulation logic that forces the implementer to reason about powers of two and minimality.

Each description below contains everything necessary (signature, detailed pre- and post-conditions, and explanatory remarks) so that a spec generator can create the corresponding Verus contract without seeing the original problem.



# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function called `safe_square`.

Signature  
    fn safe_square(a: u64) -> (c: u64)

Inputs  
    a: a 64-bit unsigned integer.

Return value  
    c: a 64-bit unsigned integer representing the square of `a`.

Preconditions (`requires`)  
1. a % 2 == 0                          // `a` must be even.  
2. a <= 4_294_967_295                  // 2³² − 1, i.e.  ⌊√u64::MAX⌋; guarantees `a * a` fits in `u64`.  

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
1. c == a * a                          // `c` is exactly the square.  
2. c % 4 == 0                          // the square of any even number is a multiple of 4.  
3. (a % 4 == 0) ==> (c % 16 == 0)      // if `a` is divisible by 4, its square is divisible by 16.  

No auxiliary spec functions are mandated, but a direct proof of the arithmetic divisibility facts must be supplied.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function called `sum_of_squares`.

Signature  
    fn sum_of_squares(arr: Seq<u32>) -> (total: u64)

Inputs  
    arr: an immutable sequence (Verus `Seq`) of 32-bit unsigned integers.

Return value  
    total: a 64-bit unsigned integer equal to the mathematical sum of the squares of every element of `arr`.

Preconditions (`requires`)  
1. forall i: 0 <= i < arr.len() ==> arr[i] <= 65_535     // each element fits in 16 bits.  
2. let s = Σ (arr[i] * arr[i]) over i in 0 .. arr.len();  
   s <= u64::MAX                                           // prevents overflow of the 64-bit result.

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
1. total == Σ (arr[i] * arr[i]) over i in 0 .. arr.len().  
2. arr.len() == 0 ==> total == 0.                         // empty sequence yields zero.  
3. arr.len()  > 0 ==> total >= arr.len()                  // each square of a positive integer ≥ 1 adds at least one; shows basic growth.

Notes  
• A loop or recursive helper is expected; appropriate loop invariants must establish the running upper-bound and the correspondence between the accumulator and the processed prefix of the sequence.  
• No element of `arr` may be mutated.

Inputs

Return value

Preconditions (`requires`)  
1. 1 <= n <= 9_223_372_036_854_775_808    // 2⁶³; stays within range while leaving one spare bit for doubling.

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
1. exists k: 0 <= k < 64 && p == (1u64 << k)        // `p` itself is a power of two.  
2. n <= p                                           // it is at least as large as `n`.  
3. p < 2 * n                                        // it is the *smallest* such power of two.  
4. (exists k: n == (1u64 << k)) ==> (p == n)        // if `n` already was a power of two, result is unchanged.

You must supply two formally verified items in Verus-Rust that match exactly the following behaviour.

1. Pure logical helper  
   spec fn between(start: nat, i: nat, end: nat) -> bool

   • Parameters  
     start: nat – a non-negative integer marking one end of an interval.  
     i:     nat – the candidate value to test.  
     end:   nat – the other end of the interval.

   • Return value  
     A Boolean that is true precisely when i lies *strictly* between start and end according to these rules:

       ─ If start < end :  true  ⇔  start < i ∧ i < end  
       ─ Otherwise       :  true  ⇔  i < end ∨ start < i

     Thus, when start ≥ end the notion of “between” wraps around the zero point, treating the naturals as laid out on a circle; equality with either bound is never considered “between”.

   • There are no requires/ensures clauses; the body shown above must be used verbatim (or an equivalent logical expression).

2. Executable verified function  
   fn max(a: int, b: int) -> (max: int)

   • Inputs  
     a: int – first arbitrary mathematical integer.  
     b: int – second arbitrary mathematical integer.

   • Output  
     max: int – the mathematical maximum of a and b.

   • Preconditions  
     requires true  (i.e. none).

   • Postconditions  
     ensures
        (max ≥ a ∧ max ≥ b)          // result is at least each argument
        ∧ (max == a ∨ max == b);     // result equals one of the arguments

     Together these two clauses force max to be exactly the larger of a and b (or that same value if they are equal).

   • Implementation freedom  
     Any executable body that provably satisfies the above contract is acceptable (e.g., an if-expression that returns a when a ≥ b else b).

Reproducing these two elements exactly—types, names, preconditions, and postconditions—will satisfy the specification.

```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::{modes::*, prelude::*, seq::*, *};

verus! {

fn smallest_list_length(s: Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (v: usize)
    requires
        s.len() > 0,
    ensures
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() as int ==> v as int <= s[i as int].len() as int,
        exists|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() as int && v as int == s[i as int].len() as int,
{
```

# Reasoning
The function `smallest_list_length` is specified to compute the minimum length among a non-empty collection of integer vectors.

1. Inputs  
   • `s`: `Vec<Vec<i32>>` – the function takes ownership of a heap-allocated Rust vector whose elements are themselves `Vec<i32>` (lists of 32-bit signed integers).  
   • No other inputs.

2. Output  
   • `v`: `usize` – an unsigned, machine-word-sized integer returned by the function.

3. Preconditions (requires)  
   • `s.len() > 0` – the outer vector must contain at least one inner vector. Nothing is assumed about the lengths of the inner vectors; they may be empty.

4. Postconditions (ensures)  
   a) `forall|i:int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> v <= s[i].len()` (casts to `int` are shown in the original): `v` is **no larger** than the length of every inner vector, i.e. it is a lower bound.  
   b) `exists|i:int| 0 <= i < s.len() && v == s[i].len()` – there is **some** index at which the inner vector’s length equals `v`, proving the lower bound is tight.  

   Together these two logical clauses characterise `v` precisely as the minimum (smallest) length among all inner vectors.

5. Helper specs / lemmas  
   • None—only the two quantifier conditions are used.

6. Subtleties  
   • Quantifiers range over mathematical `int`; the casts (`as int`) appear only so the arithmetic lives in Verus’s unbounded integer domain.  
   • Because `usize` is always non-negative, no explicit non-negativity clause for `v` is necessary.  
   • The precondition `s.len() > 0` guarantees the existential clause is satisfiable.

Any verified implementation must satisfy this contract, typically by iterating through `s` and keeping track of the current smallest length, then returning that value.

The two postconditions together require that `v` equals the minimum length among all inner vectors.

No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or additional constraints are needed. An implementation may traverse the outer vector, compute the minimum length (which could be zero if any inner vector is empty), and return it, thereby satisfying the specification.

# Reasoning
The original task is quite short: return the minimum inner-vector length and prove the two usual properties (lower bound + attainability).  
To increase the difficulty we can

• Add one or two more output values (e.g. the index where the minimum occurs) and enforce an unusual tie-breaking rule (“if several indices have the same minimum, return the *largest* such index”).  
• Strengthen the spec with an algebraic side condition (e.g. the result must be even, or must be strictly positive).  
• Require the user to reason about *counts*, not just extrema—e.g. also return “how many vectors realise the minimum”, so a second existential/universal property appears.  
• Provide a short, subtly incorrect Rust implementation as a red herring.  
• Keep the function otherwise implementable with a linear scan so as not to explode complexity.

Below are three harder, self-contained problems that satisfy these ideas; each is wrapped in <<< … >>> so that a later tool can isolate them.

Signature  
    fn min_len_last_idx(s: Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (idx: usize, len: usize)

Inputs  
    • s — an owned `Vec` whose elements are themselves `Vec<i32>`.

Outputs  
    • idx — the (0-based) *largest index* whose inner vector has the minimum length.  
    • len — that minimum length.

Specification

Preconditions (requires)  
    • The outer vector is non-empty: `s.len() > 0`.

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. Minimum-value property  
       ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ len ≤ s[i].len()  
    2. Attainment by `idx`  
       0 ≤ idx < s.len() ∧ len == s[idx].len()  
    3. Tie-breaking rule (less common)  
       ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < s.len() ∧ s[j].len() == len ⇒ j ≤ idx  
       (i.e. `idx` is the greatest index among all minimisers).

Together, the three properties state that `len` is the minimum inner length, and `idx` is the *last* position where that minimum occurs.

Erroneous reference implementation (do NOT rely on it; it breaks rule #3 when two minima exist):

```rust
pub fn min_len_last_idx(s: Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (usize, usize) {
    let mut best_idx = 0;
    let mut best_len = s[0].len();
    for i in 1..s.len() {
        if s[i].len() < best_len {
            best_len = s[i].len();
            best_idx = i;
        }
        /* BUG: does not update best_idx when equal */
    }
    (best_idx, best_len)
}
```

No auxiliary spec items other than those needed to express the three ensures clauses are necessary.

```rust
fn all_digits(s: &Vec<char>) -> (result: bool)
```

Purpose  
    Return `true` precisely when every character stored in the input vector `s` is one of the ten ASCII decimal digits `'0'`, `'1'`, … , `'9'`.

Inputs

    • result — a Boolean.

Specification  
    Preconditions (requires): none.

    Postcondition (ensures):  
        result  ⇔  ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒  
                     s[i] == '0' ∨ s[i] == '1' ∨ s[i] == '2' ∨ s[i] == '3' ∨
                     s[i] == '4' ∨ s[i] == '5' ∨ s[i] == '6' ∨ s[i] == '7' ∨
                     s[i] == '8' ∨ s[i] == '9'

That is, the returned Boolean must be logically equivalent to the statement “every valid index of the vector holds a decimal digit.” In particular, an empty vector must yield `true`.

No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are required. Any implementation that satisfies the above contract—including one that simply computes the Boolean expression mirroring the postcondition—is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is trivial because the pre-condition forces `x == 0`.  
To make the assignment more interesting I propose three stand-alone replacements.  
All three still fit easily in a single pure Verus function, yet they demand
• non-trivial arithmetic reasoning,  
• quantifiers over bits / sequences, or  
• properties that are not expressible with one equality only.  

Problem ① asks for a *bit-reversal*; proving that the result is its own inverse and
that it preserves population count adds two extra reasoning steps.

Problem ② asks for a *bounded binomial coefficient*.  The student must reason
about symmetry and provide a bound that avoids overflow; the post-conditions refer
to both the main closed formula and the symmetry property.

Problem ③ introduces sequences and gcd reasoning; the student has to supply a
witness-style specification showing minimality and divisibility, which typically
needs an auxiliary lemma, making the task longer.

Each description is completely self-contained so a spec generator can work from
the natural language alone.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function called `reverse_bits`.

Signature  
    fn reverse_bits(x: u64) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    x : unsigned 64-bit integer.

Output  
    r : unsigned 64-bit integer.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1.  true   // the function must accept every 64-bit value.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1.  For every bit position `i` with `0 <= i < 64`,
        the `i`-th bit of `r` equals the `(63 − i)`-th bit of `x`.  
        (Hint: use `(x >> i) & 1 == (r >> (63-i)) & 1`.)
    2.  Bit-reversal is an involution: `reverse_bits(reverse_bits(x)) == x`.
    3.  `r` preserves population count: `popcount(r) == popcount(x)`.  
        Feel free to define `popcount` as an auxiliary pure spec function
        using a `nat` accumulator.

No time or space complexity limits are imposed; however, the specification must
prove all three post-conditions without using unsafe blocks or external lemmas.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function named `binom`.

Signature  
    fn binom(n: u32, k: u32) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    n : unsigned 32-bit integer  
    k : unsigned 32-bit integer

Output  
    r : unsigned 64-bit integer representing “n choose k”.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1.  k <= n                                   // domain of the binomial.
    2.  n <= 20                                  // guarantees `r` fits into u64.
    3.  k != 1 || n != 1                         // add one uncommon corner case.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1.  r == n! / (k! * (n − k)!)                // standard closed formula.
    2.  r == binom(n, n − k)                     // symmetry of the coefficient.
    3.  r >= 1                                   // positive result for allowed inputs.

You may define a pure recursive helper `fac` for factorials, together with the
usual lemma `fac_positive`.  No other helper is required, but any additional
lemmas must terminate and remain pure.
>>>

Signature  
    fn gcd_slice(xs: Seq<u64>) -> (g: u64)

Inputs  
    xs : an immutable Verus `Seq<u64>` whose length may not exceed 10.

Output  
    g : unsigned 64-bit integer, the greatest common divisor of all elements.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1.  xs.len() >= 1                            // non-empty slice.
    2.  ∀i: 0 <= i < xs.len() ==> xs[i] > 0      // every element strictly positive.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1.  ∀i: 0 <= i < xs.len() ==> xs[i] % g == 0 // g divides every element.
    2.  ∀d: u64 :: d > 0 && (∀i: 0<=i<xs.len() ==> xs[i] % d == 0) ==> d <= g
                                                 // g is maximal among divisors.
    3.  g == if xs.len() == 1 { xs[0] } else { gcd_slice(xs.drop_last()) 
                                               .1.gcd(xs.last()) }  
        // structural property tying the result to a recursive two-argument gcd;
        // you may implement that two-argument gcd as an internal spec helper.

Do not use unbounded integer types; the implementation must stay in `u64`
arithmetic and satisfy the specification without overflow by carefully ordered
operations or by proving that no intermediate exceeds `u64::MAX`.

    fn gauss(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)

```rust
verus! {

fn remove_front(a: &[int]) -> (c: Vec<int>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        c.len() == a.len() - 1,
        forall|i: int| 1 <= i && i < a.len() ==> a[i] == c[i - 1],
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification describes a function called `remove_front`.

1. Inputs
   • `a`: an immutable slice (`&[int]`) whose element type is Verus’s mathematical
     integer `int`.  
2. Output  
   • `c`: a fresh, owned `Vec<int>`.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` – the slice must contain at least one element, so there is always
     a “front” element to remove.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   • `c.len() == a.len() - 1` – the resulting vector is exactly one element shorter than the input slice.  
   • `∀ i:int • 1 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == c[i-1]` – for every valid index of the
     tail of `a` (everything **except** the element at position 0), the value found
     in `a` at position `i` appears in `c` at position `i − 1`.  
     In other words, `c` is the slice `a[1 .. a.len()]` copied into a new vector,
     preserving order.

No other obligations or helper specs are referenced.

To satisfy this contract an implementation can, for example, allocate a new
vector of length `a.len() - 1` and copy the elements `a[1]`, `a[2]`, … into it.
The contents of `c` at indices outside the specified range (there are none) are
left unconstrained by the specification.

# Final Description (put between the special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Implement a verified function in Verus named `remove_front`.

Signature  
    fn remove_front(a: &[int]) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Behaviour  
• `a` is an immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• The slice is guaranteed to be non-empty (precondition).

Return value  
• `c` is a freshly allocated `Vec<int>` that contains every element of `a` except
  for the first one, in the same relative order.

Formal contract  
1. Requires:  
       a.len() > 0
2. Ensures:

Post-condition (ensures)

# Reasoning
The original `find_max` contract is straightforward: examine the first `n` elements of a non-empty slice and return **any** index holding a maximal value.  
To raise the difficulty we can:

• Introduce a second result that must be computed together with the index (e.g. the number of occurrences of the maximum).  
• Add a deterministic tie-breaking rule (e.g. “earliest index”) so the proof must additionally show minimality.  
• Return several indices that must satisfy mutual ordering constraints, forcing more quantified reasoning.  
• Require the function to reason about the *absence* of qualified elements by returning `Option`, thereby adding a disjunction to the post-conditions.

Below are three self-contained, tougher variants that incorporate those ideas.  
Each description is detailed enough for a spec generator to create the corresponding Verus contracts from it.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function called `max_index_and_count` with the following contract.

Inputs  
• `a: &[i32]` — an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.  
• `n: usize` — a positive length indicating that only the first `n` positions of `a` are relevant.

Return value  
• `(idx: usize, cnt: usize)` — a pair where `idx` is an index into `a`, and `cnt` is the number of occurrences of the maximal value within the examined prefix.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. `a.len() > 0` — the slice is not empty.  
2. `0 < n <= a.len()` — the prefix length is in bounds.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. `0 <= idx < n` (and therefore `< a.len()`), so `idx` is a valid position inside the prefix.  
2. `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < n ⇒ a[idx] ≥ a[k]` — the element at `idx` is maximal among the first `n` entries.  
3. `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < idx ⇒ a[k] < a[idx]` — if the maximal value occurs several times, `idx` is the *earliest* such position.  
4. `cnt = |{ k:int | 0 ≤ k < n ∧ a[k] = a[idx] }|` — `cnt` equals the exact count of positions whose value equals the maximum inside the prefix.  
5. `cnt ≥ 1`.

Informally: among the first `n` numbers, return the first position that holds the largest value, together with how many times that value appears in the same prefix.  The slice itself must remain unmodified.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function called `find_top_two_distinct` with the following contract.

Inputs  
• `a: &[i32]` — immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.  
• `n: usize` — number of leading elements to inspect (the “prefix length”).

Return value  
• `(max_idx: usize, second_idx: usize)` — two **different** indices into `a`.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. `a.len() ≥ 2` — at least two elements in total.  
2. `2 ≤ n ≤ a.len()` — the caller chooses a prefix containing at least two elements.  
3. `∃ k,l:int • 0 ≤ k < n ∧ 0 ≤ l < n ∧ a[k] ≠ a[l]` — within the prefix there exist at least two distinct values, guaranteeing the existence of a strict second maximum.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. Indices are valid and distinct: `0 ≤ max_idx < n`, `0 ≤ second_idx < n`, and `max_idx ≠ second_idx`.  
2. Maximality: `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < n ⇒ a[max_idx] ≥ a[k]`.  
3. Second maximality (strict):  
   • `a[second_idx] < a[max_idx]`, and  
   • `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < n ∧ k ≠ max_idx ⇒ a[second_idx] ≥ a[k]` — no other index except `max_idx` holds a larger value.  
4. Deterministic tie-breaking: if several indices tie for the maximum, `max_idx` must be the *smallest* such index; similarly, among all indices whose value equals the second maximum, `second_idx` must be the smallest.  
5. Relative order: `max_idx` may be less than, equal to, or greater than `second_idx` — no extra ordering is imposed beyond the rules above.

Informally: scan the first `n` elements and return the earliest occurrence of the largest value and the earliest occurrence of the strictly second-largest distinct value.  The slice must not be altered.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function called `find_max_where` with the following contract.

Inputs  
• `a: &[i32]` — immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.  
• `n: usize` — prefix length to consider.  
• `pred: spec fn(i32) -> bool` — a *pure specification-only* predicate supplied by the caller; it may be arbitrary but must terminate and have no side effects.

Return value  
• `opt_idx: Option<usize>` — `Some(idx)` if a qualifying element is found, or `None` otherwise.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. `a.len() > 0`.  
2. `0 < n ≤ a.len()`.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. `opt_idx.is_some()`  ⇔  `∃ k:int • 0 ≤ k < n ∧ pred(a[k])` — we return `Some` exactly when some element in the prefix satisfies `pred`.

# Reasoning
The specification is for a function named `match_string`.

1. Inputs (and types)
   • `s`: `&Vec<char>` — an immutable reference to a vector (string) of Rust `char`s that we want to test against a pattern.  
   • `p`: `&Vec<char>` — an immutable reference to a vector of `char`s acting as the pattern.

2. Output
   • `b`: `bool` — the Boolean result of pattern matching.

3. Pre-condition (`requires`)
   • `s.len() == p.len()` — the two vectors must have exactly the same length. The implementation can therefore freely index both with the same index without out-of-bounds risk.

4. Post-condition (`ensures`)
   • `b <==> forall|n: nat| 0 <= n < s.len() ==> s[n as int] == p[n as int] || p[n as int] == '?'`

   Meaning:  
   ‑ We quantify over every natural number `n` in the valid index range of the vectors (0 ≤ n < s.len()).  
   ‑ For each position `n`, the predicate is satisfied if either:
        • The character stored at that position is identical in both vectors (`s[n] == p[n]`), or
        • The pattern character `p[n]` is the literal question-mark `'?'`, which acts as a wildcard that matches any character in `s`.
   ‑ The biconditional `<==>` (logical equivalence) states that `b` must be `true` exactly when the quantified property holds for every index, and `false` otherwise.

   Consequences:
   • If the vectors are empty, the universally quantified condition is vacuously true; hence `b` must be `true`.  
   • Any mismatch where the pattern character is *not* `'?'` forces the entire quantified statement to be false, so `b` must be `false`.

5. Auxiliary items
   • No helper spec functions, lemmas, or additional attributes are referenced.  
   • Indexing uses `n as int` because Verus’s vector indexing expects mathematical `int`.

6. Responsibilities for an implementer
   • Ensure the body either checks the characters one-by-one, compares the two vectors element-wise with wildcard logic, or uses any equivalent algorithm.  
   • Maintains the precondition (arrays must be same length) and proves the postcondition.

   • If the vectors are empty, the universally quantified condition is vacuously true; hence `b` must be `true`.  
   • Any mismatch where the pattern character is *not* `'?'` forces the entire quantified statement to be false, so `b` must be `false`.

5. Auxiliary items
   • No helper spec functions, lemmas, or additional attributes are referenced.  
   • Indexing uses `n as int` because Verus’s vector indexing expects mathematical `int`.

6. Responsibilities for an implementer
   • Ensure the body either checks the characters one-by-one, compares the two vectors element-wise with wildcard logic, or uses any equivalent algorithm.  
   • Maintains the precondition (arrays must be same length) and proves the postcondition.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally‐verified Verus-Rust function with the following exact contract:

Function signature:
    fn match_string(s: &Vec<char>, p: &Vec<char>) -> (b: bool)

Inputs:
    • s : &Vec<char>      — the text to be matched.
    • p : &Vec<char>      — the pattern, possibly containing wildcard characters.

Output:
    • b : bool            — indicates whether the pattern matches the text.

Pre-condition (requires):
    s.len() == p.len()
    ‑ The caller must supply two vectors of identical length; otherwise the function is not required to operate.

Post-condition (ensures):
    b <==> (∀ n : nat • 0 ≤ n < s.len() ⇒ s[n] == p[n] ∨ p[n] == '?')

That is, `b` must be true exactly when, for every valid index n, the characters match character-for-character *or* the pattern character at that position is the wildcard question-mark `'?'`.  
If the vectors are empty the result must be true; any position where `p[n]` is neither `'?'` nor equal to `s[n]` forces the result to false.

No additional helper spec functions are involved; the body may implement the test in any way that respects the specification.

Signature

Inputs

Post-conditions (ensures)

    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < out.len() ⇒

          (m[i] % 2 == 1 ⇒ out[i] == m[i])  
       // even numbers are halved, odd numbers unchanged  
    3. // global monotone decrease:
       Σ i:int | 0 ≤ i < out.len() ⇒ out[i]
       <
       Σ i:int | 0 ≤ i < m.len()  ⇒ m[i]
       // the arithmetic sum of all elements must strictly decrease

Informally: “Return a vector of the same size where every even element is replaced by half its value, every odd element is kept, and the total sum of the vector is therefore strictly smaller.”

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Signature  
    fn remove_and_shift(m: &[u64], k: u64) -> (out: Vec<u64>)

Inputs

    k : a key to remove

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    • m.len() > 0

Post-conditions (ensures)

    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < out.len() ⇒ out[i] < out[i+1]  (when i+1 < out.len())  
       // output remains strictly increasing  
    3. ∀ x:u64 • (∃ i • 0 ≤ i < out.len() ∧ out[i] == x)  
          ⇔  
          (∃ j • 0 ≤ j < m.len()  ∧ m[j] == x ∧ x ≠ k)  
       // the output contains precisely the elements of the input except possibly k

Informally: “Given a strictly increasing slice and a key `k`, return the same sequence but with `k` removed if it was present; otherwise return an unchanged copy. The result must remain sorted and its length must drop by exactly one only if `k` occurred.”

Pre-condition (requires)

Signature  
    fn binary_search(a: &[int], key: int) -> (n: usize)

Inputs  
    • a – immutable slice of `int`; must be non-empty and sorted in non-decreasing order.  
    • key – the target value we are searching for / inserting.

Mandatory pre-conditions  
    1. a.len() > 0.  
    2. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize (length fits into 32-bit signed range).  
    3. Sortedness: for all indices i, j with 0 ≤ i < j < a.len(), we have a[i] ≤ a[j].

    n: usize – an index (possibly equal to a.len()) indicating where `key` would be inserted to keep the slice sorted.

Required post-conditions  
    • Index range: 0 ≤ n ≤ a.len().  
    • Strictly before n every element is smaller than key:  
        ∀ i ∈ [0, n) ⇒ a[i] < key.  
    • If n equals a.len(), then *all* elements are < key (key is larger than every entry).  
    • From n onward each element is ≥ key:  
        ∀ i ∈ [n, a.len()) ⇒ a[i] ≥ key.

Interpretation  
`n` is the smallest index whose element is ≥ `key`; if no such element exists, n = a.len(). This matches the usual definition of “lower bound” or the insertion position returned by many library binary-search utilities.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required; any concrete implementation that meets these contracts is acceptable.

```rust
        b.len() == a.len(),

{
```

# Reasoning
We read the specification line-by-line and translate each item into plain English:

1. Signature  
   `fn reverse(a: &Vec<char>) -> (b: Vec<char>)`  
   • The function is named `reverse`.  
   • Input: `a`, an immutable reference to a heap‐allocated runtime vector whose elements are of type `char`.  
   • Output: a brand-new vector `b` (returned by value) that will contain characters.

2. Pre-condition (`requires`)  
   `a.len() > 0` – the caller must supply a non-empty vector.  The function is *not* required to work on an empty vector; therefore implementers may use indexing without having to worry about underflow.

3. Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
   There are two obligations that must hold after the function returns.  They are expressed in mathematical logic over the ghost “int” domain (unbounded integers), hence the small type casts from `usize` to `int`.

   • `b.len() == a.len()` – the length of the returned vector must be exactly the same as that of the input.  No elements may be added or removed.

   • `forall |i:int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> b[i] == a[a.len() - i - 1]` – for every valid index `i` of the original vector, the element appearing at index `i` in `b` must equal the element that appeared at the symmetric position from the other end of `a`.  In other words, `b` is the *reverse* of `a`.

   Because `a` is borrowed immutably, the specification can refer to `a` both in the pre- and post-state without any “old” operator.  No other properties, side effects or heap modifications are mentioned, so the entire behavioural contract of the function is that it produces a reversed copy of its argument.

4. No additional helper lemmas, spec functions, or termination measures are required.

5. Subtleties / reminders for an implementation  
   • Runtime code will deal with `usize`, but the logical specification uses `int`; explicit casts will be required inside quantified expressions.  
   • Although the post-condition is also true for an empty vector, the pre-condition disallows that case.  
   • The body may allocate a fresh vector and push elements in reverse order, or clone and call `.reverse()` on it, provided it can be verified to satisfy the contract.

# Final Description
<<<
Design a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following full specification.

Function name  
    reverse

Inputs  
    a: &Vec<char>  
        – an immutable reference to a non-empty vector of Unicode scalar values (`char`).

Output  
    b: Vec<char>  
        – a brand-new vector returned by value.

Pre-condition (requires)  
    • a.len() > 0  
      The caller must supply at least one element.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. b.len() == a.len()  
       The returned vector must contain exactly the same number of characters as the input.  
    2. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ b[i] == a[a.len() − i − 1]  
       For every valid index, the element at position i of the result equals the element that originally appeared at the symmetrical position from the end of `a`.  In other words, `b` is the exact reversal of `a`.

No other side conditions, ghost state, or helper specifications are required.  Any body that satisfies the above contract—e.g., by iterating over `a` from the back to the front and collecting the items into `b`—is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original assignment is a classic “lower-bound” binary-search:  
 – single return value,  
 – simple order (≤ on `int`),  
 – no explicit complexity requirement,  
 – no attention to duplicates beyond the usual lower-bound semantics.

To make the exercise more challenging we can:

1. Add a *verified cost counter* that must be bounded by ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉, forcing the student to carry an extra ghost variable through the implementation and reason about loop variants more carefully.

2. Ask for both the lower and the upper bound (`lb`, `ub`) so that the function has to characterise an *interval* of equal elements.  This adds two indices whose relationship (0 ≤ lb ≤ ub ≤ len, every element before `lb` is < key, between `lb` and `ub` is = key, after `ub` is > key) must be proved.

3. Generalise the comparison operator: instead of the built-in ≤, the function receives a *ghost predicate* `less : int × int → bool` that must satisfy the axioms of a strict total order (irreflexive, transitive, trichotomous).  The student now has to reason *parametrically* with an arbitrary order rather than concrete integers.

Each of these modifications adds at least one substantial extra reasoning obligation while keeping the algorithmic core recognisably “binary search.”

# Output
<<<
Create a verified Verus-Rust function called `binary_search_counted` that behaves like the standard *lower-bound* routine on a sorted slice of mathematical integers **and additionally returns a verified upper bound on the number of loop iterations performed**.

Signature  
    fn binary_search_counted(a: &[int], key: int) -> (n: usize, steps: nat)

Inputs  
• a – immutable slice of `int`; must be non-empty and sorted in non-decreasing order.  
• key – the target value to locate / insert.

Mandatory pre-conditions  
1. 0 < a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  
2. Sortedness: ∀ i,j. 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].

Return values  
• n – the first index whose element is ≥ key (possibly a.len()).  
• steps – the exact number of iterations (mid-point tests) executed by the concrete algorithm.  
  `steps` is an ordinary `nat`, not a ghost.

Required post-conditions  
• 0 ≤ n ≤ a.len().  
• ∀ i ∈ [0,n) ⇒ a[i] < key.  
• ∀ i ∈ [n,a.len()) ⇒ a[i] ≥ key.  
• Complexity bound: steps ≤ ceil(log₂(a.len() + 1)).  
  (Use `((a.len() + 1).next_power_of_two().trailing_zeros() as nat)` or an equivalent integer expression in the spec.)  

No auxiliary lemmas may be assumed; proving the logarithmic bound must be done inside the function body or via helper lemmas you supply.
>>>

<<<
Design a verified Verus-Rust function `lower_upper_bound` that returns *both* the lower-bound and upper-bound indices for a given key in a non-empty sorted slice.

Signature  
    fn lower_upper_bound(a: &[int], key: int) -> (lb: usize, ub: usize)

Inputs  
• a – immutable slice of `int`; length fits in i32; sorted in non-decreasing order.  
• key – the value whose equal-range is requested.

Pre-conditions  
1. a.len() > 0 ∧ a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  
2. Sorted: ∀ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].

Post-conditions  
• 0 ≤ lb ≤ ub ≤ a.len().  
• ∀ i ∈ [0, lb)      ⇒ a[i] < key.  
• ∀ i ∈ [lb, ub)     ⇒ a[i] = key.  
  (If no element equals `key` this range is empty because lb = ub.)  
• ∀ i ∈ [ub, a.len()) ⇒ a[i] > key.  
• Minimality / maximality:  
    – If lb > 0 then a[lb-1] < key.  
    – If ub < a.len() then a[ub] > key.

Interpretation  
`lb` is where `key` would first be inserted; `ub` is where it would be inserted *after* all equal elements.  The interval `[lb, ub)` therefore comprises exactly those positions storing a value equal to `key`.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function `generic_lower_bound` that computes a lower-bound with respect to an *arbitrary strict total order* supplied as a ghost predicate.

Signature  
    fn generic_lower_bound(
        a: &[int],
        key: int,
        #[verifier::spec] less: spec_fn(int, int) -> bool
    ) -> (n: usize)

Inputs  
• a – immutable slice of `int`; may be empty; assumed sorted under `less`.  
• key – the element to insert.  
• less – a *ghost* binary predicate representing the ordering.

Pre-conditions  
1. Total-order axioms for `less` hold:  
    a) ∀x. ¬less(x,x)                  (irreflexive)  
    b) ∀x,y,z. less(x,y) ∧ less(y,z) ⇒ less(x,z)   (transitive)  
    c) ∀x≠y. less(x,y) ∨ less(y,x)     (trichotomy)  
2. Sortedness under `less`: ∀ i < j < a.len() ⇒ less(a[i], a[j]) ∨ a[i] = a[j].  
3. a.len() ≤ u32::MAX as usize.    // chosen instead of i32 to vary the requirement.

Post-conditions  
• 0 ≤ n ≤ a.len().  
• ∀ i ∈ [0,n)       ⇒ less(a[i], key).  
• ∀ i ∈ [n,a.len()) ⇒ ¬less(a[i], key).  
• Minimality: n is the least index satisfying the second clause.

Notes  
– The implementation may only *compare* elements via `less`; it must not use built-in `≤` or `>=`.  
– Since `less` is ghost, all calls to it occur only in specifications; the executable code must use ordinary integer comparisons consigning to the fact that in the concrete run `less` will coincide with `≤`, although this is *not* assumed in proofs.  
– No asymptotic bound is demanded, but the proof must work for an arbitrary total order, which usually requires extra lemmas about `less`.
>>>

7. len0  > 0 ⇒ (start + len0 == v.len() ∨ v[start + len0] ≠ 0)      (run is right-maximal)  
8. ∀ j:nat • 0 ≤ j < start ⇒ v[j] ≠ 0                               (earlier elements are non-zero)

# Spec code (given to you)

```rust
use builtin_macros::*;

verus! {

fn find(a: &[int], key: int) -> (index: usize)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        0 <= index && index <= a.len(),
        index < a.len() ==> a[index as int] == key,
{
```

# Reasoning
The declaration introduces an executable, pure function called `find`.

1. Inputs  
   • `a: &[int]` – an immutable slice containing mathematical integers (`int`).  
   • `key: int`   – the value we are searching for in the slice.

2. Output  
   • `index: usize` – a natural-number index designating a position in the slice or a sentinel equal to the slice length.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` – the slice is guaranteed to be non-empty.  
   No other assumptions are made; `key` may or may not appear in the data.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   • `0 <= index && index <= a.len()` – the returned index is always within the inclusive range `[0, a.len()]`.  
   • `index < a.len() ==> a[index as int] == key` – if the function returns a value strictly less than the length, then the element at that position is exactly `key`.

   Taken together, the contract allows two possible outcomes:
     – If `key` is present, the implementation may return *any* occurrence’s index (it need not be the first);  
     – If it decides not to—or if the key is absent—it may legally return the sentinel value `a.len()`.

   There is deliberately no obligation of the form  
   `index == a.len() ==> forall i …`  
   hence an implementation that unconditionally returns `a.len()` would still satisfy the specification, although a more useful one will attempt a real search.

5. Auxiliary items  
   No helper spec functions, predicates, or lemmas are referenced.

6. Type/logic subtleties  
   • `int` is Verus’s unbounded mathematical integer; array indices inside specifications are also written as `int`, so the cast `index as int` appears in the second post-condition.  
   • Because `index` is a `usize`, the explicit lower bound `0 <= index` is redundant from Rust’s typing standpoint but is spelled out for the verifier’s benefit.

# Final Description

Inputs  
• a – an immutable borrow of a slice of mathematical integers; the slice must contain at least one element.  
• key – the integer value to look for.

Output  
• index – a `usize` that either pinpoints an element equal to `key` or acts as a sentinel indicating that the key was (possibly) not found.

Precondition  
• a.len() > 0  (the slice is non-empty).

Postconditions  
1. 0 ≤ index ≤ a.len().  
2. If index < a.len() then a[index] == key.

Interpretation: the function may return any valid position whose element equals `key`; if it wishes, it may instead return the value `a.len()` (one past the last valid index). The specification does not compel it to prove that the key is absent when it returns the sentinel.

Implementers are free to choose any algorithm—linear scan, binary search on sorted data, constant return, etc.—provided the implementation meets the above contract exactly.

The original exercise is quite easy because *any* common upper bound of `a` and `b` is accepted.  
To raise the bar we can (a) demand the **least** or **strictly least** upper bound, (b) require that the
answer be *one of the inputs* (i.e. the exact maximum), or (c) enlarge the
interface so that more values must be considered at once.  
Those changes add extra proof obligations such as showing minimality, uniqueness,
and membership.

spirit of “finding a maximum”, yet force the student to carry out several

Meaning of parameters  
    • a – an arbitrary, unbounded mathematical integer.  
    • b – another arbitrary, unbounded mathematical integer.

Return value  
    • result – the *greatest* of the two inputs, and it must coincide with one
      of them.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
    • None.  The function must accept every pair of integers.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
    1. result ≥ a  
    2. result ≥ b  
    3. result == a  ∨  result == b        — membership: the answer is one of the arguments  
    4. ∀x:int. (x ≥ a ∧ x ≥ b) ⇒ x ≥ result   — minimality of the upper bound

Hints & clarifications  
    • Together, clauses 1–4 ensure that `result` is exactly `max(a,b)`.  
    • No ghost parameters are needed, but you may introduce helper lemmas if you
      wish.  
    • Overflow is impossible because `int` in Verus denotes the mathematical ℤ.

    than both arguments.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)
