# Reasoning
The original exercise asks the learner to (1) specify a simple “sorted” predicate and (2) implement / verify a Boolean‐returning binary search.  
To increase difficulty we can:

• Keep the familiar domain of sorted arrays so that the task is still recognisable, yet  
• Force a richer post-condition (e.g. produce an index, not just a Boolean), or reason about *where* an element should be inserted, or deal with rotated arrays, or merge two arrays.  
• Retain the same helper predicate `is_sorted` so the student does not have to reinvent it, but add extra, less-common logical obligations such as *strict* ordering, partition constraints, permutation properties, or existential proofs.  
• Require reasoning over empty as well as non-empty slices and prove boundary properties (≤, <, ==).  
Below are three harder, stand-alone specifications that satisfy those goals.


# Output
<<<
Create two items in Verus (Rust):

1. Pure specification predicate  
   spec fn is_strictly_sorted(a: Seq<int>) -> bool  
   Purpose: state that a sequence of mathematical integers is *strictly* increasing.  
   Definition:  
       returns
           forall i, j: int
               0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] < a[j]
   Note: This is the same shape as the usual “sorted” predicate but uses the less common *strict* (<) relation.

2. Executable, verified function  

   fn lower_bound(a: &[int], k: int) -> (idx: int, found: bool)

Inputs  
    • a — immutable slice containing ints (possibly empty).  
    • k — the key to search for or to insert in order.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. is_strictly_sorted(a@) — slice is strictly increasing.  
    2. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize — length fits in a 32-bit signed integer.

Outputs  
    • idx: int — an insertion position in the closed interval 0 … a.len().  
    • found: bool.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. 0 ≤ idx ≤ a.len()                                   — idx is a valid boundary.  
    2. forall j:int • 0 ≤ j < idx      ⇒ a[j] <  k         — everything before idx is < k.  
    3. forall j:int • idx ≤ j < a.len() ⇒ a[j] ≥ k         — everything from idx onwards is ≥ k.  
    4. found == (idx < a.len() ∧ a[idx] == k)              — found is true iff k is truly present.  
    5. ¬found ⇒ (idx == a.len() ∨ a[idx] > k)              — if not found, idx is the canonical insert point.

Any correct, verified implementation (e.g. logarithmic binary search or linear scan) is acceptable as long as it satisfies *all* the above contracts.
>>>

<<<
Create three items in Verus (Rust):

1. Pure specification predicate  
   spec fn is_rotated_sorted(a: Seq<int>) -> bool  
   Purpose: state that a sequence can be rotated (cyclically shifted) into non-decreasing order *without duplicates*.  
   Definition:  
       returns
           exists p:int  • 0 ≤ p < a.len()
           forall i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒
               a[(i+p) % a.len()] < a[(i+p+1) % a.len()]
   Recommends a.len() > 0 because an empty sequence is vacuously rotated-sorted.

2. Helper spec function  
   spec fn rotation_index(a: Seq<int>) -> int  
   Meaning: returns *the* smallest p that witnesses `is_rotated_sorted(a)`; if `a.len()==0` it returns 0.

   ensures
       0 ≤ result ≤ a.len()
   ensures
       is_rotated_sorted(a) ⇒
         forall p:int • 0 ≤ p < result ⇒
             ¬(forall i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[(i+p)%a.len()] < a[(i+p+1)%a.len()])
   ensures
       is_rotated_sorted(a) ⇒
         forall i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒
             a[(i+result)%a.len()] < a[(i+result+1)%a.len()]

3. Executable, verified function  

   fn rotated_search(a: &[int], k: int) -> (idx: int)

Inputs  
    • a — immutable slice containing *distinct* ints (may be empty).  
    • k — the key to search for.

Pre-conditions  
    1. is_rotated_sorted(a@) — the slice is sorted but with an unknown cyclic rotation.  
    2. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  

Output  
    • idx: int — equals −1 (use `-1` cast to `int`) if k is absent, otherwise the index where k occurs.

Post-conditions  
    1. idx == -1 ⇒ (forall i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ k).  
    2. 0 ≤ idx < a.len() ⇒ a[idx] == k.  
    3. (idx == -1) ∨ (0 ≤ idx < a.len()).  

Any verified implementation is acceptable; a typical solution first locates the rotation index and then performs a conventional binary search on one or two sub-ranges.
>>>

<<<
Create two items in Verus (Rust):

1. Pure specification predicate  
   spec fn is_sorted(a: Seq<int>) -> bool  
   Same as in the original task (non-decreasing order, duplicates allowed).

2. Executable, verified function  

   fn merge_sorted(a: &[int], b: &[int], out: &mut Vec<int>) -> ()

Inputs  
    • a — immutable slice, non-empty, sorted non-decreasing.  
    • b — immutable slice, non-empty, sorted non-decreasing.  
    • out — mutable, *empty* vector with reserved capacity ≥ a.len() + b.len().

Pre-conditions  
    1. is_sorted(a@) ∧ is_sorted(b@).  
    2. a.len() + b.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  
    3. out.len() == 0 ∧ out.capacity() ≥ a.len() + b.len().

Post-conditions  
    ensures
       out@.len() == a.len() + b.len()                           — all elements copied.  
    ensures
       is_sorted(out@)                                          — result is globally sorted.  
    ensures
       (multiset(out@) == multiset(a@) ∪ multiset(b@))          — no loss or duplication.  
    ensures
       out.capacity() ≥ a.len() + b.len()                       — capacity unchanged or larger.

The student must implement an in-order merge (linear time) that appends into `out` and verify the stronger permutation property expressed via multisets.
>>>

Design a formally-verified Verus/Rust function named `abs_it` that converts every element of an input vector to its absolute value while preserving length and handling the `i32::MIN` corner case.

Signature  
    fn abs_it(s: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    s : Vec<i32> — the function takes ownership of a non-empty vector of 32-bit signed integers.

Preconditions (requires)  
    • s.len() > 0 — the vector must contain at least one element.  
    • No restrictions on the numerical values stored in the vector.

Outputs  
    result : Vec<i32> — a new vector returned by value.

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. result.len() == s.len() — the output vector has the same length as the input.  
    2. ∀ x:int. 0 ≤ x < s.len() ⇒  
         if s[x] == i32::MIN {          result[x] == i32::MAX }  
         else if s[x] < 0 {             result[x] == -s[x]    }  
         else {                         result[x] == s[x]     }

In other words, for each index:  
    • When the input value equals the minimum representable 32-bit integer (-2³¹), the corresponding output value must be the maximum representable 32-bit integer (2³¹-1).  
    • For any other negative value, the output is its arithmetic negation.  
    • Non-negative values are copied unchanged.

No auxiliary specification functions are required.

Any implementation that consumes the input vector and produces a new vector satisfying these contracts—e.g., by iterating once over `s`, computing the appropriate transformed element, and pushing it into `result`—will be accepted by the verifier.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;
use vstd::seq::*;

verus! {

// Specification for sorted sequence
spec fn sorted(a: Seq<int>, l: int, u: int) -> bool
    recommends
        l <= u,
{
    forall|i: int, j: int| l <= i <= j && j <= u && i < a.len() && j < a.len() ==> a[i] <= a[j]
}

// Function to perform binary search
fn binary_search(a: &[int], key: int) -> (index: i32)
    requires
        sorted(a@, 0, a.len() - 1),
        a.len() < i32::MAX as usize,
    ensures
        index >= 0 ==> index < a.len() as i32 && a[index as int] == key,
        index < 0 ==> forall|k: int| 0 <= k && k < a.len() ==> a[k] != key,
{
```

# Reasoning
We must describe two things:

1. spec fn sorted:
   - Inputs: a sequence of mathematical ints, lower bound l, upper bound u.
   - The spec is recommended for l ≤ u (non-empty index span). Within that inclusive span, and only for indices that are also within the physical length of the sequence, every earlier position’s value is ≤ every later position’s value. That formalises non-decreasing order from l to u.

2. fn binary_search:
   - Inputs:
       • a: an immutable slice of ints (runtime array segment).
       • key: the value to be searched.
   - Output:
       • index: i32 sentinel/position.
   - Preconditions:
       • The whole slice is sorted in non-decreasing order, expressed by calling sorted on the ghost view a@ with l = 0 and u = a.len()-1. Because sorted has the recommends l ≤ u, this implicitly requires a.len() ≥ 1 (empty slices are not allowed).
       • The length of the slice is strictly less than i32::MAX so every valid index fits in an i32.
   - Postconditions (two-way):
       • If the function returns a non-negative value, that value must be a valid position (0 ≤ index < a.len()) and the element stored there is exactly key.
       • If it returns a negative value, key does not appear anywhere in the slice (universal negation over all indices).
   - No guarantees are given about *which* matching position is returned when duplicates occur, just that the returned index (if non-negative) indeed contains key.

The function body is unrestricted except that it must satisfy those contracts; a classic logarithmic-time binary search would be typical. Implementation must stay within i32 boundaries (already ensured by the second pre-condition).

# Final Description
<<<
We need to create, in Verus-Rust, a small verified module consisting of one auxiliary specification function and one executable, fully-verified function.

1.  spec fn sorted(a: Seq<int>, l: int, u: int) -> bool
    • Purpose: expresses that the segment a[l ..= u] is sorted in non-decreasing order.
    • Parameters
        – a : Seq<int> (the mathematical sequence that models a runtime array or slice)
        – l : int (lower index, inclusive)
        – u : int (upper index, inclusive)
    • “recommends” clause: l ≤ u (the caller must only invoke sorted when the index range is not empty).
    • Definition:
        forall i,j:int.  l ≤ i ≤ j ∧ j ≤ u ∧ i < a.len() ∧ j < a.len()  ⇒  a[i] ≤ a[j]

    In words, whenever two indices i and j lie inside the supplied range and i is not after j, the element at i is ≤ the element at j. This captures monotone non-decreasing order.

2.  fn binary_search(a: &[int], key: int) -> (index: i32)

    • Inputs
        – a : &[int] (read-only slice containing mathematical integers)
        – key : int  the value to search for
    • Output
        – index : i32  Return convention:
            •  index ≥ 0 → key was found at that position
            •  index < 0 → key is not present anywhere in the slice
    • Preconditions (requires)
        1.  sorted(a@, 0, a.len() − 1)  
            The entire slice (ghost-viewed as a sequence a@) is sorted in non-decreasing order. Because sorted itself “recommends” that the lower bound not exceed the upper bound, this implicitly means the slice must be non-empty (a.len() ≥ 1).
        2.  a.len() < i32::MAX as usize  
            The slice’s length fits into a 32-bit signed integer, ensuring any valid index is representable as an i32.
    • Postconditions (ensures)
        –  index ≥ 0 ⇒ index < a.len() as i32  ∧  a[index] == key  
           (A non-negative result is a legitimate position that indeed contains the sought value.)
        –  index < 0 ⇒ ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≠ key  
           (A negative result certifies that the key does not occur in the slice at all.)
    • No further constraints: if duplicates of key exist, any one of their indices may be returned.

A typical loop-based binary-search implementation will satisfy these contracts, but any code that meets them is acceptable. The specification provides everything a solver/programmer needs to reconstruct the exact function signature, its auxiliary predicate, and all logical obligations.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is basically a one-pass element-wise map.  
To make it more challenging I prepared three stand-alone variants that each introduce a few extra proof obligations and/or slightly rarer specification patterns:

1. “abs_unique_sorted”  
   • Still maps every entry to an absolute value with the `i32::MIN` corner case.  
   • Additionally has to return the values **sorted strictly ascending and without duplicates**.  
   • Therefore the index-by-index relation used in the original exercise is no longer sufficient; the spec has to speak in terms of multisets/sets, which is a bit less common in beginner tasks.

2. “abs_prefix_sum”  
   • Produces a prefix-sum vector of the transformed absolute values instead of the plain mapped values.  
   • Requires an extra numeric bound so that the running sum fits into `i64`, introducing a new reasoning step (the cumulative invariant).  
   • Postconditions must express both the element-wise prefix sum relation and the monotonicity of the result.

   • Parameters: two ghost (pure) sequences of characters, s and t.

   Post-condition (ensures)

   • The name ‘is_anagram’ is misleading: the specification only requires plain equality, not “permutation equality”.

# Reasoning
The original exercise is very small: move all elements into a new `Vec` (or swap in-place), then prove a *single* “mirror–index” post-condition.  
To make the task more challenging we can

• keep the core idea of “reverse a vector”, so the assignment is still recognisable,  
• add one or two *orthogonal* properties that must be tracked ghost-wise (e.g. the sum, the multiset, or a bit-level predicate on the length),  
• occasionally change the ownership pattern (take `&mut` instead of `Vec`, or return an *iterator* of indices),  
• introduce a rarer arithmetic pre-condition (e.g. “length is a power of two”) that the implementation must *assume* and later use in proofs,  
• force an additional relationship between input and output that takes more than one line of logic to verify.

Below are three **stand-alone** tougher variants, each ~10–40 words longer than the original, phrased so that a spec generator can work directly from the English text.


# Output
<<<
Write a verified Verus-Rust function called `rev_with_sum` having the signature  

    fn rev_with_sum(a: Vec<i32>) -> (rev: Vec<i32>, total: i32)

Inputs  
• a : Vec<i32> — the vector whose elements are to be reversed. Ownership is moved into the function.

Outputs  
• rev   : Vec<i32> — the vector containing exactly the elements of `a` but in reverse order.  
• total : i32      — the algebraic sum of all elements originally stored in `a`.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
• a.len() > 0  

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. Length preservation: `rev.len() == a.len()`.  
2. Reversal:  ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ rev[k] == a[a.len() − 1 − k].  
3. Sum correctness:   total == (∑_{k in 0 .. a.len()} a[k]).  
4. Multiset preservation: every value appears in `rev` exactly as many times as in `a` (can be expressed as a bag equality if helper specs are used).

No particular implementation strategy is mandated; a linear-time algorithm is sufficient.  All four post-conditions must be proven.
>>>

<<<
Develop a verified in-place procedure named `reverse_and_negate_edges` with the signature  

    fn reverse_and_negate_edges(v: &mut Vec<i32>)

Input/Output  
• v : &mut Vec<i32> — a mutable reference to the vector; it is updated in place.

Pre-conditions  
• v.len() ≥ 2 and v.len() % 2 == 0   (the vector is non-empty and has even length).

Post-conditions  
1. After the call, for every index k in 0 … v.len(), the element at k equals the pre-call element at position v.len()−1−k.  
2. Additionally, the element now stored at index 0 and the element now stored at index v.len()−1 are each the *negation* of the value that originally occupied the opposite end.  
3. All interior elements (indices 1 … v.len()−2) keep their original sign.

Equivalently, if `old` denotes the vector state on entry:  
• v[0]  == −old[old.len()−1]  
• v[last] == −old[0]  
• ∀ k: 1 ≤ k ≤ last−1 ⇒ v[k] == old[last−k].

The function returns unit; all guarantees are conveyed through the post-conditions.  A two-pointer swap algorithm is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function `rev_power_of_two` with the following contract.

Signature  
    fn rev_power_of_two(arr: Vec<i32>) -> (out: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• arr : Vec<i32> — vector to be reversed; ownership moves into the function.

Outputs  
• out : Vec<i32> — vector equal to `arr` but in reverse element order.

Pre-conditions  
• arr.len() ≥ 1  
• arr.len() is an exact power of two, i.e.  ∃ m:int • m ≥ 0 ∧ arr.len() == (1 << m).

Post-conditions  
1. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < arr.len() ⇒ out[k] == arr[arr.len() − 1 − k].  
2. out.len() == arr.len().  
3. The bit-length `m` witnessing the power-of-two property of the input length is *the same* for the output length.

No auxiliary lemmas beyond simple arithmetic are required, but the proof must explicitly use the power-of-two assumption when showing that the length relation holds after reversal.
>>>

Helper specification function

---------------------------

Inputs

Output

Preconditions (requires)

2. n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX – an explicit guarantee that the closed-form result fits in the u64 range.

Postcondition (ensures)  
s == n * (n + 1) / 2

Interpretation  
Upon return, s must equal the mathematical sum of the first n positive integers (the n-th triangular number). For example, if n = 5 the function must deliver 15; if n = 0 it must deliver 0.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn rotate(a: &[int], offset: usize) -> (b: Vec<int>)
    requires
        0 <= offset,
        offset < a.len(),
        a.len() > 0,
        a.len() < 100000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        a.len() < usize::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        offset < usize::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        b.len() == a.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> b[i] == a[(i + offset as int) % a.len() as int],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The specification describes a pure “rotate-left” operation on an immutable slice.

1. Inputs  
   • a: `&[int]` – a read-only slice whose elements are mathematical integers (Verus `int`).  
   • offset: `usize` – the amount of rotation, expressed as a non-negative machine index.

2. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • 0 ≤ offset (redundant for `usize`, but stated explicitly).  
   • offset < a.len() – we only consider meaningful, in-range rotations.  
   • a.len() > 0 – the slice must be non-empty.  
   • Two extra size guards:  
        – a.len() < 100 000  
        – a.len() < usize::MAX / 2  
        – offset < usize::MAX / 2  
     These bounds simplify verification by ruling out potential overflows in the internal arithmetic.

3. Output  
   • b: `Vec<int>` – a freshly allocated vector that will contain the rotated values.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   • `b.len() == a.len()` – the rotation keeps the length unchanged.  
   • ∀ i : int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒  
       `b[i] == a[(i + offset) mod a.len()]`  
     In other words, element i of the result equals the element that is offset positions to its right in the original slice (cyclically, thanks to the modulo).  
     Because the index inside `a` is `(i + offset) % a.len()`, the operation is a logical “left-rotation” by `offset`.

5. No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are referenced.

To satisfy the contract an implementation must:
   • Allocate a vector `b` with the same length as `a`.  
   • For every valid index i, copy `a[(i + offset) % len]` into `b[i]`.  
   • Respect the arithmetic bounds so that all concrete indices fit inside `usize`; the given preconditions are already strong enough for that.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Implement a formally verified function called rotate that performs a cyclic left-rotation of a slice.

Signature  
    fn rotate(a: &[int], offset: usize) -> (b: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    • a – an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
    • offset – a non-negative rotation distance given as `usize`.

Preconditions  
    1. 0 ≤ offset  
    2. offset < a.len()  
    3. a.len() > 0 (slice must be non-empty)  
    4. a.len() < 100 000  
    5. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2  
    6. offset < usize::MAX / 2

(The last three bounds merely prevent any risk of index overflow during the proof; they do not influence functional behaviour.)

Output  
    • b – a newly created `Vec<int>` that contains the rotated values.

Postconditions  
    1. b.len() == a.len() – result length equals the original length.  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒  
       b[i] == a[(i + offset) mod a.len()]  

Interpretation  
    After the function returns, b holds the same elements as a but shifted left by exactly `offset` positions with wrap-around. Example: if a = [1,2,3,4,5] and offset = 2, then b must be [3,4,5,1,2].

No helper spec functions are required. Any executable body that copies the elements into their required positions and provably satisfies the above contract is acceptable.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
We need to design a few *harder* variations of the very easy triangular–number exercise.  
Ways to increase difficulty while staying reasonable for a beginning Verus learner:

1. Return **two** mathematically-related results instead of one (forces the learner to manage a pair of results and prove two post-conditions).
2. Keep an auxiliary pure `spec fn` so that executable code can call it in `ensures`, but now define **two** helpers and relate them.
3. Require preservation of a small arithmetic invariant in a loop (extra reasoning steps).
4. Add a constraint that makes overflow reasoning slightly trickier (e.g., use `u128` for one result but still bound it).
5. Replace the trivial “triangular number” with a slightly less common but still closed-form series (e.g., sum of squares, alternating sum, or powers of two).

Below are three stand-alone problem statements that incorporate those ideas.  
Each block is wrapped in `<<<  …  >>>` so an automated tool can grab exactly one problem.



# Output
<<<
Design a verified function named `sum_and_squares` together with two supporting specification helpers `tri` and `sq_sum`.

Helper specification functions  
--------------------------------  
spec fn tri(n: nat) -> nat  
• Purpose: return the n-th triangular number.  
• Definition: `n * (n + 1) / 2`.

spec fn sq_sum(n: nat) -> nat  
• Purpose: return the sum of the first n perfect squares.  
• Definition: `n * (n + 1) * (2 * n + 1) / 6`.

Primary executable function  
---------------------------  
fn sum_and_squares(n: u64) -> (t: u64, ss: u128)

Inputs  
• n: a 64-bit unsigned integer.

Outputs  
• t: the triangular number of n (fits in u64).  
• ss: the sum of squares of 1 … n (returned in u128 to avoid accidental overflow).

Preconditions (requires)  
1. n ≤ 90 000 – so both closed-form results fit inside their respective machine types.  
2. tri(n) < u64::MAX.  
3. sq_sum(n) < u128::MAX.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. t == tri(n).  
2. ss == sq_sum(n).  
3. If n ≥ 1 then 2 * ss ≥ n * t (a small lemma about the two series that must also be proven).

Interpretation  
The function must simultaneously compute (a) the ordinary triangular number of n and (b) the sum of the first n squares, and it must prove the three logical obligations above.  Any algorithm—loop, formula, or recursion—written entirely with safe arithmetic is acceptable.  Typical verification will require two loop invariants or two independent direct-formula proofs.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function `alt_sum` with one helper spec `alternating`.

Helper specification function  
--------------------------------  
spec fn alternating(n: nat) -> int  
• Purpose: return Σ_{i=1..n} (-1)^{i+1} · i.  
• Closed form: if n is even then −n/2, otherwise (n+1)/2.

Primary executable function  
---------------------------  
fn alt_sum(n: u64) -> (s: i128)

Inputs  
• n: a 64-bit unsigned integer (may be zero).

Output  
• s: a signed 128-bit integer equal to the alternating sum of the first n natural numbers.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. n ≤ 2^63 – keeps the closed-form within i128 bounds.  
2. alternating(n) ∈ [i128::MIN, i128::MAX] (fits in range).

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. s == alternating(n).  
2. |s| ≤ n (obvious but must be proved).  
3. If n mod 3 == 0 then s mod 3 == 0 (forces an extra arithmetic lemma).

Implementation notes  
You may implement either a loop with an invariant “partial_sum == alternating(i)” or compute the closed form directly.  All arithmetic must be performed safely; no `unsafe` or unchecked casts are permitted.
>>>

<<<
Develop a verified function `geo_sum_pow2` and an accompanying spec helper `pow2_sum`.

Helper specification function  
--------------------------------  
spec fn pow2_sum(n: nat) -> nat  
• Purpose: return Σ_{k=0..n} 2^k, the finite geometric series of base 2.  
• Definition: `2^(n + 1) - 1`.

Primary executable function  
---------------------------  
fn geo_sum_pow2(n: u32) -> (s: u128)

Inputs  
• n: a 32-bit unsigned integer.

Output  
• s: an unsigned 128-bit integer that will hold the geometric sum.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. n ≤ 126 – guarantees 2^(n+1) − 1 < 2^127 ≤ u128::MAX.  
2. pow2_sum(n) < u128::MAX (explicit range check).

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. s == pow2_sum(n).  
2. s + 1 is a power of two (prove using bit-twiddling lemmas or pure arithmetic).  
3. For every k ≤ n, the k-th bit of s (counting from LSB as bit 0) is set to 1.

Interpretation  
The function must return 2^(n+1) − 1 and certify the three properties.  
An efficient implementation may use the formula directly or a loop doubling an accumulator with the invariant “acc == 2^i − 1”.  All code must be written with safe operations only; the verifier must discharge the bit-level obligations in postconditions 2 and 3.
>>>

Write a verified Verus/Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    find_min_index

Inputs  
    • a: &[int] — an immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
    • s: usize  — inclusive start index of the search interval.  
    • e: usize  — exclusive end index of the search interval.

Post-conditions (ensures)

    2. min_i < e

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

spec fn is_overflowing(n: u64) -> bool {
    n * (n + 1) / 2 >= u64::MAX
}

fn sum_ints(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)
    requires
        n > 0,
        !is_overflowing(n),
        n < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        sum == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
We have two items inside the `verus! { … }` block.

1. Helper specification function `is_overflowing`
   • Signature: `spec fn is_overflowing(n: u64) -> bool`
   • Pure/ghost (only for reasoning, never executed at run time).
   • Returns `true` exactly when the closed-form expression for the 1-to-n summation would no longer fit in a 64-bit unsigned integer.  
     The precise definition is  
     `n * (n + 1) / 2 >= u64::MAX`.

2. Executable, verified function `sum_ints`
   • Signature: `fn sum_ints(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)`
   • Parameters
     – `n : u64` (must be positive and “small enough”).
   • Return
     – `sum : u64`.
   • Preconditions (`requires`)
     1. `n > 0` — the caller must ask for at least one term.
     2. `!is_overflowing(n)` — the triangular number must provably fit in a `u64`.
     3. `n < 1000000` — an extra, more concrete bound that implies the previous one and also makes the arithmetic inside the implementation trivially non-overflowing.
   • Postcondition (`ensures`)
     – `sum == n * (n + 1) / 2`.  
       In words: the function must return the exact sum of the first `n` positive integers (often called the “triangular number” for `n`).

No other helper lemmas or specification constructs are referenced.

Important subtleties
• The helper `is_overflowing` is expressed using normal `u64` arithmetic; callers therefore have to guarantee that the multiplication and addition appearing inside the predicate stay within range whenever they invoke it.  This is achieved by the explicit `n < 1_000_000` bound in the `requires` clause.  
• Overflow safety for the body of `sum_ints` is likewise ensured by those same pre-conditions, so any straightforward implementation (a loop adding 1 … n or the closed-form formula) can be verified.

# Final Description
<<<
You must specify and implement two entities in Verus-Rust.

──────────────────────────────────
1. Pure specification helper
──────────────────────────────────
spec fn is_overflowing(n: u64) -> bool

Purpose  
    Determine whether the arithmetic expression for the sum 1 + 2 + … + n would overflow a 64-bit unsigned integer.

Exact definition  
    Returns the Boolean value of  
        n * (n + 1) / 2 >= u64::MAX

(This uses ordinary `u64` arithmetic; therefore whoever calls this function must only do so with values of `n` for which the multiplication and addition do not themselves overflow.)

──────────────────────────────────
2. Verified executable function
──────────────────────────────────
fn sum_ints(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)

Inputs  
    n: u64 — a positive integer indicating how many initial natural numbers to add.

Output  
    sum: u64 — the sum of the first `n` positive integers, i.e. 1 + 2 + … + n.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. n > 0                                    // the range must be non-empty  
    2. !is_overflowing(n)                       // the triangular number fits in u64  
    3. n < 1_000_000                            // concrete upper bound guaranteeing the above

Post-condition (ensures)  
    sum == n * (n + 1) / 2

This post-condition fully characterises the required behaviour: after the function returns, `sum` must equal the mathematical triangular number for the given `n`.

Implementation freedom  
Any body that satisfies the contract is acceptable.  Because the pre-conditions rule out overflow, you may:
    • use the closed-form formula directly, or
    • compute the sum in a loop,
    • or employ any other verified technique.

No additional ghost state or helper lemmas are necessary beyond the single predicate `is_overflowing`.
>>>

   • Signature: `fn sum_ints(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)`
   • Parameters
     – `n : u64` (must be positive and “small enough”).
   • Return
     – `sum : u64`.
   • Preconditions (`requires`)
     1. `n > 0` — the caller must ask for at least one term.
     2. `!is_overflowing(n)` — the triangular number must provably fit in a `u64`.
     3. `n < 1000000` — an extra, more concrete bound that implies the previous one and also makes the arithmetic inside the implementation trivially non-overflowing.
   • Postcondition (`ensures`)
     – `sum == n * (n + 1) / 2`.  
       In words: the function must return the exact sum of the first `n` positive integers (often called the “triangular number” for `n`).

No other helper lemmas or specification constructs are referenced.

Important subtleties
• The helper `is_overflowing` is expressed using normal `u64` arithmetic; callers therefore have to guarantee that the multiplication and addition appearing inside the predicate stay within range whenever they invoke it.  This is achieved by the explicit `n < 1_000_000` bound in the `requires` clause.  
• Overflow safety for the body of `sum_ints` is likewise ensured by those same pre-conditions, so any straightforward implementation (a loop adding 1 … n or the closed-form formula) can be verified.

# Final Description
<<<
You must specify and implement two entities in Verus-Rust.

──────────────────────────────────
1. Pure specification helper
──────────────────────────────────
spec fn is_overflowing(n: u64) -> bool

Purpose  
    Determine whether the arithmetic expression for the sum 1 + 2 + … + n would overflow a 64-bit unsigned integer.

Exact definition  
    Returns the Boolean value of  
        n * (n + 1) / 2 >= u64::MAX

(This uses ordinary `u64` arithmetic; therefore whoever calls this function must only do so with values of `n` for which the multiplication and addition do not themselves overflow.)

──────────────────────────────────
2. Verified executable function
──────────────────────────────────
fn sum_ints(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)

Inputs  
    n: u64 — a positive integer indicating how many initial natural numbers to add.

Output  
    sum: u64 — the sum of the first `n` positive integers, i.e. 1 + 2 + … + n.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. n > 0                                    // the range must be non-empty  
    2. !is_overflowing(n)                       // the triangular number fits in u64  
    3. n < 1_000_000                            // concrete upper bound guaranteeing the above

Post-condition (ensures)  
    sum == n * (n + 1) / 2

This post-condition fully characterises the required behaviour: after the function returns, `sum` must equal the mathematical triangular number for the given `n`.

Implementation freedom  
Any body that satisfies the contract is acceptable.  Because the pre-conditions rule out overflow, you may:
    • use the closed-form formula directly, or
    • compute the sum in a loop,
    • or employ any other verified technique.

No additional ghost state or helper lemmas are necessary beyond the single predicate `is_overflowing`.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is essentially a linear-scan minimum search on a sub-slice.  
To make the exercise more interesting we can

1. Break the “any minimal index is fine” tie by requiring the *first* (or *last*) minimal index.  
2. Ask the implementation to compute **extra information** (e.g. the multiplicity of the minimum) and verify it.  
3. Restrict the search to a subset of positions that fulfil an additional predicate (e.g. “only indices whose element is even”), which forces the learner to introduce and reason about an existential pre-condition as well as an intra-loop filter.

Below are three stand-alone, progressively harder specifications that incorporate these ideas.  
Each description is detailed enough that a spec generator can translate it directly into Verus `requires`/`ensures` clauses.

# Output
<<<
Write a verified Verus/Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    find_first_min_index

Inputs  
    • a: &[int]  — an immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
    • s: usize   — inclusive start index of the search interval.  
    • e: usize   — exclusive end index of the search interval.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() > 0                              // slice is non-empty  
    2. s < a.len()                              
    3. e <= a.len()                             
    4. e > s                                     // interval [s, e) is non-empty

Outputs  
    • min_i: usize — an index into the slice.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. s ≤ min_i < e                             // result lies in the interval  
    2. ∀ k:int • s ≤ k < e ⇒ a[min_i] ≤ a[k]     // value at min_i is minimal in the interval  
    3. ∀ k:int • s ≤ k < min_i ⇒ a[k] > a[min_i] // *strictly* greater for every earlier index  
       In words: if the minimal value occurs several times, the function must return the *left-most* occurrence.

No auxiliary ghost parameters are necessary.  Any executable body that provably returns this first minimal index (for example, a linear scan that keeps both the current minimum and its earliest position) will satisfy the specification.
>>>

<<<
Write a verified Verus/Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    min_index_and_count

Inputs  
    • a: &[int]  — immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
    • s: usize   — inclusive start index of the search interval.  
    • e: usize   — exclusive end index of the search interval.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. s < e ≤ a.len()      // non-empty half-open interval inside the slice

Outputs  
    • min_i: usize — an index into the slice.  
    • cnt  : nat   — the number of times the minimal value appears in [s, e).

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. s ≤ min_i < e.  
    2. ∀ k:int • s ≤ k < e ⇒ a[min_i] ≤ a[k].  
    3. cnt > 0.  
    4. cnt = |{ k:int | s ≤ k < e ∧ a[k] = a[min_i] }|.  
       (That is, `cnt` equals the cardinality of indices whose element equals the minimum.)  
    5. ∀ k:int • s ≤ k < min_i ⇒ a[k] > a[min_i].  
       This additionally guarantees that `min_i` is the first occurrence of the minimum.

The function may use any algorithm that fulfils the specification, but both results must be produced consistently and verified together.
>>>

<<<
Write a verified Verus/Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    find_min_even_index

Purpose  
    Among the indices in a half-open interval whose element is an *even* integer, return the index of the smallest such element.  
    (If no index inside the interval stores an even number, the function is not required to handle that case – see pre-condition 5.)

Inputs  
    • a: &[int]  — immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
    • s: usize   — inclusive start bound.  
    • e: usize   — exclusive end bound.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. s < e ≤ a.len().  
    2. ∃ k:int • s ≤ k < e ∧ a[k] % 2 = 0.  // at least one even element exists in the range  
    3. For all i: int with s ≤ i < a.len() the value fits into a machine `int` (automatically true in Verus).  

Outputs  
    • idx: usize — an index into the slice.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. s ≤ idx < e.  
    2. a[idx] % 2 = 0                        // value at idx is even  
    3. ∀ k:int • s ≤ k < e ∧ a[k] % 2 = 0 ⇒ a[idx] ≤ a[k].  
       (Minimal among *even* elements only.)  
    4. ∀ k:int • s ≤ k < idx ⇒ a[k] % 2 = 1 ∨ a[k] > a[idx].  
       (Every earlier index either holds an odd number or an even number strictly larger than the minimum.)  

No auxiliary ghost parameters are necessary.  One way to implement the specification is a single pass that skips odd elements while tracking the current minimal even value and its position.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is almost trivial because the last two pre-conditions force both inputs to be 0, so the student only needs to return 0.  
To make new tasks slightly harder—yet still suitable for a single short file—we can

• keep the same general “pure spec + verified executable” pattern,  
• switch from plain multiplication to less common arithmetic notions (fast exponentiation, gcd with Bézout coefficients, binomial coefficient),  
• add realistic overflow-avoidance pre-conditions that the student must exploit in the proof,  
• require one or two simple loop invariants or a decreases clause (but without demanding advanced data structures), and  
• keep the overall code size small enough for a learner.

Below are three standalone problem statements; each is framed so a code-generation tool can turn the English directly into a Verus spec.


# Output
<<<
Implement two items in Verus.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn pow_nat(base: nat, exp: nat) -> nat

   Purpose: return baseᵉˣᵖ (ordinary exponentiation over mathematical naturals).  
   Recursive definition (expected form, but any equivalent mathematical definition is acceptable):  
     • pow_nat(base, 0)     == 1  
     • pow_nat(base, exp+1) == base * pow_nat(base, exp)

   No separate contracts are needed because the body gives the exact value.

2. Executable, verified function  
   fn fast_pow(base: u128, exp: u32) -> (r: u128)

Inputs  
  • base: u128  
  • exp : u32      // 32-bit to keep the loop short

Output  
  • r   : u128

Additional ghost or helper functions, as well as decreases clauses, are permitted but not required.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
  1. base ≤ 10¹²            // large enough to be interesting, yet safely far from u128::MAX  
  2. exp  ≤ 60              // guarantees the mathematical result fits in u128  
  3. pow_nat(base as nat, exp as nat) < u128::MAX   // prevents overflow

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  A. r == pow_nat(base as nat, exp as nat)  
  B. r ≥ 1

Implementation hints  
  • Use the classic “repeated squaring” (binary exponentiation) loop.  
  • The loop’s invariant should relate the partially computed result, the current base²ᵏ, and the remaining exponent bits to the mathematical pow_nat.  
  • exp is a machine integer, so you may need a ghost variable of type nat to express the invariant cleanly.  
  • You must convince Verus that each multiplication cannot overflow, using the pre-condition (3).

No other libraries, unsafe code, or external assumptions are allowed.
>>>

<<<
Implement three items in Verus.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn gcd_nat(a: nat, b: nat) -> nat

   Purpose: greatest common divisor of two naturals.  
   It must satisfy:  
     • gcd_nat(a,b) | a ∧ gcd_nat(a,b) | b, and  
     • ∀d: nat. d|a ∧ d|b ⇒ d|gcd_nat(a,b)

   Any mathematically sound definition (e.g., Euclidean recursion) is acceptable.

2. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn bezout_pair(a: nat, b: nat) -> (x: int, y: int)

   Purpose: return one pair (x,y) such that x·a + y·b == gcd_nat(a,b).  
   It is sufficient to specify this with an ensures clause; an executable body is NOT required.

3. Executable, verified function  
   fn euclid(a: u64, b: u64) -> (g: u64, x: i128, y: i128)

Inputs  
  • a, b: u64, not both zero

Outputs  
  • g: u64   (the gcd)  
  • x, y: i128  (Bézout coefficients)

Pre-conditions (requires)  
  1. a > 0 ∨ b > 0  
  2. a ≤ 10¹², b ≤ 10¹²          // keeps the number of loop iterations modest

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  A. g == gcd_nat(a as nat, b as nat)  
  B. (x as int) * (a as int) + (y as int) * (b as int) == g as int  
  C. g | a ∧ g | b  
  D. ∀d: nat. d|a ∧ d|b ⇒ d ≤ g

Implementation requirements  
  • Use the classic iterative Euclidean algorithm extended to track Bézout coefficients.  
  • Provide appropriate loop invariants showing the maintained relation between current (r_old, r_new) and their coefficients.  
  • Supply a decreases clause to convince Verus of termination.  
  • Prove that x and y fit in i128 under the stated input bounds (they do).

No ghost state outside loop invariants is necessary, but it is allowed.  No external crates or unsafe code.
>>>

<<<
Implement two items in Verus.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn binom(n: nat, k: nat) -> nat

   Purpose: return “n choose k”, i.e., the size of the k-element subsets of an n-element set.  
   Accepted mathematical definition:  
     • binom(n,k) == 0 if k > n  
     • binom(n,0) == 1  
     • binom(n,k) == n * binom(n-1,k-1) / k  for 0 < k ≤ n  
   Any equivalent definition is acceptable.

2. Executable, verified function  
   fn choose(n: u32, k: u32) -> (c: u128)

Inputs  
  • n, k: u32

Output  
  • c: u128

Pre-conditions (requires)  
  1. k ≤ n  
  2. n ≤ 34               // ensures result < 2¹²⁸, so no overflow  
  3. k ≤ 17 ∨ n-k ≤ 17    // encourages students to exploit symmetry if desired  
  4. binom(n as nat, k as nat) < u128::MAX   // explicit overflow guard

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  c == binom(n as nat, k as nat)

Implementation guidance  
  • Use an iterative multiplicative formula that keeps intermediate results within u128, e.g.,  
      result ← 1  
      for i = 1 .. k { result ← result * (n+1-i) / i }  
  • Provide a loop invariant showing that after i iterations,  
        result == binom(n as nat, i as nat).  
  • Show that each multiplication/division step does not overflow, using pre-condition (4).  
  • A decreases clause is optional; Verus usually accepts the standard for-loop.

No external libraries or unsafe code are permitted. Ghost variables for stating the invariant are acceptable and need no explicit ownership proof.
>>>

nl_desc_oai_full_with_reasoning:
# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn smallest_missing_number(s: &[u32]) -> (v: u32)
    requires
        s.len() > 0,
        s.len() < 1000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        forall|i: int, j: int| 0 <= i && i < j && j < s.len() ==> s[i] <= s[j],
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < s.len() ==> s[i] >= 0,
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < s.len() ==> s[i] < u32::MAX - 1,
    ensures
        0 <= v,
        !forall|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> v == s[i],
        forall|k: int| 0 <= k && k < v ==> k == s[k],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We must describe the spec thoroughly.

Inputs:
- s: an immutable slice of 32-bit unsigned integers, length in runtime bound.

Preconditions:
1. Non-empty slice: s.len() > 0
2. Length upper-bounded by 999: s.len() < 1000. Avoids arithmetic overflow during any body reasoning.
3. Non-decreasing order (sorted): For all indices i, j with 0 ≤ i < j < s.len(), s[i] ≤ s[j].
4. Every element is a non-negative integer (redundant for u32 but expressed over mathematical ints).
5. Every element is strictly less than u32::MAX − 1. Hence there is always at least one representable value larger than any entry.

Outputs:
- v: u32

Postconditions:
1. v is a non-negative integer (trivial for u32 but stated explicitly).
2. It is not the case that v equals every element of the slice; formally ¬(∀ i • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ v = s[i]).  Equivalently, there exists at least one index where s[i] ≠ v, i.e. v does not appear everywhere, which coupled with the next clause shows v is missing altogether.
3. For all k with 0 ≤ k < v, the kth element of the slice equals k.  In words, every non-negative integer strictly smaller than v occurs at the matching index inside the (sorted) slice.

Because the slice is sorted, the third clause implies that each integer from 0 up to v − 1 is present (exactly once) and in ascending order, while the second clause guarantees that v itself is not present in the slice.  Consequently v is the least (smallest) non-negative integer that does not occur in s.

The length bound (< 1000) plus the element bound (< u32::MAX − 1) together guarantee that such a v always exists and fits in a u32.

Implementation intuition: a linear scan over the slice looking for the first index i where s[i] ≠ i suffices; if no such index exists within the slice, return s.len().

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Write a formally-verified Verus/Rust function named smallest_missing_number that, given a sorted array of unsigned 32-bit integers, returns the lowest non-negative integer that is absent from it.  The full contract is as follows.

Input
• s: &[u32] — an immutable slice of 32-bit unsigned integers.

Pre-conditions (requires)
1. s.len() > 0    // the slice is non-empty
2. s.len() < 1000 // length upper-bounded (avoids overflow in later reasoning)
3. ∀ i,j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≤ s[j]  
   The slice is monotonically non-decreasing (sorted in ascending order; duplicates allowed).
4. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≥ 0  
   (Automatically true for u32, but stated explicitly in the specification.)
5. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] < u32::MAX − 1  
   Every element is at most u32::MAX − 2, ensuring there is always at least one larger representable value.

Output
• v: u32 — the smallest non-negative integer missing from s.

Post-conditions (ensures)
1. 0 ≤ v                              // v is non-negative (trivial for u32)
2. ¬(∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ v == s[i])  
   v is not equal to every element of the slice; equivalently, the slice does not contain v.
3. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v ⇒ k == s[k]  
   For every integer strictly smaller than v, the kth entry of the slice equals k.  
   Because the slice is sorted, this guarantees that all smaller values 0,1,…,v−1 are present (and in order).

Taken together, the post-conditions state that
• every non-negative integer smaller than v appears in s (exactly at its own index), and
• v itself is absent,
so v is indeed the least (smallest) missing non-negative integer.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required.  A correct implementation can, for example, scan the slice from left to right and return the first index i where s[i] ≠ i; if no such index exists within the slice, the correct answer is s.len().
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original exercise is essentially a “hello-world” verification task: implement and verify the square of a single natural number.  
To increase difficulty in a controlled way, we can

 • keep the overall style (one or two supporting spec functions plus one executable function),  
 • require the implementer to reason about several arithmetic facts simultaneously (e.g. two results instead of one, an identity such as n² – m², or higher exponents),  
 • add a slightly less common pre-condition such as “n is a power-of-two” or “n≥m”,  
 • incorporate an overflow guard that is not completely trivial,  
 • add one extra logical consequence in the post-condition that must also be proved.

Below are three stand-alone, increasingly involved variants that satisfy those goals while remaining compact.


# Output
<<<
Create three entities:

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn is_power_of_two(k: nat) -> bool  
   Definition:  
     • returns true  ⇔  ∃e: nat. k == 1 << e.  
   Purpose: recognises natural numbers that are exact powers of two.

2. Pure specification function  
   spec fn fourth_power(k: nat) -> nat  
   Definition: returns k * k * k * k.

3. Executable, verified function  
   fn pow4_if_power_of_two(n: u64) -> (y: u64)

   • Input  
     – n: u64

   • Output  
     – y: u64

   • Preconditions (requires)  
     1. is_power_of_two(n as nat)                               // non-trivial arithmetic guard  
     2. n ≤ 2^16                                                // avoids overflow of n⁴ inside u64  
     3. (n as u128).pow(4) < u128::from(u64::MAX)               // explicit overflow exclusion

   • Post-conditions (ensures)  
     a. y == fourth_power(n as nat)                             // functional correctness  
     b. is_power_of_two((y as nat).sqrt().sqrt())               // the 4-th root of y is still a power-of-two  
        (informally: if n = 2^e, then y = 2^(4e)). No other auxiliaries required.
>>>

<<<
Create four entities:

1. Pure specification function  
   spec fn square(k: nat) -> nat { k * k }

2. Pure specification function  
   spec fn triangular(k: nat) -> nat { k * (k + 1) / 2 }

3. Ghost lemma (optional for implementation proofs, but must be declared)  
   proof fn triangle_le_square(k: nat)  
   Requires: k ≥ 2  
   Ensures:  triangular(k) ≤ square(k)

4. Executable, verified function  
   fn square_and_sum(n: u64) -> (sq: u64, tri: u64)

   • Inputs  
     – n: u64

   • Outputs  
     – sq: u64 (n²)  
     – tri: u64 (∑_{i=1}^n i)

     3. (n as u128) * (n as u128 + 1) / 2    < u128::from(u64::MAX)        // tri fits

   • Post-conditions (ensures)

     b. tri == triangular(n as nat)  
     c. tri ≤ sq                                                // additional, less common property
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn single(x: &[i32], y: &[i32]) -> (b: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        x.len() > 0,
        y.len() > 0,
        x.len() < 1000000,
        y.len() < 1000000,
    ensures
        b.len() == x.len() + y.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < x.len() ==> b[i] == x[i],
        forall|i: int| x.len() <= i < x.len() + y.len() ==> b[i] == y[i - x.len()],
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification describes a function `single` whose purpose is to concatenate two non-empty slices of 32-bit integers into one `Vec<i32>`.

Inputs  
1. `x: &[i32]` – immutable slice.  
2. `y: &[i32]` – immutable slice.

Output  
`b: Vec<i32>` – a newly created, owned vector.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
• `x.len() > 0` and `y.len() > 0` guarantee both slices are non-empty (avoids vacuous ranges).  
• `x.len() < 1_000_000` and `y.len() < 1_000_000` impose an upper bound, presumably to keep the final length within practical/verified limits and to avoid overflow of the addition `x.len() + y.len()` when it is stored in a `usize`.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. `b.len() == x.len() + y.len()` – the resulting vector’s length is exactly the sum of the two input lengths.  
2.  `forall|i:int| 0 ≤ i < x.len() ⇒ b[i] == x[i]` – every element coming from slice `x` is reproduced verbatim in the same order at the beginning of `b`.  
3.  `forall|i:int| x.len() ≤ i < x.len() + y.len() ⇒ b[i] == y[i - x.len()]` – elements coming from `y` occupy the remainder of `b`; offset `i - x.len()` re-indexes into `y`, preserving order.

No additional spec functions, ghost code, or dependent lemmas are referenced.

From the spec we can infer the exact behaviour:  
`single(x, y)` returns the vector `[ x[0], x[1], …, x[x.len()-1], y[0], y[1], …, y[y.len()-1] ]`.

Implementation details left to the programmer: allocate a vector of capacity `x.len() + y.len()` and extend it with the two slices, or any equivalent strategy that provably satisfies the above contracts.

# Final Description (between tags)
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus/Rust function named `single` that concatenates two non-empty integer slices into a single vector.

Function signature  
fn single(x: &[i32], y: &[i32]) -> (b: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• `x`: immutable slice of `i32`, length > 0 and < 1 000 000.  
• `y`: immutable slice of `i32`, length > 0 and < 1 000 000.

Output  
• `b`: a `Vec<i32>` containing first all elements of `x` followed by all elements of `y` in their original order.

Formal contract  
Pre-conditions  
1. `x.len() > 0`  
2. `y.len() > 0`  
3. `x.len() < 1_000_000`  
4. `y.len() < 1_000_000`

Post-conditions  
1. `b.len() == x.len() + y.len()`  
2. ∀ i ∈ [0, x.len()) → `b[i] == x[i]`  
3. ∀ i ∈ [x.len(), x.len()+y.len()) → `b[i] == y[i - x.len()]`

No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are needed. Any executable body that creates a vector of the required size and copies the two slices in order—thereby meeting the stated conditions—will satisfy the verifier.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise only requires a straight-line comparison of two numbers, so a learner can finish it with one `if` statement and a single `ensures`.  
To raise the bar, I propose three alternative tasks.  Each task

* keeps the “helper spec function + verified executable function” format (so the learner can still recognise the structure),
* introduces extra logical properties that must be proved,
* forces the learner to write loop invariants or reason about *multiple* output values, and
* stays small enough that it can still be completed in a classroom / interview setting.

Task 1 (“median-of-three”) adds an uncommon requirement: also return the *index of the first occurrence* of the median value, which obliges the student to reason about ties.

Task 2 (“gcd”) replaces the trivial max computation with the Euclidean algorithm and demands proofs about divisibility and minimality—classic number-theoretic reasoning that is less common in day-to-day code.

Task 3 (“min-max of a slice”) introduces mutable iteration plus universal quantification over all elements of a slice, forcing the student to supply loop invariants that relate the running extrema to every element already scanned.

All three tasks are phrased in sufficient detail (pre/postconditions, helper specs, ownership/borrowing notes) so a spec generator can create the needed Verus skeleton without extra context.

# Output
<<<
Create two items in Verus:

1. Helper specification function  
   spec fn median_of_three(x: nat, y: nat, z: nat) -> (m: nat)  
   Purpose: return the *median* (middle) value among x, y, and z in the mathematical natural-number domain.  
   Definition (one possible mathematical phrasing):  
       if (x <= y && y <= z) || (z <= y && y <= x) { y }  
       else if (y <= x && x <= z) || (z <= x && x <= y) { x }  
       else { z }

   Additionally, define a pure specification function  
   spec fn first_median_index(x: nat, y: nat, z: nat) -> (idx: nat)  
   Returns 0, 1, or 2, indicating the *first* argument position whose value equals `median_of_three(x, y, z)`.  
   Ties are broken by the lowest index.

2. Executable, fully verified function  
   fn median_with_index(a: u64, b: u64, c: u64) -> (m: u64, idx: u8)

   Inputs  
     • a, b, c: u64  

   Outputs  
     • m:   u64 — must equal the mathematical median of a, b, c.  
     • idx: u8  — must be 0, 1, or 2 and indicate the first position whose value equals `m`.

   Preconditions (requires)  
     • None.

   Postconditions (ensures)  
     • m == median_of_three(a as nat, b as nat, c as nat)  
     • idx as nat == first_median_index(a as nat, b as nat, c as nat)  
     • match idx {  
         0 => m == a,  
         1 => m == b,  
         2 => m == c,  
         _ => false  
       }

No ghost state other than what is necessary for the proof is allowed.  Any implementation (nested `if`s or sorting network) that satisfies the contract is acceptable, but all postconditions must be proven.
>>>

<<<
Create two items in Verus:

1. Helper specification function  
   spec fn gcd_nat(x: nat, y: nat) -> nat  
   Purpose: return the greatest common divisor (mathematical GCD) of x and y.  
   Required properties (must be proven separately or used in postconditions):  
     • divides: forall d: nat. d > 0 && x % d == 0 && y % d == 0 ==> d <= gcd_nat(x, y)  
     • common_divisor: x % gcd_nat(x, y) == 0 && y % gcd_nat(x, y) == 0

2. Executable, fully verified function  
   fn gcd_u64(mut a: u64, mut b: u64) -> (g: u64)

   Inputs  
     • a, b: u64 (both may be zero)

   Output  
     • g: u64

   Preconditions (requires)  
     • !(a == 0 && b == 0)      // at least one input is non-zero

   Postconditions (ensures)  
     • g as nat == gcd_nat(a as nat, b as nat)  
     • a as nat % g as nat == 0  
     • b as nat % g as nat == 0  
     • forall d: nat. (d > 0 && a as nat % d == 0 && b as nat % d == 0) ==> d <= g as nat

Implementation hint: use the classical Euclidean algorithm (`while b != 0 { let tmp = b; b = a % b; a = tmp; }`) and supply an appropriate decreasing variant to prove termination.  No additional lemmas other than what is stated above may be introduced.
>>>

<<<
Create two items in Verus:

1. Helper specification predicate  
   spec fn is_extrema_of_slice(slice: Seq<nat>, lo: nat, hi: nat) -> bool  
   Definition:  
     slice.len() > 0  
     && lo <= hi  
     && forall k: int :: 0 <= k < slice.len() ==> lo <= slice[k] <= hi  
     && exists k: int :: 0 <= k < slice.len() && slice[k] == lo  
     && exists k: int :: 0 <= k < slice.len() && slice[k] == hi

2. Executable, fully verified function  
   fn min_max(slice: &[u64]) -> (lo: u64, hi: u64)

   Inputs  
     • slice: immutable reference to a *non-empty* run-time slice of 64-bit unsigned integers.

   Outputs  
     • lo: u64 — the minimum element of the slice  
     • hi: u64 — the maximum element of the slice

   Preconditions (requires)  
     • slice.len() > 0

   Postconditions (ensures)  
     • is_extrema_of_slice(slice@.map(|x| x as nat), lo as nat, hi as nat)  
       where `slice@` is Verus’s ghost-view of the original slice and `.map` lifts each value to `nat`.

The implementation must iterate through the run-time slice exactly once (single pass) and maintain loop invariants that relate the current `(lo, hi)` to all elements inspected so far.  No sorting, cloning, or additional memory allocations are permitted.
>>>

   pub open spec fn contains(s: Seq<int>, k: int) -> bool

   • Returns `true` precisely when the sequence `s` contains the element `k`.  
   • Formal definition:  
       exists |i: int| 0 ≤ i ∧ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] == k

2. Executable function

   fn contains_k(s: &[int], k: int) -> (result: bool)

   Inputs  
     – s: an immutable slice of runtime integers.  
     – k: the integer being searched for.

   Output  
     – result: bool.

   Preconditions

   Postcondition  
     result == contains(s@, k)

   where `s@` is the ghost sequence view of the slice.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn tangent(r: &[u64], x: &[u64]) -> (b: bool)
    requires
        x.len() > 0,
        r.len() < 1000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        x.len() < 1000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        forall|i: nat, j: nat| 0 <= i <= j < x.len() ==> x[i as int] <= x[j as int],
        forall|i: nat, j: nat| (0 <= i < r.len() && 0 <= j < x.len()) ==> (r[i as int] >= 0 && x[j as int] >= 0),
    ensures
        !b ==> forall|i: nat, j: nat| 0 <= i < r.len() && 0 <= j < x.len() ==> r[i as int] != x[j as int],
        b ==> exists|i: nat, j: nat| 0 <= i < r.len() && 0 <= j < x.len() && r[i as int] == x[j as int],
{
```

# Reasoning
We must describe the function spec precisely so one could recreate it.  

‐ Function name: tangent  
‐ Inputs:  
  * r: &[u64] – an immutable slice (runtime view into a contiguous array) of 64-bit unsigned integers, length r.len(). The slice may be empty.  
  * x: &[u64] – another immutable slice of u64, length x.len(). It is required to be non-empty and sorted in non-decreasing order.  

‐ Output:  
  * b: bool – a Boolean flag telling whether the two slices share at least one identical value.  

‐ Preconditions (requires):  
  1. x.len() > 0           // x must contain at least one element.  
  2. r.len() < 1000        // both lengths are bounded (< 1000) only to simplify later reasoning about loops / overflow.  
  3. x.len() < 1000  
  4. Sortedness of x: ∀ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ x[i] ≤ x[j].  
     (Using maths indices; nat means non-negative integers. cast to int because Verus quantifiers use mathematical integers.)  
  5. Non-negativity (redundant for u64 but stated explicitly):  
     ∀ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i < r.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ r[i] ≥ 0 ∧ x[j] ≥ 0.  

‐ Postconditions (ensures):  
  • If the function returns false (¬b), the slices are disjoint:  
      ∀ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i < r.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ r[i] ≠ x[j].  
  • If it returns true (b), there exists at least one common value:  
      ∃ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i < r.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < x.len() ∧ r[i] = x[j].  

The spec does not constrain HOW the result is computed, only what logical relation must hold between b and the contents of the two slices.  Because x is sorted, an efficient implementation may take advantage of that fact (e.g. two-pointer merge walk or binary search), but a naïve nested loop is also acceptable as long as it satisfies the contracts and honours the length bounds.

Corner cases to remember:  
• r may be empty; then returning false suffices (disjointness vacuously holds).  
• Multiple duplicate elements are allowed in either slice.  
• All arithmetic in the specification uses mathematical integers, so there is no overflow risk for comparisons.

# Final Description (place between tags)
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function called `tangent` that decides whether two slices share an element.

Signature  
    fn tangent(r: &[u64], x: &[u64]) -> (b: bool)

Inputs  
• r – immutable slice of 64-bit unsigned integers, length r.len().  
• x – immutable slice of 64-bit unsigned integers, length x.len().

Output  
• b – Boolean: true exactly when the two slices have at least one identical value.

Preconditions  
1. x.len() > 0 (x is non-empty).  
2. r.len() < 1000.  
3. x.len() < 1000.  
   (The two length upper bounds merely avoid reasoning about very large loops or possible overflow.)  
4. x is sorted in non-decreasing order:  
      ∀ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ x[i] ≤ x[j].  
5. Every element mentioned is non-negative (redundant for u64 but declared):  
      ∀ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i < r.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ r[i] ≥ 0 ∧ x[j] ≥ 0.

Postconditions  
• If the returned Boolean is false, the slices are disjoint:  
      ∀ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i < r.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ r[i] ≠ x[j].  
• If the returned Boolean is true, there exists at least one position pair holding the same value:  
      ∃ i,j : nat • 0 ≤ i < r.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < x.len() ∧ r[i] = x[j].

Informally: `b` is true iff the intersection of the two value multisets is non-empty.

No other helper spec functions or lemmas are required. An implementation may use any verified algorithm (linear scan, two-pointer, binary search, etc.) as long as it satisfies the contracts stated above.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original task is very small: it only needs a single existential search and one loop (or even the standard‐library `contains()`).  
To make the assignment more challenging in Verus we can

1. Return more information than a bare Boolean (for instance, the *first* index), which forces the student to prove a *minimality* property in addition to existence.
2. Require an exact *count* of occurrences – this demands both universal (upper-bound) and existential reasoning, plus an arithmetic proof that the count is within bounds.
3. Ask for a property that requires *quadratic* reasoning such as “all elements are pairwise different”, thereby forcing a nested loop and the use of ghost state or helper lemmas.

Each revised problem is self-contained, defines all specs that must be written, and spells out precise pre- and post-conditions so that a spec generator can work directly from the text.

# Output
<<<
Create two verified components in Verus-Rust.

1. Public specification function

   pub open spec fn first_index(s: Seq<int>, k: int) -> Option<int>

   • Returns `Some(i)` for the *smallest* index `i` such that  
     0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] == k.  
   • Returns `None` iff no such index exists.  
   • Formal definition:  
       if exists |i:int| 0 ≤ i ∧ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] == k {  
           let j = choose |j:int| 0 ≤ j ∧ j < s.len() ∧ s[j] == k ∧ (forall |t:int| 0 ≤ t ∧ t < j ==> s[t] != k);  
           Some(j)  
       } else {  
           None  
       }

2. Executable function

   fn first_index_of_k(s: &[int], k: int) -> (result: Option<usize>)

   Inputs  
     – s: immutable slice of runtime integers  
     – k: integer being searched for

   Output  
     – result: Option<usize>

   Preconditions  
     • true (no additional requirements).

   Postconditions  
     • result == first_index(s@, k)  
     • if result == Some(i) then i < s.len()  (proved automatically by spec)

Function signature

Inputs

    • str2 – immutable slice of `char` (must contain fewer than 1 000 characters)

    4. Longest-prefix maximality:  
          prefix.len() == str1.len()     OR  
          prefix.len() == str2.len()     OR

       (if neither input ends exactly at the prefix length, then the very next characters differ, proving the prefix cannot be extended).

The returned vector is exactly the longest sequence of identical characters starting at index 0 in both strings; it is empty if the first characters already differ, and it equals the *entire* shorter string when one string is a full prefix of the other.

Any implementation that satisfies the above contract—e.g. a loop that walks the two inputs in lock-step until a mismatch or an end is reached—will be accepted by the Verus verifier.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Specification function for checking if an element is a peak.
spec fn is_peak(v: Seq<int>, i: int) -> bool
    recommends
        0 <= i && i < v.len(),
{
    forall|k: int| 0 <= k && k < i ==> v[i] >= v[k]
}

/// Specification function for calculating the sum of peaks.
spec fn peek_sum(v: Seq<int>, i: int) -> int
    recommends
        0 <= i && i <= v.len(),
    decreases i,
{
    if i <= 1 {
        v[0]
    } else if is_peak(v, i - 1) {
        v[i - 1] + peek_sum(v, i - 1)
    } else {
        peek_sum(v, i - 1)
    }
}

/// Function to calculate the sum of peaks in an array.
fn m_peak_sum(v: &Vec<int>) -> (sum: int)
    requires
        v.len() > 0,
        v.len() < 1000,
    ensures
        sum == peek_sum(v@, v.len() as int),
{
```

# Reasoning
We have three items to describe:

1.  spec fn is_peak  
    •  Inputs  
        – v: a mathematical sequence (Seq<int>) representing the contents of a vector.  
        – i: an index.  
    •  “recommends” clause (equivalent to a pre-condition for callers of the spec function): 0 ≤ i < v.len().  
    •  Definition: it returns true exactly when `v[i]` is greater than or equal to every element that precedes it.  
       Formally: ∀ k. 0 ≤ k < i ⇒ v[i] ≥ v[k].  
       Hence the first element (i = 0) is always a peak, while later indices qualify only if they are no smaller than all earlier values.

2.  spec fn peek_sum  
    •  Purpose: compute the sum of all peak elements among the first `i` positions of the sequence.  
    •  Inputs / pre-conditions: 0 ≤ i ≤ v.len().  
    •  Recursive definition (with decreases i to prove termination):  
        – Base case: when i ≤ 1 return v[0] (the single peak that must exist).  
        – Recursive case: look at index i–1 (the last element of the prefix).  
            •  If `is_peak(v, i-1)` is true, add that element to the recursive result on the prefix of length i-1.  
            •  Otherwise, ignore the element and recurse.  
    •  Consequently `peek_sum(v, i)` equals Σ { v[j] | 0 ≤ j < i ∧ is_peak(v, j) }.

   In particular, `peek_sum(v, v.len())` is the sum of all peak elements of the entire sequence.

3.  fn m_peak_sum  
    •  Executable, verified function.  
    •  Input: `v: &Vec<int>` — immutable reference to a runtime vector of mathematical ints.  
    •  Preconditions (requires)  
        1. v.len() > 0  — the vector is non-empty.  
        2. v.len() < 1000 — length is bounded to stay below one thousand (mainly to simplify proof obligations; not used in the postcondition).  
    •  Output: `(sum: int)` — an unbounded mathematical integer.  
    •  Postcondition (ensures)  
        sum == peek_sum(v@, v.len() as int)  
        where v@ is Verus’s “ghost” view that turns the runtime Vec into an immutable Seq<int>.  
        This states that the returned value must equal the mathematical sum of all peak elements in the vector.

No other helper functions, lemmas, or preconditions are stated.

Thus, the implementer must write a body that, for any non-empty vector of length < 1000, returns the exact sum of the elements that form a non-decreasing record (≥ all previous values) while meeting the specification above.

# Final Description
<<<
Design and verify a function named m_peak_sum together with two supporting specification functions.

1.  spec fn is_peak(v: Seq<int>, i: int) -> bool  
    •  Pre-condition: 0 ≤ i < v.len().  
    •  Returns true iff element v[i] is at least as large as every element that precedes it (indices 0 … i-1).  
       Formally: ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < i ⇒ v[i] ≥ v[k].

2.  spec fn peek_sum(v: Seq<int>, i: int) -> int  
    •  Pre-condition: 0 ≤ i ≤ v.len().  
    •  Recursively computes the sum of all peak elements among the first i entries of v.  
      Definition:  
        – If i ≤ 1 → return v[0].  
        – Else if is_peak(v, i-1) → return v[i-1] + peek_sum(v, i-1).  
        – Else → return peek_sum(v, i-1).  
      (Termination proven with decreases i.)  
    •  Consequently, peek_sum(v, v.len()) = Σ { v[j] | 0 ≤ j < v.len() ∧ is_peak(v,j) }.

3.  fn m_peak_sum(v: &Vec<int>) -> (sum: int)  
    Inputs  
        – v: immutable reference to a Vec<int>.  
    Preconditions  
        1. v.len() > 0  (vector must be non-empty)  
        2. v.len() < 1000 (length strictly less than 1000)  
    Output  
        – sum: int  
    Postcondition  
        sum == peek_sum(v@, v.len())  
        (v@ is the ghost sequence view of v).  
    Informally: return the exact sum of all “peak” elements of the vector, where an index j is a peak when v[j] is ≥ every earlier element.

Any runtime body that provably satisfies these conditions—e.g., a single scan maintaining the current running maximum and adding to an accumulator whenever a new maximum appears—will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: compare two slices, accumulate equal characters until a mismatch.  
To make the exercise more challenging (yet still spec-able in Verus), I generated three variants that each introduce additional reasoning dimensions:

Variant A – “N-ary longest common prefix”  
 • Works over a *slice of slices* (`&[&[char]]`) so the spec must quantify over an *arbitrary number* of input strings.  
 • Adds an upper bound on the number of strings (≤ 10) and keeps the per-string length bound.  
 • The post-condition must hold for *every* string in the collection, and the maximality clause is now quantified: if the prefix is not an entire input, then *there exist* two strings whose next characters differ.  
This requires nested universal/existential reasoning that is absent in the original task.

Variant B – “Longest common prefix **and suffix**”  
 • The function returns *two* vectors: the maximal common prefix and the maximal common suffix.  
 • The suffix part introduces reverse indexing and forces the student to reason about overlapping edge cases (e.g. when the whole string is both a prefix and a suffix).  
 • Requires proving that the prefix and suffix do not overlap incorrectly when one string is very short.

Variant C – “Case-insensitive longest common prefix”  
 • Equality is defined via `to_ascii_lowercase` rather than `==`, a less common comparison.  
 • The returned prefix must preserve the *original* casing of `str1`, so the spec must state a relationship between lower-cased comparisons and original characters.  
 • Adds the stipulation that only ASCII alphabetic characters participate; any non-ASCII or non-alphabetic char immediately terminates the prefix.  
These details force the learner to specify and prove properties about character normalization.

All three variants stay within small-input bounds (no extra algorithmic complexity requirements), but each one needs more sophisticated quantification or helper lemmas than the original.

# Output
<<<
Design a formally-verified Verus-Rust function called `longest_common_prefix_n`.

Function signature  
    fn longest_common_prefix_n(strs: &[&[char]]) -> (prefix: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
    • strs – immutable slice whose elements are themselves immutable `char` slices.  
      Constraints:  
        – 1 ≤ strs.len() ≤ 10  
        – ∀ s ∈ strs : s.len() < 1_000  

Output  
    • prefix – a `Vec<char>` containing the characters that form the longest prefix shared by **every** slice in `strs`.

Specification  

Preconditions  
    1. 1 ≤ strs.len() ≤ 10  
    2. ∀ s ∈ strs : s.len() < 1_000  

Postconditions  
    1. ∀ s ∈ strs : prefix.len() ≤ s.len()  
    2. ∀ i ∈ [0, prefix.len()) : ∀ s ∈ strs : prefix[i] == s[i]  
       (each element of `prefix` matches the corresponding element of every input)  
    3. Longest-prefix maximality for a *collection*:  
          prefix.len() == min_len(strs)      OR  
          (∃ a, b ∈ strs : a[prefix.len()] ≠ b[prefix.len()])  
       where `min_len(strs)` denotes the length of the shortest slice in `strs`.  
       (If the prefix does not consume the entire shortest string, then at least two  
        strings disagree on the very next character, proving non-extendability.)

Interpretation  
`prefix` is exactly the longest sequence of identical characters starting at index 0 that is common to *all* provided strings; it is empty if any first characters already differ, and it equals the entire shortest string when that string is a full prefix of every other.

>>>

<<<
Design a formally-verified Verus-Rust function called `common_prefix_and_suffix`.

Function signature  
    fn common_prefix_and_suffix(str1: &[char], str2: &[char])
        -> (prefix: Vec<char>, suffix: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
    • str1 – immutable slice of `char`, length < 1 000  
    • str2 – immutable slice of `char`, length < 1 000  

Outputs  
    • prefix – longest sequence of identical characters from the *start* of both inputs.  
    • suffix – longest sequence of identical characters from the *end* of both inputs  
               (in original order, i.e. `suffix[0]` corresponds to the first matched
                character when scanning from the right).

Specification  

Preconditions  
    1. str1.len() < 1_000  
    2. str2.len() < 1_000  

Postconditions (all must hold simultaneously)  
    Prefix properties  
      a. prefix.len() ≤ str1.len() ∧ prefix.len() ≤ str2.len()  
      b. ∀ i ∈ [0, prefix.len()) : prefix[i] == str1[i] ∧ prefix[i] == str2[i]  
      c. Maximality:  
            prefix.len() == str1.len() OR  
            prefix.len() == str2.len() OR  
            str1[prefix.len()] ≠ str2[prefix.len()]  

    Suffix properties  
      d. suffix.len() ≤ str1.len() ∧ suffix.len() ≤ str2.len()  
      e. ∀ i ∈ [0, suffix.len()) :  
             suffix[i] == str1[str1.len() - suffix.len() + i] ∧  
             suffix[i] == str2[str2.len() - suffix.len() + i]  
      f. Maximality:  
            suffix.len() == str1.len() OR  
            suffix.len() == str2.len() OR  
            str1[str1.len() - suffix.len() - 1] ≠  
            str2[str2.len() - suffix.len() - 1]  

    Non-overlap sanity (prevents impossible overlaps when one string is very short):  
      g. prefix.len() + suffix.len() ≤ min(str1.len(), str2.len())

Interpretation  
`prefix` captures the shared beginning, `suffix` captures the shared ending, and together they never exceed the length of the shorter string. Either can be empty independently.

>>>

<<<
Design a formally-verified Verus-Rust function called `longest_common_prefix_ascii_case_insensitive`.

Function signature  
    fn longest_common_prefix_ascii_case_insensitive(
        str1: &[char],
        str2: &[char]
    ) -> (prefix: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
    • str1 – immutable slice of `char`, length < 1 000  
    • str2 – immutable slice of `char`, length < 1 000  

Output  
    • prefix – longest prefix such that, when both inputs are converted to
               lowercase ASCII, the characters match.  The returned characters
               must preserve the *original casing* taken from `str1`.

Auxiliary definition  
    ascii_eq_ci(c1, c2) ≜ to_ascii_lowercase(c1) == to_ascii_lowercase(c2)

Specification  

Preconditions  
    1. str1.len() < 1_000  
    2. str2.len() < 1_000  

Postconditions  
    1. prefix.len() ≤ str1.len() ∧ prefix.len() ≤ str2.len()  
    2. ∀ i ∈ [0, prefix.len()) :  
           ascii_eq_ci(str1[i], str2[i])         // case-insensitive match  
           ∧ prefix[i] == str1[i]                // preserve original case  
    3. Termination rule for non-alphabetic or non-ASCII input:  
         If i < min(str1.len(), str2.len()) and  
            (str1[i] is non-ASCII-alphabetic ∨ str2[i] is non-ASCII-alphabetic)  
            then prefix.len() ≤ i  
       (the prefix never extends past a non-eligible character).  
    4. Maximality:  
          prefix.len() == str1.len()    OR  
          prefix.len() == str2.len()    OR  
          ¬ascii_eq_ci(str1[prefix.len()], str2[prefix.len()])  
       (if both strings continue, their next characters differ under
        case-insensitive comparison).

Interpretation  
Characters are considered equal when their lowercase ASCII forms match; the function stops before any non-ASCII-alphabetic char.  The caller gets exactly those original characters from `str1` that participate in the maximal case-insensitive match.

>>>

Signature  
    fn pot(x: u64, y: u64) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    • x : u64  
    • y : u64

Output  
    • r : u64 – the result of the multiplication.

Pre-conditions (requires)
