    2. y < u64::MAX / u64::MAX      // likewise y < 1  
    3. x * y < u64::MAX             // guarantees the product is in range

Because the first two conditions imply that both x and y must be 0, the third one is automatically satisfied.

Post-condition (ensures)  
    r == x * y

In effect, the specification dictates that whenever the function is invoked with x = 0 and y = 0, it must return r = 0, and these are the only admissible inputs. No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required. Any implementation that unconditionally returns 0 will meet the specification.

```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

// Specification function
pub open spec fn has_common_element(a: Seq<int>, b: Seq<int>) -> bool {
    exists|i: int, j: int| 0 <= i < a.len() && 0 <= j < b.len() && a[i] == b[j]
}

// Target function
pub fn has_common_element_exec(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (result: bool)
    requires
        a.len() > 0 && b.len() > 0 && a.len() < 1000 && b.len() < 1000,
    ensures
        result ==> has_common_element(a@, b@),
        !result ==> forall|i: nat, j: nat| 0 <= i && i < a.len() && 0 <= j && j < b.len() ==> a[i as int] != b[j as int],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We need to describe the spec. There is a pure spec function `has_common_element` that, given two mathematical sequences (Seq<int>) a and b, returns true if there exists at least one pair of indices (i, j) such that both indices are in range and the elements are equal. That’s expressed using an existential quantifier.

The executable function `has_common_element_exec` takes two run-time slices of Verus `int` (unbounded mathematical integers). Preconditions:
1. Both slices are non-empty (`a.len() > 0` and `b.len() > 0`).
2. Both lengths are strictly less than 1000 (≤ 999). This bound just simplifies verifying a loop, it is not logically necessary for the specification itself.

Return value `result` is a Boolean.  Two postconditions describe its meaning:

• If `result` is true, then `has_common_element(a@, b@)` must hold, where `s@` turns the slice into its ghost sequence view.  
• If `result` is false, then every element of `a` is different from every element of `b`, formally
  ∀ i,j • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < b.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ b[j].

Taken together these two clauses make `result` logically equivalent to “the two slices share at least one common value”.

Important details a programmer or solver will need:

Inputs
• a: `&[int]` – immutable slice of mathematical integers  
• b: `&[int]`

Output
• result: `bool`

Preconditions
1. a.len() > 0  
2. b.len() > 0  
3. a.len() < 1000  
4. b.len() < 1000

Postconditions
1. result ⇒ ∃ i,j in range with a[i] = b[j] (captured via the helper spec function).  
2. ¬result ⇒ ∀ i,j in range, a[i] ≠ b[j].

There are no additional ghost parameters, no decreases clauses, no permissions to modify memory, etc.

A correct implementation could intersect the two slices in any way (nested loops, hash-set, etc.) provided its correctness is provable under the above contracts.

# Final Description (put between the tags)
<<<

   Definition:  
     returns true exactly when there exist indices i and j such that  
       0 ≤ i < a.len(), 0 ≤ j < b.len(), and a[i] == b[j].

2. Executable function signature
   pub fn has_common_element_exec(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (result: bool)

3. Parameters
   • a – immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers (`int`).  
   • b – another immutable slice of `int`.

4. Return
   • result – a Boolean indicating whether the two slices share at least one common value.

5. Preconditions (requires)
   • a.len() > 0         – slice a is non-empty.  
   • b.len() > 0         – slice b is non-empty.  
   • a.len() < 1000      – length of a is at most 999.  
   • b.len() < 1000      – length of b is at most 999.

6. Postconditions (ensures)
   • If result is true: `has_common_element(a@, b@)` must hold; that is, some element occurs in both slices.  
   • If result is false: ∀ i,j (with 0 ≤ i < a.len(), 0 ≤ j < b.len()) ⇒ a[i] ≠ b[j]; i.e. the slices have no common value.

Taken together, these clauses make `result` logically equivalent to “a and b share at least one element”.

No other ghost state, lemmas, or auxiliary functions are required, and the implementation technique is up to the programmer (nested loops, hash-based search, etc.) as long as the verifier can prove the postconditions from the preconditions.
>>>

The executable function `has_common_element_exec` takes two run-time slices of Verus `int` (unbounded mathematical integers). Preconditions:
1. Both slices are non-empty (`a.len() > 0` and `b.len() > 0`).
2. Both lengths are strictly less than 1000 (≤ 999). This bound just simplifies verifying a loop, it is not logically necessary for the specification itself.

Return value `result` is a Boolean.  Two postconditions describe its meaning:

• If `result` is true, then `has_common_element(a@, b@)` must hold, where `s@` turns the slice into its ghost sequence view.  
• If `result` is false, then every element of `a` is different from every element of `b`, formally
  ∀ i,j • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < b.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ b[j].

Taken together these two clauses make `result` logically equivalent to “the two slices share at least one common value”.

Important details a programmer or solver will need:

Inputs
• a: `&[int]` – immutable slice of mathematical integers  
• b: `&[int]`

Output
• result: `bool`

Preconditions
1. a.len() > 0  
2. b.len() > 0  
3. a.len() < 1000  
4. b.len() < 1000

Postconditions
1. result ⇒ ∃ i,j in range with a[i] = b[j] (captured via the helper spec function).  
2. ¬result ⇒ ∀ i,j in range, a[i] ≠ b[j].

There are no additional ghost parameters, no decreases clauses, no permissions to modify memory, etc.

A correct implementation could intersect the two slices in any way (nested loops, hash-set, etc.) provided its correctness is provable under the above contracts.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus-Rust function called `has_common_element_exec`, together with (or using) the already supplied helper specification function `has_common_element`.

1. Helper specification (already provided, must be respected)
   spec fn has_common_element(a: Seq<int>, b: Seq<int>) -> bool
   Definition:  
     returns true exactly when there exist indices i and j such that  
       0 ≤ i < a.len(), 0 ≤ j < b.len(), and a[i] == b[j].

2. Executable function signature
   pub fn has_common_element_exec(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (result: bool)

3. Parameters
   • a – immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers (`int`).  
   • b – another immutable slice of `int`.

4. Return
   • result – a Boolean indicating whether the two slices share at least one common value.

5. Preconditions (requires)
   • a.len() > 0         – slice a is non-empty.  
   • b.len() > 0         – slice b is non-empty.  
   • a.len() < 1000      – length of a is at most 999.  
   • b.len() < 1000      – length of b is at most 999.

6. Postconditions (ensures)
   • If result is true: `has_common_element(a@, b@)` must hold; that is, some element occurs in both slices.  
   • If result is false: ∀ i,j (with 0 ≤ i < a.len(), 0 ≤ j < b.len()) ⇒ a[i] ≠ b[j]; i.e. the slices have no common value.

Taken together, these clauses make `result` logically equivalent to “a and b share at least one element”.

No other ghost state, lemmas, or auxiliary functions are required, and the implementation technique is up to the programmer (nested loops, hash-based search, etc.) as long as the verifier can prove the postconditions from the preconditions.

# Reasoning
The original exercise is trivial because its pre-conditions collapse to the single admissible input pair `(0,0)`.  
To make the task more interesting while still centring it on “multiplying two `u64`s”, I introduce pre-conditions that:

• describe non-singleton but still highly structured input sets (e.g. coprimality, twin primes, powers of two linked with Mersenne numbers);  
• force the student to employ auxiliary lemmas (gcd facts, primality, bit-level identities) rather than a one-liner implementation;  
• remain strong enough to guarantee absence of overflow, so the post-condition is still the straightforward equality `r == x * y`.

Each rewritten specification is self-contained and precise enough that a spec generator can translate it directly into Verus `requires`/`ensures` clauses.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus/Rust function named `coprime_mul`.

Signature  
    fn coprime_mul(x: u64, y: u64) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    • x : u64  
    • y : u64

Output  
    • r : u64 – the product of the two numbers.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. 2 ≤ x < (1u64 << 32)              // both operands fit into 32 bits but are ≥ 2  
    2. 2 ≤ y < (1u64 << 32)  
    3. ∀d: nat. d > 1 ∧ d | x ⇒ d ∤ y    // x and y are pairwise coprime  
    4. x * y ≤ u64::MAX                  // multiplication cannot overflow

Post-condition (ensures)  
    r == x * y

Hints / remarks  
• Condition 3 can equivalently be stated as `gcd(x,y) == 1`; the learner will likely need a small helper lemma showing that coprimality of two 32-bit numbers implies their product still fits into 64 bits, satisfying condition 4 automatically.  
• No additional functional behaviour is required besides returning the mathematical product under the stated constraints.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus/Rust function named `twin_prime_product`.

Signature  
    fn twin_prime_product(p: u64, q: u64) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    • p : u64  
    • q : u64

Output  
    • r : u64 – the product `p * q`.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. 3 ≤ p < q < (1u64 << 32)          // both primes are less than 2³²  
    2. |q − p| == 2                      // the numbers form a twin-prime pair  
    3. ∀d: nat. 1 < d < p ⇒ d ∤ p        // p is prime (standard definition)  
    4. ∀d: nat. 1 < d < q ⇒ d ∤ q        // q is prime  
    5. p * q ≤ u64::MAX                  // guaranteed non-overflow

Post-condition (ensures)  
    r == p * q

Additional notes  
Verifying the pre-conditions obliges the solver (and thus the learner) to reason about the relation between `p` and `q`, making the task longer than a single-step proof.  Showing that the overflow guard holds is immediate from the 32-bit bound, but the primality clauses require either built-in or helper lemmas.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus/Rust function named `pow2_mersenne_mul`.

Signature  
    fn pow2_mersenne_mul(x: u64, y: u64) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    • x : u64  
    • y : u64

Output  
    • r : u64 – their product.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. ∃k: nat. 1 ≤ k ≤ 63 ∧ x == (1u64 << k)          // x is an exact power of two  
    2. ∃k: nat. 1 ≤ k ≤ 63 ∧ y == (1u64 << (k + 1)) − 1 // y is the next Mersenne number
    3. Using the same k in (1) and (2)                  // k is shared between the two facts
    4. x * y < u64::MAX                                 // multiplication stays in range

Post-condition (ensures)  
    r == x * y

Clarifications  
• Condition 3 ties the two existential witnesses together: once a single `k` is chosen, `x` must be `2ᵏ` and `y` must be `2^(k+1)−1`.  
• Because `k ≤ 63`, `x` uses at most bit 63 and `y` is at most all ones below that bit, so overflow is impossible, but the learner still has to formalise that argument.  
• The interesting reasoning step is proving that the same `k` satisfies both existential clauses, forcing the student to extract and reuse that witness in the specification.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn rotate_right(l: &[int], n: usize) -> (r: Vec<int>)
    requires
        n <= l.len(),
        l.len() < 1000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < 1000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        l.len() < usize::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < usize::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < l.len(), // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        r.len() == l.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < l.len() ==> r[i] == l[(i - n as int + l.len() as int) % l.len() as int],
{
```

# Reasoning
The function’s purpose is to return a new vector that represents the slice l rotated to the right by n positions.  

Inputs  
• l: an immutable slice (borrowed view) of `int` values. In Verus, `int` is an unbounded mathematical integer type whose executable representation is `i128`, so indexing arithmetic is carried out in the mathematical domain and later translated to finite-width operations.  
• n: a `usize` indicating how many positions to rotate to the right.

Outputs  
• r: a fresh `Vec<int>` holding the rotated elements.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. `n <= l.len()` and, more restrictively, `n < l.len()` – the rotation amount must be strictly smaller than the slice length.  
2. Several “relaxation” bounds are added solely to keep every arithmetic expression inside the trusted range of the back-end:  
   – `l.len() < 1000` and `n < 1000`,  
   – `l.len() < usize::MAX / 2` and `n < usize::MAX / 2`.  
   None of those bounds influence the logical meaning of the algorithm; they simply rule out enormous values that could provoke overflow in the compiled code.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. `r.len() == l.len()` – the rotation preserves length.  
2. For every integer index `i` in the valid range `0 ≤ i < l.len()`, the element stored in `r` at position i equals the element stored in l at position `(i − n + len) mod len`, i.e. the element that originally lay `n` places to the left in circular fashion. Written formally:  
   ```
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < l.len() ⇒
       r[i] == l[(i - n + len) mod len]
   ```  
   where all three quantities are cast to `int` so that the subtraction and modulo operation take place on unbounded integers before being mapped back to machine integers.

Interpretation  
• A right rotation by n means every element moves `n` places toward higher indices, with wrap-around at the end of the slice.  
• Example: l = [a, b, c, d, e] and n = 2 ⇒ r must be [d, e, a, b, c].

Implementation freedom  
Any runtime algorithm that satisfies the contract is acceptable: allocate a new vector and copy elements using modulo arithmetic, perform an in-place rotation via three reversals, etc. The added “< 1000” constraints guarantee that the copy will never exceed modest memory usage, so no additional overflow reasoning is necessary.

Edge cases  
• If `l.len() == 1`, the only admissible value for n is 0 (because n < len); the returned vector must be a single-element clone of l.  
• `n == 0` is always allowed; it leaves the slice unchanged.  
• The specification explicitly disallows `n == l.len()` even though it would be a no-op rotation.

# Final Description
<<<
Design a formally verified Verus function named `rotate_right`.

Signature  
fn rotate_right(l: &[int], n: usize) -> (r: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• l – an immutable slice of `int` (mathematical integers, executable as `i128`).  
• n – a `usize` rotation amount.

Output  
• r – a fresh `Vec<int>` containing the elements of l rotated to the right by n positions.

Pre-conditions  
1. 0 ≤ n < l.len() (the rotation amount is strictly less than the slice length).  
2. Auxiliary bounds to rule out extreme cases that could cause overflow in executable arithmetic:  
   a. l.len() < 1 000,  
   b. n        < 1 000,  
   c. l.len() < usize::MAX / 2,  
   d. n        < usize::MAX / 2.

Post-conditions  
1. r.len() == l.len() – length is preserved.  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < l.len() ⇒  
      r[i] == l[(i − n + l.len()) mod l.len()]  
   This formula states that the element appearing at index i of the result comes from the element that was `n` places to the left in the original slice, with indices taken modulo the slice length. Concretely, the rotation pushes every element `n` positions toward higher indices, wrapping around at the end (right rotation).

Notes for the implementer  
• Any algorithm that satisfies the specification may be used (e.g., allocate a new vector and fill it with `r[i] = l[(i - n + len) % len]`, or perform an in-place triple-reverse).  
• Because of the explicit numeric upper bounds, you do not need additional reasoning about overflow when translating the mathematical modulo expression to executable code.  
• The function must allocate and return a new vector; modifying the slice in place is not allowed because slices are immutable.

With these details a developer can reconstruct the entire Verus specification (including pre- and post-conditions and the precise indexing formula) from scratch.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original exercise is almost trivial: prove a single‐step subtraction does not under-flow and returns the correct result.  
To make the test more interesting we can:

• Replace the single numbers by a *slice* of numbers whose sum must be subtracted – this obliges the student to introduce (and verify) a loop or a recursive helper and to use quantified reasoning about the slice.  
• Introduce an *auxiliary modulus* and insist that the answer is the difference **mod m**, where `m` is prime and larger than every intermediate value.  This forces a proof that the intermediate arithmetic is below the modulus and that the modular result coincides with ordinary subtraction when no wrap-around occurs.  
• Ask for an *exact quotient/remainder decomposition* of the difference with regard to an extra divisor `d`, thereby combining subtraction and division correctness in the same routine.

All three ideas remain “simple enough” implementation-wise yet add several proof obligations (loop invariants, modular arithmetic lemmas, division properties) absent from the original.

Below are three completely standalone, detailed problem statements a spec-generator can use.

# Output
<<<
Problem 1 – Subtracting a Slice of Positive Integers

Implement a verified Verus-Rust function `slice_subtract` that subtracts *all* elements of a slice `arr` from an initial unsigned 64-bit value `total`, and returns the non-negative remainder.

Signature  
    fn slice_subtract(total: u64, arr: &Seq<u64>) -> (rest: u64)

Inputs  
  • total: u64 — an unsigned 64-bit integer, strictly positive.  
  • arr: &Seq<u64> — an immutable sequence of length `len > 0`.  Every entry of `arr` is strictly positive.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
  1. total < 2⁶³  (this prevents overflow in the executable body).  
  2. ∀i: 0 ≤ i < arr.len() ⇒ arr[i] < 2⁶³.  
  3. sum(arr) ≤ total. (sum(arr) denotes the mathematical sum of the sequence entries.)  
     That is, the elements of `arr` never exhaust `total`.

Output  
  • rest: u64 — the final remainder after all subtractions.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
  1. rest == total − sum(arr).  
  2. rest ≤ total. (trivial but must be proved from the algorithm.)  
  3. rest < 2⁶³.  
  4. The function has no side effects and does not mutate `arr`.

Hints / remarks  
• Any correct implementation will need either an explicit `while` loop with an invariant over the running index and accumulated sum, or a recursive helper on the slice.  
• Quantified facts about `Seq` (e.g. `sum`) may be stated in helper lemmas.  
>>>

<<<
Problem 2 – Modular Safe Difference

Write a verified Verus-Rust function `modular_difference` that returns the difference of two 64-bit unsigned integers *taken modulo* a third 64-bit unsigned integer `m` which is assumed to be prime.

Signature  
    fn modular_difference(a: u64, b: u64, m: u64) -> (diff_mod: u64)

Inputs  
  • a: u64 — an unsigned 64-bit integer, strictly smaller than m.  
  • b: u64 — an unsigned 64-bit integer, strictly smaller than m.  
  • m: u64 — a prime, 2 < m < 2⁶³.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
  1. 2 < m < 9_223_372_036_854_775_808  (the largest 2⁶³ value).  
  2. prime(m). (The number `m` is mathematically prime.  You can assume an `#[verifier::opaque]` helper `prime` exists.)  
  3. a < m ∧ b < m.

Output  
  • diff_mod: u64 — the modular difference.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
  1. diff_mod < m.  
  2. (diff_mod + b) mod m == a. (Equivalently diff_mod == (a + m − b) mod m.)  
  3. If a ≥ b then diff_mod == a − b. (This links the modular result to ordinary subtraction when no wrap-around occurs.)

Additional notes  
• The concrete body may compute `let raw = if a >= b { a - b } else { a + m - b };` and then return `raw % m`, but the proof must justify Post-condition 3.  
• You will need lemmas about modular arithmetic and basic properties of a prime modulus.  
>>>

<<<
Problem 3 – Difference, Quotient and Remainder

Create a verified function `diff_divide` that subtracts a positive integer `n` from a larger integer `k`, then divides the difference by a positive divisor `d` and returns both the exact quotient and remainder according to Euclidean division.

Signature  
    fn diff_divide(k: u128, n: u128, d: u128) -> (q: u128, r: u128)

Inputs  
  • k: u128 — strictly positive 128-bit unsigned integer.  
  • n: u128 — unsigned 128-bit integer satisfying 0 < n < k.  
  • d: u128 — unsigned 128-bit integer with 1 < d ≤ 2⁶³.

Pre-conditions  
  1. k < 2¹²⁷,  n < 2¹²⁷,  d ≤ 2⁶³.  
  2. k − n < 2¹²⁷. (This forbids underflow and overflow.)  
  3. gcd(d, k − n) == 1 → q is unique. (`gcd` can be assumed available as a pure ghost function.)

Outputs  
  • q: u128 — the Euclidean quotient.  
  • r: u128 — the Euclidean remainder.

Post-conditions  
  1. k − n == q * d + r.  
  2. 0 ≤ r < d.  
  3. If (k − n) % d == 0 then r == 0 and q == (k − n)/d.  
  4. The function is pure; it has no side effects.

Hints  
• A straightforward executable body is  
      let diff = k - n;  
      let q = diff / d;  
      let r = diff % d;  
      (q, r)  
  but you must supply proofs of all four post-conditions.  
• Use helper lemmas about division and modulus on natural numbers.  
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn generate(n: usize) -> (perm: Vec<usize>)
    requires
        n >= 0,
        n < 1000,
    ensures
        perm.len() == n,
        forall|i: nat| 0 <= i && i < n ==> perm[i as int] < n,
        forall|i: nat, j: nat| 0 <= i && i < n && 0 <= j && j < n && i != j ==> perm[i as int] != perm[j as int],
{
```

# Reasoning
• Function name: `generate`  
• Parameter:  
  – `n: usize` (a non-negative machine word sized integer).  
• Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
  1. `n >= 0` (trivial for `usize`, but stated explicitly)  
  2. `n < 1000` — the caller may request at most 999 elements.  

• Return value:  
  – `perm: Vec<usize>` — a runtime vector of `usize`s that will hold the permutation.  

• Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
  1. `perm.len() == n` — the vector’s length exactly matches the requested size.  
  2. ∀ `i` with `0 ≤ i < n`: `perm[i] < n` — every stored element is itself an index of the interval `[0, n-1]`.  
  3. ∀ `i, j` with `0 ≤ i < n`, `0 ≤ j < n`, and `i ≠ j`: `perm[i] ≠ perm[j]` — no two positions hold the same value; all elements are pairwise distinct.  

Taken together, (2) and (3) imply that the contents of `perm` constitute a permutation of the numbers `0 … n-1`, although the specification does not state the surjectivity property directly; it follows because exactly `n` distinct values are taken from a domain of size `n`.

• No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are referenced.  
• `n` may be `0`; in that case the vector must be empty and the universals are vacuously true.  
• Any implementation strategy that fulfils the above contract is acceptable (e.g., the identity ordering `0,1,2,…`, Fisher–Yates shuffle, etc.).  
• All reasoning in the post-conditions uses Verus’s mathematical integers (`nat` for non-negative ints); when indexing into the vector the index is cast to `int` because `Vec` indices are expressed in that domain.

# Final Description (put between tags)
<<<
Create a verified Verus-Rust function with the exact signature

fn generate(n: usize) -> (perm: Vec<usize>)

Contract

Pre-conditions (requires)
1. n ≥ 0 (explicit even though usize is non-negative)
2. n < 1000

Post-conditions (ensures)
1. perm.len() == n —the returned vector’s length matches the requested size.
2. ∀ i:nat • 0 ≤ i < n ⇒ perm[i] < n —every element stored in perm is itself less than n.
3. ∀ i,j:nat • 0 ≤ i < n ∧ 0 ≤ j < n ∧ i ≠ j ⇒ perm[i] ≠ perm[j] —elements at different indices are different.

There are no additional helper specs or lemmas.

Interpretation
The function must produce a vector that contains n pairwise-distinct values, each drawn from the range 0 … n-1. Consequently, the multiset of values is a permutation of that range (including the empty permutation when n==0). n is additionally capped at 999 by the pre-condition. Any runtime implementation that satisfies these properties is acceptable and can be verified against the above specification.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is very small:  
• only one parameter (an immutable vector),  
• a single, easy‐to-express functional property (reversal),  
• and a trivial size bound (< 1000).

To make the task richer we can
1. change the *kind* of parameter (e.g. make it **mutable** so the code has to reason about `old(xs)` versus the new state),  
2. tighten or refine the size bound with a rarer arithmetic predicate (e.g. “length is a power of two”),  
3. add extra semantic obligations (e.g. a sum that must be preserved, or relating monotonicity of the input to the output),  
4. demand additional results be returned, which forces the author to prove multiple properties,  
5. keep everything fully self-contained so the spec generator can work from the description alone.

Below are three standalone, strictly harder variants that satisfy these goals.

# Output
<<<
Design a formally verified, **in–place** reversing procedure in Verus-Rust with the exact specification below.

Function signature  
    fn reverse_in_place(xs: &mut Vec<int>)

Input / receiver  
    xs : a mutable reference to a vector of unbounded mathematical integers.

Outputs  
    (none) – the function operates by mutating `xs` directly.

Preconditions  
    • xs.len() > 0                                  (vector is non-empty)  
    • xs.len() <= 1024                              (moderate size)  
    • (∃ k:int • k >= 0 ∧ (1 << k) == xs.len())     (length is a power of two)

Postconditions  
    1. xs.len() is unchanged.  
       Formally: xs.len() == old(xs).len().

    2. Element-wise reversal of the original contents.  
       ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ⇒  
           xs[i] == old(xs)[old(xs).len() − 1 − i]

In words, after the call finishes the vector referenced by `xs` contains precisely the elements it originally held, but in the opposite order. No new storage may be returned; all effects must be through mutation of the existing vector.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function in Verus-Rust that reverses a vector **and simultaneously returns the total sum of its elements**.

Function signature  
    fn reverse_and_sum(xs: &Vec<int>) -> (r: Vec<int>, total: int)

Inputs  
    xs : immutable reference to a vector of mathematical integers.

Outputs  
    r     : a fresh vector containing the elements of `xs` in reverse order.  
    total : an integer equal to the sum of all elements of `xs`.

Preconditions  
    • 0 < xs.len() ≤ 1000  
    • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ xs[i] ≤ 1_000_000

Postconditions  
    1. r.len() == xs.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ⇒ r[i] == xs[xs.len() − 1 − i]  
    3. total == Σ_{i=0}^{xs.len()−1} xs[i]  
       (the result `total` equals the mathematical sum of the input’s elements)  
    4. Σ_{i=0}^{r.len()−1} r[i] == total  
       (the sum is preserved after reversal)

The implementer must therefore prove both the reversal property and that the computed `total` is correct.
>>>

<<<
Specify a function that reverses a **strictly increasing** sequence and establishes the corresponding monotonicity of the output.

Function signature  
    fn reverse_of_strictly_increasing(xs: &Vec<int>) -> (r: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    xs : immutable reference to a vector of integers that is known to be strictly increasing.

Outputs  
    r : a fresh vector of integers.

Preconditions  
    • 1 ≤ xs.len() ≤ 512  
    • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < xs.len() − 1 ⇒ xs[i] < xs[i + 1]     (strict increase)

Postconditions  
    1. r.len() == xs.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ⇒ r[i] == xs[xs.len() − 1 − i]  
       (reversal)  
    3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < r.len() − 1 ⇒ r[i] > r[i + 1]  
       (the resulting vector is strictly **decreasing**)  
    4. xs[0] < xs[xs.len() − 1]                             (redundant but illustrative; must be provable from the premises)

The verification must therefore reason about order relations in addition to simple index mirroring.
>>>

Signature

Inputs
    • s — an owned Vec<i32>.  
      All of its elements must remain intact and appear first in the result.  
    • a — a shared reference (&) to a Vec<i32>.

    3.  ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len()  ⇒  r[s.len() + i] == a[i]

# Reasoning
The module declares one auxiliary *spec* function and one executable function.

1.  `distinct_elements`
        spec fn distinct_elements(nums: Seq<int>) -> bool
    • Input: a pure mathematical sequence of ints.  
    • Output: `bool`.  
    • Definition:  
      `forall |i:int, j:int| 0 ≤ i < j ∧ j < nums.len() ⇒ nums[i] ≠ nums[j]`  
      i.e. returns **true** exactly when every pair of different indices stores different values (no duplicate entries in the whole sequence).

2.  `contains_duplicate`
        fn contains_duplicate(nums: Vec<int>) -> (result: bool)
    • Runtime input: `nums`, a heap vector whose element type is the mathematical `int`.  
    • Pre-conditions (requires)
        a. `1 ≤ nums.len() ≤ 100_000` – the vector is non-empty and not larger than one hundred thousand items;  
        b. ∀ i. 0 ≤ i < nums.len() ⇒ −1 000 000 000 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 1 000 000 000 – every stored integer lies within ±10⁹ (helps the implementer choose an algorithm that fits in machine ints if desired).
    • Post-condition (ensures)
        `result ⟺ distinct_elements(nums@)`  
        The returned Boolean must be logically equivalent to the predicate “all elements are pair-wise different” applied to the *ghost* sequence view `nums@` of the vector.  
        Hence:
            – If the vector has **no duplicates** then `result` must be `true`;  
            – If the vector contains **any duplicate value** then `result` must be `false`.  
    • No other helper lemmas or spec functions are required.

Important nuance: despite the function name “contains_duplicate”, the specification makes the result express *absence* of duplicates (true means distinct). An implementation must follow the specification, not the name.

# Final Description
<<<
You must supply two items:

1.  Pure helper specification function

    spec fn distinct_elements(nums: Seq<int>) -> bool

    Purpose: return true exactly when the sequence contains no repeated values.

    Formal definition:
        forall |i:int, j:int| 0 ≤ i < j ∧ j < nums.len() ⇒ nums[i] ≠ nums[j]

2.  Verified executable function

    fn contains_duplicate(nums: Vec<int>) -> (result: bool)

    • Parameters  
        nums : a runtime vector whose elements are mathematical integers.

    • Preconditions  
        1. 1 ≤ nums.len() ≤ 100 000  
        2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < nums.len() ⇒ −1 000 000 000 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 1 000 000 000

    • Postcondition  
        result ⇔ distinct_elements(nums@)

        (nums@ is the ghost sequence view of the vector.)  
        Consequently, result must be true when every element of nums is unique, and false if any value appears at least twice.

No additional requirements, decreases clauses, or helper lemmas are needed. Any body that fulfills these contracts—e.g. by inserting the elements into a ghost set or by sorting—will verify. >>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is essentially a straight‐forward concatenation; the verifier only has to track indices and lengths.  
To make the task more challenging we can:
• introduce set/multiset properties so that the implementer must reason about *content* as well as position;  
• require the inputs to satisfy stricter conditions (e.g. being duplicate-free or sorted) and then prove that the result preserves or combines those conditions;  
• add one or two extra index-arithmetic clauses so that more case-splitting is needed.

Below are three stand-alone, progressively harder, alternatives built on those ideas.  Each description is self-contained and contains enough detail for a spec generator to produce precise Verus contracts.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following exact specification.

Function name  
    append_unique_concat

Signature  
    fn append_unique_concat(s: Vec<i32>, a: &Vec<i32>) -> (r: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    • s — an owned Vec<i32>, containing **no duplicate values**.  
    • a — a shared reference (&) to a Vec<i32>, also containing **no duplicate values**.  
      Both vectors are guaranteed to be **pair-wise disjoint**: ∀ v • v ∈ s ⇒ v ∉ a.

Output  
    • r — a newly returned Vec<i32> that is the exact concatenation “s followed by a”.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() > 0                                   // second vector must be non-empty  
    2. no_dups(s)                                    // helper predicate meaning: ∀ i≠j • s[i]≠s[j]  
    3. no_dups(a)  
    4. ∀ v:int • v_in_vec(v, s) ⇒ !v_in_vec(v, *a)   // disjointness

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. r.len() == s.len() + a.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len()  ⇒  r[i] == s[i]  
    3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len()  ⇒  r[s.len() + i] == a[i]  
    4. no_dups(r)                                     // result still has no duplicates  
    5. ∀ v:int • v_in_vec(v, r) ⇔ (v_in_vec(v, s) ∨ v_in_vec(v, *a))

Helpers that may be declared as pure/ghost functions:  
    – no_dups(v: Vec<i32>) -> bool  
    – v_in_vec(v: int, vec: Vec<i32>) -> bool

No other lemmas are required; any executable implementation that consumes s, reads a, and satisfies the above contracts will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following exact specification.

Function name  
    merge_sorted_vecs

Signature  
    fn merge_sorted_vecs(left: Vec<i32>, right: &Vec<i32>) -> (merged: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    • left  — an owned Vec<i32> that is **strictly increasing**:  
      ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < left.len()-1 ⇒ left[i] < left[i+1]  
    • right — a shared reference (&) to a Vec<i32> that is also strictly increasing.

Output  
    • merged — a newly returned Vec<i32> that is the stable merge of the two inputs.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    left.len() > 0  
    right.len() > 0

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. merged.len() == left.len() + right.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < merged.len()-1 ⇒ merged[i] ≤ merged[i+1]      // merged is non-decreasing  
    3. multiset(merged) == multiset(left) ∪ multiset(*right)            // content equality as multisets  
    4. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < left.len()  ⇒  ∃ j:int • merged[j] == left[i]  // every element preserved  
    5. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < right.len() ⇒  ∃ j:int • merged[j] == right[i] // every element preserved

A ghost helper multiset(v: Vec<i32>) → Multiset<int> may be assumed to exist; no other lemmas are mandatory.  
The implementation must run in O(left.len() + right.len()) time (single linear scan is acceptable).
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following exact specification.

Function name  
    interleave_with_tail

Signature  
    fn interleave_with_tail(x: Vec<i32>, y: &Vec<i32>) -> (r: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    • x — an owned Vec<i32>.  
    • y — a shared reference (&) to a Vec<i32>.  
      Either vector may be empty.

Output  
    • r — a newly returned Vec<i32> built by **alternating elements** from x and y, starting with x.  
      When the shorter input runs out, the remaining elements of the longer input appear at the end of r **in their original order**.

Pre-condition (requires)  
    true   // no additional requirements

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. r.len() == x.len() + y.len()  
    2. ∀ k:int •  
         0 ≤ k < r.len() ⇒  
             (k % 2 == 0 && k/2 < x.len() → r[k] == x[k/2]) ∧  
             (k % 2 == 1 && k/2 < y.len() → r[k] == y[k/2])  
    3. If x.len() > y.len(), then  
         ∀ t:int • 0 ≤ t < x.len()-y.len() ⇒  
             r[2*y.len() + t] == x[y.len() + t]  
       Likewise, if y.len() > x.len(), the symmetrical property holds for the tail from y.  
    4. ∀ v:int • count_in_vec(v, r) == count_in_vec(v, x) + count_in_vec(v, *y)

Helper (ghost) predicate/functions allowed:  
    • count_in_vec(v: int, v: Vec<i32>) -> nat

No extra lemmas are necessary; any executable body that consumes x, reads y, and meets the stated contracts is acceptable.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn triangle_number(n: u64) -> (t: u64)
    requires
        n as int >= 0,
        n < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX, // added check to prevent overflow
    ensures
        t as int == n as int * (n as int + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
The snippet declares an executable, fully-verified function called `triangle_number`.

1. Inputs  
   • `n: u64` – a non-negative 64-bit unsigned integer that designates the index `n` whose triangular number Tₙ is to be computed.

2. Output  
   • `t: u64` – the 64-bit unsigned integer value that must equal the mathematical triangular number of `n`.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a. `n as int >= 0` – runtime values are non-negative (redundant for `u64` but made explicit for the logic domain `int`).  
   b. `n < 1_000_000` – an upper bound chosen purely to make later overflow reasoning easy.  
   c. `n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX` – a direct, numeric guarantee that the triangular value itself fits strictly inside the range representable by `u64`.  
   Together these three clauses make it impossible for the implementation to overflow when doing `n*(n+1)/2` with 64-bit arithmetic.

4. Postcondition (`ensures`)  
   • `t as int == n as int * (n as int + 1) / 2`  
   In words: after the function returns, the logical integer view of the result `t` must equal the exact mathematical formula Tₙ = n(n+1)/2.  
   There are no additional constraints; the function is pure.

5. Helper spec functions / lemmas – none are referenced.

6. Behavioural summary  
   The function must deterministically compute the nth triangular number, provided that `n` satisfies the above bounds, and return it as the 64-bit unsigned integer `t`. For example, `triangle_number(0) == 0`, `triangle_number(5) == 15`, and so on. Because of the preconditions, runtime arithmetic may be carried out directly in `u64` without fear of overflow, although an implementer is free to up-cast to a wider type if they wish.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

fn triangle_number(n: u64) -> (t: u64)

Inputs  
• n: u64 – the index of the triangular number to compute.

Output  
• t: u64 – must equal the triangular number Tₙ = n·(n+1)/2.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. n ≥ 0 (already true for u64, but stated explicitly as n as int ≥ 0 in the spec).  
2. n < 1_000_000 – an arbitrary upper bound that simplifies overflow reasoning.  
3. n · (n+1)/2 < u64::MAX – the resulting triangular value fits strictly inside the u64 range.

Postcondition (ensures)  
t as int == n as int * (n as int + 1) / 2  
In other words, the logical integer value of t must be exactly the mathematical triangular number of n.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are needed. Any executable body that provably meets these contracts (for example, computing (n*(n+1))/2 in u64 or u128) is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is straightforward: copy a vector while conditionally
rewriting each entry.  
To raise the level of difficulty, I introduce extra *observational* properties
the implementation must prove, return additional information that is formally
related to the result, or force more sophisticated data-structure handling
(e.g. power-of-two padding).  
The three proposals below all

• keep the basic “replace elements that are > k by −1” idea so the learner
  can still recognise the core problem,  
• add at least one non-standard post-condition that requires an extra

    requires

1. Function name and kind  
   contains_consecutive_numbers is an ordinary (executable) function, not a spec-only construct.

   • a: `&[i32]` – an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.

3. Return value

        – j == i + 1 (the second index is exactly the next position)  
        – a[i] + 1 == a[j] (the value stored at position j equals the value at position i plus one).

6. No additional helper specs, no decreases annotations, etc.

   • The spec reasons in mathematical ints, so there is no overflow issue inside the ensures clause, but any concrete implementation must avoid overflow in the executable code if it computes `a[i] + 1`.

   overflow. Any internally‐chosen algorithm (direct formula, loop, etc.) that

# Reasoning
The original exercise is almost vacuous: the only admissible input is `n == 0`, so the executable code can literally be the constant `return 0`.  
To make the task more interesting we can:

• Broaden the valid input range so that real arithmetic must be performed, yet still avoid machine-integer overflow by placing a precise upper-bound pre-condition.  
• Insist that the implementation be computed by an *iterative loop* (or tail-recursion) whose correctness is justified by an explicit loop invariant, forcing the learner to write ghost-state and invoke lemmas.  
• Ask for an auxiliary lemma that proves a mathematical property needed by the loop invariant, adding an extra reasoning step.  
• Replace the “triangular number” with something a bit less common––e.g. Fibonacci numbers or central binomial coefficients.  
• Optionally require the student to use wider intermediates (u128) and prove that the final result casts down to u64 without loss of information.

Below are three increasingly difficult, completely self-contained problem statements that incorporate those ideas.

    requires
        y >= 0,

        x >= i32::MIN as i64,
        x <= i32::MAX as i64,
        x * y as i64 >= i64::MIN,

        y < 100000,
    ensures
        z == x * y as i64,

# Reasoning
The snippet contains two separate items that must be re-expressed:

1. `string_to_set`

   • Signature: `spec fn string_to_set(s: Seq<char>) -> Set<char>`

   • There are no explicit pre- or post-conditions; the body alone defines the function: “result = to_set(s)”.

2. `foo`

   • Inputs  
     – `y: u64` (an unsigned 64-bit integer)  
     – `x: i32` (a signed 32-bit integer)  
   • Output  
     – named return value `z: i64` (signed 64-bit integer)  
   • Preconditions (`requires`)  
     1. `y >= 0` (trivially true for every `u64`, but stated explicitly)  
     2. `y < 1_000_000`  
     3. `x >= i32::MIN as i64`  
     4. `x <= i32::MAX as i64`  
     5. `x * y as i64 >= i64::MIN`  
     6. `x * y as i64 <= i64::MAX`  
     7. `y < 100_000` (this is stricter than 1 000 000, effectively reducing the bound on `y` to < 100 000).  
     Together, the numeric constraints guarantee that the product of `x` and `y` fits inside a 64-bit signed integer.  
   • Postcondition (`ensures`)  
     – `z == x * y as i64`.  After the function returns, the single output `z` must equal the mathematical product of `x` and `y`, where `y` is first cast to `i64` so that both operands share the same width.  
   • No other specification elements (no decreases clause, no additional helper lemmas).  
   • The body itself is omitted; any implementation that meets the above contract (most obviously, `let z = (x as i64) * (y as i64);`) will verify.

• Because `x` is already an `i32`, the two range checks on `x` are automatically satisfied, but they are included so the product is expressed in the `i64` domain.

• The product bounds (requirements 5 and 6) are critical: without them Verus would have to prove that overflow cannot occur; here the user guarantees it instead.

# Reasoning
The original exercise is almost trivial: with the overflow-avoiding pre-condition in place, one line of code is enough to satisfy both the *requires* and the *ensures*.  
To raise the level of difficulty we can

1.  Remove the “no–overflow” guarantee and instead ask the implementer to *detect* overflow and return a saturating answer.  
2.  Ask for an *exponentiation* function (`pow`) whose pre-condition still forbids overflow, but which now requires termination arguments (a `decreases` clause) and additional logical properties.  More reasoning steps are needed, because either a recursive or an iterative loop with an invariant is unavoidable.  
3.  Introduce modular arithmetic with three inputs (`x`, `y`, `m`) where the result must be `(x·y) mod m`.  The learner now has to state a few non-obvious facts (e.g. `0 < m`, `r < m`) and express modular equality in Verus (`==`).  Handling the *mixed-width* multiplication that avoids overflow in the mathematical domain but fits into `u128` in the executable domain is also more intricate.

All three tasks are still small enough to be reasonable in an interview setting, yet each demands a significantly richer specification and a non-trivial body compared to the original.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. No arithmetic pre-conditions: the function must accept *all* pairs `(x, y)` in the full `u64` range.

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. If the mathematical product `x * y` is strictly less than `u64::MAX`, then `r == x * y`.  
    2. Otherwise `r == u64::MAX`.  
    3. In all cases `r ≥ x` and `r ≥ y`.          (extra reasoning clause)  
    4. `r` is never smaller than the larger of the two inputs, guaranteeing monotonic saturation behaviour.  

Hints / remarks  
    • You will need to detect overflow; a typical technique is to perform the multiplication in `u128` and compare against `u64::MAX` before casting back.

# Reasoning
We want to raise the bar a little beyond the original “produce any sorted `Vec<u32>` of length `n`” exercise.  Good ways to do that are  

* prove a *semantic* relation between indices and the stored value (not just “it is sorted”)  
* introduce arithmetic overflow constraints that must be made explicit in the pre-conditions  
* make the post-condition talk about *two* returned objects and the relationship between them  
* require a permutation/equality‐of‐multisets property rather than mere sortedness  
* bring in modular arithmetic or conditional logic that stretches the learner’s quantifier skills.

Below are four independent, self-contained problem statements that do exactly this.  Each one is still short enough to be approachable but requires noticeably more reasoning steps than the original.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    pub fn squares_upto(n: usize) -> (a: Vec<u32>)

with the following behaviour and specification.

Inputs  
• `n: usize` – number of square numbers to generate (so the output will contain the squares of `0, 1, …, n-1`).

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `0 < n ≤ 65_536` – strictly positive; the upper bound guarantees that `(n-1)²` still fits into a `u32`.  
2. `n < 1_000_000` – keeps the vector from becoming unreasonably large.

Output  
• `a: Vec<u32>` – the vector that the function constructs and returns.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. Length          : `a.len() == n`.  
2. Value relation  : for every index `i` with `0 ≤ i < n`, we have `a[i] == (i as u32)*(i as u32)`.  
3. Strict order    : for all indices `i`, `j` with `0 ≤ i < j < n`, we have `a[i] < a[j]`.  
4. Last element    : `a[n-1] == ((n-1) as u32)*((n-1) as u32)`.  
5. Non-overflow    : every element is strictly less than `u32::MAX`.

No ghost objects or helper spec functions other than those required to express the arithmetic are allowed.  All properties above must be proven.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    pub fn fibonacci_prefix(n: usize) -> (fib: Vec<u64>)

that returns the first `n` Fibonacci numbers.

Inputs  
• `n: usize`.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `0 < n ≤ 93` – ensures the largest value fits in a `u64` (since `F₉₃ > 2⁶⁴-1`).  

Output  
• `fib: Vec<u64>`.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. `fib.len() == n`.  
2. Base cases: `n ≥ 1 ⇒ fib[0] == 0`; `n ≥ 2 ⇒ fib[1] == 1`.  
3. Recurrence  : for every `i` with `2 ≤ i < n`, `fib[i] == fib[i-1] + fib[i-2]`.  
4. Monotone    : for all `i`, `j` with `0 ≤ i < j < n`,  `fib[i] < fib[j]`.  
5. Upper bound : for every `i < n`, `fib[i] < (1_u128 << 64)` (auto-satisfied but must be proven).

No additional spec helpers besides simple arithmetic are permitted.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    pub fn rotate_left(v: Vec<u32>, k: usize) -> (w: Vec<u32>)

which returns `v` rotated left by `k` positions.

Inputs  
• `v: Vec<u32>` – the original vector (may be empty).  
• `k: usize`    – rotation amount.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `k < v.len() ⇔ v.len() > 0` – when the vector is non-empty, `k` must be strictly less than its length; if it is empty, `k` must be `0`.  

Output  
• `w: Vec<u32>` – the rotated vector.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. Same length   : `w.len() == v.len()`.  
2. Rotation rule : for every index `i` with `0 ≤ i < v.len()`, we have  
                   `w[i] == v[(i + k) mod v.len()]`.  
3. Permutation   : multiset of `w` equals multiset of `v` (follows from 2 but must still be stated and proven).  
4. Copy-free     : the implementation must run in `O(v.len())` time and `O(v.len())` additional *memory* (the function may allocate a fresh vector of the same length but no more).

The specification must be complete without introducing ghost arrays other than what Verus automatically derives from `Vec`.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    pub fn merge_and_dedupe(a: Vec<u32>, b: Vec<u32>) -> (c: Vec<u32>)

that merges two already *strictly* increasing input vectors into a single strictly increasing vector without duplicates.

Inputs  
• `a: Vec<u32>` – strictly increasing.  
• `b: Vec<u32>` – strictly increasing.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `forall i, j | 0 ≤ i < j < a.len()  ⇒ a[i] < a[j]`.  
2. `forall i, j | 0 ≤ i < j < b.len()  ⇒ b[i] < b[j]`.

Output  
• `c: Vec<u32>`.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. Strictly increasing: `forall i, j | 0 ≤ i < j < c.len()  ⇒ c[i] < c[j]`.  
2. Set equality      : the set of values in `c` equals the union of the sets of values in `a` and `b`.  
3. Length bound      : `c.len() ≤ a.len() + b.len()`.  
4. Stability hint    : for every value `x` contained in both `a` and `b`, `x` appears exactly once in `c`, and its relative order with respect to other shared elements matches the order in `a` (ties broken toward `a`).  

No helper spec functions other than the standard set abstraction for `Vec` are permitted, and the implementation should run in `O(a.len() + b.len())` time.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

spec fn abs(x: i32) -> u32 {
    if x > 0 {
        x as u32
    } else {
        -x as u32
    }
}

fn abs_func(x: i32) -> (y: u32)
    requires
        x >= i32::MIN && x <= i32::MAX - 1,
    ensures
        y >= 0,
        x >= 0 ==> x == y,
        x < 0 ==> -x == y,
        y == abs(x),
{
```

# Reasoning
The file contains two items: a pure specification‐only helper `abs`, and the executable (but still fully verified) function `abs_func`.

SPEC FUNCTION `abs`
1. Signature: `spec fn abs(x: i32) -> u32`.
2. Behaviour (definition provided):
   • If the argument `x` is strictly positive (`x > 0`), the function returns `x` itself, cast to an unsigned 32-bit integer.  
   • Otherwise (`x <= 0`) it returns `-x`, again cast to `u32`.  
   Hence `abs` denotes the mathematical absolute value of a signed 32-bit number but expressed as an unsigned 32-bit result.
3. Because the return type is `u32`, the value is guaranteed non-negative.  
4. Note the branch guard is `x > 0` (strict) rather than `x >= 0`. For `x == 0`, control flows to the `else` branch and `-x` evaluates to `0`, so the function still returns the correct absolute value.  
5. The function lives purely in the specification domain; it is not executed at runtime.

EXECUTABLE FUNCTION `abs_func`
1. Inputs  
   • `x: i32` — the signed 32-bit integer whose absolute value we wish to compute.
2. Output  
   • `y: u32` — the absolute value.
3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `x >= i32::MIN` (trivially true for every `i32`, but written explicitly).  
   • `x <= i32::MAX - 1` — the caller is forbidden from passing the largest representable positive value `i32::MAX`. The interval therefore is `[−2³¹, 2³¹−2]`. This range restriction eliminates the risk of arithmetic overflow that could arise were the implementation to add one to `x` or cast beyond the `u32` limit.  
4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   • `y >= 0` — tautological for `u32`, yet stated explicitly.  
   • `x >= 0 ==> x == y` — if the argument is non-negative the function must return it unchanged (as an unsigned integer).  
   • `x < 0 ==> -x == y` — if the argument is negative the function must return its negation.  
   • `y == abs(x)` — the result must coincide with the auxiliary specification function defined above, providing a concise summary of the requirement and tying the two contracts together.
5. No additional ghost state or lemmas are referenced.  Any runtime implementation that satisfies the above contract (e.g., branching on the sign of `x`, or doing bit-twiddling) will verify.

Subtleties / Edge-cases
• `x == 0` falls under the “non-negative” clause and the result must be `0`.  
• `x == i32::MIN` (−2³¹) is allowed by the precondition.  However, its negation (2³¹) does not fit in an `i32`; the implementer must therefore be careful to avoid overflowing a signed 32-bit temporary when computing `-x`.  Casting to a wider type before negation or working directly in `u32` are typical work-arounds.  
• The exclusion of `i32::MAX` (2³¹−1) from the allowed domain is unusual but must be respected; calls with that value are ill-formed with respect to the specification.

The combination of these clauses fully determines the behaviour: `abs_func` is a total function from the specified subset of `i32` into `u32` that returns the absolute value of its input.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function named `abs_func` together with an auxiliary pure specification function `abs`.

1. Helper spec function  
   • Name: `abs`  
   • Signature: `spec fn abs(x: i32) -> u32`  
   • Definition:  
       – If `x > 0` return `x as u32`.  
       – Else return `(-x) as u32`.  
   • Purpose: expresses the mathematical absolute value of a signed 32-bit integer while producing an unsigned 32-bit result.  
   • It lives only in the logical/specification world.

2. Primary executable function  
   Signature: `fn abs_func(x: i32) -> (y: u32)`

   Inputs  
     x : i32 — any signed 32-bit number in the range [i32::MIN, i32::MAX - 1].

   Preconditions (`requires`)  
     • x >= i32::MIN  
     • x <= i32::MAX - 1  
     (Together these bounds forbid exactly one value, i32::MAX.)

   Output  
     y : u32 — the absolute value.

   Postconditions (`ensures`)  
     • y >= 0  
     • If x ≥ 0 then y == x (after the implicit cast to u32).  
     • If x < 0 then y == -x (still as u32).  
     • y == abs(x) — i.e., the result matches the helper spec function.

Any body that meets these contracts is correct. Commonly the implementation branches on the sign of `x`, taking care to avoid overflow for `x == i32::MIN` (for example by casting to `i64` or `u32` before negation). No additional lemmas or ghost variables are required.

With this information one can reconstruct the exact Verus specification supplied above.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise simply asks the student to return a Boolean that says whether one slice is a prefix of another.  
That can be solved by one straight‐line loop (or even by a library call) and the specification is equally short.  
To make the exercise more interesting we can

 • Keep the same data domain (slices of `char`) so that no new libraries are needed.  
 • Ask for a value result (index, length, ordering code) instead of a mere Boolean so the student must supply a bit more functional correctness information.  
 • Introduce universally-quantified post-conditions that bind an existentially-quantified witness (e.g. “smallest shift”, “longest length”).  Such specs require the implementer to prove minimality / maximality properties, which is a non-trivial extra reasoning step.  
 • Leave the size-bound (<1000) so arithmetic over `usize` remains ghost-friendly and we don’t have to fight overflow.

Below are three variants that each meet those goals yet are still short enough to fit into a single function.  Each description is fully self-contained so a spec generator will not need any other context.

# Output
<<<
Function name: rotation_index

Purpose  
Given two slices of `char` having the same length, return the amount of left-rotation that turns the first slice into the second slice, or −1 if no rotation matches.

Inputs  
• original: `&[char]`  
• rotated : `&[char]`

Returns  
• res: `int` (−1 means “not a rotation”, otherwise 0 ≤ res < original.len())

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. original.len() == rotated.len()  
2. original.len() < 1000         // to avoid arithmetic overflow in proofs

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. −1 ≤ res < original.len()  
2. res == −1  ⇔  ¬∃k: int. 0 ≤ k < original.len() ∧  
       ∀i: int. 0 ≤ i < original.len() ⇒ original@\[i] == rotated@\[(i + k) mod original.len()]  
3. res ≠ −1  ⇒  
   a. 0 ≤ res < original.len()                                             // a valid shift  
   b. ∀i: int. 0 ≤ i < original.len() ⇒ original@\[i] == rotated@\[(i + res) mod original.len()]  
   c. ∀k: int. 0 ≤ k < res ⇒ ¬∀i: int. 0 ≤ i < original.len() ⇒  
          original@\[i] == rotated@\[(i + k) mod original.len()]            // minimality of res
>>>

<<<
Function name: longest_proper_prefix_suffix

Purpose  
Return the length of the longest *proper* prefix of a slice that is also a suffix of that slice.

Inputs  
• s: `&[char]`

Returns  
• res: `usize`

Pre-conditions  
1. s.len() > 0                        // the notion of “proper prefix” is non-empty  
2. s.len() < 1000                     // prevent arithmetic overflow

Post-conditions  
1. 0 ≤ res < s.len()                  // “proper” means strictly shorter than the whole string  
2. ∀i: int. 0 ≤ i < res ⇒ s@\[(i)] == s@\[(s.len() as int − res as int) + i]   // prefix equals suffix  
3. ∀r: int. 0 ≤ r < s.len() ∧  
          (∀i: int. 0 ≤ i < r ⇒ s@\[(i)] == s@\[(s.len() as int − r) + i])  
       ⇒ r ≤ res                      // res is the *longest* such length
>>>

<<<
Function name: lex_compare

Purpose  
Perform a three-way lexicographic comparison of two char slices, returning −1, 0, or 1.

Inputs  
• a: `&[char]`  
• b: `&[char]`

Returns  
• res: `int`   // −1 if a<b, 0 if a==b, 1 if a>b

Pre-conditions  
1. a.len() < 2000  
2. b.len() < 2000

Post-conditions  
1. res ∈ {−1, 0, 1}  
2. res == 0  ⇔  a@ == b@  
3. res == −1 ⇔ (∃k: int. 0 ≤ k < min(a.len(), b.len()) ∧  
                   (∀j: int. 0 ≤ j < k ⇒ a@\[(j)] == b@\[(j)]) ∧  
                   a@\[(k)] < b@\[(k)])  
                ∨ (a.len() < b.len() ∧ a@ is a prefix of b@)  
4. res == 1  ⇔  res ≠ −1 ∧ res ≠ 0          // exhaustiveness

Helper definition (to be provided in the spec code if desired)  
is_prefix_predicate(pre: Seq<char>, str: Seq<char>) returns true exactly when pre is a prefix of str as in the original exercise.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Specification function to check if a number is max
spec fn is_max(m: int, numbers: Seq<int>) -> bool {
    exists|i: int| 0 <= i < numbers.len() && numbers[i] == m &&
    forall|i: int| 0 <= i < numbers.len() ==> numbers[i] <= m
}

/// Function to find max
fn max_func(numbers: &[int]) -> (result: int)
    requires
        numbers.len() > 0,
    ensures
        is_max(result, numbers@),
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We have:

1. Helper specification function `is_max(m, numbers)`:
   - Inputs: `m: int`, `numbers: Seq<int>` (logical sequence of mathematical ints).
   - Returns Boolean indicating whether `m` is a maximum value of the sequence and is present in the sequence.
   - Definition: There exists an index `i` within bounds such that `numbers[i] == m` and for all indices in bounds, `numbers[i] <= m`. So `m` is at least as large as every element and is actually one of the elements.

2. Executable function `max_func`:
   - Input: `numbers: &[int]` (immutable slice of Verus `int`, i.e., mathematical unbounded integer values stored at runtime).
   - Output: `result: int`.
   - Precondition: slice length > 0 (non-empty).

  forall|i: int| 0 <= i < numbers.len() ==> numbers[i] <= m

  ensures

No other preconditions are imposed, and because `int` is unbounded there are no overflow considerations.

The body may employ any algorithm (single pass, divide-and-conquer, etc.) as long as it can be verified to satisfy the above contract.

The original assignment is extremely small in scope: it asks the student to prove that the larger of two integers is returned.  
To raise the difficulty while staying within the same general theme (“selecting an extremum under proofs”), I generated three independent stand-alone problem statements that progressively add:

• More inputs (three integers or an entire sequence) – this forces additional case distinctions.  
• Auxiliary information that must be returned (a uniqueness flag or an index), introducing logical equivalences the proof must cover.  
• Extra post-conditions such as “smallest index of a maximum”, “median property” or “flag correctness”.  
All three tasks remain feasible for a beginner who already solved the two-argument maximum, yet require noticeably more specification and proof effort.

# Output
<<<
Verified function that returns the maximum of two non-negative integers **and** a Boolean
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Auxiliary (ghost-only) specification helper  
    spec fn max(x: int, y: int) -> int  
        – Returns x when x ≥ y, otherwise returns y.  

Primary executable function  
    fn max_with_flag(x: int, y: int) -> (r: int, strictly_greater: bool)

Inputs  
    x, y : int — both non-negative.

Pre-conditions  
    1. x ≥ 0  
    2. y ≥ 0  

Outputs  
    r : int                   — the selected value.  
    strictly_greater : bool   — true exactly when the chosen value is *strictly* larger than the other argument.

Post-conditions  
    1. r ≥ x ∧ r ≥ y                                  — r dominates both inputs.  
    2. r == x ∨ r == y                                — r equals one of the inputs.  
    3. strictly_greater ↔ (x ≠ y)                     — the flag is true iff the inputs differ.  
    4. strictly_greater ==> ( (r == x ∧ x > y) ∨ (r == y ∧ y > x) )  
       ¬strictly_greater ==> (x == y ∧ r == x ∧ r == y)  

No other requirements; any verified body that fulfils all clauses is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Verified function that returns the *median* of three non-negative integers
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Auxiliary helper (optional)  
    spec fn median3(a: int, b: int, c: int) -> int  
        – Returns the value that is ≥ the smallest argument and ≤ the largest.

Primary executable function  
    fn median_of_three(a: int, b: int, c: int) -> (m: int)

Inputs  
    a, b, c : int — all non-negative.

Pre-conditions  
    1. a ≥ 0  
    2. b ≥ 0  
    3. c ≥ 0  

Output  
    m : int

Post-conditions  
    1. (m == a) ∨ (m == b) ∨ (m == c)                               — m is one of the original values.  
    2. ∃ lo, hi : int.  lo ≤ m ≤ hi ∧ {lo,hi} ⊆ {a,b,c}             — m lies between the min and max of the inputs.  
       (Equivalently: m ≥ min(a,b,c) ∧ m ≤ max(a,b,c)).  
    3. At least one argument is ≤ m and at least one argument is ≥ m.

Together these ensure that m is the unique “middle” value when the three numbers are sorted (ties allowed).

The implementation may be branchy or branch-free, but must be fully verified.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

spec fn is_even(n: int) -> bool {
    n % 2 == 0
}

fn is_product_even(a: &[u32]) -> (result: bool)
    requires
        true,
    ensures
        result <==> exists|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() as int && is_even(a[i as int] as int),
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The specification defines two things:

1. A pure specification helper  
   spec fn is_even(n: int) -> bool  
   • Input: n (mathematical integer, unbounded).  
   • Output: Boolean that is true exactly when n is an even integer, expressed by the arithmetic test n % 2 == 0.  

2. An executable, verified function  
   fn is_product_even(a: &[u32]) -> (result: bool)

   Inputs & type:
   • a: &[u32] – an immutable slice of 32-bit unsigned integers.  The slice may be empty; there are no restrictions.

   Output:
   • result: bool.

   Preconditions (requires):
   • true – i.e., none.  The function must cope with any slice.

   Postcondition (ensures):
   • result <==> ∃ i:int . 0 ≤ i < a.len()  ∧  is_even( (a[i]) )  
     (The spec casts a.len() to int and a[i] from u32 to int so the helper can be used.)
     In words: the returned Boolean must be true exactly when the slice contains at least one even element.  
     • If the slice is empty, the existential is false, so result must be false.  
     • If at least one element satisfies a[i] % 2 == 0, result must be true.

   No other ghost state or lemmas are referenced.

   Subtleties:
   • All arithmetic in the spec is on the unbounded int domain; this avoids overflow issues even though the runtime data are u32.  
   • The biconditional (<==>) demands equivalence, not just implication: the implementation may neither under- nor over-approximate the predicate.
   • Index variable i is int; casts are needed when using it in concrete indexing code.

An implementation can simply iterate through the slice and return true on the first even element or false if none are found.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function named is_product_even together with a helper specification predicate.

Helper spec predicate
  spec fn is_even(n: int) -> bool
  – Parameter: n (mathematical integer).
  – Returns true iff n is divisible by 2, i.e. n % 2 == 0.

Primary executable function
  fn is_product_even(a: &[u32]) -> (result: bool)

Inputs
  • a: an immutable slice of 32-bit unsigned integers (can be empty).

Output
  • result: bool.

Preconditions (requires)
  • None (the specification explicitly lists requires true).

Postcondition (ensures)
  result is logically equivalent to the existence of at least one even element in the slice:
      result  ⇔  ∃ i:int · 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ is_even( a[i] as int )
  The slice length a.len() (usize) is cast to int for the specification, and each element a[i] (u32) is likewise cast to int before being fed to is_even.

Interpretation
  • result must be true exactly when some index within bounds stores an even value; otherwise it must be false.
  • For an empty slice the existential is false, hence result must be false.

No other ghost helpers or lemmas are needed. Any body that provably satisfies the contract—e.g. a simple loop checking each element’s parity—will be accepted.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is extremely small‐scale: two arguments, a single `if`, and two straightforward postconditions.  
To make the assignment more interesting while still staying in the “small, self-contained” realm appropriate for a first Verus exercise, I introduce

• more parameters (or a slice) so that a loop or nested conditionals are needed,  
• stable tie-break rules or extra return values that force additional reasoning,  
• a precise ghost-level specification (extra helper predicates) that the executable code must match, and  
• small numeric bounds so the learner cannot ignore overflow or indexing details.

Three progressively harder stand-alone variants follow.  Each provides enough natural-language detail for a spec-generator to create helper `spec fn`s, input/output types, preconditions, and postconditions without any additional context.

# Output
<<<
Verified function pair: maximum of three non-negative integers with tie-break and uniqueness flag

1. Helper specification functions (ghost-only)

   spec fn max3(x:int, y:int, z:int) -> int  
     Definition: the greatest of {x, y, z}.  
   spec fn is_unique_max(x:int, y:int, z:int) -> bool  
     Definition: exactly one value attains `max3(x,y,z)`.

2. Executable function

   fn max3_func(x:int, y:int, z:int) -> (r:int, unique:bool)

Inputs  
• x, y, z : int

Preconditions (requires)  
• 0 ≤ x ∧ 0 ≤ y ∧ 0 ≤ z          // all arguments are non-negative

Outputs  
• r      : int   — the chosen maximum value  
• unique : bool  — `true` iff the maximum appears only once

Postconditions (ensures)  
• r = max3(x,y,z)                                // value correctness  
• (r = x ∨ r = y ∨ r = z)                        // result is one of the inputs  
• unique = is_unique_max(x,y,z)                  // flag correctness  
• if unique { (r > x ⇒ r ≠ x) ∧ (r > y ⇒ r ≠ y) ∧ (r > z ⇒ r ≠ z) }  
  else        { at least two of {x,y,z} equal r }  // explanatory guarantee

Additional requirement  
• If two or three inputs share the same maximal value, the function must return the *first* parameter among them (stable, left-to-right tie-break).
>>>

<<<
Verified function pair: first occurrence of maximum in a bounded slice of u64

1. Helper specification functions (ghost-only)

   spec fn seq_max(s:Seq<u64>) -> u64  
     Definition: mathematical maximum of the sequence’s elements.  
   spec fn first_index_of_max(s:Seq<u64>) -> nat  
     Definition: smallest i such that s[i] = seq_max(s).

2. Executable function

   fn max_index_u64(slice:&[u64]) -> (idx:nat, val:u64)

Inputs  
• slice : & [u64]

Preconditions (requires)  
• slice.len() > 0                               // non-empty  
• slice.len() ≤ 1_000                           // small bound, rules out overflow in proofs

Outputs  
• idx : nat   — index of the chosen element  
• val : u64   — value stored at that index

Postconditions (ensures)  
• idx < slice.len()                             // index validity  
• val = slice[idx]                              // returned value matches slice content  
• val = seq_max(slice@)                         // value is globally maximal (`@` turns slice into Seq)  
• idx = first_index_of_max(slice@)              // stable first-occurrence tie-break  
• ∀j:nat :: j < slice.len() ⇒ slice[idx] ≥ slice[j] // usual “no element is larger”

Complexity expectation  
• Implementation must run in O(slice.len()) time and O(1) additional space (straightforward single scan).
>>>

<<<
Verified function pair: lexicographic maximum of two integer pairs

We compare pairs (a₁, a₂) and (b₁, b₂) using standard lexicographic order: the pair with the larger first component wins; if the first components are equal, the larger second component wins.

1. Helper specification functions (ghost-only)

   spec fn lex_ge(p:(int,int), q:(int,int)) -> bool  
     Definition: p ≥ q in lexicographic order.  
   spec fn lex_max(p:(int,int), q:(int,int)) -> (int,int)  
     Definition: if lex_ge(p,q) { p } else { q }.

2. Executable function

   fn lex_max_pair(p:(int,int), q:(int,int)) -> (r:(int,int))

Inputs  
• p = (p1,p2) : (int,int)  
• q = (q1,q2) : (int,int)

Preconditions (requires)  
• −1_000_000_000 ≤ p1,p2,q1,q2 ≤ 1_000_000_000   // bounded range prevents overflow  
• (p1,p2) ≠ (q1,q2)                               // pairs are not identical, forces genuine comparison

Output  
• r : (int,int)

Postconditions (ensures)  
• (r = p) ∨ (r = q)                               // result is exactly one of the inputs  
• lex_ge(r, p) ∧ lex_ge(r, q)                     // r is ≥ both operands  
• ∀t:(int,int) :: (t = p ∨ t = q) ⇒ lex_ge(r,t)   // maximal wrt the two  
• If p1 = q1 then r = (p1, max(p2,q2)) else r.first = max(p1,q1) // explicit component-wise behaviour

Implementation hint  
Use a single `if` on the first components, then possibly a second `if` on the second components; prove the helper lemmas connecting `lex_ge` and ordinary integer comparison.
>>>

Design and verify a Verus-Rust function named `up`.

Signature
    fn up(n: u64) -> (s: Vec<u64>)

Inputs
    n : u64 — the unsigned 64-bit start value.

Pre-conditions (requires)
    1. n ≥ 0. (Redundant but stated explicitly for clarity.)
    2. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX. Because u64::MAX ÷ u64::MAX = 1, this forces n to be strictly less than 1, i.e. n = 0. The intention is to guarantee that any arithmetic that follows (notably n + 1) cannot overflow.
    3. n * n < u64::MAX. A secondary guard against overflow; for n = 0 it trivially holds.

Output
    s : Vec<u64> — a heap vector of 64-bit unsigned integers.

Post-conditions (ensures)
    • s.len() == 2 — the result vector has exactly two elements.  
    • s[0] == n     — the first element equals the input n.  
    • s[1] == n + 1 — the second element equals the successor of n.

In other words, given a start value n that satisfies the above range checks, `up` must return the two-element vector `[n, n + 1]`.

No auxiliary specification functions, lemmas, or ghost state are involved; the body may be a straightforward construction of the vector. Verification obligations are limited to proving that the returned vector meets the length and element constraints and that no arithmetic overflows, which is ensured by the stated preconditions.

```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Specification function to check if a character is a digit
spec fn is_digit(c: u8) -> bool {
    48 <= c && c <= 57
}

/// Function to check if a string is an integer
fn is_integer(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)
    ensures
        result <==> s.len() > 0 && forall|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> is_digit(s[i]),
{
```

# Reasoning
The file contains two declarations: a pure specification helper `is_digit` and an executable, verified function `is_integer`.

1. `is_digit`
   • Kind: `spec fn` (ghost-only, compile-time logic).
   • Input: `c: u8` – one byte.
   • Output: `bool`.
   • Definition: `48 <= c && c <= 57`.  
     In ASCII these codes correspond to ‘0’ through ‘9’.  
     Therefore `is_digit(c)` is true exactly when `c` encodes one of the ten decimal digits.

2. `is_integer`
   • Kind: normal `fn` with a specification.
   • Input: `s: &[u8]` – an immutable slice of bytes that we treat as a string.
   • Output: `(result: bool)`.
   • Preconditions (`requires`): none – the function must accept any slice, even empty.
   • Postcondition (`ensures`):
         result <==> s.len() > 0
                      && (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ is_digit(s[i]))
     `<==>` is logical equivalence.  
     Hence:
       – The function must return `true` exactly when the slice is non-empty AND every byte `s[i]` is an ASCII digit according to `is_digit`.  
       – If the slice is empty, or if any byte lies outside the digit range, `result` must be `false`.

   • There are no additional helpers, no decreases clauses, no loop attributes, and no run-time side effects required—the body can be any Rust code that provably satisfies the single post-condition.

Important subtleties:
   – The function recognises only unsigned decimal digits 0-9; there is no optional ‘+’ or ‘−’, no whitespace trimming, and no allowance for UTF-8 multibyte numerals.
   – Because specification arithmetic uses mathematical `int`, the range checks (`0 ≤ i < s.len()`) are exact and overflow-free.
   – Returning the Boolean expression that appears in the post-condition is the simplest correct implementation, but any implementation that Verus can verify is acceptable.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement two items in Verus-Rust.

1. Specification helper

   spec fn is_digit(c: u8) -> bool

   Purpose: decide whether byte `c` represents an ASCII decimal digit.
   Definition: return `true` exactly when 48 ≤ c ≤ 57 (the ASCII codes for ‘0’..‘9’).

2. Verified executable function

   fn is_integer(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

   Inputs  
     – s: an immutable slice of bytes, viewed as a raw ASCII string.

   Output  
     – result: bool.

   Preconditions  
     – None.

   Postcondition  
     result is logically equivalent to  
        s.len() > 0              // the slice is non-empty
        &&
        (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ is_digit(s[i]))
        // every byte in the slice is an ASCII digit according to is_digit

   In other words, the function must return true iff the slice contains at least one character and each character is ‘0’–‘9’; otherwise it must return false.

No additional spec items, decreases clauses, or runtime side effects are required.
>>>

Important subtleties:
   – The function recognises only unsigned decimal digits 0-9; there is no optional ‘+’ or ‘−’, no whitespace trimming, and no allowance for UTF-8 multibyte numerals.
   – Because specification arithmetic uses mathematical `int`, the range checks (`0 ≤ i < s.len()`) are exact and overflow-free.
   – Returning the Boolean expression that appears in the post-condition is the simplest correct implementation, but any implementation that Verus can verify is acceptable.

1. Specification helper

   Purpose: decide whether byte `c` represents an ASCII decimal digit.

   Output  
     – result: bool.

   Preconditions  
     – None.

   Postcondition  
     result is logically equivalent to  
        s.len() > 0              // the slice is non-empty
        &&
        (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ is_digit(s[i]))
        // every byte in the slice is an ASCII digit according to is_digit

   In other words, the function must return true iff the slice contains at least one character and each character is ‘0’–‘9’; otherwise it must return false.

No additional spec items, decreases clauses, or runtime side effects are required.
