# Reasoning
The original task only required returning the two–element vector `[n, n + 1]` under an extremely strong pre-condition that already forced `n = 0`.  
To raise the difficulty I propose three alternative problems that

• introduce additional parameters (step, count) so more arithmetic must be verified,  
• require universally-quantified post-conditions that range over every index of the returned vector,  
• demand reasoning about multiplication as well as addition, so overflow arguments are less trivial, and  
• use less common correctness properties such as “strict arithmetic progression” or the inductive Fibonacci rule.

Each specification is self-contained, long enough, and clearly states all inputs, pre- and post-conditions so that a Verus spec can be generated directly from it.

# Output
<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function named `up_stride`.

Signature  
    fn up_stride(start: u64, step: u64, count: u64) -> (s: Vec<u64>)

Inputs  
    start : u64  — first element of the arithmetic progression.  
    step  : u64  — strictly positive stride between consecutive elements.  
    count : u64  — number of elements to generate; must be at least 1.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. 1 ≤ count ≤ 10_000.                              (avoid pathological loops)  
    2. 1 ≤ step   ≤ u64::MAX / count.                   (prevents `step*count` overflow)  
    3. start ≤ u64::MAX − step * (count − 1).           (prevents final addition overflow)  
       Together 2 and 3 guarantee every term `start + step*i` fits in `u64`.

Output  
    s : Vec<u64> — heap vector containing the generated progression.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    • s.len() == count.  
    • forall i: nat | i < count ==> s[i] == start + step * i.  
    • forall i: nat | i + 1 < count ==> s[i] < s[i + 1].      (strictly increasing)  
    • s.capacity() == count.                                  (no wasted capacity)

No auxiliary ghost state other than loop invariants is allowed. Heap allocation may occur exactly once when constructing `s`; no intermediate `Vec` objects may be created.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function named `square_tail`.

Signature  
    fn square_tail(offset: u64, k: u64) -> (s: Vec<u64>)

Inputs  
    offset : u64 — starting base; the first element will be `(offset)^2`.  
    k      : u64 — number of consecutive squares to generate; must be non-zero.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. 1 ≤ k ≤ 1_000_000.                                          
    2. offset ≤ 4_294_967_295.                 // so (offset + k − 1)^2 < u64::MAX  
    3. offset + k − 1 ≤ 4_294_967_295.        // same bound for highest base

Output  
    s : Vec<u64>

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    • s.len() == k.  
    • forall i: nat | i < k ==> s[i] == (offset + i)*(offset + i).  
    • s[0]      == offset * offset.  
    • s[k - 1]  == (offset + k - 1)*(offset + k - 1).  
    • forall i: nat | i + 1 < k ==> s[i + 1] − s[i] == 2*(offset + i) + 1.  
      (Shows you proved the algebraic property of consecutive squares.)

Only one heap allocation for the final vector is allowed.  You may use pure ghost arithmetic helpers, but no external math library calls.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function named `fib_prefix`.

Signature  
    fn fib_prefix(n: u64) -> (s: Vec<u64>)

Inputs  
    n : u64 — length of the Fibonacci prefix requested.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. 2 ≤ n ≤ 93.                     //  Fib(93) = 12 200 160 415 121 876 738 < 2⁶⁴  
    2. n is even.                      //  less common, forces extra reasoning step

Output  
    s : Vec<u64>

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    • s.len() == n.  
    • s[0] == 0   &&   s[1] == 1.  
    • forall i: nat | 2 ≤ i < n ==> s[i] == s[i-1] + s[i-2].  
    • forall i: nat | i < n ==> s[i] < u64::MAX.   // proven from bound on n  
    • s[n-1] ≥ s[n-2] ≥ … ≥ s[0].                 // monotone non-decreasing  
    • sum (0 ≤ i < n) s[i] == s[n] − 1.           // classical Fibonacci identity

The implementation may use a loop with ghost invariants; recursion is disallowed to avoid stack overuse.  All arithmetic must be shown free of overflow under the stated range.
>>>

   These conditions guarantee that the multiplication `n * n` cannot overflow in the `u64` domain.

    fn square(n: u64) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    n: an unsigned 64-bit integer.

1. n ≥ 0.  
   (Redundant for `u64`, but must appear exactly as stated.)
2. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.  
   Because `u64::MAX / u64::MAX` evaluates to `1`, the caller may in practice only supply `n == 0`.  
   This guard exists to rule out overflow of the expression `n * n`.

>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is essentially “return a negative number if `x` is not in `s`, else return any non-negative number”.  
That is a single linear scan with a very weak post-condition, so the proof is trivial.

To increase the difficulty only “a bit” we can:

• Strengthen the post-conditions so that the **exact** index (or some computed sentinel) must be returned.  
• Add a mild structural property to the input (sortedness, uniqueness, monotonicity, …).  
• Keep the function single-pass and without auxiliary lemmas, yet force the student to write and maintain richer loop invariants.  

Below are three standalone, self-contained problem statements that satisfy those goals.  
Each replaces a common requirement (simple existence) with a more specific one (exact index, insertion point, counting occurrences), and each adds ~10–20 words of extra specification text.


# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function signature  
    fn locate_leftmost(x: int, s: &Vec<int>) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
  • x: int  
    The mathematical integer to be searched for.  
  • s: &Vec<int>  
    A borrowed immutable vector of integers, assumed NON-DECREASING (every later element is ≥ the previous one).

Output  
  • r: i32  
    The index of the first occurrence of `x` if it exists, otherwise a negative sentinel value.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
 1. s is sorted:                ∀ i:int, j:int. 0 ≤ i < j < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≤ s[j]  
 2. Length fits in i32:          s.len() < i32::MAX

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  • If r ≥ 0 then  
        0 ≤ r < s.len()                ∧  
        s[r] = x                       ∧        // value found  
        ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < r ⇒ s[j] ≠ x           // it is the *leftmost* occurrence
  • If r < 0  then  
        ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≠ x     // x is absent

No guarantees are made about the precise negative value chosen, only its sign.  
The implementation may use linear search; logarithmic time is NOT required, but the proof must establish the stronger “leftmost index” property.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function signature  
    fn binary_search_like(x: int, s: &Vec<int>) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
  • x: int  
  • s: &Vec<int> (strictly increasing, i.e. s[i] < s[i+1])

Outputs  
  • r: i32  
    If `x` exists, `r` equals its unique index.  
    If it does not, `r` equals −(k + 1) where k is the index at which `x`
    could be inserted while preserving order (the Java/Go convention).

Pre-conditions  
 1. Strict order: ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i+1 < s.len() ⇒ s[i] < s[i+1]  
 2. s.len() < i32::MAX

Post-conditions  
  • (r ≥ 0)  ⇒  0 ≤ r < s.len() ∧ s[r] = x  
  • (r < 0)  ⇒  
        let k:int = (-r) - 1 in              // insertion point encoded in r  
            0 ≤ k ≤ s.len()                 ∧
            ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < k  ⇒ s[i] < x  ∧
            ∀ i:int. k ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ x < s[i]

No complexity guarantee is required, yet the student must prove correctness of the
encoded insertion-point arithmetic together with the ordering lemmas.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function signature  
    fn count_occurrences(x: int, s: &Vec<int>) -> (exists: bool, cnt: u32)

Inputs

  • s: &Vec<int>

Outputs

  • cnt:   u32        // the exact number of elements equal to x

Pre-conditions  
 1. s.len() ≤ u32::MAX   // so `cnt` cannot overflow

Post-conditions  
  • cnt = |{ i:int | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] = x }|   // cardinality of the set  
  • exists = (cnt > 0)                               // Boolean consistent with count

Hint: you will need a ghost variable or an invariant that tracks the
running count while scanning the vector.

Design and verify a function in Verus called `reverse`.

Signature  
fn reverse(a: &[int]) -> (b: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a: immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers (`int`).

Output  
• b: a newly returned vector (`Vec<int>`).

Preconditions

Postconditions

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Finds the maximum element in a sequence.
fn maximum(values: &Vec<int>) -> (max: int)
    requires
        values.len() > 0,
        values.len() < 1000, // Added a relaxation on the size of the input sequence
    ensures
        exists|i: int| 0 <= i && i < values.len() as int && values[i] == max,
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < values.len() as int ==> values[i] <= max,
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification is for a single executable function named `maximum`.

1. Inputs  
   • `values`: an immutable reference to a `Vec<int>`.  
   In Verus, `int` denotes the mathematical, unbounded integer type used for specification as well as (here) the concrete element type of the vector.

2. Output  
   • `max: int` – the function returns one `int` value, bound to the identifier `max` in the post-state.

3. Preconditions (requires)  
   a. `values.len() > 0` – the caller must supply a non-empty vector.  
   b. `values.len() < 1000` – the vector length is strictly less than 1000. (This is probably there to simplify later proofs of termination or resource usage; it has no semantic influence on the definition of “maximum”.)

4. Postconditions (ensures)  
   a. `exists|i: int| 0 <= i && i < values.len() as int && values[i] == max`  
      – there exists at least one valid index whose element is **exactly** the returned value. Thus the result is an element of the vector.  
   b. `forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < values.len() as int ==> values[i] <= max`  
      – every element of the vector is less than or equal to the result.  

   Taken together, these clauses say: “`max` is an element of `values` that is greater than or equal to every other element”, i.e. `max` is a maximum of the list.

5. Helper spec items  
   None are referenced; the function is entirely described by its own contract.

6. Subtleties / observations  
   • Because the domain is `int`, there is no risk of arithmetic overflow; the specification lives in the mathematical universe.  
   • Uniqueness of the maximum is not required—if the largest value occurs several times, returning any one of them satisfies the spec.  
   • The upper-bound precondition (< 1000) does not change the logical meaning but must still be checked by the caller and proved by the implementation if it manipulates lengths explicitly.

# Final Description
<<<
Design a verified Verus-Rust function named `maximum` that returns the greatest element of a non-empty vector of mathematical integers.

Signature  
    fn maximum(values: &Vec<int>) -> (max: int)

Inputs  
    values  – immutable reference to a `Vec<int>` containing arbitrary (possibly negative) integers.

Output  
    max     – an `int` that will be proven to be a maximum element of the vector.

Preconditions (requires)  
1.  values.len() > 0          — the vector must not be empty.  
2.  values.len() < 1000        — its length is strictly less than 1000.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1.  ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < values.len() ∧ values[i] == max  
    (the returned value actually occurs in the vector).  
2.  ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < values.len() ⇒ values[i] ≤ max  
    (every element is less than or equal to the returned value).

Taken together these conditions mean that `max` is one (not necessarily unique) element of `values` that is at least as large as every other element.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required. Any executable body that meets the above contract—e.g. a simple linear scan through the vector—is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original “reverse” problem is essentially a one-step permutation: prove that the output vector is the input slice read backward.  
To increase the difficulty, I introduce variations that

• add index-bounded reversal or pair-wise swapping, forcing the learner to reason about *sub-ranges* and not the whole slice at once;  
• require preservation of more subtle properties (e.g. `capacity`, or a second call restoring the original order), which demands the use of `old(·)` and additional ghost values;  
• insert extra pre-conditions (such as “length is even”) that the prover must carry through; and  
• involve *in-place* mutation, meaning the post-state of the *same* `Vec` has to be related to the pre-state, rather than synthesising a fresh one.

Each proposed spec is self-contained and sufficiently detailed for an automatic Verus-spec generator.

# Output
<<<
Design and verify a function in Verus called `reverse_in_place`.

Signature  
fn reverse_in_place(v: &mut Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• v: a unique, mutable reference to a vector of unbounded mathematical integers (`int`).

Outputs  
• none (unit).

Preconditions (`requires`)  
1. v is not aliased elsewhere (the caller holds the only mutable reference).  
2. true for all other aspects (no length restriction).

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
1. The length of the vector is preserved:  
   old(v.len()) == v.len()  
2. The *capacity* of the vector is preserved:  
   old(v.capacity()) == v.capacity()    (capacity‐preservation is less common and forces reasoning about allocation).  
3. The content is exactly the reverse of the original content:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < v.len() ⇒ old(v[i]) == v[v.len() − 1 − i]  
4. Reversing twice restores the original order:  
   Calling reverse_in_place again immediately after the first call yields  
   v == old(old(v)) (i.e., the vector equals the original snapshot).  
   This premise must be proven within the specification by showing the operation is an involution.

No helper lemmas beyond those found in the Verus standard library may be assumed.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a function in Verus called `reverse_prefix`.

Signature  
fn reverse_prefix(a: &[int], k: int) -> (b: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a: immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers (`int`).  
• k: an integer satisfying 0 ≤ k ≤ a.len().

Outputs  
• b: a newly returned vector (`Vec<int>`).

Preconditions (`requires`)  
1. 0 ≤ k ≤ a.len()    (the prefix length to be reversed).

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
1. b has exactly the same length as a:  
   b.len() == a.len()  
2. Elements with indices < k appear in *reverse* order relative to the first k elements of a:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < k ⇒ a[i] == b[k − 1 − i]  
3. Elements with indices ≥ k are untouched:  
   ∀ i:int • k ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == b[i]  
4. A boundary guarantee: if k == 0 ∨ k == a.len(), then b is respectively identical to a or the full reverse of a.

These properties force the implementer to split the reasoning into two disjoint ranges and to relate them precisely.
>>>

Signature  
fn swap_adjacent_pairs(a: &[int]) -> (b: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a: immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers (`int`).

Outputs

Preconditions (`requires`)

Postconditions (`ensures`)

   b.len() == a.len()  
2. Adjacent elements are swapped pair-wise:

       b[2*j + 1] == a[2*j]  
3. The multiset of elements is identical to that of a (no loss or duplicates).  
4. Performing the operation twice yields the original order (a second call on b produces a vector equal to a), making the learner prove involution on an even-indexed permutation.

Proving correctness now involves modular arithmetic, quantifiers over half the indices, and an extra structural invariant (length must stay even).

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#[allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
#[allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Takes an array and an index, and returns the sequence of elements from the index to the end of the array.
fn to_seq(a: &[int], i: usize) -> (res: Vec<int>)
    requires
        i <= a.len(),
        a.len() < usize::MAX / 2,
    ensures
        res.len() == a.len() - i,
        forall|j: nat| j < res.len() ==> res[j as int] == a[i + j as int],
    decreases
        a.len() - i,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We must describe this function spec in natural language for re-creation.

Name: to_seq.

Inputs:
  • a — immutable slice reference of int (mathematical, unbounded signed integer) values. a.len() returns usize.
  • i — usize index.

Preconditions (requires):
 1. i ≤ a.len().  Index can point anywhere inside or exactly one past the end of the slice, guaranteeing every position from i to a.len()−1 is valid and that a.len()−i is non-negative.
 2. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2.  A technical guard that guarantees (a.len() − i) and each expression i + j fits inside usize during verification, preventing arithmetic overflow in the executable body.

Output:
  • res — Vec<int> (heap-allocated, growable vector of integers).

Postconditions (ensures):
 1. res.len() == a.len() − i.  The resulting vector’s length equals the number of elements from the chosen index to the end of the original slice.
 2. ∀ j:nat • j < res.len() ⇒ res[j] == a[i + j].  Every element of res is an exact copy of the corresponding element of a, starting at offset i.  When i == a.len() the result must be the empty vector, vacuously satisfying the predicate.

Termination metric (decreases): a.len() − i.  The body is expected to be implemented recursively or via a loop whose variant is the number of remaining elements; this annotation convinces the verifier that the function terminates.

No additional helper functions or lemmas are referenced.

Essential behaviour:
to_seq returns a freshly allocated vector that is the “suffix” of the input slice beginning at index i.  In ordinary Rust terms this is equivalent to `a[i..].to_vec()`, except it operates on Verus’ mathematical `int` type and is fully specified with formal pre- and post-conditions.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `to_seq` that produces the suffix of a slice.

Signature  
fn to_seq(a: &[int], i: usize) -> (res: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a : immutable slice (`&[int]`) containing values of type `int` (the unbounded mathematical integer).  
• i : usize — the starting index inside the slice.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. i ≤ a.len() (the index can be anywhere from the beginning of the slice up to, and including, one-past-the-end).  
2. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2 (a technical bound that prevents any of the arithmetic done in the specification or in the body, such as a.len() − i or i + j, from overflowing a usize).

Output  
• res : Vec<int> — a new vector that will contain exactly the elements a[i], a[i+1], …, a[a.len()−1] in that order.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. res.len() == a.len() − i.  
2. ∀ j : nat. j < res.len() ⇒ res[j] == a[i + j].  
   (In other words, for every valid position j in the resulting vector, the element stored there is precisely the element of the original slice located at offset i + j.)

Decreases clause  
decreases a.len() − i  
The termination metric is the number of elements still to be copied; this allows an implementation that uses recursion or a loop verified with this variant.

Behaviour in plain language  
`to_seq` must return a vector that is an exact copy of the portion of the input slice that starts at index i and extends to the slice’s end.  
If i == a.len() the function returns an empty vector.  
If i == 0 the entire slice is copied.  
No other side effects are permitted; the original slice remains unchanged.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are needed beyond what is provided in the prelude.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is straightforward: consume a vector, scan it once, and build a new vector in which every `x` is turned into `y`.  Its proof obligations are all element-wise equalities that follow from a simple map combinator.

To make the task a little harder (but still reasonable for an experienced Verus student) we can:
 • add one or two extra results to be returned, whose correctness must be proven;
 • vary the replacement policy (e.g. “replace **at most** the first *n* matches” or “replace only if a user-supplied predicate holds”) so that the spec needs quantifiers *and* counting;
 • add capacity / in-place constraints, forcing the learner to reason about aliasing or vector capacities in addition to mere contents.

Below are three independent, self-contained problem statements that incorporate these ideas.  Each one is still short enough to be solved with elementary inductive or iterative proofs, yet they demand more sophisticated invariants than the original.



# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus function named `replace_first_n`
that performs an in-order “replace some” transformation on a vector,
limited to the first `n` occurrences of a given value.

Signature  
    fn replace_first_n(v: Vec<i32>, x: i32, y: i32, n: usize)
        -> (v_new: Vec<i32>, replaced: usize)

Meaning of the parameters  
• `v` – the consumed input vector.  
• `x` – value to search for.  
• `y` – value that should overwrite `x`.  
• `n` – a non-negative bound saying *at most how many* matches may be replaced.

Returned values  
• `v_new` – a new vector obtained from `v` after the transformation.  
• `replaced` – the number of positions that were actually modified
  (necessarily `0 ≤ replaced ≤ n`).

Preconditions (requires)  
1. `v.len() < 100_000`.  
2. `n as u64 <= v.len() as u64`.  
   (The bound fits in `usize`, but we make the relation to `v.len()` explicit.)

Postconditions (ensures)  
Let `L = v.len()`.  For every integer index `k` with `0 ≤ k < L`:

A. If `v[k] == x` *and* `k` is **strictly smaller** than the index of the
   `replaced`-th occurrence of `x` in `v`
   (or if there are fewer than `replaced` occurrences overall),
   then `v_new[k] == y`.

B. Otherwise `v_new[k] == v[k]`.

C. Exactly `replaced` indices satisfy condition A.
   Hence the number of modifications equals the returned counter.

D. `replaced ≤ n`.  
E. The relative order of all elements is preserved; i.e. `v_new.len() == L`.

No helper functions need to be exposed in the public interface,
but any inductive helper specs you need may be added privately.
>>>

<<<
Develop a verified generic function called `selective_map`
that applies a user-supplied predicate and mapping to the elements of a vector.

Signature  
    fn selective_map<T>(
        v: Vec<T>,
        pred: spec_fn(&T) -> bool,
        f: spec_fn(&T) -> T
    ) -> (out: Vec<T>)
    where
        T: Copy + PartialEq

Semantics  
For every index `k` in the original vector:

• If `pred(&v[k])` is true, `out[k]` must equal `f(&v[k])`.  
• Otherwise `out[k] == v[k]`.

Additional requirements  
1. `v.len() < 50_000` (smaller limit than the original).  
2. For all `t`, if `pred(&t)` holds then `f(&t)` **does not** satisfy
   `pred(&f(&t))`.  (This prevents repeated self-triggering replacements and
   must be proven from the given functions.)

Postconditions  
a) Element-wise correctness as stated above.  
b) Multiset preservation for the *unaffected* values:
   the multiset of elements `{ v[k] | ¬pred(&v[k]) }`
   equals the multiset `{ out[k] | ¬pred(&v[k]) }`.  
c) `out.len() == v.len()`.

Because higher-order specifications are involved, you will need
to declare the predicate and mapping as `spec_fn` parameters and reason
about them explicitly in the proof.
>>>

<<<
Write a verified **in-place** procedure `replace_in_place_exact`
that mutates a vector without changing its capacity.

Signature  
    fn replace_in_place_exact(v: &mut Vec<i32>, x: i32, y: i32)
        -> (count: usize)

Preconditions  
1. `v.len() <= 1_000_000`.  
2. `v.len() == v.capacity()`  
   (the vector is at full capacity, so no reallocation may occur).

Postconditions  
P1. For all indices `k` with `0 ≤ k < old(v).len()`:

    • If `old(v)[k] == x` then `v[k] == y`.  
    • Otherwise          `v[k] == old(v)[k]`.

P2. `count` equals the number of indices where `old(v)[k] == x`.  
P3. `v.len()` and `v.capacity()` are unchanged from their old values.  
P4. The function performs the transformation **in place**; you may not allocate
    any auxiliary `Vec` of length proportional to `v.len()`, though temporary
    constant-size stacks are allowed and need not be specified in Verus.

Because the vector is passed by mutable reference and must not grow,
the proof must track frame conditions for both the element contents and the
vector’s capacity field.>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Specification function for maximum
spec fn max(x: int, y: int) -> int {
    if x >= y {
        x
    } else {
        y
    }
}

/// Function to find maximum of two numbers
fn max_func(x: int, y: int) -> (r: int)
    requires
        x >= 0,
        y >= 0,
    ensures
        r >= x && r >= y,
        r == x || r == y,
{
```

# Reasoning
The file contains two items that must be understood and possibly re-implemented.

1. `spec fn max`
   • Pure, logical (ghost-only) function, operating on Verus `int`, i.e. the mathematical integers with no overflow.  
   • Signature: takes two `int` parameters `x` and `y`, returns an `int`.  
   • Body: classic conditional returning the larger argument (`x` if `x ≥ y`, otherwise `y`).  
   • It has no additional pre- or post-conditions; the return value is determined exclusively by the conditional.  
   • Because it is a spec function, it will never be executed at run-time; it is purely for reasoning inside the verifier.

2. `fn max_func`
   • Executable (non-ghost) function.  
   • Inputs:
        – `x: int`  
        – `y: int`  
     Both belong to the unbounded `int` domain; however they are restricted by pre-conditions described below.
   • Output: a single value `r: int`.  
   • Preconditions (`requires`):
        – `x ≥ 0` — first argument must be non-negative.  
        – `y ≥ 0` — second argument must also be non-negative.  
     No other assumptions can be used inside the function body.
   • Postconditions (`ensures`):
        1. `r ≥ x && r ≥ y` — the result is greater than or equal to each input.  
        2. `r == x || r == y` — the result is exactly one of the two inputs (not some other number).  
     Taken together, these clauses express that `r` must equal the maximum of `x` and `y`, but stated in a way that is easy for the prover to manipulate.
   • No explicit mention of the helper `max` appears in the contract, so the executable function may implement the comparison any way it likes as long as it satisfies the above logical obligations.

Important subtleties
 • The specification works in mathematical integers, so overflow cannot occur.  
 • The positivity requirements in `requires` are not logically necessary for the concept of a maximum, but they could simplify proof obligations in the eventual implementation and are therefore mandatory.  
 • Because the ensures state that `r` must be either `x` or `y`, implementations that create a fresh integer value (e.g. by adding 0) would violate the contract unless the value is literally one of the two parameters.  
 • An implementation as simple as `if x >= y { x } else { y }` will satisfy the contract, but any equivalent mechanism is permitted.

# Final Description (put the answer between <<< and >>>)
<<<
Design two related items in Verus:

1. A pure specification helper  
   spec fn max(x: int, y: int) -> int  
   • Parameters: x, y (mathematical integers).  
   • Returns: the larger of the two.  
   • Definition:  
        if x ≥ y { x } else { y }  
   • No extra pre- or post-conditions; termination is obvious.

2. An executable, verified function  
   fn max_func(x: int, y: int) -> (r: int)

   Inputs  
     – x: int  
     – y: int  

   Preconditions  
     1. x ≥ 0  
     2. y ≥ 0  

   Output  
     – r: int  

   Postconditions  
     1. r ≥ x ∧ r ≥ y   (the result is at least as large as both inputs)  
     2. r = x ∨ r = y   (the result is exactly one of the inputs)  

   Together these clauses guarantee that r equals the maximum of x and y (restricted to non-negative values by the preconditions).

No other ghost code, lemmas or attributes are required. Any body that produces one of the inputs that is not smaller than the other—e.g.  
   if x >= y { x } else { y }  
—will satisfy the specification and verify.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise only asks the learner to restate the familiar associativity law A∪(B∪C) = (A∪B)∪C for plain sets of integers.  To raise the difficulty slightly (without making it overwhelming) I will:

• Keep the general two-layer structure (pure spec helper + executable wrapper).  
• Introduce one or two extra logical properties that must also be verified, so that the learner has to write additional `ensures` clauses or helper lemmas.  
• Generalise from three concrete sets to either a list/sequence of sets or to multisets, forcing induction or additional reasoning.  
• Occasionally add a simple pre-condition (e.g. non-empty sequence) so the learner must carry it through to the post-conditions.

I will supply three standalone upgraded tasks:

1.  “Fold-left vs fold-right” associativity for a non-empty sequence of sets, plus an equality on resulting cardinalities.  
2.  Associativity of multiset (bag) union, together with an element-wise multiplicity equation.  
3.  Associativity of set union on generic relation sets (pairs) together with preservation of symmetry.

Each description contains everything needed for a spec generator to reconstruct the Verus code.

# Output
<<<
Create a pair of functions in Verus that formalise and expose the associativity of set union when the union is taken over an entire *non-empty* finite sequence of sets, rather than just three arguments.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn fold_union_left_equals_right(xs: Seq<Set<int>>) -> bool  
   Definitions and notation:  
     • Let “⌀” denote the empty set.  
     • Define foldl(⌀, xs) recursively as:  
         – If xs.len() == 0 then return ⌀.  
         – Else return foldl(⌀, xs.drop_last()) ∪ xs.last().  
     • Define foldr(⌀, xs) recursively as:  
         – If xs.len() == 0 then return ⌀.  
         – Else return xs.first() ∪ foldr(⌀, xs.drop_first()).  
     • The helper returns true exactly when foldl(⌀, xs) == foldr(⌀, xs) *and*  
       |foldl(⌀, xs)| == |foldr(⌀, xs)|  (cardinality equality is redundant but must be stated).  

2. Executable, verified wrapper  
   fn fold_union_left_equals_right_func(xs: Seq<Set<int>>) -> (result: bool)

   Inputs  
     • xs: Seq<Set<int>>

   Output  
     • result: bool

   Preconditions (requires)  
     • xs.len() > 0    // sequence must be non-empty

   Postconditions (ensures)  
     • result == fold_union_left_equals_right(xs)

The learner must implement any additional helper lemmas necessary to define `foldl` and `foldr` in the spec context and to relate their cardinalities, then write the executable wrapper whose post-condition exactly mirrors the helper.
>>>

<<<
Create a trio of functions in Verus that expose the associativity of *multiset* (bag) union on integer elements and simultaneously state the element-wise multiplicity equation.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn multiset_union_is_associative(a: Multiset<int>, b: Multiset<int>, c: Multiset<int>) -> bool  
   Definition:  
     return ((a + b) + c) == (a + (b + c))  
            && (forall x: int :: ((a + b) + c).count(x) == a.count(x) + b.count(x) + c.count(x));

2. Pure helper proving the multiplicity equality alone  
   spec fn multiset_union_count_formula(a: Multiset<int>, b: Multiset<int>, c: Multiset<int>, x: int) -> bool  
   Definition:  
     return ((a + b) + c).count(x) == a.count(x) + b.count(x) + c.count(x);

3. Executable, verified wrapper  
   fn multiset_union_is_associative_func(a: Multiset<int>, b: Multiset<int>, c: Multiset<int>) -> (result: bool)

   Inputs  
     • a, b, c: Multiset<int>

   Output  
     • result: bool

   Preconditions  
     • none

   Postconditions  
     • result == multiset_union_is_associative(a, b, c)

All auxiliary lemmas (such as proving that `+` on multisets adds multiplicities) must be provided in the specification layer so the wrapper can be verified.
>>>

<<<
Create two functions in Verus that demonstrate that set union on *relations* (sets of ordered pairs) is associative and that it preserves symmetry.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn relation_union_properties<T>(r1: Set<(T,T)>, r2: Set<(T,T)>, r3: Set<(T,T)>) -> bool  
     requires	t:		T:		// T is any type with builtin equality  
   Definitions:  
     • let left  = (r1 + r2) + r3;  
     • let right = r1 + (r2 + r3);  
     • A relation s is symmetric iff ∀(x,y) ∈ s ⇒ (y,x) ∈ s.

──────────────────────────
Helper specification functions

1. `spec fn is_upper_case(c: char) -> bool`  
   Returns `true` exactly when the ASCII code of `c` is between 65 and 90 inclusive (the characters ‘A’..‘Z’).  
   Definition: `65 <= c as int && c as int <= 90`.

2. `spec fn is_upper_lower_pair(c: char, d: char) -> bool`  
   Returns `true` when `d` is the character whose ASCII code is **32 larger** than that of `c`.  
   Definition: `(c as int) == (d as int) - 32`.  
   Consequently, when `c` is an uppercase letter, `d` is the corresponding lowercase letter.

3. `spec fn shift_32(c: char) -> char`  
   Pure function that produces the character `(c + 32) mod 128`.  
   Definition: `((c as int + 32) % 128) as char`.  
   For uppercase ASCII letters this yields the lowercase counterpart.

──────────────────────────
Primary executable function
──────────────────────────
```rust
fn to_lower_case(s: &Vec<char>) -> (v: Vec<char>)
```

Inputs  
  • `s : &Vec<char>` – an immutable reference to a (non-empty) vector of characters.

Precondition (`requires`)  
  • `s.len() > 0` – the input vector must contain at least one element.

Output  
  • `v : Vec<char>` – a new vector produced by the function.

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
 1. `v.len() == s.len()` – the resulting vector has the same length as the input.  
 2. For every valid index `i` (0 ≤ i < s.len()):  
      • If `is_upper_case(s[i])` holds, then `is_upper_lower_pair(s[i], v[i])` must hold – i.e. `v[i]` equals `s[i]` shifted by 32 (the lowercase version).  
      • Otherwise `v[i] == s[i]` – characters that are not uppercase ASCII letters are copied unchanged.

In essence, `to_lower_case` produces a lowercase copy of its input string (regarded as a vector of `char`), performing the classic ASCII case conversion on uppercase letters while leaving all other bytes intact, and preserving the original length.

No additional pre- or post-conditions are required. Any executable body that satisfies the above specification—and that may optionally use `shift_32` in its reasoning—is acceptable.

use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;

impl Benchmark2 {
    fn binary_search(&self, a: &[int], key: int) -> (result: i32)
        requires
            a.len() > 0,
            a.len() < i32::MAX as usize,
            forall|i: int, j: int| 0 <= i < j && j < a.len() as int ==> a[i] <= a[j],
        ensures
            -1 <= result && result < a.len() as i32,
            0 <= result ==> a[result as int] == key,
            result == -1 ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < a.len() as int ==> a[i] != key,
    {
```

# Reasoning
The snippet defines the complete logical contract—pre- and post-conditions—of a method Benchmark2::binary_search.

1. Signature and ownership
   • It is an immutable (&self) method; self carries no information and is not mutated.  
   • Parameter a: `&[int]` – an immutable slice of mathematical integers (unbounded `int`, not `i32`).  
   • Parameter key: `int` – the value that we are looking for in `a`.  
   • Return value result: `i32`.

2. Preconditions (requires)
   • `a.len() > 0` – the slice must be non-empty.  
   • `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize` – its length must be strictly less than 2 147 483 647 so that any valid index fits into an `i32`.  
   • Sortedness: `∀ i,j. 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j]`.  The slice is monotonically non-decreasing.

   No hypotheses are made about duplicates or about the relationship between `key` and the elements; the algorithm must work for any integer key.

3. Postconditions (ensures)
   • Bounds: `-1 ≤ result < a.len() as i32`.  Therefore the method either returns ‑1 or a legitimate index in the half-open range [0, len).  
   • Success case: `0 ≤ result ⇒ a[result] == key`.  If the method delivers a non-negative index, that index points to an occurrence of `key`.  
   • Failure case: `result == -1 ⇒ ∀ i: 0 ≤ i < len ⇒ a[i] ≠ key`.  If the method reports ‑1, it is a formal proof that `key` is absent from the slice.

   No minimality/first-occurrence constraint is imposed: when multiple copies of `key` exist, any one of them may be returned.

4. Observations
   • The postconditions constitute a complete specification of a classical binary-search: total, deterministic (it must always return a value satisfying the contract), and with a conventional “not found ⇒ -1” convention.  
   • All arithmetic inside the spec (`int`) is unbounded; nevertheless the precondition `len < i32::MAX` ensures that casting the chosen index to `i32` is safe in the implementation.  
   • The contract leaves the algorithmic strategy and complexity completely open; only the functional behaviour is fixed.

fn binary_search(&self, a: &[int], key: int) -> i32

• `a` – an immutable slice (runtime read-only array) whose elements are mathematical integers (`int`).  
• `key` – the integer value being searched for in `a`.

Returned value  
An `i32` named `result`.  The method uses the following convention:

    – If `key` occurs in the slice, `result` is an index (0-based) pointing to a position where `a[result] == key`.  
    – If `key` is not present, `result` is ‑1.

Formal preconditions (`requires`) that callers must satisfy  
1. `a.len() > 0` – the slice is not empty.  
2. `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize` – its length is strictly below 2 147 483 647, guaranteeing that any valid index fits in an `i32`.  
3. Sortedness: for every pair of indices i < j inside the slice, `a[i] ≤ a[j]`.  The slice is therefore sorted in non-decreasing order.

Formal postconditions (`ensures`) that the implementation must prove  
1. Range guarantee: `-1 ≤ result < a.len() as i32`.  
2. Success correctness: if `result ≥ 0`, then `a[result] == key`.  
3. Failure correctness: if `result == -1`, then for all indices i in `[0, a.len())` we have `a[i] ≠ key`.

Notes and subtleties  
• When several occurrences of `key` exist, the specification does not constrain which one is returned; any correct index is acceptable.  
• The method must never panic or loop forever: for every caller that satisfies the preconditions, it is obliged to return a value fulfilling the postconditions.  
• The contract makes no performance claim—the implementation may or may not use an actual binary-search algorithm—as long as the functional behaviour is satisfied.  
• Because the elements of `a` are of type `int` (unbounded), overflow cannot happen while comparing them, but the index that is returned must fit into `i32`; the precondition on the length ensures this.

The original task asks the learner to verify a very small “ASCII‐to‐lowercase” function.  
It involves a single forward traversal, a single simple case-split, and no global
properties beyond “length is preserved”.  To make the assignment more challenging I
proposed three independent replacements that each introduce at least one of the
following difficulties:

 •  A global, quantified property (e.g. “no uppercase letters remain” or “result
    equals the reverse of the input”).
 •  A numerical summary value that has to be updated consistently (a counter).
 •  Index–range reasoning that forces the prover to distinguish several disjoint
    regions of the vector.

All three variants keep the familiar helper specs so the focus is still on data-
dependent reasoning rather than on unrelated arithmetic tricks.  Each description
is self-contained and gives enough detail for an automatic spec generator to

The original `get_even` specification is straightforward: it performs a per-element parity fix with no auxiliary information to track.  
To increase difficulty, I introduce new outputs and global-style invariants that force the learner to reason about whole-vector properties such as (1) counting how many elements were changed, (2) maintaining aggregate sums, and (3) producing an additional structure (e.g., a prefix-sum array).  
All proposed variants keep the core “turn odd into even” flavour, so they remain recognisably related to the original task, but each variant requires additional reasoning steps, quantified properties, or algebraic facts that make the verification substantially richer.

Three harder, yet still self-contained, standalone problems are given below.

Inputs

Outputs  
    • result — a newly created vector whose elements are all even.  
    • odd_count — the number of positions in the input that were odd.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. s.len() > 0                                             // the input vector is non-empty  
    2. s.len() ≤ 1000                                          // its length does not exceed 1000  
    3. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] < u64::MAX / 2          // each element is below half the u64 range  
       (guarantees that adding 1 to any odd element cannot overflow)

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. result.len() == s.len()                                 // length is preserved  
    2. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒                              // element-wise correspondence  
           if s[i] % 2 == 1                                    //   – when the original element is odd  
              then result[i] == s[i] + 1                       //       incremented to become even  
              else result[i] == s[i]                           //   – otherwise left unchanged and already even  
    3. odd_count == |{ i:int | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] % 2 == 1 }|  
       // odd_count exactly equals the number of odd indices in the original vector
    4. Σ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() → result[i]                    // the sum of all result elements
       == Σ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() → s[i]  +  (odd_count as u64)  
       // the total value grows by precisely one per corrected odd element

    2. s.len() ≤ 1000  
    3. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] < u64::MAX / 4

       // 1000 elements still fits into a u64 even after each odd increment.

Postconditions (ensures)

    2. prefix_sum.len() == s.len()                              // prefix_sum mirrors the size  
    3. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒                               // per-element evenisation

              then evens[i] == s[i] + 1  
              else evens[i] == s[i]  
    4. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒                               // prefix-sum definition  
           prefix_sum[i] == Σ j:int. 0 ≤ j ≤ i → evens[j]  
    5. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() − 1 ⇒ prefix_sum[i] < prefix_sum[i + 1]  
       // every cumulative sum is strictly increasing

/// Finds the maximum value in an array.
fn max(a: &[u64]) -> (m: u64)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        forall|i: nat| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> a[i as int] <= m,

{
```

# Reasoning
The specification introduces a single executable function called `max`.

1. Inputs and their types  
   • `a`: `&[u64]` – an immutable slice of 64-bit unsigned integers.  
   The slice can have any positive length; zero‐length slices are ruled out by the precondition.

2. Output and its type  
   • `m`: `u64` – the value the function returns.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` – the caller must pass a non-empty slice. No other assumptions about the contents are made (values may repeat, be in any order, etc.).

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   The result `m` must satisfy two logical facts expressed with mathematical quantifiers that range over *nat* (non-negative mathematical integers).  
   • Universal bound:  
     `forall |i:nat| 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ m`  
     Meaning: every element stored in the slice is less than or equal to `m`; i.e., `m` is an upper bound for all elements.  
   • Witness of attainability:  
     `exists |i:nat| 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ m == a[i]`  
     Meaning: there exists at least one (possibly several) index in the slice for which the element equals `m`. Together with the first postcondition this forces `m` to be exactly the maximum element of the slice, not merely an arbitrary upper bound.

5. Auxiliary items  
   No helper spec functions, lemmas, or additional attributes are referenced. The body may be implemented however the developer chooses (single pass, fold, etc.) as long as it provably meets the contracts.

6. Subtleties  
   • Indexes inside the quantifiers are of type `nat`; when accessing the runtime slice they are cast to `int` with `as int` because Verus quantifiers work over mathematical integers.  
   • No overflow issues exist: `m` is computed from existing `u64` values and returned as `u64`.  
   • The precondition is minimal; even slices with all identical values are allowed – in that situation any of those identical values must be returned.

# Final Description

Inputs

Output

Contract  
Precondition

Postconditions  
    1. ∀ i:nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ m  
       (the returned value is an upper bound for every element).  
    2. ∃ i:nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ m == a[i]  
       (the upper bound is actually attained by some element in the slice).  

Together these clauses specify that `m` is exactly the maximum element contained in `a`.

No additional helper spec functions or lemmas are required; any verified implementation that satisfies the above contract is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise only asks the learner to prove an “all–elements < n” predicate and to return a matching Boolean.  
We can raise the difficulty in three different ways.

1. Return a *witness* when the property is false.  
   Besides proving equivalence, the implementation must also prove that the reported
   index is the *first* place where the predicate fails.  This introduces an extra
   existential/uniqueness proof obligation.

2. Ask for the position where a value `n` could be inserted into a *strictly
   increasing* slice while maintaining order.  Now the learner has to reason about
   two universally-quantified regions (before and after the returned index) and must
   understand how strict ordering interacts with the returned boundary.

3. Replace the “greater-than-every-element” requirement by the less common
   “greater-than-the-arithmetic-average” requirement.  The programmer must define a
   `sum` specification function, reason about multiplication with `len`, and prove
   the Boolean equivalence.  This adds arithmetic reasoning and an extra loop to
   compute the total.

All three variants remain fully executable and do not rely on external helpers
other than the ones explicitly described in the problem statements.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Function to find the maximum length list in a sequence of lists
fn max_length_list(lists: Vec<Vec<int>>) -> (max_list: Vec<int>)
    requires
        lists.len() > 0,
    ensures
        forall|l: int| 0 <= l && l < lists.len() ==> lists[l as int].len() <= max_list.len(),
        max_list == lists[0] || (exists|l: int| 1 <= l && l < lists.len() ==> max_list == lists[l as int]),
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification is for a pure (side-effect–free) function called `max_length_list`.

Inputs  
• `lists`: `Vec<Vec<int>>` – a run-time vector whose elements are themselves run-time vectors of the mathematical integer type `int`.  
The vector is passed by value, so the function gets ownership of it.

Output  
• `max_list`: `Vec<int>` – one of the inner vectors taken from `lists`.

Pre-condition  
• `lists.len() > 0` – the outer vector must contain at least one inner list, otherwise a “maximum” would be undefined.

Post-conditions  
1. `forall|l: int| 0 <= l && l < lists.len() ==> lists[l].len() <= max_list.len()`  
   Every list stored in `lists` has length *no greater* than `max_list.len()`. Hence `max_list` is guaranteed to reach the maximum length found in the collection.

2. `max_list == lists[0] || (exists|l: int| 1 <= l && l < lists.len() ==> max_list == lists[l])`  
   The value returned is *exactly* one of the original inner vectors: either the first element or some later element. Combined with the first clause this means the result is an element of the input that attains the maximal length, but when several lists tie for that maximum the function may choose any of them.

Nothing else is constrained: ties may be resolved arbitrarily, element contents are irrelevant, and no additional helper lemmas or spec functions are referenced.

The implementer therefore has to:
• Accept a non-empty `Vec<Vec<int>>`.  
• Locate any inner vector whose length is greater than or equal to every other inner vector’s length.  
• Return that very vector (by value), satisfying both post-conditions.  
Because `int` is an unbounded mathematical type, the lengths in the specification are also unbounded, so overflow is not a concern at the spec level.

# Final Description (put your answer between the tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Construct a verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    max_length_list

Signature  
    fn max_length_list(lists: Vec<Vec<int>>) -> (max_list: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    lists – a run-time vector whose elements are vectors of `int`.

Pre-condition (requires)  
    • lists.len() > 0  
      The outer vector must be non-empty.

Output  
    max_list – one of the inner vectors.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. For every valid index l (0 ≤ l < lists.len()), the length of the list at that index is at most the length of max_list:  
         ∀ l:int. 0 ≤ l < lists.len() ⇒ lists[l].len() ≤ max_list.len()

    2. The returned vector is an element drawn from the original collection:  
         max_list == lists[0] ∨ (∃ l:int. 1 ≤ l < lists.len() ∧ max_list == lists[l])

Interpretation  
    – max_list achieves the maximum length among all lists provided.  
    – If several lists share that maximum length, any one of them may be returned.  
    – No other side effects or invariants are required.

Any implementation that, given a non-empty collection of lists, returns one of its members whose length is at least as large as every other member’s length will satisfy the specification.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original task only asks the learner to prove that the returned vector is the *reverse* of the input.  
That requires a single bijective-index argument.  
To raise the difficulty we can  
 • keep the domain (Vec<i32>) so the learner’s Rust background still helps,  
 • introduce *two* or more parameters, so the proof has to relate several vectors,  
 • add richer post-conditions that talk about *length*, *permutation*, and *specific positional mapping* instead of a single “mirror” condition,  
 • require total as well as modular arithmetic reasoning, or an alternation pattern, etc.

Three tougher but still self-contained variants therefore suggest themselves:

1. Cyclic rotation by an arbitrary, non-trivial offset k.  
   The learner must prove both the modular-arithmetic index calculation and that the output is a *permutation* (length preserved and every element appears exactly once).

2. Alternating interleave of two equal-length arrays.  
   The learner must prove the shape (even indices from first vector, odd indices from second), the length relationship (doubling), and a *disjoint union* permutation property.

3. Prefix-sum production where every entry j of the result equals the sum of the first j + 1 inputs.  
   This demands an inductive argument over indices and arithmetic reasoning about sums.

Each problem is still expressible with standard Verus quantifiers and `ensures` clauses, but needs more lines of specification and a multi-step proof, not a single mirror argument.

# Output
<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function called rotate_right that performs a *cyclic* right-rotation of the elements of a vector.

Signature  
    fn rotate_right(a: Vec<i32>, k: usize) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    • a: Vec<i32> — ownership of a non-empty vector of 32-bit signed integers.  
    • k: usize     — the rotation distance.

Outputs  
    • result: Vec<i32> — the vector obtained by moving the last k elements of a to the front while shifting the remaining elements to the right.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    • a.len() > 0.  
    • k > 0.  
    • k < a.len()      (the rotation distance is strictly between 0 and the length).  

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    • result.len() == a.len().  
    • (Permutation) The multiset of elements in result is identical to the multiset of elements in a.  
    • (Positional mapping) For every index i with 0 ≤ i < a.len(),  
          result[(i + k) % a.len()] == a[i].

Interpretation  
    The function must return a vector whose element at index j equals the element that was at index (j − k) mod a.len() in the original array.  
    A fresh vector may be allocated, or the argument may be modified in-place and returned.

No auxiliary lemmas beyond what is necessary to express the permutation property are mandated, but the proof must establish all three post-conditions.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function called interleave_equal that alternately merges two vectors of equal length.

Signature  
    fn interleave_equal(x: Vec<i32>, y: Vec<i32>) -> (z: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    • x: Vec<i32> — ownership of a vector of 32-bit signed integers.  
    • y: Vec<i32> — ownership of a second vector of 32-bit signed integers.

Outputs  
    • z: Vec<i32> — a vector whose even indices come from x and odd indices from y.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    • x.len() == y.len() (> 0).          // Equality and non-emptiness in one statement.  

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    • z.len() == 2 * x.len().  
    • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < x.len() ⇒ z[2 * i]     == x[i].  
    • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < y.len() ⇒ z[2 * i + 1] == y[i].  
    • (Disjoint-union permutation) The multiset of elements of z equals the disjoint union of the multisets of x and y, i.e. every value occurs in z exactly as many times as in x plus y.

Interpretation  
    Starting with i = 0, the function must place x[0], y[0], x[1], y[1], … into the output.  
    Implementations may allocate a new vector or fill an existing one, but must prove all positional and permutation properties.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function called prefix_sums that returns the running totals of an input vector.

Signature  
    fn prefix_sums(a: Vec<i32>) -> (sums: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    • a: Vec<i32> — ownership of a non-empty vector of 32-bit signed integers.

Outputs  
    • sums: Vec<i32> — a vector of the same length containing cumulative sums.

Pre-condition (requires)  
    • a.len() > 0.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    • sums.len() == a.len().  
    • sums[0] == a[0].  
    • ∀ j:int • 1 ≤ j < a.len() ⇒ sums[j] == sums[j − 1] + a[j].  
      (Equivalently: sums[j] equals the sum of a[0] … a[j]).

Interpretation  
    The j-th entry of sums is the total of all elements of a with index not exceeding j.  
    The optional second quantifier-style clause makes the arithmetic obligation explicit and may require an auxiliary lemma about finite sums to discharge the proof.

    recommends

    requires

        sorted(a@),
        a.len() < i32::MAX as usize,
    ensures

# Reasoning
The original exercise is deliberately trivial:  
• the domain of `n` is reduced to the single value `0`;  
• the post-condition is a simple arithmetic identity;  
• no auxiliary specifications or deeper mathematical facts are required.  

To make the task more interesting while still keeping it suitable for a beginner in Verus, we can:

1. Remove the strange “`u64::MAX / u64::MAX`” trick so that many inputs are now permitted.  
2. Require the real Fibonacci number (or a closely related value) instead of the triangular number.  That already adds a classical inductive property that the student must prove.  
3. Add one or two less common but still manageable constraints, e.g.  
   – the function must also return `fib(n+1)` so that the caller can reuse it;  
   – the result must fit into `u128`, which forces an explicit numeric upper bound on `n`;  
   – a small logical property such as `fib(n) >= n` that must be ensured and justified;  
   – the implementation must be *iterative* (no recursion), which can be stated as a hint in the spec and then proven with a loop invariant.  

Below are three alternative, standalone problem statements that incorporate these ideas and therefore require more reasoning steps (induction or loop invariants), a slightly richer arithmetic library, and possibly a helper lemma or ghost code.  Each description is self-contained so a spec generator can work directly from it.

# Output
<<<
Design a verified Verus/Rust function that satisfies the exact specification below.

Function name: `compute_fib_pair`

Inputs  
• `n: u64`

Outputs  
• `f_n : u128` – the n-th Fibonacci number, with the convention `F(0)=0, F(1)=1`.  
• `f_next : u128` – the (n+1)-st Fibonacci number.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `n < 186`  
   (Reason: `F(186) = 3311648143516982017180081`, the largest index whose Fibonacci value is ≤ `u128::MAX`.)  
2. The implementation must be *iterative* (no self-recursive calls) and may use at most `n + 2` arithmetic operations on `u128`.  This operational restriction must be expressed with a ghost counter that the student introduces and proves is bounded.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. `f_n == fib(n)` and `f_next == fib(n + 1)`, where `fib` is the mathematical Fibonacci function defined by  
   `fib(0)=0`, `fib(1)=1`, `fib(k+2)=fib(k)+fib(k+1)` for all `k`.  
2. `f_n + f_next == fib(n + 2)`.  
3. `f_n <= f_next` and `f_n >= n`   (these two inequalities are true for every `n ≥ 0` and must be proven).  
4. The ghost counter described in the pre-condition does not exceed `n + 2`.

No other outputs or side-effects are permitted.  The student may introduce helper lemmas (e.g. to prove the monotonic and lower-bound properties) but the public contract above completely characterises the routine.
>>>

<<<
Design a verified Verus/Rust function with the specification below.

Function: `range_triangular_sum`

Inputs  
• `a: u64`  
• `b: u64`

Output  
• `s: u128`

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `a <= b`.  
2. `b ≤ 2_000_000`.  
3. `b - a ≤ 1_000_000`   (the length of the range is at most one million).  

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. `s == (b - a + 1) * (a + b) / 2`  – i.e. `s` equals the sum of the arithmetic series `a + (a+1) + … + b`.  
2. `s ≤ 2_000_000 * 2_000_001 / 2`  (fits easily into `u128`; this is implied by #1 but must still be shown).  
3. If `a = 0`, then `s` coincides with the classical triangular number `b*(b+1)/2`.  
4. The implementation must use a single constant-time arithmetic formula, not an explicit loop that iterates through the range.  This *no-loop* requirement must be captured in the spec by forbidding ghost counters or state that would count iterations.

No auxiliary state is allowed.  The above contract fully determines the function.
>>>

<<<
Design a verified Verus/Rust function according to the following specification.

Function: `binom_mod_prime`

Inputs  
• `n: u64`  
• `k: u64`  
• `p: u64` – a prime modulus

Output  
• `c: u64`

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `p` is prime and `2 ≤ p ≤ 2^31 − 1`.  
2. `k ≤ n < p`   (so that every factorial up to `n` is non-zero modulo `p`).  
3. The implementation must compute the result in time `O(log p)` using Fermat’s little theorem and modular inverses; this must be witnessed by a ghost variable that counts modular-multiplication steps and is proven ≤ `6 * log2(p)`.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. `c == (n choose k) mod p` where  
      `(n choose k) = n! / (k! * (n−k)!)`  over ℤ.  
2. `0 ≤ c < p`.  
3. Symmetry: if `k = 0` or `k = n`, then `c == 1`.  
4. Mirror: `c == binom_mod_prime(n, n-k, p)` – this follows from the mathematics and must be proven.  
5. The ghost step counter mentioned in the pre-condition is ≤ `6 * log2(p)`.

The student may add helper ghost functions to compute modular inverses, as well as lemmas establishing primality implies invertibility, but the public contract above must be met exactly and nothing else may be observable to callers.
>>>

Detailed contract  

Inputs

Output

Pre-condition  
  • `a.len() > 0` – the input vector must be non-empty.

Post-conditions  
  1. `result.len() == a.len()` – the returned vector has exactly the same length as the input.

No additional helper specifications or lemmas are required.

# Reasoning
The original exercise asks the learner to write and verify a very small, *by-value* vector-reversal function.  
To make the task more interesting we can:

1. Require **in-place** reversal (aliasing & mutability reasoning, no fresh vector).
2. Add a second numeric parameter and demand reversal of **fixed-size blocks** only when the length is an exact multiple of that size, forcing nested‐index arithmetic in the spec.
3. Couple the reversal with an **aggregate property**—computing and returning the arithmetic sum of the elements—so the implementation must prove both a structural and a numeric post-condition.

Each new problem is worded so that a spec generator can write the complete Verus pre-/post-conditions from the English text alone.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified function in Verus called `reverse_in_place`.

Signature  
    fn reverse_in_place(v: &mut Vec<i32>)

Detailed contract  

Inputs  
  • `v`: a mutable reference to a `Vec<i32>` whose ownership is *not* consumed.

Outputs  
  • none – the function mutates `v` directly.

Pre-conditions  
  1. `v.len() > 1` – the vector must contain at least two elements so that reversal is observable.

Post-conditions  
  1. `v.len()` is **unchanged** – the number of elements after the call equals its length before the call.  
  2. ∀ `i : int` • 0 ≤ i < `old(v.len())` ⇒ `v[i] == old(v[old(v.len()) − 1 − i])` – every element now stored at position `i` equals the element that formerly occupied the mirror position.  
  3. `v.capacity() == old(v.capacity())` – the operation must not change the underlying buffer capacity, so only `O(1)` additional stack memory may be used.  

No additional helper specifications or lemmas are required.  Any body that performs an in-place reversal, allocates no heap memory, and is accepted by the Verus verifier satisfies this specification.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified function in Verus called `reverse_every_k`.

Signature  
    fn reverse_every_k(a: Vec<i32>, k: usize) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Detailed contract  

Inputs  
  • `a`: a `Vec<i32>` taken by value.  
  • `k`: a block size strictly greater than one.

Outputs  
  • `result`: a new `Vec<i32>`.

Pre-conditions  
  1. `a.len() > 0`.  
  2. `1 < k && k <= a.len()` – the block size is meaningful and no larger than the vector.  
  3. `a.len() % k == 0` – the length of `a` is an *exact multiple* of `k`.

Post-conditions  
  1. `result.len() == a.len()`.  
  2. ∀ `block : int`, ∀ `i : int` •  
        0 ≤ block < a.len() / k ∧ 0 ≤ i < k  
        ⇒ `result[block * k + i] == a[block * k + (k − 1 − i)]` – within every contiguous block of length `k`, the elements appear in reverse order, while blocks themselves remain in their original left-to-right sequence.  

No additional helper specifications or lemmas are required.  An implementation may allocate a fresh vector, reverse each block individually, and return it.
>>>

Pre-condition

Post-conditions

  2. ∀ `i : int` • 0 ≤ i < result.len() ⇒ `result[i] == a[a.len() − 1 − i]`.  
  3. `total == Σ (i : int) • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i]` – the second component returned equals the sum of the original elements, represented exactly as a signed 64-bit integer.

Any body that consumes `a`, produces `result` and `total` satisfying these guarantees, and is accepted by the Verus verifier, satisfies this specification.

The original exercise is *trivial* because requirement 3 (“`n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX`”, i.e. `n < 1`) forces the only legal input to be `n = 0`.  
To make the task more interesting I propose three alternative problems that

• keep the same spirit (closed-form arithmetic sums, combinatorics, or powers of two) so that no heavy algorithmic work is needed,  
• still fit naturally in Verus specs,  
• require strictly more reasoning: extra parameters, symmetry arguments, monotonicity or bit-level properties, and sharper overflow bounds.

Each problem description below is **stand-alone** and contains every detail a spec generator needs: exact signature, precise mathematical meaning, pre- and post-conditions, overflow limits, and the auxiliary facts that must be proven.

Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `sum_range`.

Signature  
    fn sum_range(m: u64, n: u64) -> (s: u128)

Inputs  
    m: u64 — lower bound of the range, inclusive.  
    n: u64 — upper bound of the range, inclusive.

Output  
    s: u128 — the arithmetic sum of all integers from m through n.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
    1. m ≤ n                                                       // non-empty range  
    2. n ≤ 1_000_000                                               // practical bound  
    3. (n as u128) * (n + 1) / 2 < u128::MAX                       // triangular(n) fits  
    4. (m as u128) * (m - 1) / 2 < u128::MAX                       // triangular(m-1) fits  
    5. ( (n as u128)*(n+1) - (m as u128)*(m-1) ) / 2 < u128::MAX   // the desired sum fits

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
    a. s == Σ_{i = m}^{n} i                                        // direct definition  
    b. s == ( n*(n+1) - (m-1)*m ) / 2                              // closed-form formula  
    c. if m < n { s ≥ n }                                          // monotonicity sanity check

No helper lemmas are strictly required, but a short ghost proof that
   Σ_{i=m}^{n} i = triangular(n) – triangular(m-1)
is expected.  Any executable body meeting the contract will be accepted by the verifier.

    3. k == 0 ∨ k ≤ n/2                                            // caller must pass the *smaller* side for symmetry  
    4. binomial(n, k) < u128::MAX                                  // explicit overflow guard

Post-conditions (`ensures`)

    b. c == binomial(n, n-k)                                       // symmetry property  
    c. c ≤ 2^n                                                     // coarse upper bound, must be proven  
    d. if k = 0 ∨ k = n { c == 1 }                                 // edge cases handled

Here `binomial` is the usual combinatorial definition

We examine the specification of `search_recursive`.

Inputs

Output  
• `k: i32` – the index at which `x` is found, or `-1` when `x` does not appear in the chosen slice.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. `0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ a.len()` – the slice `[i, j)` is well-formed and lies inside the vector.  
2. `j - i < 100 000` – an explicit bound that guarantees the slice has fewer than 100 000 elements; used so that the recursive algorithm can be proved to terminate with the `decreases j-i` annotation.  
3. `j ≤ i32::MAX as usize` – every legal index of the slice fits in an `i32`, which is necessary because the function returns an `i32` index.

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
• `i ≤ k < j ∨ k = −1` – the returned index is either inside the search interval or the sentinel `−1`.  
• If `k ≠ −1`  
  – `a[k] == x` – the element at position `k` is exactly the target.  
  – `∀ r:nat • k < r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ x` – no later position in the interval contains `x`; therefore `k` is the right-most (last) occurrence of `x` in `[i, j)`.  
• If `k = −1`  
  – `∀ r:nat • i ≤ r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ x` – the whole slice is free of `x`.

Termination proof  
`decreases j - i` shows that every recursive call must be made on a strictly shorter slice, ensuring that the recursion finishes.

Informally: the function recursively scans the slice `[i, j)` from right to left. It either returns the greatest index in that range that holds `x`, or `-1` if `x` is absent.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Design a formally verified Verus/Rust function named `search_recursive` that performs a recursive search for a value in a slice of a vector and returns the index of the last occurrence within that slice, or `-1` when the value is missing.

Signature  
```
fn search_recursive(a: &Vec<i32>, i: usize, j: usize, x: i32) -> (k: i32)
```

Meaning of the parameters  
• `a` – immutable reference to a dynamic vector of `i32`.  
• `i` – inclusive start index of the search interval.  
• `j` – exclusive end index of the interval (`[i, j)`).  
• `x` – the integer value to look for.

Return value  
`k` – an `i32`.  
  – If `x` occurs in the interval `[i, j)`, `k` equals the greatest index in that interval holding `x`.  
  – If `x` does not occur, `k` is `-1`.

Preconditions (`requires`)  
1. `0 ≤ i ≤ j ≤ a.len()` – the slice limits are valid.  
2. `j - i < 100_000` – the slice length is under 100 000 (needed for the recursive termination argument).  
3. `j ≤ i32::MAX as usize` – every possible index in the slice fits into an `i32` so it can be returned.

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
1. `i ≤ k < j  ∨  k == -1` – result is either inside the interval or the sentinel `-1`.  
2. If `k ≠ -1`:  
   a. `a[k] == x` – the index indeed contains `x`.  
   b. `∀ r:nat • k < r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ x` – there is no occurrence of `x` at any position strictly greater than `k` but still less than `j`. In other words, `k` is the last (right-most) appearance of `x` in the interval.  
3. If `k == -1`:  
   `∀ r:nat • i ≤ r < j ⇒ a[r] ≠ x` – `x` does not appear anywhere in `[i, j)`.

Termination measure  
`decreases j - i` – every recursive call must shrink the interval, guaranteeing eventual termination.

No additional helper functions or lemmas are required; any implementation that satisfies the above contract—typically a right-to-left recursive scan—is acceptable.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: element-wise `+1` on a non-empty vector with no risk of overflow.  
To make the exercise more challenging while still remaining suitable for a first-order logic verifier such as Verus, I introduce:

• Extra logical properties that must be proven (sortedness, set equivalence, prefix sums, rotation mapping).  
• Subtler pre-conditions that rule out simple “loop once” proofs and instead demand reasoning about *all* indices or about the entire collection at once.  
• Edge-case constraints (e.g. prime length, minimum gap between neighbouring elements) that force learners to express uncommon predicates such as “is_prime(len)”.  
• Post-conditions that relate the returned vector to the input via index arithmetic or multiset equality, requiring more than a single map operation to verify.

Below are three independent, stand-alone, harder versions of the original specification.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus/Rust function named rotate_left_prime 
with the following precise contract.

Inputs  
• a: Vec<i32> — passed by value, giving the function full ownership.  
• k: usize     — the number of positions to rotate left.

Output  
• result: Vec<i32>.

Helper definitions (you may introduce them in the spec)  
• is_prime(n: usize) -> bool — returns true exactly when n is a prime number ≥ 2.  
• idx_after_left_rot(n: usize, k: usize, i: usize) -> usize — computes the
  index in the result vector that an element originally at position i occupies
  after a left rotation by k modulo n, i.e. `(i + n - k) % n`.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() ≥ 2.  
2. is_prime(a.len()) — the length of the vector is prime.  
3. 0 < k < a.len() — the rotation distance is non-trivial and strictly less than the length.  
4. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize — length fits into 32-bit signed range (matches the
   original style).

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. result.len() == a.len().  
2. For every valid index i (0 ≤ i < a.len()):  
     result[idx_after_left_rot(a.len(), k, i)] == a[i].  
   (Equivalently, `result[j] == a[(j + k) % n]` for all j, but expressed via the helper
   to keep the spec uniform.)  
3. All elements are preserved multiplicity-wise:  
     multiset(result) == multiset(a).  

Informally: the function must perform a *cyclic* left rotation by `k` positions on a
vector whose length is prime, return the rotated vector, and prove that no element is
lost, duplicated, or reordered beyond the precise rotation mapping.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus/Rust function named prefix_sums_safe 
with the following precise contract.

Inputs  
• a: Vec<i32> — passed by value.

Output  
• result: Vec<i64> — each prefix sum is stored in 64-bit signed form.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0.  
2. For every i in 0 ≤ i < a.len():  
     −1_000_000_000 ≤ a[i] ≤ 1_000_000_000.  
3. The 64-bit signed integer range is sufficient for every *prefix* sum:  
     For every j in 0 ≤ j < a.len():  
         Sum_{t = 0..j} a[t] lies strictly within i64::MIN … i64::MAX.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. result.len() == a.len().  
2. For every index j in 0 ≤ j < a.len():  
      result[j] == Σ_{t = 0..j} a[t].  
3. The last element equals the total sum of the entire input:  
      result[a.len() − 1] == Σ_{t = 0..a.len() − 1} a[t].

Informally: given any integer vector where every running total fits into 64 bits,
return the vector of prefix sums.  The specification forces the implementer to reason
about cumulative properties rather than element-wise transformations.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus/Rust function named dedup_and_sort 
with the following precise contract.

Inputs  
• a: Vec<i32> — passed by value.

Output  
• result: Vec<i32>.

Helper definitions  
• to_multiset(v: &Vec<i32>) -> Multiset<i32> — abstraction view treating the vector
  as an unordered multiset.  
• is_strictly_sorted(v: &Vec<i32>) -> bool — true iff for all i<j, v[i] < v[j].

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  (Bounds the length.)  

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. is_strictly_sorted(&result).  
2. to_multiset(&result) == to_multiset(&a)  with all multiplicities *clamped to 1*.  
   Formally: for every value x,  
      multiplicity(x, to_multiset(&result)) ==  
                min(1, multiplicity(x, to_multiset(&a))).  
   (So each distinct element of the input appears exactly once in the output.)  
3. result.len() ≤ a.len().  
4. For any two indices p < q in result: result[p] < result[q].  
   (Redundant with #1 but restated index-wise for ease of verification.)

Informally: the function takes an arbitrary vector of 32-bit integers, removes all
duplicate values, sorts the remaining distinct values in ascending order, and returns
the new vector.  The proof must show that every value present in the output originated
from the input, no new values were introduced, no original value appears more than
once, and the order is strictly increasing.
>>>

Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following exact specification.

Signature  
fn binary_search(int_seq: &[i32], key: i32) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
• int_seq – an immutable slice (borrowed array segment) of 32-bit signed integers.  
• key – the integer value to search for.

Output  
• r – a 32-bit signed integer used to communicate the result:
  – if r ≥ 0, it represents a valid index where key occurs inside the slice;  
  – if r < 0, the slice does not contain key at any position.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. int_seq is non-empty: int_seq.len() > 0.  
2. The length is strictly less than 2,147,483,647 so every index fits in i32:  
   int_seq.len() < i32::MAX as usize.  
3. The slice is sorted in non-decreasing order:  
   ∀ i, j ∈ ℤ. 0 ≤ i ≤ j < int_seq.len() ⇒ int_seq[i] ≤ int_seq[j].

Postconditions (ensures)  
• Success case (r ≥ 0):  
  r < int_seq.len() as i32 ∧ int_seq[r] == key.  
  That is, the returned non-negative index is within bounds and points to a cell whose value equals key.  
• Failure case (r < 0):  
  ∀ i ∈ [0, int_seq.len()). int_seq[i] ≠ key.  
  In words, returning a negative value certifies that key is absent from the slice.

No auxiliary spec functions are needed. Any implementation that satisfies these contracts—typically a classic logarithmic-time binary search—is acceptable.
