# Reasoning
The original task is a single-step pattern-matching exercise.  
To make it more interesting we can:  
• Allow an arbitrary (possibly negative) number of 90° quarter-turns, so the student has to reason modulo 4.  
• Add an auxiliary value (coordinates) that must be rotated together with the direction, forcing simultaneous reasoning about two datatypes.  
• Ask for a function that returns the *clockwise distance* between two directions and prove it satisfies certain algebraic identities (less common than the usual “turn_left / turn_right”).  

Each new statement below is completely self-contained, written so that a spec generator can work from it directly.

# Output
<<<
Implement and verify a Verus-Rust function `rotate_direction`
that rotates a cardinal direction an arbitrary number of 90-degree
clockwise quarter-turns.

Datatype provided:
    enum Direction { North, East, South, West }

Function to write:
    fn rotate_direction(direction: Direction, quarters: int)
        -> (result: Direction)

Contract details
• Preconditions (requires):
    The integer `quarters` may be any 32-bit signed value;
    no further restrictions.

• Postconditions (ensures):
    1. `result` equals the direction obtained by performing
       `quarters` clockwise quarter-turns starting from `direction`.
       Formally, let `q = ((quarters % 4) + 4) % 4` (i.e. the least-non-negative
       remainder modulo 4).  Then the following exhaustive pattern must hold:

           match (direction, q, result) {
               (North, 0, North) | (North, 1, East)  | (North, 2, South) | (North, 3, West)
             | (East,  0, East)  | (East,  1, South) | (East,  2, West)  | (East,  3, North)
             | (South, 0, South) | (South, 1, West)  | (South, 2, North) | (South, 3, East)
             | (West,  0, West)  | (West,  1, North) | (West, 2, East)   | (West, 3, South)
             => true,
               _ => false,
           }

    2. When `quarters` is a multiple of 4, `result == direction`.
       (Useful lemma the implementation must satisfy.)

No helper lemmas are given; any that are needed must be supplied.
>>>

<<<
Implement and verify a Verus-Rust function `rotate_pose`
that simultaneously rotates a grid position (x, y) and its facing
direction an arbitrary number of 90° clockwise quarter-turns.

Datatypes provided:
    enum Direction { North, East, South, West }

Function to write:
    fn rotate_pose(x: int, y: int, dir: Direction, quarters: int)
        -> (new_x: int, new_y: int, new_dir: Direction)

Contract details
• Preconditions (requires):
    – No bounds on `x`, `y`, or `quarters`.

• Postconditions (ensures):
    Let q = ((quarters % 4) + 4) % 4.  Then

    1. The coordinate pair (new_x, new_y) is the result of rotating
       the original point (x, y) q times clockwise about the origin:
           q == 0 ⇒ (new_x, new_y) == ( x,  y)
           q == 1 ⇒ (new_x, new_y) == ( y, -x)
           q == 2 ⇒ (new_x, new_y) == (-x, -y)
           q == 3 ⇒ (new_x, new_y) == (-y,  x)

    2. new_dir equals the direction obtained by applying the same q
       clockwise quarter-turns to `dir`, according to the table in
       requirement 1 of the previous problem.

    3. Euclidean distance from the origin is preserved:
           new_x * new_x + new_y * new_y == x * x + y * y.

All three ensures clauses must be proven for every input.
No other helper specs are supplied; any needed lemmas must be added.
>>>

<<<
Implement and verify a Verus-Rust function `clockwise_distance`
that returns the minimal non-negative number of 90° clockwise
quarter-turns needed to transform one direction into another.

Datatype provided:
    enum Direction { North, East, South, West }

Function to write:
    fn clockwise_distance(from: Direction, to: Direction)
        -> (turns: int)

Contract details
• Preconditions: none.

• Postconditions:
    1. `turns` is an integer between 0 and 3 inclusive.
    2. Rotating `from` clockwise by exactly `turns` quarter-turns
       yields `to` (use the same table as in the first problem).
    3. For any integer k, rotating `from` by `k` quarter-turns yields
       `to`  ⇔  k ≡ turns (mod 4).  In other words, `turns` is the
       unique canonical representative in {0,1,2,3} of that residue
       class.

The verification must prove all three properties without relying on
external lemmas beyond what the student supplies in the file.
>>>

Function signature  
fn rotate_right(a: Vec<i32>) -> (a_rotated: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• a — an owned heap vector (`Vec<i32>`) containing 32-bit signed integers.

Output  
• a_rotated — a vector of the same element type and length, containing the elements of a after a single right-rotation.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0 (the vector must be non-empty).  
2. a.len() < 1000 (an upper-bound used only to simplify reasoning about indices; any value below 1000 is allowed).

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. For every index i with 1 ≤ i < a_rotated.len(), the element at that position equals the element that was previously at index i − 1:  
   ∀ i:int • 1 ≤ i ∧ i < a_rotated.len() ⇒ a_rotated[i] == a[(i-1)]  
2. The first element of the result is the last element of the original vector:  
   a_rotated[0] == a[a_rotated.len() − 1]

Taken together, these clauses precisely describe a cyclic right shift of the input by one position. No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are required. Any body that satisfies these contracts—e.g. swapping in place, using slice rotation, or constructing a new vector—is acceptable and will verify.

Edge cases  
• When the length is 1, the result is identical to the input.  
• All indices in the postconditions are guaranteed to be within bounds thanks to the length equalities implied by the two ensures clauses.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn binary_search(a: &[i32], val: i32) -> (pos: i32)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
        a.len() < i32::MAX as usize,
        a.len() > 1,
        forall|i: int, j: int| 0 <= i && i < j && j < a.len() as int ==> a[i] <= a[j],
    ensures
        0 <= pos && pos < a.len() as i32 ==> a[pos as int] == val,
        pos < 0 || pos >= a.len() as i32 ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < a.len() ==> a[i] != val,
{
```

# Reasoning
Inputs & their types  
• a: &[i32] – an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.  
• val: i32 – the value we are looking for inside the slice.

Return value  
• pos: i32 – an index (or a sentinel) telling where ‟val” is located.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0 — the slice is not empty.  
2. a.len() > 1 — it actually has at least two elements (supersedes the first clause, but both appear).  
3. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize — the length is strictly less than the maximum 32-bit signed integer so that every valid index fits in an i32.  
4. Sortedness: ∀ i,j • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].  
   This means the slice is monotonically non-decreasing (duplicates allowed).

Post-conditions (ensures)  
• If the returned integer pos is **inside** the legal index range, i.e. 0 ≤ pos < a.len(), then the element at that index must be exactly val.  
• If pos is **outside** the legal range (either negative or ≥ a.len()), then val is guaranteed *not* to appear anywhere in the slice: ∀ i in 0..a.len() → a[i] ≠ val.

In other words, the function must behave like a classic binary search on a sorted array:
– return a valid index where the requested value resides, if such a value exists,  
– otherwise return any out-of-bounds sentinel (for example −1 or a.len()).

Nothing is said about which duplicate occurrence to return when several equal elements exist – any correct index is acceptable.

There are no auxiliary spec functions, lemmas or extra attributes; all reasoning lives in the pre- and post-conditions.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified function

    fn binary_search(a: &[i32], val: i32) -> (pos: i32)

satisfying the following contract:

Inputs
• a: an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers whose length is at least 2 and strictly smaller than i32::MAX.  
• val: the integer to locate.

Pre-conditions (requires)
1. a.len() > 1            — the slice has at least two elements (and therefore is non-empty).  
2. a.len() < i32::MAX     — every valid index of a fits into an i32.  
3. The slice is sorted in non-decreasing order:  
   ∀ i,j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].

Outputs
• pos: i32

Post-conditions (ensures)
A. If 0 ≤ pos < a.len() then the value was found and  
      a[pos] == val.  
B. If pos < 0  OR  pos ≥ a.len() then the value is absent:  
      ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ val.

Interpretation
– The function must return a valid in-bounds index whenever the search key is present (any of the matching positions is acceptable when duplicates exist).  
– When the key is absent it must return a sentinel that is either negative or greater than or equal to the length of the slice; which sentinel is chosen is implementation-defined.  

No additional guarantees (e.g. about the first or last occurrence) are required.

This description completely characterises the function’s specification; from it one could reconstruct the Verus code above and implement a standard binary-search algorithm that fulfils the proof obligations.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original exercise is quite straightforward: rotate a non-empty vector to the right by exactly one position and return the new vector.  
To make the assignment(s) a bit harder we can:

• Accept an arbitrary rotation distance k (possibly larger than the length or even negative), requiring the implementer to reason about modular arithmetic.  
• Insist on an *in-place* rotation, which forces the student to reason about before/after states of the same memory region and forbids heap allocation.  
• Ask for an additional returned value (e.g. the sum of the elements) and require its correctness, adding an orthogonal property that must be preserved.  
• Add an explicit permutation/multiset property or capacity preservation requirement, both of which need auxiliary reasoning.  
• Slightly raise the “size” upper bound so indices stay small but proofs of arithmetic bounds are less trivial.

Below are three stand-alone, more demanding versions of the problem that incorporate some of these ideas.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust procedure with the following exact specification.

Function signature  
fn rotate_right_in_place(a: &mut Vec<i32>, k: usize)

Inputs  
• a — a *mutable* owned heap vector (`Vec<i32>`) containing 32-bit signed integers.  
• k — a non-negative integer indicating how many positions the vector must be rotated to the right.

Output  
• The function returns `()`; it must transform the vector *in place*.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0 (the vector must be non-empty).  
2. a.len() ≤ 2000 (keeps arithmetic simple for the prover).  
3. k < 2000 (the caller may supply very large k values but never beyond this bound).  

Postconditions (ensures)  
Let n = a.len() in the *post-state* and let old_a denote the contents of the vector in the *pre-state*.  
Define effective = k mod n (the usual Euclidean remainder, 0 ≤ effective < n).  
1. Length is unchanged: a.len() == old_a.len()  
2. Capacity is unchanged: a.capacity() == old(a.capacity()) (no heap reallocation allowed).  
3. For every index i with 0 ≤ i < n, the element now found at position (i + effective) mod n equals the element that was previously at index i:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i ∧ i < n ⇒ a[(i + effective) % n] == old_a[i]  
4. No other memory outside the slice owned by `a` is modified.

Edge cases  
• If k == 0 or k is an exact multiple of n, the vector is left untouched.  
• If n == 1, the vector is also left unchanged (all indices map to themselves).  
The implementer may use any correct algorithm (three-reverse trick, juggling with gcd, etc.) as long as it performs the rotation in O(n) time and O(1) additional memory.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following exact specification.

Function signature  
fn rotate_right_and_sum(a: Vec<i32>) -> (rotated: Vec<i32>, total: i32)

Inputs  
• a — an owned heap vector (`Vec<i32>`) of 32-bit signed integers.

Outputs  
• rotated — a vector containing the elements of `a` after a single cyclic right rotation.  
• total — the arithmetic sum of all elements of *the original* vector `a`.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0  
2. a.len() < 1500

Postconditions (ensures)  
Let n = a.len().  
1. rotated.len() == n  
2. ∀ i:int • 1 ≤ i ∧ i < n ⇒ rotated[i] == a[i-1]  
3. rotated[0] == a[n-1]  
4. total == (∑_{j=0}^{n-1} a[j])  
5. The multiset of elements contained in `rotated` equals the multiset of elements in `a` (a direct consequence of 2 and 3 but must still be proved).

Edge cases  
• If n == 1, `rotated` is exactly `a` and `total` equals that single element’s value.  
Any implementation strategy is acceptable (cloning, slice operations, etc.) as long as these contracts are met.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following exact specification.

Function signature  
fn rotate_wrapping(a: Vec<i32>, steps: i32) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• a — an owned heap vector (`Vec<i32>`) containing 32-bit signed integers.  
• steps — possibly negative, possibly larger than `a.len()`, indicating how many positions to rotate; positive values rotate right, negative values rotate left.

Output  
• result — the rotated vector.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0  
2. a.len() < 1000  
3. steps.abs() ≤ 1_000_000  (limit prevents huge arithmetic in the proof).

Postconditions (ensures)  
Let n = a.len() and let k = ((steps % n as i32) + n as i32) % n as i32 (the canonical non-negative remainder in the range 0 ≤ k < n).  
1. result.len() == n  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i ∧ i < n ⇒ result[((i + k) % n) as int] == a[i]  
   (rotating right by k is equivalent to rotating left by n-k when steps is negative).  
3. The multiset of elements in `result` equals that of `a`.

Edge cases  
• If k == 0, `result` is identical to `a`.  
• If n == 1, the single element remains in place.  
The body may allocate new memory, reuse `a`, or move elements one-by-one, provided the specification is satisfied.
>>>

   • Inside the proof environment Verus reasons about `u64` in the bounded machine domain, so the overflow risk is already avoided by the precondition.

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original exercise is almost trivial: it asks the student to prove that the body  
`let r = x * y;` meets an elementary post-condition, provided that an anti-overflow
precondition is already given.  
To raise the difficulty we can

•  remove the “guaranteed-no-overflow” assumption and instead ask the student to
  return an *indicator* of overflow (forces bifurcated reasoning);

•  add algebraic properties (commutativity, parity) that must be proven in the
  `ensures` clause, so the student has to use additional ghost variables or
  auxiliary lemmas;

•  demand an iterative algorithm (e.g. exponentiation by squaring) so that
  quantified loop invariants are required;

•  sprinkle a short but *incorrect* Rust snippet that *looks* plausible yet
  violates one of the new guarantees, nudging the learner toward subtle
  verification issues without telling them the fix.

Below are three stand-alone, harder specifications that embody those ideas.

# Output
<<<
Create a verified function named `checked_mult` with the following contract.

Signature  
    fn checked_mult(x: u64, y: u64) -> (overflow: bool, r: u64)

Inputs  
    • x: u64 — first unsigned 64-bit factor  
    • y: u64 — second unsigned 64-bit factor

Outputs  
    • overflow: bool — true exactly when the mathematical product does **not** fit
      in 64 bits  
    • r: u64         — if no overflow occurred, the true product; otherwise any
      value is allowed (0 is conventional)

Preconditions  
    • none (both arguments are always valid u64 values)

Postconditions  
    1. !overflow ⇒ r == x * y ∧ (x as u128) * (y as u128) < 2⁶⁴  
    2.  overflow ⇒ (x as u128) * (y as u128) ≥ 2⁶⁴              (r is ignored)  
    3.  For every swap of inputs the observable result is the same:  
        let (ov1,r1) = checked_mult(x,y);  
        let (ov2,r2) = checked_mult(y,x);  
        then ov1 == ov2 ∧ r1 == r2.  
        (Hint: use ghost calls in an `ensures` clause.)

Implementation hint (erroneous on purpose — does **not** satisfy (3)):  
```rust
fn checked_mult(x: u64, y: u64) -> (bool, u64) {
    match x.checked_mul(y) {
        Some(p) => (false, p),
        None     => (true , 0),
    }
}
```
Students must discover why property (3) can fail for some naïve rewrites and fix
the code or the reasoning accordingly.
>>>

<<<
Develop a verified function `pow_u64` computing an integer power by *iterative
squaring*.

Signature  
    fn pow_u64(base: u64, exp: u32) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    • base: u64 — strictly positive base  
    • exp : u32 — non-negative exponent

Preconditions  
    1. base > 0  
    2. exp ≤ 63                     (guarantees result fits in 64 bits)  

Postconditions  
    1. r == base^exp                (mathematical exponentiation)  
    2. exp == 0  ⇒ r == 1  
       exp  > 0 ⇒ r ≥ base  
    3. base even ⇒ r even,   base odd ⇒ r odd  
    4. Time complexity of the *implementation* must be O(log exp) by using the
       standard repeated-squaring algorithm; a straight `for` loop over `exp`
       is disallowed.

No helper lemmas are provided; students must supply loop invariants that relate
the accumulator, the current power of `base`, and the remaining bits of `exp`.
>>>

<<<
Write a verified function `product_or_overflow` that multiplies every element of
a slice, stopping early if the running product can no longer fit in 64 bits.

Signature  
    fn product_or_overflow(arr: &[u64]) -> (ok: bool, prod: u64)

Inputs  
    • arr: slice of unsigned 64-bit integers, possibly empty.

Outputs  
    • ok  : bool — true iff no intermediate or final multiplication overflowed  
    • prod: u64  — if ok is true, the exact product of all elements;
                   otherwise an unspecified value (e.g., 0)

Preconditions  
    • arr.len() ≤ 10_000  (artificial limit to keep verification scalable)

Postconditions  
    1. ok ⇒ prod == ∏_{i=0}^{arr.len()-1} arr[i]  
                ∧ that product is < 2⁶⁴  
    2. !ok ⇒ the mathematical product of at least one processed prefix was
             ≥ 2⁶⁴                                             (prod ignored)  
    3. The function must read the slice only once and use O(1) extra space.

Misdirection snippet (does **not** meet (2) when the product is 0):  
```rust
fn product_or_overflow(arr: &[u64]) -> (bool, u64) {
    let mut acc: u64 = 1;
    for &v in arr {
        if let Some(p) = acc.checked_mul(v) {
            acc = p;
        } else {
            return (false, 0);
        }
    }
    (true, acc)
}
```
Students need to supply precise loop invariants and fix corner cases where the
product becomes zero *without* overflow (e.g., an element is 0).
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn sqr_sum(n: usize) -> (s: usize)
    requires
        n < 100000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n * (n + 1) / 2 < usize::MAX, // added check to prevent overflow
    ensures
        s == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
The piece of Verus specification above declares one executable function named `sqr_sum`.  
Break-down of the contract:

1. Inputs & their types  
   – `n: usize` — a non-negative machine integer whose bit-width equals the pointer size of the target platform (e.g. 64-bit on most modern machines).

2. Output & its name  
   – `s: usize` — the single value returned by the function.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a. `n < 100000`  
      A pragmatic bound introduced only to keep the implementation simple and guarantee fast verification.  
   b. `n * (n + 1) / 2 < usize::MAX`  
      A second, more general guard that explicitly guarantees the arithmetic expression `n*(n+1)/2` fits in the `usize` range, i.e. no run-time overflow can occur when computing the result.

4. Postcondition (`ensures`)  
   – `s == n * (n + 1) / 2`  
     The returned value must be exactly the triangular number Tₙ = n(n+1)/2, i.e. the sum of the first `n` positive integers (also equal to the sum from 0 to `n`, 0 contributing nothing).  
     The equality sign indicates logical equivalence; the function is pure in the sense that the specification alone dictates the only admissible result.

5. Supporting spec functions / auxiliary lemmas  
   – None are referenced; the contract is fully self-contained.

6. Subtleties  
   – Arithmetic inside the contract is performed in the *runtime* `usize` domain, so the overflow checks are material.  
   – No `decreases` clause is necessary because the function needn’t be recursive.  
   – There is no postcondition about side effects; the function is expected to be total with respect to its domain dictated by the two preconditions.

Hence, to satisfy the spec, an implementation merely has to compute the expression `n*(n+1)/2` while honouring the overflow constraints already enforced by the `requires` clauses.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function with the following exact signature and contract:

fn sqr_sum(n: usize) -> (s: usize)

Inputs  
  • n: usize — a non-negative integer less than 100 000.

Return value  
  • s: usize — the triangular number corresponding to n.

Preconditions (requires)  
  1. n < 100000  
  2. n * (n + 1) / 2 < usize::MAX  
     (Both together guarantee every intermediate multiplication, addition and the final result fit in a `usize` without overflow.)

Postcondition (ensures)  
  s == n * (n + 1) / 2

In words: when called with an argument n that satisfies the two preconditions, sqr_sum must return exactly the value n·(n+1)/2, i.e. the sum 1 + 2 + … + n (or equivalently 0 + … + n). No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required; any implementation that provably obeys this contract is acceptable.
>>>

• Problem ① keeps the multiplication theme but forces an *iterative* Russian-peasant algorithm together with a non-obvious bit-level post-condition.  
• Problem ② replaces plain multiplication with *modular* multiplication and adds a secondary algebraic guarantee.  
• Problem ③ moves to arrays: the learner must verify a loop that computes the product of an entire slice while establishing several logical properties about the result.

The original exercise is almost trivial: prove the closed‐form for the triangular number with a small upper bound, no ghost state, and only one post-condition.  
To “raise the bar” I will

• keep the general flavour (small numerical maths routine) so that the task is recognisably related,  
• add 1–3 extra post-conditions that force the learner to reason about *relations* between several returned values,  
• introduce slightly more exotic algebraic identities (sum of squares, sum of cubes, etc.) that are still straightforward but require more steps than n(n+1)/2,  
• occasionally oblige the implementation to choose a *loop* (by banning direct closed-form arithmetic inside the body) so that an invariant and a decreasing measure must be supplied,  
• widen the numeric range so that 128-bit temporaries or explicit overflow lemmas become necessary, and  
• sprinkle one or two uncommon requirements such as “the result must be *odd*” or “the routine must prove the returned boolean really matches the parity”.

I propose three harder—yet still self-contained—specification tasks.

# Reasoning
The original exercise merely asks the learner to implement `test_prime` whose only contractual obligation is
`result == is_prime(i)`.  
To make the task slightly harder we can

1.  Demand an *additional witness* that must satisfy a quantified property (e.g. the smallest
    non-trivial divisor).  
2.  Ask for a *count* of all primes in a range – this forces induction-style reasoning instead of
    a single Boolean equality.  
3.  Require the function to return the *next* prime that is ≥ `i`, together with a proof that no
    smaller number in the searched interval is prime.

Each variant stays in the same mathematical domain (divisibility / primality) so the learner’s existing
helper predicates can still be re-used, but the post-conditions now contain nested implications,
universal quantifiers or recursive counting specifications, which introduce several extra
reasoning steps compared with the starter problem.

Create a formally verified Verus function called `prime_with_min_factor` together with three pure helper
specifications.

Helper predicates / functions (pure specification only):

1. `spec fn divides(f: u64, n: u64) -> bool`
   • Interprets “f divides n”.  
   • Defined as `n % f == 0`.  
   • Has `recommends 1 <= f`.

2. `spec fn is_prime(n: u64) -> bool`
   • Returns true iff  
        a) `1 < n`; and  
        b) `∀f: u64 :: 1 < f && f < n ==> !divides(f, n)`.

3. `spec fn is_minimal_nontrivial_divisor(d: u64, n: u64) -> bool`
   • Returns true iff all of the following are satisfied  
        a) `1 < d < n`,  
        b) `divides(d, n)`, and  
        c) `∀f: u64 :: 1 < f && f < d ==> !divides(f, n)`.

Primary executable function:

`fn prime_with_min_factor(n: u64) -> (is_p: bool, d: u64)`

Inputs  
   • `n: u64` – caller guarantees `1 < n`.

Outputs  
   • `is_p: bool` – indicates whether `n` is prime.  
   • `d: u64`   – a witness value tied to `n` and `is_p`.

Contract

requires  
   • `1 < n`.

ensures  
   • `is_p == is_prime(n)`.  
   • If `is_p` holds then `d == n`.  
   • If `!is_p` holds then `is_minimal_nontrivial_divisor(d, n)`.  
   • In any case `2 <= d`.

No other side-effects, pre- or post-conditions exist.  Any implementation that, under the given
precondition, returns values satisfying the above ensures-clauses is correct—whether it uses trial
division, wheel factorisation, or another method.

fn generic_max(a: &[int]) -> (max: int)
    requires

# Reasoning (you output)
We have two pieces of specification.

1. spec fn compare(x:int, y:int) -> bool
   Pure logical helper that simply returns the Boolean x <= y. It formalises the order relation using mathematical ints (unbounded). Any time compare(u,v) is true it means u is less or equal to v.

2. fn generic_max(a:&[int]) -> (max:int)
   Executable function.

   Inputs:
     - a: immutable slice of int values (unbounded mathematical integers at runtime too, as Verus allows slices of int). The slice length is established at entry.

   Preconditions (requires):
     - a.len() > 0 : the slice must not be empty, otherwise maximum is undefined.
     - a.len() < 1000 : an arbitrary upper-bound placed to keep later proofs away from potential integer-overflow issues when manipulating indices or counters.

   Output:
     - max: an int that will be returned.

   Postcondition (ensures):
     - For every integer index x with 0 ≤ x < a.len(), the predicate compare(a[x], max) holds.
       Because compare is defined as ≤, this means a[x] ≤ max for every element.
       Therefore max is an upper bound of the slice’s contents.
       Note: the spec does NOT require max to be an element of the slice, nor does it insist that it is the least such upper bound; any value that is greater than or equal to all elements is acceptable.

   Nothing else is stipulated; no decreases clause, no extra lemmas. Implementation freedom: the body may compute the real maximum or simply find an upper bound; but to satisfy practical intent programmers will likely return the concrete maximum element.

Important subtleties:
   - Range variables in the postcondition are mathematical ints, so 0 ≤ x < a.len() as int is necessary.

# Reasoning
The original task (“`array_sum` adds two same-length integer arrays element-wise”) is essentially a one-loop proof that each output cell equals the sum of the two inputs.  
To make follow-up exercises a bit harder, I introduce additional logical obligations that force the learner to combine *two or three* inductive arguments, reason about *auxiliary properties* (prefix sums, sortedness, carries), or cope with *slightly asymmetric input lengths*. None of the new tasks demand heavy algorithmic ingenuity, but each requires a more sophisticated post-condition and therefore a richer Verus proof.

Proposed harder variants:

1. `array_sum_prefix` – besides the element-wise sums, the function must also return a *running prefix-sum* vector and prove that the last prefix value equals the total sum of all element-wise sums. This adds an extra loop invariant.

2. `sorted_zip_sum` – the inputs are required to be *sorted non-decreasingly*; the post-condition must prove that the output is still sorted.  The learner now has to combine two proofs: correctness of addition *and* preservation of order.

3. `digit_array_add` – the two slices represent big integers in base-10, stored **least-significant digit first** and possibly of *different* lengths.  The function must return a new digit vector including a potential final carry.  Proof obligations include correct per-digit addition, carry propagation, and that each result digit lies in 0‥9.  The varying lengths force a reasoning split between the shared prefix and the “tail” of the longer slice.

Each description below is completely self-contained so that a spec generator sees *only one problem at a time*.

# Output
<<<
Design a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `array_sum_prefix` that, given two equal-length slices of mathematical integers, returns BOTH their element-wise sums and the running prefix sums of those sums.

Signature  
    fn array_sum_prefix(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (elem_sums: Vec<int>, prefixes: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    • a : immutable slice of `int`  
    • b : immutable slice of `int`

Outputs  
    • elem_sums : a vector where elem_sums[i] = a[i] + b[i]  
    • prefixes  : a vector where prefixes[j] = Σ_{i=0}^{j} elem_sums[i]

Contract  

Preconditions  
    1. a.len() == b.len()                                              – equal lengths  
    2. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ a[k] + b[k] ≤ i32::MAX - 1      – each pairwise sum fits safely  
       (note the upper bound is now **i32::MAX-1**, a slightly rarer requirement)

Postconditions  
    1. elem_sums.len() == a.len()  
    2. prefixes.len()  == a.len()  
    3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ elem_sums[i] == a[i] + b[i]  
    4. ∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < a.len() ⇒ prefixes[j] == Σ_{t=0}^{j} elem_sums[t]  
    5. prefixes[a.len()-1] == Σ_{u=0}^{a.len()-1} elem_sums[u]          – final prefix equals total

Thus the function must compute both arrays and prove the prefix-sum relationship in addition to the element-wise sums.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified Verus-Rust function called `sorted_zip_sum` that element-wise adds two *sorted, non-decreasing* integer slices and guarantees that the resulting vector remains sorted.

Signature  
    fn sorted_zip_sum(x: &[int], y: &[int]) -> (z: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    • x, y : immutable slices of `int`

Output  
    • z : vector of the pairwise sums

Contract  

Preconditions  
    1. x.len() == y.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < x.len() ⇒ x[i] ≤ x[i+1] ∧ y[i] ≤ y[i+1]   – both inputs sorted  
    3. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < x.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ x[k] + y[k] < i64::MAX / 2      – rarer 64-bit bound

Postconditions  
    1. z.len() == x.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < x.len() ⇒ z[i] == x[i] + y[i]  
    3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < z.len() ⇒ z[i] ≤ z[i+1]                   – output remains sorted

The learner must prove not only element-wise correctness but also that the pairwise addition of two sorted sequences is still sorted under the given non-overflow constraint.
>>>

<<<
Implement and formally verify a Verus-Rust function `digit_array_add` that adds two base-10 digit arrays of possibly different lengths, least-significant digit first, and returns the resulting digit vector including any final carry.

Signature  
    fn digit_array_add(lhs: &[int], rhs: &[int]) -> (sum_digits: Vec<int>)

Representation  
    Each slice encodes a non-negative integer:  
        value(slice) = Σ_{i=0}^{slice.len()-1} slice[i] * 10^i

Inputs  
    • lhs, rhs : immutable slices where every element is a digit 0‥9

Output  
    • sum_digits : vector representing value(lhs) + value(rhs) in the same encoding

Contract  

Preconditions  
    1. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < lhs.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ lhs[i] ≤ 9  
    2. ∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < rhs.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ rhs[j] ≤ 9

Postconditions  
    1. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < sum_digits.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ sum_digits[k] ≤ 9      – each result digit is valid  
    2. value(sum_digits) == value(lhs) + value(rhs)                     – numerical correctness  
    3. |sum_digits| == max(lhs.len(), rhs.len()) OR that length + 1     – at most one extra carry  
    4. If sum_digits.last() == 0 { sum_digits.len() == max(lhs.len(), rhs.len()) }  
       else                       { sum_digits.len() == max(lhs.len(), rhs.len()) + 1 }

The function must correctly propagate carries across the differing lengths, ensure each digit remains within 0-9, and justify the precise length relationship stated above.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is a straightforward element-wise multiplication with simple length and overflow checks.  
To make it more challenging, I designed several variants that introduce additional logical obligations, filtering, ordering constraints, or an auxiliary return value.  

Variant 1 (modular product) forces the implementation to reason about prime moduli and the range `0 ≤ c[i] < m`.  
Variant 2 (sparse product) adds dynamic output length, ascending index bookkeeping, and specification of two result vectors.  
Variant 3 (product with maximum) requires an extra aggregate value together with a witness that it actually occurs.  
Variant 4 (strictly increasing inputs) obliges the prover to show that products of two strictly increasing positive sequences are themselves strictly increasing.

Each variant keeps the spirit of “array product” but requires several more verification steps than the original.

# Reasoning
The original exercise merely checks for the presence of a single dot with exactly two trailing bytes, regardless of what those bytes are.  
To make the task marginally harder we can:

• Constrain the *kind* of bytes that may appear (e.g. they must be ASCII decimal digits).  
• Demand the absence of any *extra* dots.  
• Require at least one digit before the dot, thereby excluding the trivial string ".42".  
• Possibly add subtle formatting rules such as prohibiting a leading ‘0’ unless “0.xx”.  
These additions add only a few words but force the implementer to inspect the entire slice and reason about exclusivity and content, rather than just the location of a single byte.

Below are three self-contained, incrementally harder problems that satisfy the brief. Each problem fully defines the required spec predicate, the executable function, its inputs, outputs and the precise pre- and post-conditions.


# Output
<<<
Design a verified function plus its supporting specification predicate with the following exact behaviour.

1. Pure logical helper (spec function)

spec fn is_price_with_two_decimals(s: Seq<u8>) -> bool

– Purpose: In the logical domain, decide whether the byte sequence `s` encodes a *non-empty* string of ASCII decimal digits, followed by a single decimal point, followed by **exactly two** ASCII decimal digits.  
– Formal definition:  
  `is_price_with_two_decimals(s)` ≜  
       ∃ i:int •  
           1 ≤ i < s.len() − 2 ∧                                 // dot not first, space for 2 digits
           s[i] == b'.' ∧                                        // single decimal point
           (∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < i ⇒ ascii_is_digit(s[j])) ∧        // all bytes before dot are digits
           ascii_is_digit(s[i+1]) ∧ ascii_is_digit(s[i+2]) ∧     // exactly 2 digits after dot
           (∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < s.len() ∧ k ≠ i ⇒                  // dot uniqueness
                 if k < i { true } else { k > i + 2 }            // no other dot inside string
           )

(The helper predicate `ascii_is_digit(b)` is the usual `b'0' ≤ b ≤ b'9'`.)

2. Executable function

fn is_price_with_two_decimals_func(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Inputs  
    • `s`: immutable slice of bytes.

Output  
    • `result: bool` — must be true exactly when the predicate above holds for `s`.

Pre-conditions  
    • none.

Post-conditions  
    • `result ⇔ is_price_with_two_decimals(s@)`.

No additional lemmas, `requires` clauses or termination metrics are needed.
>>>

<<<
Extend the previous specification with a stricter formatting rule: forbid a leading zero unless the sequence *is exactly* “0.xx”.

1. Pure logical helper

spec fn is_price_no_leading_zero(s: Seq<u8>) -> bool

Definition:  
  `is_price_no_leading_zero(s)` ≜  
       is_price_with_two_decimals(s) ∧
       ( if s[0] == b'0' then                                     // leading ‘0’
             s.len() >= 4 ∧ s[1] == b'.'                          // must be the unique zero case
         else
             true )

(Here `is_price_with_two_decimals(s)` is the helper defined in the first problem.)

2. Executable function

fn is_price_no_leading_zero_func(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Post-conditions  
    • `result ⇔ is_price_no_leading_zero(s@)`.

All other details (inputs, absence of preconditions, etc.) are identical to the earlier problem.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function that recognises a slightly more elaborate ASCII money literal.

1. Pure logical helper

spec fn is_money_literal(s: Seq<u8>) -> bool

Informal requirements (all must hold):

A) The string is either “0.xx” or starts with a non-zero digit.  
B) Groups of exactly three digits may optionally be separated by commas **only before** the decimal point; commas must be placed correctly (e.g. “1,234,567.89” or “1234.89” are valid, “12,34.89” is not).  
C) Exactly one decimal point occurs, followed by *precisely* two digits.  
D) No other characters appear; every non-separator byte is an ASCII digit, every separator is either `b','` or the single `b'.'`.  

Precise definition sketch (pseudo-logic):

```
is_money_literal(s) ≜
    ∃ dot:int •                                     // index of '.'
        1 ≤ dot < s.len() - 2 ∧
        s[dot] == b'.' ∧
        ascii_is_digit(s[dot+1]) ∧ ascii_is_digit(s[dot+2]) ∧
        (∀ k:int • dot+3 ≤ k < s.len() ⇒ false) ∧  // exactly 2 digits after dot
        exactly_one_dot(s) ∧
        well_formed_integer_part(s[0 .. dot])       // see below
```

where  

• `exactly_one_dot(s)` asserts there is no `b'.'` elsewhere.  
• `well_formed_integer_part(t)` holds when  
    – `t.len() ≥ 1`,  
    – if `t[0] == b'0'` then `t.len() == 1`,  
    – commas, if present, appear every fourth position from the right (counting digits only) and never consecutively,  
    – all other bytes in `t` are ASCII digits.

(The auxiliary predicates `exactly_one_dot` and `well_formed_integer_part` must be specified at the logical level; feel free to introduce any ghost helpers you find convenient.)

2. Executable function

fn is_money_literal_func(s: &[u8]) -> (result: bool)

Post-conditions  
    • `result ⇔ is_money_literal(s@)`.

No pre-conditions, helper lemmas or termination metrics are required beyond those already indicated.
>>>

fn subtract_sequences(a: Vec<i32>, b: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Inputs

Postconditions (ensures)

2. For every index i in 0 .. result.len():

   Each element of result is exactly the pair-wise difference of the corresponding input elements.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are needed; the behaviour is captured entirely by the above pre- and post-conditions.

The implementation is free to iterate, map, or reuse storage so long as the verified contracts are satisfied. The precondition guarantees overflow safety; the length bound can be employed as a loop termination measure.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Searches for an element in an array of integers.
fn busca(a: &[i32], x: i32) -> (r: i32)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
        a.len() < i32::MAX as usize, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        0 <= r ==> r < a.len() as i32 && a[r as int] == x,
        r < 0 ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> a[i] != x,
{
```

# Reasoning

The function `busca` (Spanish/Portuguese for “search”) is specified as a classic “index-of” routine for an array slice of 32-bit signed integers.  
Below we restate every aspect of the specification so that an implementer could recreate it from scratch.

1. Inputs  
   • `a: &[i32]` — an immutable slice (borrowed view) of i32s.  
   • `x: i32`    — the value to look for inside `a`.

2. Output  
   • `r: i32` — the result index, or a negative sentinel indicating “not found”.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` – the slice must be non-empty; calling the function on an empty slice is disallowed.  
   • `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize` – the length must be strictly smaller than the maximum positive value representable by i32.  
     (This guarantees that any valid index of the slice can be losslessly stored in the signed 32-bit result.)

4. Postconditions (`ensures`) — behaviour encoded by two mutually exclusive cases:

   Case 1 – Found (`r >= 0`):  
     • `r < a.len() as i32` — the returned non-negative index lies within the bounds of the slice.  
     • `a[r as int] == x`  — the element at that index equals the searched value.  

   Case 2 – Not found (`r < 0`):  
     • `∀ i:int . 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ x` — every element of the slice differs from `x`; i.e. the value is absent.

   The spec deliberately says nothing about which occurrence is returned when `x` appears more than once, nor does it require the *first* or *last* index; any valid index holding `x` satisfies the contract.

5. No additional helper specifications, lemmas, or decreasing clauses are used; the function need not terminate in a specific number of steps (termination is automatic for finite slices).

6. Implementation freedom:  
   A typical implementation would iterate linearly through the slice and return the index on the first match, else `-1`, but any algorithm that satisfies the above contracts (binary search on sorted data, etc.) is acceptable. The result must remain in the i32 range guaranteed by the precondition.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified function in Verus-Rust with the exact behaviour described below.

Signature  
    fn busca(a: &[i32], x: i32) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
  • a — an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers, length > 0 and length < 2,147,483,647.  
  • x — the integer value to search for.

Output  
  • r — a 32-bit signed integer that serves either as a valid index into the slice or a negative sentinel.

Contract  
Preconditions  
  1. The slice is non-empty: a.len() > 0.  
  2. Its length fits into an i32: a.len() < i32::MAX as usize.

Postconditions  
  • If r ≥ 0 then  
        r < a.len() as i32                    (r is within bounds)   and  
        a[r] == x                             (the element at r equals x).  

  • If r < 0 then  
        ∀ i ∈ [0, a.len()) → a[i] ≠ x         (x is absent from the entire slice).

No other guarantees are imposed: when x occurs multiple times, r may point to any one of them; minimality or uniqueness is not required.

The body of the function can be implemented by a straightforward linear scan or any other method that meets these logical obligations.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise asks the learner to verify a very small, single-pass loop that simply subtracts two equal-length vectors.  
To raise the difficulty we can

• Add extra outputs whose values depend on all elements (e.g. the sum of absolute differences or a running prefix sum).  
  – Verifying such an aggregate requires an additional loop invariant or a fold-style proof.  

• Introduce structural restrictions (length must be a multiple of 8, matrices must be lower-triangular, etc.) that have to be expressed with universal & existential quantifiers.  

• Require global algebraic properties of the result (monotonicity, preservation of triangularity) that force the student to reason about *all indices* instead of pair-wise independence.  

• Occasionally switch to a different integer width (i64 / i128 / i16) so that overflow arguments become less trivial.  

Below are three stand-alone, harder variants that incorporate those ideas.  Each description is self-contained so a spec generator can work directly from it.

# Output
<<<
Design and formally verify, in Verus-Rust, a function that simultaneously performs element-wise subtraction of two 64-bit integer vectors and computes the sum of the absolute value of every difference.

Function signature  
fn subtract_and_accumulate(a: Vec<i64>, b: Vec<i64>) -> (result: Vec<i64>, total_abs: i128)

Inputs  
• a – a Vec<i64> moved into the function.  
• b – a Vec<i64> of exactly the same length, also moved into the function.

Outputs  
• result – a new Vec<i64> that stores a[i] − b[i] for all indices i.  
• total_abs – an i128 equal to Σ |a[i] − b[i]| over the entire range.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. a.len() == b.len()                                   – identical length.  
2. 0 < a.len() ≤ 1024                                   – non-empty, at most 1024 elements.  
3. a.len() % 8 == 0                                     – the length is a multiple of eight.  
4. ∀k: 0 ≤ k < a.len(): i64::MIN ≤ a[k] − b[k] ≤ i64::MAX  
   (overflow cannot occur in any individual subtraction).  
5. Σ_{k=0}^{a.len()-1} |a[k] − b[k]| < 2^127             – the accumulation fits into i128.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. result.len() == a.len()                                – length preserved.  
2. ∀i: 0 ≤ i < result.len(): result[i] == a[i] − b[i].    – element-wise correctness.  
3. total_abs == Σ_{i=0}^{result.len()-1} |result[i]|.     – total_abs is the exact L¹ norm of result.

Function signature  
fn subtract_lower_tri(a: Vec<Vec<i16>>, b: Vec<Vec<i16>>) -> (c: Vec<Vec<i16>>)

Inputs  
• a – ownership of an n×n lower-triangular matrix: for every row r and every column c > r, a[r][c] == 0.  
• b – another n×n lower-triangular matrix of the same shape.

4. ∀r: 0 ≤ r < b.len(): b[r].len() == b.len().  
5. Both a and b are lower-triangular:

3. ∀r,c: 0 ≤ r < n, r < c < n ⇒ c[r][c] == 0          – the result is still lower-triangular.  
4. (Optional but recommended) The diagonal of c is the element-wise difference of the diagonals of a and b.

No auxiliary spec functions are supplied; the student must establish structural properties (equal row lengths, preservation of triangular zeros) in addition to the arithmetic correctness and overflow freedom.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Takes an array of integers and returns the absolute values of the integers.
fn abs_it(s: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        s.len() < 1000,
    ensures
        result.len() == s.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> if s[i] == i32::MIN { result[i] == i32::MAX } else if s[i] < 0 { result[i] == -s[i] } else { result[i] == s[i] },
```

# Reasoning
The function is called `abs_it`.

1. Inputs & their types  
   • `s` – an owned `Vec<i32>` containing 32-bit signed integers.  
   Because the vector is taken by value (not by reference), the function consumes `s`.

2. Output & its type  
   • `result` – a newly returned `Vec<i32>`.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `s.len() < 1000` – the input vector must contain strictly fewer than 1000 elements.  
   There are no other constraints on the contents of `s`.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   • `result.len() == s.len()` – the returned vector must have exactly the same length as the original.  
   • Element-wise specification: for every index `i` in the valid range `0 ≤ i < s.len()` the following holds:  
        – If the original element is the smallest representable 32-bit value (`s[i] == i32::MIN`, i.e. −2 147 483 648) then the corresponding output element must be `i32::MAX` (2 147 483 647).  
        – Else, if the original element is negative (`s[i] < 0`) but not `i32::MIN`, the output element must be its arithmetic negation `-s[i]`.  
        – Else (the original element is non-negative) the output element is left unchanged, `result[i] == s[i]`.

   In short, `result` is an element-wise absolute-value transform of `s`, except that the unnegatable sentinel value `i32::MIN` is mapped to `i32::MAX` to avoid overflow.

5. No additional helper spec functions, lemmas, or ghost state are required.

6. Subtleties / motivation  
   • Negating `i32::MIN` would overflow in a 32-bit signed integer; therefore the spec explicitly substitutes `i32::MAX` in that case.  
   • Because `s` is consumed, the implementation can freely reuse or mutate the original vector as long as the postconditions are met.  
   • The length upper bound of 1000 is the only runtime restriction imposed on callers.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following behaviour:

Signature  
    fn abs_it(s: Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>

Inputs  
    • s: a heap-allocated vector of 32-bit signed integers (ownership is transferred to the function).

Output  
    • A new vector, hereafter called result, also of type Vec<i32>.

Contract  
Precondition  
    • s.len() < 1000

Postconditions  
    1. result.len() == s.len()  
    2. ∀ i:int with 0 ≤ i < s.len():  
         – If s[i] == i32::MIN (−2 147 483 648) → result[i] == i32::MAX (2 147 483 647)  
         – Else if s[i] < 0 → result[i] == −s[i]  
         – Else (s[i] ≥ 0) → result[i] == s[i]

Informally, the function must return a vector whose ith element is the absolute value of the ith element of the input, except that the unnegatable sentinel value i32::MIN is converted to i32::MAX. The result vector must have the same length as the input, and no other requirements are imposed.

Any implementation that satisfies these specifications and verifies in Verus is acceptable (e.g., by iterating over the elements, applying the described transformation, and collecting the results into a new vector or by modifying the input vector in place).
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is trivial because (a) the type `u32` already guarantees the only pre-condition, and (b) the post-condition can be met by a single `return n;`.  
To make things slightly harder while still staying in the “intro” range, I introduce three new stand-alone problems.  They all

• keep a small, fixed signature (only integers or slices),  
• force the student to write an actual loop or recursive call that demands loop invariants or termination metrics,  
• add an extra ghost or mathematical property to reason about, and  
• avoid relying on external helpers.

Problem 1 adds a ghost `Set<u32>` that must end up containing every value the concrete counter ever assumed.  
Problem 2 asks the student to compute the triangular number, proving a closed-form formula instead of merely returning the last iteration value.  
Problem 3 replaces the common “return the input” with a less common requirement: build `2^exponent` exclusively by repeated doubling, prove the power-of-two property, and observe an overflow bound.

Each specification stays short, yet now requires multiple reasoning steps, classic loop invariants, and (for problem 3) a termination metric that decreases by exactly 1.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function with the exact signature

fn count_and_collect(n: u32) -> (r: u32)

Contracts and auxiliary items:

1. Ghost state  
   A ghost variable `visited: Set<u32>` must be introduced and initially equal to the empty set.

2. Pre-condition  
   requires n <= 10_000                     // keep the concrete run small

3. Post-conditions  
   ensures r == n                           // the concrete result is the final counter value  
   ensures visited == set_int_range(0, n+1) // `visited` contains every integer 0 ≤ k ≤ n exactly once

4. Operational requirement  
   The concrete code must reach `r == n` by **iteratively** starting with a mutable `i = 0`, incrementing `i`
   until `i > n`, and updating `visited = visited + {i}` on every loop iteration.  No early `return`s.

No external helper specs, traits, or modules are allowed.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function with the exact signature

fn triangular(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)

Contracts:

• Pre-condition  
  requires n <= 4_294_967_295     // prevents 64-bit overflow of n·(n+1)/2

• Post-condition  
  ensures sum == n * (n + 1) / 2

Operational requirements:

a. The result must be computed **by an explicit loop that adds one term at a time**; direct closed-form computation in the executable code is forbidden.  
b. A loop invariant must connect the running total to the current index so the final assertion of the closed-form succeeds.  
c. No ghost arithmetic is allowed inside the loop body—only ordinary `u64` additions on the concrete accumulator.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function with the exact signature

fn power_of_two(exponent: u32) -> (res: u64)

Contracts:

• Pre-condition  
  requires exponent <= 63            // 2^64 would overflow u64

• Post-conditions  
  ensures res == (1u64 << exponent)  // equality with the bit-shift specification  
  ensures res.is_power_of_two()      // prove the standard library predicate

Implementation constraints:

1. The function must start from `res = 1` and perform **exactly `exponent` iterations**, each doubling `res`.  
2. Provide a strictly decreasing variant based on the remaining number of doublings to establish loop termination.  
3. Do NOT use exponentiation, built-in shift operators, or multiplication by constants other than 2 inside the loop; only `res = res + res` is permitted per iteration.
>>>

/// Specification function to calculate power of 2

    } else {
        n * 2
    }
}

/// Function to calculate power of 2 using iteration
fn compute_power(n: u64) -> (p: u64)
    requires
        n > 0,
        n < u64::MAX / 2,
        n * 2 < u64::MAX, // added precondition to prevent overflow
        n < 1000, // added precondition to prevent overflow
    ensures
        p == power(n as nat),
{

   Name: `compute_power`

The original task is a very small boolean‐returning scanner that only needs to prove one simple existential postcondition.  
To make the assignment a bit harder while still remaining suitable for automatic spec generation we can:

• Introduce an `Option` return or an in-place mutation, so the post-state as well as the return value must be related to the pre-state through quantified conditions.  
• Provide an extra parameter (e.g. `k`) that interacts with the slice, creating a need for cross-parameter arithmetic reasoning and a precondition (`k ≤ s.len()`).  

Below are three stand-alone, more demanding variants that build on these ideas.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Proof function to calculate the value of f2 function
fn f2(n: u64) -> (result: u64)
    requires
        n < 1000, // added precondition to prevent overflow
        n < 1000000, // added precondition to prevent overflow
    ensures
        result == n / 3 * 5 + n % 4,
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification introduces a single executable function called `f2`.

1. Inputs  
   • `n: u64` – an unsigned 64-bit integer provided by the caller.

2. Output  
   • `result: u64` – the value that the function returns.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `n < 1000`  
   • `n < 1_000_000`  
   The first bound (`< 1000`) is actually stronger, so the second is redundant; nonetheless both must be satisfied by the caller.  These bounds guarantee that every arithmetic operation performed inside the function stays well within the `u64` range.

4. Postcondition (`ensures`)  
   • `result == n / 3 * 5 + n % 4`  
   Interpreting Rust/Verus operator precedence, the expression means  
   `result = (n / 3) * 5 + (n % 4)`  
   where  
   – `/` is integer division that discards any remainder (floor for positive numbers).  
   – `%` yields the remainder of the division by 4 and is always in `0..=3`.  
   Both operations are performed in the `u64` domain, so everything is non-negative.

5. Auxiliary items  
   • There are no helper spec functions, lemmas, or decreases clauses; the spec is entirely contained in the single postcondition equation.

6. Behavioural summary  
   The function must compute five times the quotient of `n` divided by three, then add the remainder obtained when `n` is divided by four, and return that value.  
   Formally:  
      f2(n) = 5·⌊n/3⌋ + (n mod 4)  for every `n < 1000`.

7. Implementation freedom  
   Any body that provably satisfies the postcondition is acceptable.  A straightforward one-liner
   `let r = (n / 3) * 5 + (n % 4);`  
   followed by `return r;` (or simply the expression) will verify because the arithmetic never overflows under the given preconditions.

# Final Description

Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

fn f2(n: u64) -> (result: u64)

Inputs  
• n – an unsigned 64-bit integer.

2. n < 1_000_000  
(The first bound already implies the second; both must hold.)

Output  
• result – an unsigned 64-bit integer.

Postcondition  
result == (n / 3) * 5 + (n % 4)

Here “/” is the usual integer division that yields the quotient ⌊n/3⌋, while “%” gives the remainder when dividing n by 4 (a value in 0..=3).  
Thus the function must return 5·⌊n/3⌋ plus the remainder of n modulo 4.  No auxiliary spec functions are needed, and any implementation that computes this value without overflowing (which is guaranteed by the preconditions) will satisfy the specification.

Inputs  
• `a`: immutable slice of 64-bit unsigned integers.  
• `b`: immutable slice of 64-bit unsigned integers (divisors).

Outputs

Postconditions (`ensures`)

2. For every valid index `i` (0 ≤ i < a.len()):

      • `rem[i]  == a[i] % b[i]`.  
      • `quot[i] * b[i] + rem[i] == a[i]`.   (Euclidean division law.)  
      • `rem[i] <  b[i]`.                     (Non-negative remainder is strictly smaller.)  
3. The maximum remainder never exceeds the maximum divisor minus one:

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: element-wise addition under simple bounds.  
To make it more challenging I create four alternative specs.  
Key ideas for raising difficulty:

1. Add structural properties (sorted, strictly increasing) that must be preserved by the algorithm, forcing the student to reason about order as well as arithmetic.
2. Introduce in-place mutation, so the post-state has to be related to both the inputs and the mutably-aliased slice, demanding extra ownership/aliasing reasoning.
3. Require a *partial* addition (prefix length `n` given at runtime) together with intricate range guarantees, so loop invariants must mention two different lengths.
4. Mix arithmetic with an aggregated property (cumulative maximum) that is less common than direct sums and needs more deductive steps.

Each spec is completely self-contained and written with enough detail that a generator could turn it directly into Verus contracts.

Signature  
fn add_sorted_arrays(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a — immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• b — another immutable slice of integers.

Returned value  
• c — a newly allocated Vec<int> containing the element-wise sums.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. Equal length: a.len() == b.len().  
2. Strictly increasing inputs: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len()-1 ⇒ a[i] < a[i+1] ∧ b[i] < b[i+1].  
3. Non-negative elements: ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ a[k] ∧ 0 ≤ b[k].  
4. Range safety: ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] + b[k] < i32::MAX.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. Length preserved: c.len() == a.len().  
2. Element-wise correctness: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < c.len() ⇒ c[i] == a[i] + b[i].  
3. Strictly increasing result: ∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < c.len()-1 ⇒ c[j] < c[j+1].

No auxiliary ghost state is mandatory, but you may introduce helper lemmas if needed.  The implementation may use any verified iteration strategy.

Signature  
fn cumulative_max_sum(values: &[int], increments: &[int]) -> (out: Vec<int>)

Informal description  
For each index i, compute s = values[i] + increments[i], then set out[i] to the maximum of all such s seen so far (from index 0 up to i).

# Reasoning
The original task only asks for the single largest element.  
To make variants that require more sophisticated reasoning we can:

• Ask for *where* the maximum first appears, forcing an interplay between value‐ and index‐based properties.  
• Ask for the two largest *distinct* values, introducing conditional behaviour (Option) and a “strictly less than” relation.  
• Ask for the maximum *sub-array* sum, which needs quantified reasoning over every contiguous slice and an existential that guarantees the optimum is attained.

Each variant keeps the data structure (Vec<i32>) but adds new logical clauses and/or case splits, lengthening both proof obligations and the number of verification steps while still being manageable for a learner familiar with Verus.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus-Rust function called `max_with_first_index` that returns  
(a) the maximum element of a non-empty vector of 32-bit signed integers *and*  
(b) the smallest index at which that maximum occurs.

Signature  
    fn max_with_first_index(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (max: i32, idx: usize)

Inputs  
    a : &Vec<i32> – immutable reference to a run-time vector of `i32`.

Outputs  
    max : i32  – the greatest value stored in `a`.  
    idx : usize – the first position (lowest index) in `a` whose element equals `max`.

Contract

Pre-condition  
    • a.len() > 0          — the vector must contain at least one element.

Post-conditions  
    1. Upper-bound condition  
       ∀ i : nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ max  
       (Verus: `forall|i: nat| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> a[i as int] <= max`)

    2. Attainment condition (value)  
       a[idx] = max

    3. Attainment condition (index)  
       0 ≤ idx < a.len()

    4. Minimal-index condition  
       ∀ j : nat. 0 ≤ j < idx ⇒ a[j] < max  
       (i.e., `idx` is the *first* occurrence of the maximum value)

No helper lemmas are mandatory; all of the above must be proven directly in the spec of `max_with_first_index`.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus-Rust function called `two_distinct_max` that returns the two largest *distinct* values contained in a vector of 32-bit signed integers, if such a pair exists.

Signature  
    fn two_distinct_max(a: &Vec<i32>) -> (res: Option<(i32, i32)>)

Inputs  
    a : &Vec<i32>

Output  
    res : Option<(i32, i32)>  
        • `Some((m1, m2))`  – when the vector contains at least two different values.  
        • `None`            – when every element of `a` is identical (or the vector has length 1).

Contract

Pre-condition  
    • a.len() > 0

Post-conditions  
    1. Case `res == None`  
         ∀ i, j : nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] = a[j]  
         (all elements equal, therefore no second distinct maximum)

    2. Case `res == Some((m1, m2))`  
       a. Distinctness & ordering  
          m1 > m2

       b. Maximality of `m1`  
          ∀ i : nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ m1  
          ∃ i : nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] = m1

       c. Second-max property of `m2`  
          • m2 appears in the vector: ∃ i : nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] = m2  
          • There is no element strictly between m1 and m2:  
            ∀ x : i32. (∃ i : nat. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] = x ∧ m2 < x) ⇒ x = m1
>>>

   • `result == a || result == b` – moreover, the value returned is exactly one of the two inputs.

   Combined, these three clauses characterise `result` as the *greater* (or equal) of `a` and `b`; if the two numbers are equal, either can be returned.

4. Preconditions (requires)  
   • None. The function must operate correctly for all integer inputs (positive, negative, or zero).

5. Auxiliary spec artifacts  
   • None; no ghost functions or lemmas are referenced.

6. Subtleties  
   • Because the type is `int`, all reasoning is done in the mathematical integer domain, so overflow is impossible in the specification.  
   • An implementation can be the familiar `if a >= b { a } else { b }`.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function named `max` that computes the larger of two mathematical integers.

Signature  
fn max(a: int, b: int) -> (result: int)

Inputs  
• `a` – an arbitrary integer (type `int`)  
• `b` – another arbitrary integer (type `int`)

Output  
• `result` – the integer that is at least as large as both inputs and equal to one of them.

Specification  
Pre-conditions: none.

Post-conditions:  
1. `result >= a`  
2. `result >= b`  
3. `result == a || result == b`

The three post-conditions together ensure that `result` is exactly the maximum of `a` and `b` (returning either one when they are equal). No additional helper functions or lemmas are required. Any body that satisfies these contracts—e.g., `if a >= b { a } else { b }`—is acceptable.
>>>

The original task asks the learner to implement `minimum`, which is solved with a single forward scan.  
To increase difficulty we can:
 • Return extra information (e.g. the multiplicity of the minimum) – this adds a counting obligation and forces two simultaneous invariants.  
 • Change the notion of “minimum” to something derived (e.g. minimum *prefix-sum*), which introduces a running accumulator and an additional level of reasoning (sum + min).  
 • Ask for two distinct extrema (smallest and 2nd-smallest) while proving they are different – this introduces conditional updates, extra existential witnesses and an ordering proof.  

Each proposed variant below is self-contained, specifies inputs, outputs, pre-/post-conditions in detail, and is roughly ~10 words longer than the original.

Inputs

  (No restrictions on the individual values.)

Output  
• Returns `(copy: Vec<int>)` — a heap-allocated vector of the same element type.

Precondition

   – No other preconditions.

Postconditions

2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ `copy[i] == s[i]`  
   – every element at every valid index is identical to the corresponding element in the original slice.

Interpretation  
The function must return a **deep, element-wise copy** of the input slice with no reordering, omission, or alteration of values. No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required; satisfying the two ensures clauses is sufficient for correctness.

Implementation freedom

/// Specification function for maximum

    } else {
        b
    }
}

fn max_func(a: int, b: int) -> (c: int)
    requires

   • Preconditions:
        – `a >= 0`
        – `b >= 0`
     So the caller must supply non-negative integers.
   • Postconditions:
        – `a <= c && b <= c`  → The result is at least as large as each argument.
        – `a == c || b == c` → The result equals exactly one of the two arguments.
     Together these guarantee that `c` is the maximum of `a` and `b`.  
     If the inputs are equal, both conjuncts hold, so any of the equal values is acceptable.

Utility of the helper: although the postcondition does not explicitly reference it, the spec function `max` can be used inside the implementation proof to shorten arguments.

No additional lemmas, ghost parameters, or decreases clauses are required.

Implementation constraints:
   • Must respect the preconditions (only invoked for non-negative inputs).
   • Must produce a value satisfying the two postconditions—e.g., by returning `a` when `a >= b`, otherwise `b`.

Implement two verified items in Verus-Rust:

1. spec fn max(a: int, b: int) -> int  
   • Pure, logical-only function.  
   • Parameters: two mathematical integers `a`, `b`.  
   • Returns: the larger of the two, defined by

2. fn max_func(a: int, b: int) -> (c: int)

   • Inputs:  
        – a: int (must satisfy a ≥ 0)  
        – b: int (must satisfy b ≥ 0)  
   • Output:  
        – c: int  
   • Preconditions (requires):  
        1. a ≥ 0  
        2. b ≥ 0  
   • Postconditions (ensures):  
        1. a ≤ c  ∧  b ≤ c   (the result is at least both inputs)  
        2. (a = c) ∨ (b = c) (the result equals one of the inputs)  

These two guarantees together mean that `c` is exactly the maximum of the two non-negative arguments. If the arguments are equal, either value (they are the same) satisfies the spec.

No other helper functions, lemmas, or special attributes are required. The implementation can simply compare the inputs and return whichever is larger; any equivalent method that proves the stated postconditions is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is little more than “allocate a new vector and copy a slice.”  
To make the assignment more challenging but still appropriate for a Verus-Rust spec-writing exercise, I introduce tasks that

* add moderately intricate mathematical post-conditions (e.g. preservation of multiset equality, order-related guarantees, index–element relations);
* include at least one *less common* requirement such as “the result must be strictly increasing” or “no element may overflow a 64-bit signed integer after a transformation”;
* force one or two additional reasoning steps (computing counts, filtering, or folding); and
* retain a clear, self-contained description so that a spec generator can work from the text alone.

I give three alternative, self-contained problems.  
Each problem is enclosed in <<< … >>> to allow automated parsing.

# Output
<<<
Task: Write a formally verified Verus-Rust function called `filter_out_value`.

Inputs  
• `s: &[int]` — an immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers.  
• `v: int`        — a single integer value to be removed.  

Output  
• Returns `(out: Vec<int>)` — a heap-allocated vector of `int`.

Pre-conditions  
1. `s.len() < i32::MAX` — slice length fits in a 32-bit signed integer.  
   No other pre-conditions.

Post-conditions  
1. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < out.len() ⇒ out[i] ≠ v  
   – the result never contains the forbidden value `v`.  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] ≠ v ⇒  
      ∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < out.len() ∧ out[j] == s[i]  
   – every element of the original slice *except* the ones equal to `v` appears in `out`.  
3. out.len() == (#{ i:int | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] ≠ v })  
   – the length equals the number of retained elements.  
4. Strict stability: for all indices i₁,i₂ with 0 ≤ i₁ < i₂ < s.len(),  
      if s[i₁] ≠ v ∧ s[i₂] ≠ v then  
      the index of s[i₁] in `out` is smaller than the index of s[i₂] in `out`.  
   – relative order of kept elements is preserved exactly.

Interpretation  
`filter_out_value` returns a deep copy of `s` with *all* occurrences of `v` removed, without re-ordering the remaining elements.  
No auxiliary spec lemmas are mandatory; satisfying the four ensures clauses suffices.

Implementation freedom  
Any panic-free executable body (e.g., `retain` or manual loop) that verifies is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Task: Write a formally verified Verus-Rust function called `duplicate_elements`.

Inputs  
• `s: &[int]` — immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers.  

Output  
• Returns `(dup: Vec<int>)`.

Pre-conditions  
1. s.len() ≤ (i32::MAX / 2)  
   – duplicating every element never exceeds 32-bit signed length.

Post-conditions  
1. dup.len() == 2 * s.len()  
   – output length doubles the input length exactly.  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ dup[2*i]   == s[i]   ∧ dup[2*i + 1] == s[i]  
   – each element of `s` appears *twice consecutively* in `dup`.  
3. ∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < dup.len() ⇒  
      dup[j] == s[j/2]  
   – equivalent single-formula restatement, aiding proof automation.

Interpretation  
`duplicate_elements` performs an order-preserving deep copy where every source element is emitted twice in adjacent positions.

Implementation freedom  
Any verified body (iterator + `extend`, explicit loop, etc.) is acceptable. No panics or arithmetic overflows may occur.
>>>

<<<
Task: Write a formally verified Verus-Rust function called `prefix_sums_mod`.

Inputs  
• `s: &[int]`  — immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers.  
• `m: int`     — a modulus with  2 ≤ m ≤ 2³¹ − 1  (strictly positive, fits in 32-bit).  

Output  
• Returns `(ps: Vec<int>)`.

Pre-conditions  
1. s.len() < i32::MAX  
2. 2 ≤ m ≤ 2_147_483_647            – modulus in 32-bit signed range.

Post-conditions  
1. ps.len() == s.len()  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ 0 ≤ ps[i] < m  
   – every prefix sum is reduced modulo `m`.  
3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒  
      ps[i] == (Σ 0≤k≤i s[k]) mod m  
   – element `i` equals the sum of the first `i+1` slice elements, taken modulo `m`.  
4. ps[0] == (s[0] mod m)            – explicit base case for clarity.  

Interpretation  
`prefix_sums_mod` computes running totals of `s` under modular arithmetic with modulus `m`, returning them in a new vector.

Implementation freedom  
The body may use a running accumulator updated inside a loop.  
All arithmetic on the executable side must use Verus’ mathematical integers, then reduce with `mod`.  
No panics or division-by-zero occur because of the modulus pre-condition.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

struct Reg;

fn find_first_occurrence(x: int, s: &Vec<int>) -> (r: i32)
    requires
        s.len() > 0,
        s.len() < i32::MAX,
    ensures
        r < 0 ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < s.len() ==> s[i] != x,
        r >= 0 ==> exists|i: int| 0 <= i && i < s.len() && s[i] == x,
{
```

# Reasoning
1. Data types and inputs  
   • `x: int` – a mathematical (unbounded) integer whose presence in the vector we want to check.  
   • `s: &Vec<int>` – an immutable reference to a runtime vector whose elements are also mathematical `int` values. The vector is **not** allowed to be empty and its length must fit in a 32-bit signed integer (two explicit pre-conditions).

2. Output  
   • `r: i32` – a 32-bit signed integer returned by the function.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a) `s.len() > 0` – the caller must pass a non-empty vector.  
   b) `s.len() < i32::MAX` – the length is strictly less than the largest positive `i32` so any valid index of `s` fits into the return type.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`) – the meaning of the return value  
   • If the function returns a **negative** number (`r < 0`) then it guarantees  
     `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≠ x`, i.e. *the element `x` does not occur anywhere in the vector*.  
   • If the function returns a **non-negative** number (`r ≥ 0`) then it guarantees the weaker property  
     `∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] = x`, i.e. *the element `x` is present somewhere* (the spec does **not** say that the particular index returned equals the location of `x`, only that such a location exists).

5. What the spec does **not** require  
   • No promise that `r` equals the *first* index (or any index) where `x` occurs.  
   • No upper bound on `r` besides the inherent `i32` limits. The proof obligation only looks at its sign.  
   • No side effects are mentioned and no ghost/state manipulation is required.

6. Practical consequences for an implementation  
   • A simple, provably correct implementation could scan the vector:  
        – If it finds `x`, return `0` (or the actual index, or any non-negative value).  
        – If it reaches the end without finding `x`, return `-1`.  
     Any choice that preserves the sign-based contract is acceptable.  
   • The length restrictions already make every legal index representable as an `i32`, avoiding overflow issues for the return value.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified function in Verus named `find_first_occurrence`.

Signature  
    fn find_first_occurrence(x: int, s: &Vec<int>) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
    • x – an unbounded mathematical integer to search for.  
    • s – an immutable reference to a vector of integers.

Outputs  
    • r – a 32-bit signed integer whose *sign* conveys whether `x` is present in `s`.

Pre-conditions  
    1. The vector is non-empty: `s.len() > 0`.  
    2. Its length fits in `i32`: `s.len() < i32::MAX`.

Post-conditions  
    • If `r` is negative (`r < 0`) then `x` is **not** in the vector:  
          ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≠ x  
    • If `r` is non-negative (`r ≥ 0`) then `x` **does** occur somewhere in the vector:  
          ∃ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] = x

No other guarantees are demanded; in particular the numeric value of `r` need not equal the index where `x` was found—only its sign matters. Any implementation meeting the above contract (for example, returning −1 when absent and the first index when present) is valid.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise asks for the maximum element of a non-empty slice.  
To increase difficulty without making the task unreasonable for a newcomer to Verus, the revised problems add one or more of the following:

• Require the index (or two indices) at which the optimum value(s) occur, and in one case the *first* such index.  
• Ask for the two largest *distinct* values, which obliges the implementer to reason about duplicates and strict inequalities.  
• Impose a parameterised sub-range, so the caller can select an interval inside the slice whose maximum must be returned, together with an index that must lie inside that range.

Each new problem is still solvable in a single forward scan of the slice, but proving the extra post-conditions requires more loop invariants and thus more sophisticated reasoning than the original task.


# Output
<<<
Implement a formally-verified function named `max_with_first_index`.

Signature  
    fn max_with_first_index(a: &[int]) -> (m: int, i: nat)

Inputs  
    a — an immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers.

Outputs  
    m — the greatest value found in the slice.  
    i — the *lowest* index at which that greatest value occurs.

Precondition  
    • a.len() ≥ 1.

Postconditions  
    1. Index validity  
       0 ≤ i < a.len().

    2. Value correspondence  
       m == a[i].

    3. Upper-bound property  
       ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ m ≥ a[k].

    4. Minimal-index (stability) property  
       ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < i ⇒ a[k] < m.  
       (i is the first position holding the maximum.)

No auxiliary traits or lemmas must be exposed; any implementation that, under the sole assumption that the slice is non-empty, returns `(m,i)` satisfying all four postconditions meets the specification.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified function named `two_largest_distinct`.

Signature  
    fn two_largest_distinct(a: &[int]) -> (max1: int, max2: int)

Inputs  
    a — an immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers.

Outputs  
    max1 — the greatest value in the slice.  
    max2 — the second-greatest *distinct* value in the slice.

Preconditions  
    1. a.len() ≥ 2.  
    2. There exist at least two indices holding different values:  
       ∃ p,q:int • 0 ≤ p < q < a.len() ∧ a[p] ≠ a[q].

Postconditions  
    1. Ordering  
       max1 > max2.

    2. Coverage  
       ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ max1.

    3. Maximality of max2 among values ≠ max1  
       ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ a[k] ≠ max1 ⇒ a[k] ≤ max2.

    4. Attainment  
       (∃ p:int • 0 ≤ p < a.len() ∧ a[p] == max1) ∧  
       (∃ q:int • 0 ≤ q < a.len() ∧ a[q] == max2).

Any body that satisfies these conditions is acceptable; a typical solution maintains two running candidates while scanning the slice once.
>>>
