Implement a formally-verified function named `range_max_with_index`.

Signature  
    fn range_max_with_index(a: &[int], start: nat, end: nat) -> (m: int, idx: nat)

Inputs  
    a      — an immutable slice of unbounded mathematical integers.  
    start  — inclusive lower bound of the sub-range.  
    end    — exclusive upper bound of the sub-range.

Outputs  
    m   — the greatest value found in the interval a[start .. end).  
    idx — an index where that value is attained and that lies inside the interval.

Preconditions  
    1. 0 ≤ start < end ≤ a.len().  
       (The interval is non-empty and within bounds.)

Postconditions  
    1. Index validity  
       start ≤ idx < end.

    2. Value correspondence  
       m == a[idx].

    3. Range upper-bound property  
       ∀ k:int • start ≤ k < end ⇒ m ≥ a[k].

    4. Irrelevance of values outside the interval  
       (No requirement is imposed on positions k < start or k ≥ end.)

A single forward scan of the designated sub-range, keeping current best value and its index, suffices to meet the specification; no dynamic allocation is necessary.
>>>

The function must return a vector that is an exact element-by-element copy of the input vector. No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are required; the specification is fully described by the two ensures clauses.

Any executable implementation that provably satisfies these contracts—such as calling `s.clone()` or iterating and pushing each character—is acceptable.

# Reasoning
The original task is trivial: “clone a `Vec<char>`.”  
To make the exercise more interesting we add requirements that force the
student to (a) introduce helper spec-functions, (b) reason about indices
and order, and (c) cope with non-trivial functional behaviour.  
Three stand-alone, progressively harder variations are proposed:

1.  Keep only the first occurrence of every character – the student must
    specify a permutation-free, order-preserving “distinct” filter and
    prove both soundness and completeness of the result set.

2.  Interleave the two halves of an even-length vector – this requires
    a mapping that jumps back and forth between the first and second
    half and forces an explicit reasoning about `2*i`/`2*i+1` indices.

3.  Extract characters that sit on prime indices (0,1,2,3 …) while
    preserving order – the student now has to define `is_prime`,
    `prime_count`, and an auxiliary “k-th prime below n” function, then
    connect them with the produced vector length and element mapping.

All three tasks are still feasible inside a short function, yet require
significantly more specification work and logical steps than a simple
clone.


# Output
<<<
Create a verified function in Verus/Rust that removes duplicates while
preserving the first occurrence order.

Signature  
fn retain_first_unique(s: &Vec<char>) -> (v: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
• s: immutable reference to a vector of `char` (Unicode scalar values).

Output  
• v: an owned `Vec<char>` that contains a subset of the elements of `s`.

Preconditions (requires)  
• none – the function must operate on any input vector, including the
  empty vector and vectors containing repeated characters.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. Subsequence:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < v.len() ⇒ ∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < s.len() ∧ v[i] == s[j].  
   Every character in `v` occurs somewhere in `s`.
2. No duplicates in the result:  
   ∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i < v.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < v.len() ∧ i ≠ j ⇒ v[i] ≠ v[j].
3. First-occurrence property:  
   ∀ c:char •  
       (∃ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v.len() ∧ v[k] == c)
       ⇔
       (∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < s.len() ∧ s[j] == c  
          ∧ ∀ t:int • 0 ≤ t < j ⇒ s[t] ≠ c).  
   A character `c` appears in `v` iff it appears in `s`, and in that
   case it is taken from the earliest index where `c` occurs in `s`.
4. Relative ordering of those first occurrences is preserved:  
   ∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i < v.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < v.len() ∧ i < j ⇒  
       first_index_in_s(v[i]) < first_index_in_s(v[j]),  
   where  
       spec fn first_index_in_s(c:char) -> int  
       { /* the smallest index j with s[j] == c, or −1 if absent */ }.

Interpretation  
The returned vector contains each distinct character of `s` exactly
once, in the order of their very first appearance within `s`.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function in Verus/Rust that interleaves the two halves
of an even-length character vector.

Signature  
fn interleave_halves(s: &Vec<char>) -> (v: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
• s: immutable reference to a vector of `char`.

Output  
• v: an owned `Vec<char>` whose length equals `s.len()`.

Preconditions (requires)  
• s.len() % 2 == 0,  i.e. the input length is even.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. v.len() == s.len().  
2. Let n = s.len() / 2.  For all i:int with 0 ≤ i < n  
      (a) v[2*i]     == s[i]             // element from first half  
      (b) v[2*i + 1] == s[i + n].       // element from second half  
3. No other positions exist: every index of `v` matches exactly one of
   the two cases above.

Interpretation  
If the input is  
   s = [a0, a1, …, a(n-1), b0, b1, …, b(n-1)]  
the result must be  
   v = [a0, b0, a1, b1, …, a(n-1), b(n-1)].

No auxiliary lemmas are mandatory, but a small spec function computing
`n = s.len()/2` and the explicit mapping `2*i` / `2*i+1` is likely
helpful.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function in Verus/Rust that extracts all characters
that reside at prime indices of the input vector, preserving their
original order.

Signature  
fn primes_only(s: &Vec<char>) -> (v: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
• s: immutable reference to a vector of `char`.

Output  
• v: an owned `Vec<char>` consisting precisely of those `s[i]` where
  `i` is prime (using the standard mathematical definition: primes are
  integers ≥ 2 divisible only by 1 and themselves).

Preconditions (requires)  
• none.

Helper spec-functions (must be provided by the implementer)  
  spec fn is_prime(n:int) -> bool  
  spec fn prime_count_below(n:int) -> int  
    ensures: returns the number of primes p with 0 ≤ p < n.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. v.len() == prime_count_below(s.len()).  
2. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v.len() ⇒  
       let i = k_th_prime_below(s.len(), k); // 0-based “kth prime < n”  
       v[k] == s[i].  
   (The student may introduce a helper function `k_th_prime_below`
   together with a lemma that it indeed returns the k-th prime.)
3. Completeness:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ is_prime(i) ⇒  
       ∃ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v.len() ∧ v[k] == s[i]  
       ∧ k == index_of_prime(i).  
   In other words, every character whose position is prime appears
   exactly once in the result and at the correct order position.

Interpretation  
`v` is exactly the subsequence of `s` formed by the elements at indices
2, 3, 5, 7, 11, … (primes), with no omissions and no reordering.
>>>

Implement a verified function in Verus–Rust named `best_time_to_buy_and_sell_stock`.

Signature  
```
fn best_time_to_buy_and_sell_stock(prices: &Vec<u32>) -> (max_profit: u32)
```

Inputs  
• `prices`: immutable reference to a vector of 32-bit unsigned integers, representing daily stock prices.

Output  
• `max_profit`: a `u32` value that upper-bounds every possible single-transaction profit obtainable from the input sequence.

Formal contract  

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
1. The vector is non-empty and not too large: `1 ≤ prices.len() ≤ 100 000`.  
2. Each recorded price is at most 10 000:  
   `∀ i:nat.  i < prices.len() ⇒ prices[i] ≤ 10 000`.

Post-condition (`ensures`)  
For all natural indices `i, j` with `i` strictly before `j` and both inside the vector, the return value dominates the “buy at i, sell at j” profit:  
```
∀ i:nat, j:nat.
       0 ≤ i < j ∧ j < prices.len()
   ⇒  max_profit ≥ prices[j] - prices[i]
```

Interpretation  
• The contract guarantees that `max_profit` is no *smaller* than the largest achievable profit using exactly one buy-then-sell operation.  
• It may equal that maximum or exceed it; the spec does not enforce minimality.  
• When `prices.len() == 1`, there are no admissible `(i, j)` pairs, so any `u32` value would satisfy the post-condition (returning `0` is conventional).

No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are involved; the whole behaviour is captured by the single quantified post-condition.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

struct Extractor {
    thresholds: Vec<i32>,
}

impl Extractor {
    spec fn valid(&self) -> bool {
        forall|m: nat, n: nat| 0 <= m && m < n && n < self.thresholds.len() ==> self.thresholds[m as int] <= self.thresholds[n as int]
    }

    fn rotate(&self, a: &[int], offset: usize) -> (b: Vec<int>)
        requires
            0 <= offset,
            offset < a.len(),
            a.len() > 0,
            a.len() < 100000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
            a.len() < usize::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
            offset < usize::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        ensures
            b.len() == a.len(),
            forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> b[i] == a[(i + offset as int) % a.len() as int],
    {
```

# Reasoning
The program declares

1. A concrete data‐structure
   • struct Extractor { thresholds: Vec<i32> } – a heap vector of 32-bit signed integers.

2. A pure specification method on Extractor
   • spec fn valid(&self) -> bool
     expresses that thresholds is non-decreasing: for every pair of natural
       indices m,n with 0 ≤ m < n < thresholds.len()
       we have thresholds[m] ≤ thresholds[n].

3. An executable (but still fully verified) impl method
   • fn rotate(&self, a: &[int], offset: usize) -> (b: Vec<int>)

   Inputs
     – &self: only needed so the function can live inside the impl block;
       rotate’s contract does not reference self nor require self.valid().
     – a: &[int]   an immutable slice of mathematical ints (the ghost view of an
       array; int is the unbounded integer type in Verus).
     – offset: usize   the amount by which the slice is to be rotated.

   Pre-conditions (requires)
     1. 0 ≤ offset
     2. offset < a.len()              (offset is strictly inside the index range)
     3. a.len() > 0                   (slice must be non-empty so modulo is defined)
     4. a.len() < 100 000             (runtime safeguard against very large vectors)
     5. a.len() < usize::MAX/2        (overflow-avoidance guard)
     6. offset < usize::MAX/2         (same idea for offset)

   Post-conditions (ensures)
     1. b.len() == a.len()            (result has exactly the same length)
     2. ∀ i:int, 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒
            b[i] == a[(i + offset) mod a.len()]
        i.e. the result is a *cyclic left rotation* of the input by ‘offset’
        positions: element at logical index i in the returned vector equals the
        element that was at index (i+offset) of the original slice, computed
        modulo the length.

   Output
     – b: Vec<int>    a newly allocated vector that realises the rotated sequence.

No other ghost code or helper lemmas are referenced; only the two clauses above
completely characterise rotate’s required behaviour.

# Final Description
<<<
You must implement a verified method Extractor::rotate that produces a left-rotation of an integer slice, as well as understand the auxiliary specification Extractor::valid.

DATA TYPE
  struct Extractor { thresholds: Vec<i32> }

SPECIFICATION METHOD
  spec fn valid(&self) -> bool
    Purpose: state that ‘thresholds’ is sorted in non-decreasing order.
    Formal definition:
      ∀ m,n : nat • 0 ≤ m < n < thresholds.len() ⇒
        thresholds[m] ≤ thresholds[n].

PRIMARY FUNCTION TO IMPLEMENT
  fn rotate(&self, a: &[int], offset: usize) -> (b: Vec<int>)

Inputs
  • &self               (not otherwise constrained)
  • a: &[int]           an immutable slice of mathematical integers
  • offset: usize       number of positions to rotate

Pre-conditions (requires)
  1. 0 ≤ offset
  2. offset < a.len()
  3. a.len() > 0
  4. a.len() < 100_000
  5. a.len() < usize::MAX / 2
  6. offset   < usize::MAX / 2
  (Rules 4-6 merely ensure arithmetic on usize cannot overflow inside the body.)

Output
  • b: Vec<int>         the rotated sequence stored in a newly created vector

Post-conditions (ensures)
  1. b.len() == a.len()                                   – length is preserved
  2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒
         b[i] == a[(i + offset) mod a.len()]              – cyclic left rotation

Interpretation: element i of the result equals the element that was originally
located ‘offset’ positions to its right in the input slice. When offset = 0 the
function must return a faithful copy of a; when offset = 1 it must move every
element one place to the left, wrapping the first element to the end, etc.

No additional helper functions or ghost state are necessary. Any executable
body that satisfies the pre-/post-conditions will be accepted.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original task is easy because  
• the implementer is allowed to return *any* number that is **at least** the real maximum profit, so a trivial implementation such as “always return `u32::MAX`” passes;  
• no information about the days of the trade is demanded, and  
• no minimal-value or stability requirements exist.

To increase the difficulty slightly yet keep the exercise focused on elementary array reasoning, I propose three independent, self-contained problems:

Problem 1 (moderately harder)  
  – The function must return the *exact* maximum profit, **and** the indices of the optimal buy/sell days.  
  – In case several day-pairs yield the same profit, a stability rule chooses the lexicographically smallest pair.  
  – The learner must therefore prove both optimality and the tie-breaking property.

Problem 2 (conceptual extension)  
  – Exactly one transaction is still required, but the function must additionally prove that the returned buy day occurs *strictly before* any day on which the minimal price of the whole array appears.  
  – This introduces an uncommon, more specific constraint which forces finer reasoning about the first occurrence of minima.

Problem 3 (added reasoning steps)  
  – The vector can contain *at most* 100 000 entries, but the function must show that it runs in *O(n)* time **and** in *O(1)* extra space.  
  – Verus cannot check asymptotic bounds mechanically, so the learner has to insert ghost counters and fuel lemmas in the spec, making the proof longer.

All three problems keep the same thematic domain (best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock) yet require fuller functional correctness proofs, stability arguments, or explicit complexity/contracts, thereby raising the bar while remaining tractable in Verus.

```rust
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn fib(n: u64) -> (result: u128)
    requires
        n < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        result == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We have one executable function (no ghost helpers) whose signature and contracts we must re-express unambiguously.

1.  Name  
    fib  — despite the name it is NOT the usual Fibonacci function; the post-condition shows it actually computes the “triangular number” Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ i = n(n+1)/2.

2.  Inputs  
    n : u64  
    A non-negative 64-bit unsigned integer supplied by the caller.

3.  Output  
    result : u128  
    A 128-bit unsigned integer returned by the function.

4.  Preconditions (requires)  
    • n < 1_000_000.  
    • n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.  
      Since u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, the second line collapses to n < 1.  
      Therefore both lines together force n to be 0 (the only u64 smaller than 1).  
      In other words the function is only allowed to be called with n == 0.

5.  Postcondition (ensures)  
    result == n * (n + 1) / 2.  
    The arithmetic takes place in the u128 domain (because result is u128 and the
    multiplication/division are implicitly widened), so no overflow occurs.
    Under the precondition (n == 0) this reduces to: result == 0.

6.  Practical consequence  
    The contract is extremely restrictive: every legal call must use n = 0 and
    the function must return 0.  Any implementation that always returns 0 and
    checks/assumes the precondition will satisfy the spec.

There are no helper lemmas, no decreases clauses, and no additional attributes relevant to the spec itself.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function with the following exact contract.

Signature
    fn fib(n: u64) -> (result: u128)

Inputs
    n: a 64-bit unsigned integer.

Return value
    result: a 128-bit unsigned integer.

Preconditions (requires)
    1.  n < 1_000_000
    2.  n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX   // this is equivalent to n < 1

Because u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, the two requirements jointly imply that the
only admissible argument is n == 0.

Postcondition (ensures)
    result == n * (n + 1) / 2

Since the caller can only supply n = 0, the post-condition practically demands
that the function return 0.

There are no auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or decreases clauses.  An
implementation may simply assert the preconditions and then return 0 to satisfy

```rust
    requires

        y.value >= 0,
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We read the specification:

1. A concrete data‐type is declared

   struct Nat { value: u64 }

   It is nothing more than a wrapper around an unsigned 64-bit integer.  
   Because the inner type is u64, every Nat is guaranteed to store a non-negative number; nevertheless the spec writer redundantly restates that fact in the pre-conditions.

2. The executable function under specification is

   fn add(x: Nat, y: Nat) -> (result: Nat)

   • x and y are passed by value, i.e. ownership is moved into the function.  
   • The function returns a brand-new Nat called result.

3. Preconditions (requires)

   a) x.value >= 0  
   b) y.value >= 0  
      - redundant for u64 but explicitly asserted.

   c) x.value < u64::MAX / 2  
   d) y.value < u64::MAX / 2  
      - guarantee that each operand is strictly less than half the maximum representable 64-bit unsigned integer.  
      - This implies x.value + y.value < u64::MAX, so the addition can be performed without risk of overflow in both the executable code and the logical post-condition.

   There are no additional constraints; x and y may be equal, zero, etc.

4. Postcondition (ensures)

   result.value == x.value + y.value

   In words: the Nat returned by the function must store the exact sum of the two input values (interpreted in the mathematical ℕ domain). Because of the range guard above, this sum is representable in a u64.

5. No other clauses

   • No “requires” involving ownership/borrowing.  
   • No “ensures” about aliasing because all data are moved.  
   • No helper spec functions, no decreases clause—it is a single expression function.  
   • No mention of ghost fields; everything is concrete.

6. Subtleties for somebody re-implementing the spec

   • The parameter and return types are Nat, not plain u64.  
   • Preconditions must include all four listed inequalities, even though two of them are tautologies for u64.  
   • The postcondition must compare result.value to the sum of the two operands’ .value fields with exact equality (==).  
   • The function is total only for arguments satisfying the range guards; otherwise its behaviour is unspecified.  

A faithful re-implementation needs to:

   • Recreate the Nat struct.  
   • Write the add function with the exact signature, the four requires clauses, and the single ensures clause.  
   • Make sure the ensures refers to result.value and to x.value + y.value in that order.  
   • No ghosts or extra parameters are necessary.

    4. y.value < u64::MAX / 2

   The last two bounds ensure that x.value + y.value is strictly less than u64::MAX, so the addition cannot overflow.

No additional helper spec functions, decreases clauses, or attributes are required. The body can be a straightforward unchecked addition because the range guards make overflow impossible, but any implementation that satisfies the contract is acceptable.

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: return the minimum element of a non-empty slice.  
To make the exercise more involved I considered a few orthogonal ways to increase the reasoning burden yet still keep the task suitable for a single routine:

1. Make the function return *more than one* piece of information that must be mutually consistent – e.g. the minimum **and** how many times it appears.
2. Demand that some *structural* property (first/last position of the minimum) be preserved, which forces the implementer to reason simultaneously about values **and** indices.
3. Generalise from the single minimum to the *k-th* smallest element, requiring an additional parameter and universally-quantified proofs that cover all “earlier” elements.

Each new version remains small enough for one routine, but it now needs auxiliary ghost state or an additional pass to prove the richer post-conditions.  
All problems are written as fully-stand-alone descriptions so that a spec generator can work directly from the text.

      The slice must not be empty.

Postconditions (ensures)

    2. occurrences = |{ i:int | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] = min }|  
       (occurrences equals the cardinality of the set of positions holding the minimum)
    3. occurrences > 0

    4. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] = min ⇒ i < s.len()               
       (well-formedness of the quantified set above; automatically discharged)

In plain words, the function must return both the smallest value of the slice and exactly how many times that value occurs.

No other behavioural guarantees are required, and f must be used only for specification-level reasoning.

```rust
fn mult(a: u64, b: u64) -> (x: u64)
    requires
```

The snippet declares a single executable function mult with an accompanying formal specification.

1. Inputs  
   • a: u64 — an unsigned 64-bit integer  
   • b: u64 — another unsigned 64-bit integer  

2. Output  
   • x: u64 — the function’s sole return value

3. Preconditions (requires)  
   • a ≥ 0 and b ≥ 0 — redundant for unsigned types but explicitly listed  
   • a * b < u64::MAX — the mathematical product of the two inputs must be strictly less than the maximum representable u64 value (2⁶⁴ − 1).  This guarantees that the runtime multiplication will not overflow.

4. Postcondition (ensures)  
   • x == a * b — the function must return exactly the mathematical product of a and b (within the u64 domain).

5. Helper functions / lemmas  
   • None; the specification is self-contained.

6. Subtleties  
   • Because the precondition rules out any pair whose product is ≥ u64::MAX, the implementer may safely use normal Rust multiplication without worrying about wrap-around.  
   • If either input is 0 the third precondition is automatically satisfied since the product is 0.  
   • The specification does not accept the edge case where the product equals u64::MAX itself; the bound is strict (<, not ≤).

Postcondition (ensures)

# Reasoning
The original problem is straightforward: allocate a new vector and copy `a[i] ^ b[i]`.  
To increase the difficulty we can

• Introduce *state-changing* behaviour, forcing the learner to use Verus’ `old` expressions and in-place modification reasoning.  
• Combine *aggregation* with element-wise reasoning, so they must prove a fold-like property in addition to the per-index fact.  
• Apply the XOR only on indices satisfying a non-trivial predicate (e.g. “prime index”), which requires the learner to supply and verify an auxiliary `is_prime` spec.  

Below are three standalone, detailed problem statements reflecting those ideas.

# Output
<<<
Implement and formally verify a Verus-Rust procedure with the following contract.

Function name  
    bitwise_xor_in_place

Signature  
    fn bitwise_xor_in_place(a: &mut Vec<u32>, b: &Vec<u32>)

Inputs  
• `a` – mutable reference to a `Vec<u32>` that will be **overwritten in place**  
• `b` – immutable reference to another `Vec<u32>`

Output  
• The function returns `()` (unit).

Preconditions (requires)  
1. The two vectors have identical length: `a.len() == b.len()`.  
2. The two references are *disjoint*: `a` and `b` must not alias (Verus’ `&mut` vs `&` already enforces this, but state it explicitly).

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. Length is preserved: `a.len() == old(a).len()`.  
2. Element-wise update:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == old(a)[i] ^ b[i]  
   (every element of `a` after the call equals the bitwise XOR of its **old** value and the corresponding element of `b`).  
3. Vector `b` is unchanged: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < b.len() ⇒ b[i] == old(b)[i].  
4. Time and additional memory complexity are both Θ(n) and Θ(1) respectively (only a constant number of extra `u32` temporaries may be allocated).

You must supply all helper lemmas (if any) needed to reason about `old` values, loop invariants and slice mutation.  Verification must establish every postcondition.
>>>

<<<
Implement and formally verify a Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    bitwise_xor_fold

Signature  
    fn bitwise_xor_fold(a: &Vec<u32>, b: &Vec<u32>) -> (accum: u32)

Inputs  
• `a`, `b` – immutable references to two equally-long `Vec<u32>`.

Output  
• `accum` – a single `u32` value.

Preconditions (requires)  
• Equal length: `a.len() == b.len()`.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. Folding correctness:  
   `accum == (∀* i:int | 0 ≤ i < a.len() :: a[i] ^ b[i])`  
   where `∀*` denotes the bitwise XOR fold (i.e. `accum` equals the XOR of **all** `a[i] ^ b[i]` values, not just pairwise).  
2. Idempotence on empty input: if `a.len() == 0` then `accum == 0`.  
3. The function allocates **no heap memory at all** (only local scalar variables are permitted).

Hint: provide a ghost specification of a fold helper lemma  
`pub closed spec fn xor_fold(v: &Vec<u32>) -> u32`  
and prove that your implementation coincides with that definition.  Your verification should exhibit a loop whose invariant maintains the running fold and the processed prefix length.
>>>

<<<
Implement and formally verify a Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    selective_xor_on_primes

Signature  
    fn selective_xor_on_primes(a: &Vec<u32>, b: &Vec<u32>) -> (out: Vec<u32>)

Inputs  
• `a`, `b` – immutable references to two `Vec<u32>` of the same length.

Output  
• `out` – newly allocated `Vec<u32>`.

Preconditions (requires)  
• Same length: `a.len() == b.len()`.  
• Length does not exceed 10 000 (prevents large primality proofs): `a.len() ≤ 10_000`.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. Length preserved: `out.len() == a.len()`.  
2. Element-wise rule, using the *index’s primality*:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < out.len() ⇒  
      (is_prime(i as nat) ⇒ out[i] == a[i] ^ b[i]) ∧  
      (!is_prime(i as nat) ⇒ out[i] == a[i]).  
3. Vectors `a` and `b` remain unchanged.

Additional requirements  
• You must supply a ghost, purely-spec `is_prime(n: nat) -> bool` definition and any lemmas needed to reason about it (e.g. that 0 and 1 are not prime, and that primes are ≥ 2).  
• The implementation must run in O(n log n) time or better and may allocate memory only for `out`.  
• Proof obligations must cover both correctness of the index test and the preservation of untouched elements.

Because Verus cannot symbolically execute an arbitrary primality test, your *code* may call a compiled helper `fn fast_is_prime(u: u32) -> bool` for the runtime check, but the *spec* must rely exclusively on the logical `is_prime` predicate you define, and you must connect the two with an `ensures` clause on the helper to make the proof go through.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task (“find the index of *a* maximum element in a non-empty slice of mathematical integers”) is essentially a one–loop proof obligation:  
• show the loop preserves the current-maximum invariant,  
• show termination, then  
• show the post-conditions.

To make it more challenging without changing the didactic flavour, I prepared three strictly harder variants, each of which forces the student to combine several invariants or reason about a less common order relation.

Variant 1 (“find_max_and_count”)  
– Adds a second return value: the **number of occurrences** of the maximum.  
– Demands that the chosen index is the **left-most** maximum, not just any.  
– Proof obligations: (a) traditional maximum correctness, (b) counting argument, and (c) minimality of the index.  
– Adds only one loop variable but already requires an extra quantified post-condition and an argument about minimality.

Variant 2 (“find_two_distinct_maxes”)  
– Input must have at least two *different* values.  
– Returns indices of the (strict) maximum and of the **largest strictly smaller** value (“second maximum”).  
– Post-conditions involve three mutually interacting properties and exclusion of ties; forces a more delicate case analysis.

Variant 3 (“find_lexicographic_max”)  
– The element type is `(int,int)` with the **lexicographic** order (first field primary key, second field tie-breaker).  
– Students must encode and use this non-standard order relation formally in the specification.  
– Proof becomes multi-field: they must carry and compare two integers simultaneously.

All three specs remain implementable with an O(n) traversal and no auxiliary lemmas, but each requires a noticeably richer invariant set than the original exercise.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function called `find_max_and_count` that, given a non-empty slice of mathematical integers, returns (1) the **first** index at which the maximum value occurs and (2) the total number of times that maximum appears.

Signature  
    fn find_max_and_count(a: &[int]) -> (i: usize, c: nat)

Contracts  
• Preconditions  
    – a.len() > 0                                           // slice is non-empty

• Postconditions  
    – 0 <= i && i < a.len()                                 // index is in range  
    – c > 0                                                // at least one occurrence  
    – ∀ k:int. 0 <= k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i]               // a[i] is a maximum value  
    – ∀ k:int. 0 <= k < i     ⇒ a[k] <  a[i]               // i is the left-most maximum  
    – c == |{ k:int | 0 <= k < a.len() && a[k] == a[i] }|   // c counts all maxima exactly

Interpretation  
The function must locate the earliest maximum and also report how many times that maximum appears in the slice. Any implementation whose proof establishes the five post-conditions above is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function `find_two_distinct_maxes` that returns indices of the largest and the second largest *distinct* elements in a slice of mathematical integers.

Signature  
    fn find_two_distinct_maxes(a: &[int]) -> (i_max: usize, i_second: usize)

Contracts  
• Preconditions  
    – a.len() >= 2                                                   // need at least two elements  
    – ∃ k:int, l:int. 0 <= k < l < a.len() && a[k] ≠ a[l]            // not all elements equal

• Postconditions  
    – 0 <= i_max    && i_max    < a.len()                            // both indices in range  
    – 0 <= i_second && i_second < a.len()  
    – i_max ≠ i_second                                               // indices are different  
    – ∀ k:int. 0 <= k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i_max]                    // a[i_max] is (a) maximum  
    – ∀ k:int. 0 <= k < a.len() && k ≠ i_max ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i_second]    // a[i_second] ≥ all others except maybe the true max  
    – a[i_second] < a[i_max]                                         // second value is strictly lower  
    – ∀ k:int. 0 <= k < a.len() && a[k] < a[i_max] ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i_second] // maximal among the rest

Interpretation  
The slice has at least two distinct values. The function must return an index of a (strict) maximum and an index of the largest strictly smaller element, proving the ordering and minimality conditions listed above.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function `find_lexicographic_max` that finds an index of the maximum element in a non-empty slice of pairs `(int, int)` under **lexicographic order**:  
`(x₁,y₁) < (x₂,y₂)`  iff  `x₁ < x₂`  ∨  (`x₁ == x₂` ∧ `y₁ < y₂`).

Signature  
    fn find_lexicographic_max(a: &[(int,int)]) -> (i: usize)

Contracts  
• Preconditions  
    – a.len() > 0

• Postconditions  
    – 0 <= i && i < a.len()                                                  // index inside slice  
    – ∀ k:int. 0 <= k < a.len() ⇒                                           // chosen element is ≥ every other element  
        (a[k].0 <  a[i].0) ∨  
        (a[k].0 == a[i].0 && a[k].1 ≤ a[i].1)

Interpretation  
The function must return any index whose element is maximal under lexicographic ordering of the pair components described above. If several indices qualify, any one may be returned as long as the quantified post-condition is satisfied.
>>>

```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Returns true if and only if array a is sorted.
fn is_sorted(a: &[i32]) -> (is_sorted: bool)
    ensures
        is_sorted <==> forall|i: int, j: int| 1 <= i && i < a.len() && j == i - 1 ==> a[i] >= a[j],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The signature depicts one executable function, is_sorted.

1. Inputs and their types  
   • a : &[i32] — an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.  
   The slice may be empty or of any length; there are no pre-conditions.

2. Output  
   • is_sorted : bool — the Boolean result returned by the function.

3. Post-condition  
   `ensures is_sorted <==> forall|i: int, j: int| 1 <= i && i < a.len() && j == i - 1 ==> a[i] >= a[j]`

   Read aloud:  
   “The returned Boolean is_sorted is true exactly when for every integer index i in the range 1 … a.len()-1, and for j equal to i-1 (so j is the predecessor of i), the element at index i is greater than or equal to the element at index j.”

   • `<==>` is logical equivalence: the function may return true only if the property holds, and must return false if the property does not hold.  
   • The quantified indices are mathematical integers (`int`) to avoid overflow concerns in the specification.  
   • Because the predicate is expressed only on consecutive pairs, it is equivalent to the usual “non-decreasing order” definition: every element is at least as large as the element immediately before it.  
   • When the slice has length 0 or 1, the antecedent of the implication is never satisfied, so the quantified statement is vacuously true; hence the post-condition demands that is_sorted be true in those cases.

4. Pre-conditions  
   None (`requires` clause absent). The function must work for any slice reference.

5. Auxiliary spec artefacts  
   None. The property is stated directly in the post-condition; no helper lemmas or spec functions are referenced.

6. Implementation freedom  
   Any runtime algorithm that evaluates the sortedness of the slice and returns the appropriate Boolean will satisfy the contract, provided it proves the post-condition. A typical implementation would iterate once through the slice and compare consecutive elements.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Write a formally verified Verus-Rust function called `is_sorted`.

Signature  
fn is_sorted(a: &[i32]) -> (is_sorted: bool)

Inputs  
• a — an immutable slice (`&[i32]`) containing 32-bit signed integers.  
There are no restrictions on the slice’s length or on the numeric values stored in it.

Output  
• is_sorted — a Boolean that must indicate whether the slice is sorted in non-decreasing order.

Specification

# Reasoning
The original task merely asked the student to decide whether *all* characters inside a slice are identical.  
To make variants that are still “one–file” Verus specs, yet require noticeably deeper reasoning (extra quantifiers, alternation, counting, existence of witnesses, etc.), I propose three alternative problems:

1.  Alternating-pair property – the slice must consist of two *different* characters that strictly alternate (`c₀, c₁, c₀, c₁ …`).  Verifying this forces the learner to reason about both parity and equality simultaneously and to *produce* the two witnesses `c₀`, `c₁` in the post-condition.

2.  Majority-element finder – return `Option<char>` that is `Some(c)` **iff** `c` occurs *strictly more* than half the time.  The post-conditions must mention counts, uniqueness of the majority element, and the `None` case.  This introduces multiset / counting reasoning that is rarer in beginner examples.

3.  Exactly-one-mismatch palindrome – determine whether the slice is a palindrome **allowing at most one differing position**.  The student must reason over pairs of mirrored indices, count mismatches, and supply an *existence* witness when the answer is `false`.

Each description below is completely standalone, giving enough detail for an automatic spec generator to create the Verus signature, helper spec-functions, `requires`, and `ensures` clauses.

# Output
<<<
Function name: `is_strictly_alternating_pair`

Inputs  
• `s: &[char]` – an immutable slice of Unicode scalar values.

Return value  
• `result: bool`

Informal behaviour  
`result` is `true` exactly when the slice `s` is non-empty **and** there exist **two different characters** `first` and `second` such that  
 • every even index `2k` contains `first`,  
 • every odd index `2k+1` (that is still in bounds) contains `second`.

In other words, the slice must look like `first, second, first, second, …` for its entire length.  
If the slice has length 1 that element may be considered `first` and the property is trivially satisfied.

Pre-condition (`requires`)  
• `true` (no pre-condition).

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. `result == true` ⇒  
   ∃ `first: char`, `second: char` .  
   `first ≠ second ∧  
     for all valid indices i:  
        (i % 2 == 0 ⇒ s[i] == first) ∧  
        (i % 2 == 1 ⇒ s[i] == second)`  

2. `result == false` ⇒  
   `s.len() ≥ 2` ∧  
   for every possible pair of characters `c₀ ≠ c₁` either parity is violated or some index fails to match the required character.

Hints / helper spec-functions that may be useful  
• `is_alternating(s: Seq<char>, first: char, second: char) -> bool` verifying the two-character alternation.  
• parity helper `is_even(i:int)`.

Time / space expectations  
Linear time over the length of the slice, constant extra memory.
>>>

<<<
Function name: `majority_char`

Inputs  
• `s: &[char]`

Return value  
• `result: Option<char>` (use Rust’s `Option`).

Informal behaviour  
`result` is `Some(c)` **iff** the character `c` appears **strictly more than half** of the total positions of `s`.  
If no such character exists (including the empty slice) `result` must be `None`.

Pre-condition (`requires`)  
• `true`

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
Let `n = s.len()`.  
1. `result == Some(c)` ⇒  
   `#(i | 0 ≤ i < n ∧ s[i] == c) > n / 2` ∧  
   for every character `d ≠ c`  
   `#(i | 0 ≤ i < n ∧ s[i] == d) < #(i | 0 ≤ i < n ∧ s[i] == c)`  

2. `result == None` ⇒  
   for **all** characters `d`  
   `#(i | 0 ≤ i < n ∧ s[i] == d) ≤ n / 2`

Helper spec-functions that may be defined  
• `count(s: Seq<char>, ch: char) -> nat` returning the number of occurrences.  
• `is_majority(s, ch) -> bool`.

Complexity requirement  
The implementation should be better than quadratic; an `O(n)` scan with constant extra storage (e.g., Boyer-Moore majority vote plus verification pass) is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Function name: `almost_palindrome`

Inputs  
• `s: &[char]`

Return value  
• `result: bool`

Informal behaviour  
`result` is `true` when the slice forms a palindrome **allowing at most one mismatched pair**.  
Formally, let `n = s.len()`.  Consider all index pairs `(i, n-1-i)` with `0 ≤ i < n/2`.  
Count how many of those pairs have `s[i] ≠ s[n-1-i]`.  
If that count is `0` or `1`, the function should return `true`; otherwise `false`.

Pre-condition (`requires`)  
• `true`

Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
1. `result == true` ⇒  
   `#(i | 0 ≤ i < n/2 ∧ s[i] ≠ s[n-1-i]) ≤ 1`

2. `result == false` ⇒  
   `n ≥ 3` ∧  
   `#(i | 0 ≤ i < n/2 ∧ s[i] ≠ s[n-1-i]) ≥ 2`  

 Additionally provide a witness of failure:  
   ∃ indices `i`, `j` with `0 ≤ i < j < n/2` such that both pairs `(i, n-1-i)` and `(j, n-1-j)` are mismatches.

Suggested helper spec-functions  
• `mirror_index(n:int, i:int) -> int` returning `n-1-i`.  
• `mismatch_count(s: Seq<char>) -> nat`.

Expected complexity  
Linear time, constant extra memory.
>>>

The original exercise is quite straightforward: simply copy every element from a non-empty slice into a `Vec`, keeping the order intact.  
To make the task more challenging we can:
1. Introduce richer post-conditions (e.g. permutation, order, or uniqueness properties) so that the implementation needs auxiliary proofs instead of a single “index-by-index” equality.
2. Add extra parameters that force modular arithmetic or conditional behaviour, thereby lengthening the proof path.
3. Require the result to satisfy a structural property (sortedness, prefix sums, rotation, filtering, etc.) that is not trivially inherited from the input slice.

Below are three strictly-harder variants, each self-contained and expressed with enough precision for a Verus spec generator to work from the text alone.

Signature  
    pub fn slice_to_sorted_vec(a: &[int]) -> (s: Vec<int>)

Specifications  

Pre-condition  
• a.len() > 0                                                   — the slice must contain at least one element.

Post-conditions  
1. s.len() == a.len()                                           — no element has been lost or duplicated by length.  
2. multiset(s) == multiset(a)                                   — treating each input as a bag element, multiplicities are preserved.  
3. ∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i ≤ j < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≤ s[j]           — the resulting vector is sorted in non-decreasing order.

Notes  
• You may implement any algorithm (e.g., `sort_unstable`) as long as all three properties are formally proven.  
• You may define helper ghost functions or lemmas such as `multiset` or `is_sorted` if necessary.
>>>

<<<
Write a public Verus-Rust function called `rotate_left` that rotates a non-empty slice of mathematical integers to the left by `k` positions and returns the result as a new vector.

Signature  
    pub fn rotate_left(a: &[int], k: nat) -> (r: Vec<int>)

Specifications  

Pre-conditions  
1. a.len() > 0                                                  — input slice cannot be empty.  
2. k < a.len()                                                  — rotation amount must be strictly less than the length.

Post-conditions  
1. r.len() == a.len()                                           — rotation never changes length.  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ r[i] == a[(i + k) mod a.len()]   — element at index `i` in the result equals the element that was `k` positions to its right (with wrap-around) in the original slice.  
3. permutation(r, a)                                            — `r` is merely a re-ordering of `a` (you may provide a helper definition of `permutation`).

Hints  
• A typical algorithm copies two continuous segments, but any provably correct method is acceptable.  
• Use modular arithmetic lemmas to discharge the wrap-around proof obligations.
>>>

<<<
Implement a Verus-Rust function named `positive_prefix_sums` that converts a non-empty slice of integers into the vector of cumulative sums of its strictly positive elements, preserving encounter order of positives and omitting non-positives entirely.

Signature  
    pub fn positive_prefix_sums(a: &[int]) -> (s: Vec<int>)

Definitions  
Let `pos_indices` be the strictly increasing sequence of indices i where a[i] > 0.  
Let `p(j)` denote the j-th element of `pos_indices` (0-based).

Pre-condition  
• a.len() > 0.

Post-conditions  
1. s.len() == pos_indices.len()                                 — exactly one output per positive element.  
2. ∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < s.len() ⇒                                  — each entry is a prefix sum over positives only  
       s[j] == Σ_{t=0}^{j} a[p(t)].  
3. s.len() == 0 ⇔ ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ 0         — the result is empty precisely when no positive inputs exist.  
4. ∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < s.len() − 1 ⇒ s[j] < s[j+1]               — consecutive prefix sums are strictly increasing.

Guidance  
• You will need ghost code to reason about the list of positive indices and the summation.  
• A single traversal suffices, but any verified algorithm meeting the spec is acceptable.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]

use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Proof function to calculate the Fibonacci sequence
fn fib(n: u64) -> (x: u128)
    requires
        n < 100000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        x == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We must describe the specification of function fib in natural language, very detailed.

Inputs: n of type u64. Output: x of type u128.

Preconditions:

- `n < 100000`.
- `n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX`.

Since `u64::MAX` is the maximum value representable in u64, dividing it by itself yields 1, so the second pre-condition simplifies to `n < 1`, i.e., `n == 0`. The first precondition is redundant once the second one is understood but is still part of the contract.

Postcondition:

- `x == n * (n + 1) / 2`.

That identity is the closed-form of the sum of the first `n` natural numbers (the n-th triangular number), not the Fibonacci sequence suggested by the comment. The arithmetic in the post-condition is carried out in the `u128` domain because `x` is a 128-bit unsigned integer; nevertheless the two pre-conditions guarantee the product never overflows either operand’s type.

No helper spec functions, no decreases clauses, no additional ensures or modifies. The function’s body is not shown; any implementation that satisfies the contract is allowed.

Subtleties:

- The strange second pre-condition (`n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX`) effectively forces `n` to be zero, because the right-hand side is `1`.
- Because of that, the only admissible input is `n == 0`, and therefore the only admissible output is `x == 0` (since 0 * 1 / 2 = 0).
- Despite the doc comment, the contract has nothing to do with Fibonacci numbers.

Pre-conditions (requires)
    1. n < 100 000.
    2. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.  
       Because u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, this second condition is equivalent to n < 1, i.e. the caller may only pass the value n = 0.  Both requirements must be asserted explicitly.

Post-condition (ensures)
    x == n * (n + 1) / 2.  
    In other words, the function must return the n-th triangular number, computed exactly in the mathematical sense (the sum of the integers 1 through n), and produced as a u128 value.

Auxiliary specification items
    None — no helper spec functions, lemmas or ghost state are referenced.

Observations / subtleties essential for an implementer
    • Although the doc comment mentions Fibonacci, the contract concerns triangular numbers; follow the contract, not the comment.  
    • Given the second pre-condition, the only legal runtime input is n = 0.  Consequently every admissible call must result in x = 0.  Your implementation must still satisfy the stated post-condition for that (unique) admissible case.  
    • The arithmetic in the ensures clause is in the unbounded mathematical domain, but n’s small bound means concrete code using u128 arithmetic will not overflow.  
    • The function body may be anything that Verus can prove to meet the contract; it could even be a one-liner returning 0 after asserting the pre-conditions.

This description contains all information necessary to recreate the original function specification verbatim in Verus.

       – every character that appears in `src` appears exactly once in `dst`, and no extra characters are introduced.  
    3. ∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < dst.len() ⇒ dst[i] < dst[j]  
       – the resulting vector is strictly ascending in Unicode scalar value order.  
    4. The capacity of `dst` is *exactly* its length (no over-allocation).  

In words: produce a new vector that contains each distinct character of the input **once**, with the characters arranged in strictly increasing order of their Unicode scalar value, and with no surplus capacity.  A proof must show set-equality between the multiset of `src` and the set of `dst`, show strict ordering, and confirm zero duplicates.

Inputs

Preconditions (requires)

Postconditions (ensures)

       – each prefix is an exact left segment of the input.  
    4. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k ≤ s.len() ⇒ p[k].capacity() == k  
       – every stored prefix vector is allocated with capacity exactly equal to its length (no slack space).  
    5. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < s.len() ⇒ p[k].ptr() != p[k+1].ptr()  
       – adjacent prefix vectors do **not** share the same underlying allocation; each prefix owns distinct memory.  
    6. The total number of `char` elements allocated across the whole return value is (s.len() * (s.len()+1)) / 2.

The spec additionally requires capacity-tight allocations and non-aliasing between consecutive prefix vectors. # Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn fib(n: u64) -> (r: u64)
    requires
        n >= 0,
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        r == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The snippet introduces an executable Verus function called `fib`.

1. Signature  
   • Name: `fib`  
   • Parameter: `n: u64` (an unsigned-64 runtime value).  
   • Return: `r: u64`.

2. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a) `n >= 0` – tautologically true for every `u64`, therefore redundant.  
   b) `n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX` – since integer division of identical, positive
      `u64` constants yields `1`, this reduces to `n < 1`.  
      Consequently the only admissible concrete argument is `n == 0`.

3. Postcondition (`ensures`)  
   `r == n * (n + 1) / 2` – the classic closed-form for the
   sum of the first `n` natural numbers (the “triangular number” of `n`).

4. Absence of helpers  
   No auxiliary lemmas or specification functions are referenced.

5. Practical reading  
   Under the stated contract, the implementation may assume `n == 0`,
   and must return `r == 0`, because the formula evaluates to zero in this
   single admissible case.  Any body that does so will satisfy the spec.
   (If the precondition were broadened, the same mathematical formula would
   have to hold for arbitrary `n`, but with the current precondition no
   overflow issues arise and the range of `n` is trivial.)

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Implement a verified Verus function with the exact header

fn fib(n: u64) -> (r: u64)

Contract:

• Preconditions  
  1. n ≥ 0 (redundant for u64).  
  2. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.  Because u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, this
     effectively constrains callers to pass only n == 0.

• Postcondition  
  r == n * (n + 1) / 2.

In other words, subject to the (very restrictive) precondition, the function must
return the triangular number of n.  Given that n can only be 0, the only legal
behaviour is to return r = 0.

No helper spec functions, lemmas, or additional ghost code are necessary.  Any
implementation that provably yields r = 0 when n = 0 will satisfy the
specification.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise only asks the student to return the minimum inner-vector length.  
To make the assignment slightly harder (but still reasonable for an introductory Verus
exercise) we can layer on more *certifiable* properties that naturally arise from the
same data.  Typical ways to do this are

• Ask for auxiliary information (e.g. *how many* vectors achieve the minimum, or the
  *earliest index* where it occurs).  
• Introduce an additional universal pre-condition that must be carried through to the
  post-conditions (e.g. “every inner length is even”).  
• Require several logically-related results to be returned in one shot (e.g. the triple
  ⟨min, count, first-index⟩) so that the student has to prove they are mutually
  consistent.

Below are three stand-alone upgraded problem statements that follow those ideas.  Each
is written in enough detail that a spec generator could translate it directly into
Verus code.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus-Rust function with the following exact specification.

Function name  
    min_length_count_first_index

Signature  
    fn min_length_count_first_index(s: Vec<Vec<i32>>)
        -> (len: usize, count: usize, first_idx: usize)

Inputs  
    s : Vec<Vec<i32>>  
        A non-empty outer vector whose elements are vectors of 32-bit signed integers.

Outputs  
    len       : usize   – the length of the shortest inner vector in `s`.  
    count     : usize   – the number of inner vectors whose length equals `len`.  
    first_idx : usize   – the smallest index at which an inner vector of length `len` occurs.

Preconditions (requires)  
    • s.len() > 0

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. ∀ i : int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ len ≤ s[i].len()  
       (`forall|i:int| 0 <= i < s.len() as int ==> len as int <= s[i as int].len() as int`)

    2. count = |{ i | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i].len() = len }|  
       (`count as int == set_int![i | 0 <= i < s.len() as int && s[i as int].len() as int == len as int].len()`)

    3. count > 0

    4. 0 ≤ first_idx < s.len() ∧ s[first_idx].len() = len

    5. ∀ j : int. 0 ≤ j < first_idx ⇒ s[j].len() > len  
       (i.e. `first_idx` is the *first* place the minimum length appears)

Time-/space-complexity requirement  
    The executable body must run in O(total number of inner elements) time; no extra
    asymptotic space beyond the returned triple is allowed.

No helper lemmas or spec functions are strictly necessary; any body that is accepted by
Verus under these conditions suffices.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus-Rust function with the following exact specification.

Function name  
    smallest_even_list_length

Signature  
    fn smallest_even_list_length(s: Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> (v: usize)

Inputs  
    s : Vec<Vec<i32>>  
        A non-empty outer vector of inner vectors.

Preconditions (requires)  
    • s.len() > 0  
    • ∀ i : int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i].len() % 2 == 0  
      (every inner vector has *even* length)

Output  
    v : usize  
        The smallest (and therefore even) inner-vector length.

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. ∀ i : int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ v ≤ s[i].len()  
    2. ∃ i : int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ v = s[i].len()  
    3. v % 2 == 0   (the result is itself even)

Thus `v` is the minimum of the set of even inner lengths supplied.  No extra helper
definitions are required.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus-Rust function with the following exact specification.

Function name  
    min_length_and_occurrence_statistics

Signature  
    fn min_length_and_occurrence_statistics(s: Vec<Vec<i32>>)
        -> (len: usize, at_least: usize, at_most: usize)

Inputs  
    s : Vec<Vec<i32>>  
        A non-empty outer vector of inner vectors.

Outputs  
    len      : usize   – the minimum inner-vector length.  
    at_least : usize   – the number of vectors whose length is *strictly less* than the
                         mean inner-vector length (rounded down).  
    at_most  : usize   – the number of vectors whose length is *at most* `len + 1`.

Auxiliary definition used in postconditions  
    • mean_len = floor( (Σ_i s[i].len()) / s.len() )

Preconditions (requires)  
    • s.len() > 0

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. ∀ i : int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ len ≤ s[i].len()  
    2. ∃ i : int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ len = s[i].len()  
    3. at_least = |{ i | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i].len() < mean_len }|  
    4. at_most  = |{ i | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i].len() ≤ len + 1 }|

In other words, the function must simultaneously compute the minimum inner length,
count how many lengths fall strictly below the (integer) mean, and count how many
lengths are within one unit of the minimum.  The body must run in O(total number of
inner elements) time and may allocate only O(1) additional space.

```rust
/// Function to zap negatives in an array
fn zap_negatives(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
        a.len() < 1000,
    ensures
        result.len() == a.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> if a[i as int] < 0 { result[i as int] == 0 } else { result[i as int] == a[i as int] },
```

# Reasoning
We have one executable function, zap_negatives.

1. Inputs and ownership
   • a: Vec<i32> (owned value, not a reference).  
     – Because it is moved into the function, the implementation may freely consume, mutate, or iterate over it without cloning.

2. Output
   • result: Vec<i32> – a new vector returned to the caller.

3. Preconditions (requires)
   • a.len() > 0  → the input vector must contain at least one element.  
   • a.len() < 1000 → the vector length is strictly less than 1000.  
     These bounds are likely there to ease verification of loops; the spec must reject vectors that are empty or have ≥ 1000 elements.

4. Postconditions (ensures)
   • result.len() == a.len() → the returned vector is exactly the same length as the input.  
   • ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒  
      if a[i] < 0 then result[i] == 0  
      else     result[i] == a[i]  
     – In other words, every negative element of the input is “zapped” (replaced) with 0, while non-negative elements are preserved verbatim and kept at the same index.

5. No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or recursion appear; only a straightforward loop or iterator is necessary in the body.

6. Subtleties
   • The specification speaks in mathematical ints; casts such as a[i as int] are needed when indexing the runtime Vec.  
   • Because we return a brand-new Vec, the easiest verified implementation is to iterate over ‘a’, push either 0 or the original value into a fresh Vec<i32>, and then return it.  
   • The precondition upper bound (len < 1000) means a simple `decreases` annotation on the loop counter is enough for Verus to prove termination.

# Final Description
<<<

Function name: zap_negatives

Signature:
    fn zap_negatives(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Outputs

Preconditions (requires)
    1. a.len() > 0            // the input vector must be non-empty
    2. a.len() < 1000         // its length must be strictly less than 1000

Postconditions (ensures)
    1. result.len() == a.len()                        // length is preserved
    2. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒                    // element-wise constraint:
         if a[i] < 0                                  //   • when the original element is negative
            then result[i] == 0                       //     it is replaced by 0
         else                                         //   • otherwise
            result[i] == a[i]                         //     it is copied unchanged

Informally: the function must return a vector that is identical to the input except that every negative entry has been replaced by zero, while vector length and ordering remain unchanged.

No helper spec functions or additional lemmas are required.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task just asks the student to prove that every element in a vector is a decimal digit.  
To make the exercise more challenging we can

1. Introduce an additional existential requirement (e.g. *at least one even and one odd digit*).  
2. Ask for a *minimal-counter-example* index via an `Option<usize>` return type.  This forces both universal and existential reasoning over indices.
3. Combine two properties (all–digits **and** palindrome) so that the learner has to relate symmetric indices in the post-condition.

Each new task is still small enough to be solvable in a few dozen lines of Verus, but they require trickier specifications and proofs than the original.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

fn all_digits_even_and_odd(s: &Vec<char>) -> (result: bool)

Inputs  
    • s: an immutable reference to a Vec<char> that may contain arbitrary characters and may be empty.

Output  
    • result: bool

Pre-conditions  
    • None.

Post-condition  
    result ⇔ (
        // Every character in s is an ASCII decimal digit
        (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒
                '0' ≤ s[i] ≤ '9')
        ∧
        // The vector contains **at least one even digit** and **at least one odd digit**.
        (∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ ((s[i] - '0') % 2 == 0))    // an even digit exists
        ∧
        (∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < s.len() ∧ ((s[j] - '0') % 2 == 1))    // an odd digit exists
    )

Special notes  
    • If the vector is empty the function must return **false**, because the existential requirements cannot be satisfied.  
    • No auxiliary helper predicates or functions need to be written; the quantified predicates may appear directly in the post-condition.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

fn first_non_digit(s: &Vec<char>) -> (idx: Option<usize>)

Inputs  
    • s: an immutable reference to a Vec<char> that may contain any characters and may be empty.

Output  
    • idx: Option<usize>  
        – `None`  means *every* character in `s` is one of the ten ASCII digits.  
        – `Some(i)` indicates the smallest index whose character is **not** an ASCII digit.

Pre-conditions  
    • None.

Post-conditions  
    1. (idx.is_None() ⇔ (∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < s.len() ⇒ '0' ≤ s[k] ≤ '9')).  
    2. (idx.is_Some(i) ⇒  
          0 ≤ i < s.len()                                       ∧
          ¬('0' ≤ s[i] ≤ '9')                                   ∧
          (∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < i ⇒ '0' ≤ s[k] ≤ '9')).

That is, when a non-digit is returned its index is guaranteed to be within bounds and is the *left-most* such violation. If no violation exists the function returns `None`.

You may freely introduce ghost variables or helper lemmas to discharge the proof obligations.
>>>

Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

fn is_numeric_palindrome(s: &Vec<char>) -> (result: bool)

Inputs  
    • s: an immutable reference to a Vec<char> that may contain any characters and may be empty.

Output  
    • result: bool

Pre-conditions  
    • None.

Post-condition  
    result ⇔ (
        // All characters are decimal digits
        (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ '0' ≤ s[i] ≤ '9')
        ∧
        // The vector reads the same forwards and backwards
        (∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] == s[s.len() − 1 − i])
    )

Observations  
    • An empty vector is considered a numeric palindrome, so the result must be `true`.  
    • The proof must relate symmetric indices (i and len−1−i) while simultaneously maintaining the all-digits invariant.  Ghost code or auxiliary lemmas may be added as needed.

```rust
fn sum_of_squares_of_first_n_odd_numbers(n: u64) -> (sum: u64)
```

# Reasoning
The original task is conceptually simple: delete exactly one element from the head of a non-empty slice and show that every other element is shifted left by one.  
To raise the difficulty we can:

•  Parameterise the number of elements to be removed (k), so the spec must quantify over an arbitrary natural k and relate two indices that differ by k, not 1.  
•  Add an auxiliary return value whose correctness must also be proved (e.g. the exact multiset—or the arithmetic sum—of the removed elements).  
•  Require an additional property that is less common than the usual “tail-preserves-order”, such as “all elements that remain are ≥ every element that was removed”, forcing the learner to reason both about indices and values.  
•  Keep the operation purely functional to avoid low-level aliasing issues, but insist on a non-trivial pre- and post-condition set that needs several logical steps.

I will therefore propose three harder standalone problems, each expressed in detailed natural language so a Verus spec generator can work directly from it.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally verified function named remove_prefix that deletes the first k elements of a slice.

Signature  
fn remove_prefix(a: &[int], k: nat) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Contracts  

Pre-conditions  
1. a.len() > 0                              — the slice is non-empty.  
2. k <= a.len()                             — it is legal to drop k elements.  
3. k > 0                                    — we must remove at least one element.

Post-conditions  
1. c.len() == a.len() − k.  
2. ∀ i:int. k ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == c[i − k].  
   (The element originally at index i appears at index i − k in c.)  
3. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < k ⇒ !(∃ j:int. 0 ≤ j < c.len() ∧ a[i] == c[j]).  
   (No element that was removed occurs anywhere in the resulting vector; this forces the proof to reason about value equality as well as indices.)

Semantics  
Given a slice a = [a₀, a₁, …, aₙ] and a natural k (1 ≤ k ≤ n+1), remove_prefix returns the vector  
c = [a_k, a_{k+1}, …, aₙ].  
No helper lemmas are provided; they may be introduced if necessary.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally verified function called drop_and_sum that trims the first k elements of a non-empty slice and simultaneously returns the arithmetic sum of the elements that were removed.

Signature  
fn drop_and_sum(a: &[int], k: nat) -> (c: Vec<int>, s: int)

Contracts  

Pre-conditions  
1. 1 ≤ k ≤ a.len()                          — we drop a legal, positive amount.

Post-conditions  
1. c.len() == a.len() − k.  
2. ∀ i:int. k ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == c[i − k].  
3. s == ∑_{i=0}^{k−1} a[i].                 — s is exactly the sum of the removed elements.  
4. ∑_{j=0}^{c.len()−1} c[j] + s == ∑_{i=0}^{a.len()−1} a[i].  
   (The overall multiset of values is preserved; only their partition between s and c changes.)

Semantics  
Given a slice a = [a₀, a₁, …, aₙ] and k satisfying 1 ≤ k ≤ n+1, drop_and_sum must return  
• c = [a_k, a_{k+1}, …, aₙ]  
• s = a₀ + a₁ + … + a_{k−1}.  
No additional helper functions are supplied.
>>>

<<<
Design a formally verified function strip_while_even that removes the longest even-valued prefix of a slice.

Signature  
fn strip_while_even(a: &[int]) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Contracts  

Pre-condition  
• true  (the function must work for any finite slice of ints).

Post-conditions  
1. There exists an m: nat such that  
   a) 0 ≤ m ≤ a.len(),  
   b) ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < m  ⇒ a[i] % 2 == 0,            — every removed element is even,  
   c) m == a.len() ∨ a[m] % 2 != 0,                    — the first remaining element (if any) is odd,  
   d) c.len() == a.len() − m,  
   e) ∀ i:int. m ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == c[i − m].    — suffix correspondence.  
2. ∀ x:int. (∃ j:int. 0 ≤ j < c.len() ∧ c[j] == x) ⇒  
            !(∃ i:int. 0 ≤ i < m ∧ a[i] == x).        — no removed value re-appears among the kept ones.

Semantics  
The function computes the shortest suffix of a that starts immediately after the longest prefix consisting solely of even numbers. If every element of a is even, the result is the empty vector.  No helper lemmas are provided by default.
>>>

 •  Keeps “pair-wise sum” but additionally requires that both inputs are *strictly ascending* sequences.  
 •  The post-conditions must therefore prove that the result is also strictly ascending in addition to the usual element-wise equality.  
 •  Showing preservation of strict order adds one non-trivial inductive reasoning step.

Variant 2 (weighted_add_lists)  
 •  Generalises the operation to a *weighted* linear combination r[i] = c · a[i] + d · b[i] with two extra scalar parameters c and d.  
 •  The student must carry an extra overflow guard that now depends on all four values (a[i], b[i], c, d).  
 •  Forces them to work with a slightly less common requirement (weighted sum rather than plain sum).

Variant 3 (add_lists_and_total)  
 •  Returns not only the pair-wise sum vector but also the *total* of all produced entries.  
 •  Requires an additional post-condition connecting the returned scalar with the vector content, plus a larger overflow envelope (i64 for the grand total).  
 •  Adds a second output and therefore an extra layer of functional correctness reasoning.

Each variant has roughly 10–25 extra words compared to the original, introduces at least one new logical obligation, yet remains short enough to be feasible in a single Verus file.

Inputs  
  • `s`: immutable reference to a `Vec<char>` – the text to be checked.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

spec fn is_sorted(a: Seq<int>) -> bool
    recommends
        a.len() > 0,
{
    forall|i: int, j: int| 0 <= i && i < j && j < a.len() ==> a[i] <= a[j]
}

fn bin_search(a: &[int], k: int) -> (b: bool)
    requires
        is_sorted(a@),
        a.len() < i32::MAX as usize,
        a.len() > 0,
        forall|i: int, j: int| 0 <= i && i < j && j < a.len() as int ==> a[i] <= a[j],
    ensures
        b == exists|i: int| 0 <= i && i < a.len() as int && a[i] == k,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We have two pieces to describe: a pure specification helper `is_sorted` operating over the mathematical sequence view of a collection, and the executable (but verified) routine `bin_search`.

1. `is_sorted`
   • Signature: `spec fn is_sorted(a: Seq<int>) -> bool`.
   • Purpose: say whether the passed sequence is monotonically non-decreasing.
   • Definition: it universally quantifies over every ordered pair of indexes i<j inside the valid range and demands `a[i] ≤ a[j]`.
   • `recommends a.len() > 0`: Verus’ “recommends” clause states that the body of the spec function is only guaranteed to be meaningful when this condition holds (here: the sequence must be non-empty).  Calls that violate the recommendation are still allowed but the function then behaves like an uninterpreted symbol.

2. `bin_search`
   • Signature: `fn bin_search(a: &[int], k: int) -> (b: bool)`.
        - `a` is an immutable slice whose concrete elements are of type `int` (the unbounded mathematical integer—not i32).
        - `k` is the key we are searching for.
        - It returns one Boolean `b`.

   • Pre-conditions (`requires`)
        1. `is_sorted(a@)` – the ghost view (`a@`) of the slice must be sorted according to the helper predicate above.
        2. `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize` – length must fit into a signed 32-bit value; this will later allow us to cast the length to `int` safely during proofs.
        3. `a.len() > 0` – empty inputs are disallowed.
        4. Redundant but stronger sorting guarantee written directly over runtime indexing:  
           `∀ i,j : int . 0 ≤ i < j < a.len()  ⇒  a[i] ≤ a[j]`.  
           (The redundancy helps proof automation inside the executable body.)

   • Post-condition (`ensures`)
        `b == (∃ i : int • 0 ≤ i < a.len()  ∧  a[i] == k)`.  
        In words, the function must return `true` exactly when the key `k` appears at some valid index of the slice, and `false` otherwise.

   • No other ghost functions or lemmas are referenced; the body the implementer will write is expected to be a classic binary search that satisfies the specification.

Important subtleties:
  – The ordering is “less than or equal” (duplicates are allowed).
  – All numbers inside the specification live in the mathematical `int` domain, so there is no risk of overflow in the logic.  
  – The function may assume the slice is non-empty owing to the `requires` clause, but must handle keys smaller, equal, or greater than all elements.
  – Because both `is_sorted(a@)` and the explicit `forall` condition appear in the pre-conditions, either can be used as the starting point in proofs.

# Reasoning
The original task is a very small, single–quantifier post-condition that can be satisfied with one loop.  
To make it just a bit harder while still being expressible in pure first–order logic, I will create three independent, **stand-alone** alternatives that force the student to introduce (1) an existential quantifier, (2) a counting argument, or (3) an additional data structure plus a universal quantifier over *pairs* of indices.  

1. **`glob_match`** – adds the “*” wildcard (matches an arbitrary, **possibly empty** contiguous subsequence) in addition to “?”.  
   • Post-condition now contains both an ∀ and an ∃: the implementation must show that *there exists* a sequence of positions in the text where the non-star pattern symbols align, and for all those positions the characters match or are “?”.  
   • Extra 10(ish) words: “the first star may absorb zero characters” etc.  
   • Student must reason about backtracking or DP.

2. **`count_pattern_occurrences`** – no wildcards, but the function must return the *number* of non-overlapping occurrences of the pattern inside the text.  
   • Ensures introduces a set of start indices `I` and properties over that set (disjointness, maximality).  
   • The learner has to prove that the count is bounded by `text.len() − pattern.len() + 1`, and that every reported window really equals the pattern.

3. **`match_with_complements`** – enriches the pattern language with bracket classes like `[abc]` and their complement `[^abc]`.  
   • Replaces the common “?’ wildcard with the less common but more specific bracket notation.  
   • Post-condition now quantifies over characters, indices, *and* membership in an explicitly given set, forcing the student to define a helper predicate “char_in_class”.  
   • Also insists that the pattern and text must have *prime* lengths, adding an unusual arithmetic precondition.

Each description below is entirely self-contained and contains enough detail for a Verus spec generator to work from: signature, inputs, outputs, pre-/post-conditions, helper-predicates that are allowed, and implementation hints.

# Output
<<<
Write a verified Verus-Rust function named `glob_match` that decides whether a character vector `text` matches a *glob* `pattern`.  
The pattern language is:

  • Ordinary characters equal themselves.  
  • `'?'` matches *exactly* one arbitrary character.  
  • `'*'` matches *any contiguous sequence* of zero or more characters.

Signature  
    fn glob_match(text: &Vec<char>, pattern: &Vec<char>) -> (b: bool)

Inputs  
  • `text`: immutable reference to a `Vec<char>` – the string being tested.  
  • `pattern`: immutable reference to a `Vec<char>` – the glob pattern.  Its length may differ from `text.len()`.

Output  
  • `b`: `bool` – `true` exactly when the entire `text` conforms to the glob.

Preconditions (requires)  
  • `text.len() <= 256` and `pattern.len() <= 256` (keeps proofs finite; any small bound is acceptable).  

Postcondition (ensures)  
  • `b` is true ⇔  
      ∃ function `f: nat → nat`, total on the set `{ i | 0 ≤ i < pattern.len() ∧ pattern[i] ≠ '*' }`,  
        strictly increasing into `[0, text.len())`, such that  
        ∀ i with 0 ≤ i < pattern.len() and pattern[i] ≠ '*':  
            pattern[i] == '?' ∨ text[f(i)] == pattern[i],  
        and `f(last_index)` = `text.len() - 1` whenever the last pattern symbol is not `'*'`.  
    (Intuitively, `f` chooses where each non-star pattern symbol lands in `text`; gaps between
     consecutive `f(i)` values correspond to the characters absorbed by the `'*'`s.)

Implementation notes  
  • Any algorithm is allowed (backtracking, dynamic programming, etc.) provided it satisfies the contract.  
  • Helper *specification* functions are allowed for defining `f`, but no ghost state may leak into execution.  
  • All array indices must be cast to `int` when used.  
  • No I/O or mutation of the input vectors is permitted.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function `count_pattern_occurrences` that returns how many *non-overlapping* times a shorter vector `pat` appears inside a longer vector `txt`.

Signature  
    fn count_pattern_occurrences(txt: &Vec<char>, pat: &Vec<char>) -> (k: nat)

Inputs  
  • `txt`: immutable reference to a `Vec<char>` with length `m`.  
  • `pat`: immutable reference to a `Vec<char>` with length `n`.

Outputs  
  • `k`: a non-negative integer.

Preconditions (requires)  
  • `pat.len() > 0`  
  • `pat.len() ≤ txt.len()` (otherwise zero matches are obvious).

Postconditions (ensures)  
  • `k ≤ txt.len() - pat.len() + 1`  
  • ∃ set `I: set<nat>` such that  
        — |I| = k,  
        — ∀ i ∈ I: 0 ≤ i ≤ txt.len() - pat.len() ∧  
                   (∀ j:nat | 0 ≤ j < pat.len() ⇒ txt[i + j] == pat[j]),  
        — ∀ i₁, i₂ ∈ I: i₁ ≠ i₂ ⇒ i₁ + pat.len() ≤ i₂ ∨ i₂ + pat.len() ≤ i₁  
          (the occurrences do not overlap),  
        — ∀ i: nat | 0 ≤ i ≤ txt.len() - pat.len()  
              if (∀ j:nat | 0 ≤ j < pat.len() ⇒ txt[i + j] == pat[j])  
              then ∃ i₀ ∈ I: i = i₀ ∨ (i₀ + pat.len() ≤ i)  
              (each maximal non-overlapping occurrence is counted).

Hints  
  • You may scan left-to-right, greedily skipping by `pat.len()` after each hit.  
  • Ghost variables or vectors may be introduced to record `I` during the proof, but run-time code
    must stay O(txt.len()).
>>>

<<<
Design a verified Verus-Rust function named `match_with_complements` that compares two vectors of equal, *prime* length: a `text` and a “class pattern” `patt`.  
Each entry of `patt` is itself a two-character slice describing a character class:

  • `"^x"` (caret followed by `x`) matches any character *other than* `x`.  
  • `" x"` (space followed by `x`) matches exactly the character `x`.  
  • No other forms are legal.

Signature  
    fn match_with_complements(text: &Vec<char>, patt: &Vec<char>) -> (ok: bool)

Inputs  
  • `text`: `Vec<char>` – the data string.  
  • `patt`: `Vec<char>` – pattern vector, same length as `text`; each element is length-2 class descriptor.

Outputs  
  • `ok`: `bool`.

Helpers allowed  
  • A pure spec-only function `char_in_class(class: char, c: char) -> bool`
    interpreting a descriptor (its first char tells if it is complemented).

Preconditions (requires)  
  • `text.len() == patt.len()`  
  • `text.len() ≥ 2` and `is_prime(text.len())`  
  • ∀ i:nat | 0 ≤ i < patt.len() ⇒  
        (patt[i*2] == '^' ∨ patt[i*2] == ' ')  ∧  // first of the two chars  
        true                                       // second char unrestricted

Postconditions (ensures)  
  • `ok` ⇔ ∀ i:nat | 0 ≤ i < text.len() ⇒
              char_in_class(patt[i*2], text[i])

where  
  is_prime(n) := n ≥ 2 ∧ ∀ d:nat | 2 ≤ d*d ≤ n ⇒ n mod d ≠ 0.

Additional constraints  
  • The implementation must run in O(n) time **without early exit**: every
    position must be examined exactly once, ensuring constant-time behaviour
    with respect to the first mismatch.  (Use a running conjunction instead
    of returning early.)

Proof obligations are limited to functional correctness; timing compliance
is asserted via a simple loop invariant stating the loop iterates `n` times.
>>>

   • allocate a fresh vector and push transformed elements,

   • or employ any other strategy, so long as the postconditions are provably satisfied and the function terminates.
