# Reasoning
The original task (“reverse a non-empty vector of `char`s”) is essentially a one-loop problem with a single, well-known postcondition.  
To make it more challenging I will:

• introduce slice-bounded reversal, so the implementation must copy three disjoint segments and reason about each;  
• add an element-wise transformation (successor code-point with wrap-around) that forces an additional mapping postcondition;  
• switch from producing data to a decision procedure (palindrome test), which requires a proposition-valued result and quantifier reasoning.

All three variants remain small enough for a classroom setting but each needs several additional proof steps compared with the original.  

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

Function signature  
    fn reverse_range(a: &Vec<char>, start: usize, end_excl: usize) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Meaning  
    Construct a new vector that is identical to the input vector except that the
    sub-slice `a[start .. end_excl]` is reversed in place. Elements outside the
    given half-open range are preserved exactly.

Inputs  
  • a : &Vec<char> — immutable reference to the original vector of Unicode scalar values.  
  • start : usize  — inclusive lower bound of the sub-slice to reverse.  
  • end_excl : usize — exclusive upper bound of the sub-slice to reverse.

Output  
  • b : Vec<char> — a freshly allocated vector containing the modified data.

Preconditions (requires)  
  1. start < end_excl                                       – range must be non-empty.  
  2. end_excl ≤ a.len()                                     – range must lie inside `a`.  
  3. a.len() > 0                                            – original vector is non-empty.

Postconditions (ensures)  
  1. b.len() == a.len()                                     – length preserved globally.  
  2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < start ⇒ b[i] == a[i]                 – prefix left unchanged.  
  3. ∀ i:int • end_excl ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ b[i] == a[i]        – suffix left unchanged.  
  4. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < end_excl − start ⇒  
       b[start + k] == a[end_excl − k − 1]                  – middle region reversed.

Notes  
  • No mutation of `a` occurs.  
  • You may allocate exactly one auxiliary buffer if desired.  
  • All arithmetic is guaranteed to stay within `usize` bounds by the preconditions.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

Function signature  
    fn succ_and_reverse(a: &Vec<char>) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Meaning  
    Return a new vector whose contents are the reverse of `a`, but *each*
    character is first mapped to its immediate Unicode successor
    (wrapping from `'\u{10FFFF}'` back to `'\u{0000}'`).

Inputs  
  • a : &Vec<char> — immutable reference to a vector of Unicode scalar values.

Output  
  • b : Vec<char> — the transformed, reversed vector.

Preconditions (requires)  
  1. a.len() > 0                                 – input must be non-empty.

Postconditions (ensures)  
  1. b.len() == a.len()                          – length preserved.  
  2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒  
       b[i] == successor(a[a.len() − i − 1])     – both reversal and successor map applied.

Helper specification  
  • spec fn successor(c: char) -> char  
        ensures |result as u32| == if c as u32 == 0x10FFFF { 0 } else { c as u32 + 1 };

Notes  
  • The helper `successor` is *spec-only*; its executable counterpart can use
    integer casting with an `if` for wrap-around.  
  • Because of the wrap-around rule the function is total over all valid `char`s.  
  • The implementation may iterate once over the vector, computing the successor
    and writing into a pre-allocated result in reverse order.

Meaning

Helper specification  
  • spec fn fold(c: char) -> char  
       ensures fold(c).to_uppercase() == c.to_uppercase() &&  
               fold(c).to_lowercase() == c.to_lowercase();  
    (Any total, deterministic Unicode case-folding spec is acceptable.)

Notes  
  • Empty and single-character vectors are *by definition* palindromes.

Design and verify a Verus/Rust function named `max` that computes the maximum value of a slice of unsigned 64-bit integers, with a conventional default for the empty slice.

Signature
    fn max(a: &[u64]) -> (m: u64)

Behavioural contract

Pre-conditions (requires): none.  
The function must accept any slice, including the empty slice.

Post-conditions (ensures)
1. If the slice is empty (a.len() == 0) the function must return 0:

       a.len() == 0 ⇒ m == 0

2. If the slice is non-empty (a.len() > 0), the returned value must be an upper bound on all elements:

       a.len() > 0 ⇒ ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ m ≥ a[k]

3. Still in the non-empty case, the returned value must equal at least one element of the slice, i.e. the maximum actually occurs in the data:

       a.len() > 0 ⇒ ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ m == a[i]

Together, clauses (2) and (3) state that `m` is exactly the maximum element of `a`.  
When several elements share this maximum value, any of them may be chosen.  
When the slice is empty, the specification dictates the neutral value 0.

No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or additional attributes are required.  
Any implementation that satisfies these contracts—e.g., a single linear scan that keeps track of the current maximum—will verify.

The specification introduces a single executable function `add` whose purpose is to numerically add two non-negative mathematical integers and return their sum.

Although the function works purely in the `int` domain, this extra bound guarantees that the sum fits into a 32-bit signed integer, thereby ruling out any overflow concerns if an implementer chooses to cast to `i32` inside the body.

4. Postcondition (`ensures`)

   • There is no requirement on memory, mutability, or side effects: the function is expected to be pure and deterministic.

# Reasoning
The original task (compute the maximum of a slice, returning 0 for the empty slice) is a single-pass, single-quantifier verification exercise.  
To make the exercise more challenging while staying in the same thematic “maximum” family, I prepared three stand-alone variants that introduce additional reasoning obligations:

1. Return both the maximum and **the first index** at which it occurs.  
   Verifying “earliest occurrence” forces the learner to combine a maximality property with a *strict* inequality on all earlier elements.

2. Return the maximum **and how many times it occurs**.  
   This introduces a cardinality constraint that must be expressed with a set comprehension or explicit counting quantifier.

3. Return the two largest **distinct** values in the slice (or an agreed-upon fallback when the slice does not have two distinct values).  
   The spec must cover three structurally different cases (length 0, length 1, ≥2 with/without distinct values), forcing careful disjunction handling.

Each problem is self-contained, states its signature, pre-conditions, and post-conditions in detail, and stays within the “max” theme while adding about ten extra words of constraint per problem.

# Output
<<<
Design and verify a Verus/Rust function named `max_and_first_index` that returns
both the maximum value in a slice of unsigned 64-bit integers *and* the index of
the first position where this maximum occurs.

Signature  
    fn max_and_first_index(a: &[u64]) -> (m: u64, idx: usize)

Behavioural contract

Pre-conditions (requires)  
• none – the function must accept any slice, including the empty slice.

Post-conditions (ensures)

1. Empty slice:  
      a.len() == 0 ⇒ m == 0 ∧ idx == 0

2. Non-empty slice:  
      a.len() > 0 ⇒
         (a) 0 ≤ idx < a.len()                                           // index in range  
         (b) m == a[idx]                                                 // value agrees with element at idx  
         (c) ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ m ≥ a[k]                        // m is an upper bound  
         (d) ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < idx  ⇒ a[k] < m                           // idx is the *first* occurrence

Together, clauses (b)–(d) state that `m` is the maximum element and `idx` is
the least index where that maximum appears.  When the slice is empty the
neutral pair (0, 0) is mandated.  No auxiliary spec items are required; a
verified linear scan with running maximum and position suffices.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus/Rust function named `max_and_count` that returns the
maximum value of a slice of unsigned 64-bit integers **and** how many times that
maximum occurs.

Signature  
    fn max_and_count(a: &[u64]) -> (m: u64, cnt: u64)

Behavioural contract

Pre-conditions (requires)  
• none – the function must accept any slice, including the empty slice.

Post-conditions (ensures)

1. Empty slice:  
      a.len() == 0 ⇒ m == 0 ∧ cnt == 0

2. Non-empty slice:  
      a.len() > 0 ⇒
         (a) ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ m ≥ a[k]                        // m upper bound  
         (b) ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ m == a[i]                       // m occurs in data  
         (c) cnt == |{ k:int | 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ a[k] == m }|            // cnt is exact multiplicity  
         (d) cnt > 0                                                    // multiplicity positive

Clause (c) uses set-comprehension notation for readability; any equivalent
formalisation that counts occurrences is acceptable.  When the slice is empty
the pair (0, 0) is returned.  A one-pass algorithm that tracks the current
maximum and its frequency is sufficient.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus/Rust function named `top_two_distinct` that returns
the two largest *distinct* values present in a slice of unsigned 64-bit
integers, following a well-defined fallback policy when fewer than two distinct
values exist.

Signature  
    fn top_two_distinct(a: &[u64]) -> (max1: u64, max2: u64)

Behavioural contract

Pre-conditions (requires)  
• none – the function must accept any slice, including the empty slice.

Post-conditions (ensures)

Let `D` be the set { a[k] | 0 ≤ k < a.len() } of data values.

1. Empty slice:  
      a.len() == 0 ⇒ max1 == 0 ∧ max2 == 0

2. Exactly one element (a.len() == 1):  
      max1 == a[0] ∧ max2 == 0

3. General non-empty case (a.len() > 1):  
      (a) max1 ≥ max2                                                    // ordered pair  
      (b) ∀ v ∈ D • v ≤ max1                                             // max1 is an upper bound  
      (c) ∃ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ a[k] == max1                       // max1 occurs  
      (d) If D contains at least two distinct values then                // real second maximum
           • max1 > max2                                                // they differ
           • max2 ∈ D                                                   // max2 is present
           • ∀ v ∈ D • v < max1 ⇒ v ≤ max2                              // maximal among non-max1
         else                                                           // all data identical
           • max1 == max2                                               // both equal that value

In prose:  
• When the slice has two or more distinct values, `max1` is the overall
  maximum, `max2` is the largest value strictly less than `max1`, and both
  appear in the slice.  
• When all elements are equal, the function returns that value twice.  
• In the degenerate lengths 0 and 1 cases, (0, 0) or (a[0], 0) is returned
  respectively.

The specification requires careful case analysis and guarantees are
implementable with a linear scan maintaining two candidate maxima.
>>>

Implement a formally verified function called `find` in Verus-Rust whose behaviour is fully characterised below.

Signature  
    fn find(a: &[int], key: int) -> (index: usize)

Inputs  
  • `a`: an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
  • `key`: the integer value to be searched for.

Output  
  • `index`: a `usize` that indicates a position in the slice or one past the end.

Contract  

Pre-condition (requires)  
  1. The slice is non-empty: `a.len() > 0`.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  1. The returned value is always within bounds: `0 ≤ index ≤ a.len()`.  
  2. If the returned index actually points inside the slice (`index < a.len()`), then the element at that position equals the sought key: `a[index] == key`.

Interpretation  
  • Returning any index whose element equals `key` satisfies the contract.  
  • If the key is not (or need not be) reported, the function may legally return `a.len()` to indicate “not found”.  
  • The spec places no obligation to return the first occurrence, nor to prove the key is absent when `index == a.len()`; it merely forbids returning an in-range index whose element is different from `key`.

No auxiliary specification functions, lemmas, or additional assumptions are required. Any executable body that meets the above contract—e.g. a linear scan—is acceptable.

# Reasoning
The original task is a very small, linear-scan “maybe-find” with only two post-conditions.  
To make the exercise more challenging, we can:

•  Assume additional structure (e.g. the slice is *strictly* increasing, or merely *non-decreasing*).  
•  Require the function to return more *precise* information (the *first* occurrence, the *insertion point*, or a *half-open range* of equal elements).  
•  Demand extra logical guarantees that force the student to quantify over the slice and to use lemmas or ghost code (e.g. proving absence of the key, or monotone properties before/after the returned index).

Below are three self-contained, incrementally harder variants that satisfy the brief.  
Each description is long and precise enough for a spec generator to build the full Verus `requires` / `ensures` clauses.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified function `lower_bound` in Verus-Rust whose behaviour is fully characterised below.

Signature  
    fn lower_bound(a: &[int], key: int) -> (idx: usize)

Inputs  
  • `a`: an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
  • `key`: the integer value whose insertion position is queried.

Output  
  • `idx`: a `usize` that represents an index **inside** the slice or exactly one past the end.

Contract  

Pre-conditions (requires)  
  1. The slice may be empty.  
  2. The slice is *strictly* increasing: for every indices `i < j`, we have `a[i] < a[j]`.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  1. Always in bounds: `0 ≤ idx ≤ a.len()`.  
  2. All elements **before** the returned position are *strictly* smaller than `key`:  
         ∀i: usize,  i < idx  ⇒  a[i] < key.  
  3. All elements **at or after** the returned position are *at least* `key`:  
         ∀i: usize,  idx ≤ i < a.len()  ⇒  key ≤ a[i].  
  4. If `idx` lies *inside* the slice (`idx < a.len()`), then either `a[idx] == key`  
     (meaning the key is present) or `a[idx] > key` (meaning the key is absent but would
     be inserted at `idx`).  
  5. The result is *uniquely determined* by Conditions 2 and 3; any two indices satisfying
     both conditions must be equal.  (This can be stated as: for all j, if
     Conditions 2 and 3 hold with `j` in place of `idx`, then `j == idx`.)

Interpretation  
  • The function returns the classical “lower-bound” / binary-search insertion point.  
  • Returning `a.len()` is permitted and indicates that `key` is larger than every element.  
  • An implementation **must** certify that the returned index obeys the global
    quantified properties 2 & 3; a linear scan or binary search is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified function `find_first` in Verus-Rust whose behaviour is fully characterised below.

Signature  
    fn find_first(a: &[int], key: int) -> (idx: usize)

Inputs  
  • `a`: an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
  • `key`: the integer value to search.

Output  
  • `idx`: a `usize` that is either the index of the *first* occurrence of `key`
           or exactly one past the end of the slice.

Contract  

Pre-conditions (requires)  
  1. The slice may be empty.  
  2. The slice is sorted in *non-decreasing* order:
         ∀i, j: usize, i < j ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  1. Bounds: `0 ≤ idx ≤ a.len()`.  
  2. If `idx < a.len()`, then `a[idx] == key` (the key was found).  
  3. If `idx == a.len()`, then the key is **absent** from the entire slice:
         ∀i: usize, i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ key.  
  4. First-occurrence guarantee:  
         ∀j: usize, j < idx ⇒ a[j] ≠ key.  
     (No earlier index holds the key.)

Interpretation  
  • Compared with a plain “maybe-find”, the function must now either prove absence
    or return the *left-most* position where the key appears.  
  • Quantified conditions 3 & 4 force the use of non-trivial reasoning over the slice.  
  • Any correct algorithm—e.g. a refined binary search—is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified function `equal_range` in Verus-Rust that locates the contiguous block
of elements equal to a given key in a sorted slice.

Signature  
    fn equal_range(a: &[int], key: int) -> (start: usize, end: usize)

Inputs  
  • `a`: a (possibly empty) slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
  • `key`: the target value.

Outputs  
  • `start`, `end`: `usize` indices, with the convention that the half-open interval
    `[start, end)` contains *all* keys equal to `key`.  It is allowed that `start == end`.

Contract  

Pre-conditions (requires)  
  1. The slice is sorted in *non-decreasing* order:
         ∀i, j: usize, i < j ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[j].

Post-conditions (ensures)  
  1. Bounds: `0 ≤ start ≤ end ≤ a.len()`.  
  2. Every index in the half-open interval holds the key:  
         ∀i: usize, start ≤ i < end ⇒ a[i] == key.  
  3. No index *before* `start` holds the key:  
         ∀i: usize, i < start ⇒ a[i] ≠ key.  
  4. No index *after or at* `end` holds the key:  
         ∀i: usize, end ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ key.  
  5. The pair `(start, end)` is therefore *uniquely determined* by the slice and key.

Interpretation  
  • If the key does not appear, then `start == end` and both indices form the canonical
    insertion point.  
  • Implementations typically combine two binary searches, but any method that meets all
    ensures—including proofs of absence outside `[start, end)`—is acceptable.  
  • Compared with simpler “find” tasks, the student must reason about two indices,
    global quantifiers, and the maximal contiguous block of equal values.
>>>

Inputs
    1. a: `&[int]` – an immutable slice of mathematical integers.
    2. b: `&[int]` – another immutable slice of mathematical integers.

Output

Required pre-conditions

    2. Length bound: `a.len() < 10_000`.
    3. No division by zero: for every natural index k less than the common length, `b[k] ≠ 0`.
    4. Bounded quotient: for every natural index k less than the common length, the integer division `a[k] / b[k]` lies within the signed 32-bit range: `i32::MIN ≤ a[k] / b[k] ≤ i32::MAX`.

Guaranteed post-conditions
    1. The result vector’s length equals the input length: `result.len() == a.len()`.
    2. Element-wise correctness: for every index i within that length,  
       `result[i] == a[i] / b[i]`.

There are no auxiliary spec functions or lemmas; the above pre- and post-conditions fully describe the expected behaviour. Any implementation that, under the stated assumptions, returns a vector satisfying those two properties is acceptable (e.g. a straightforward loop constructing the vector). # Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn rev(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        forall|k: int| 0 <= k && k < a.len() ==> result[k] == a[a.len() - 1 - k],
{
```

# Reasoning
We analyse the specification line by line.

1. Function signature  
   fn rev(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)  
   • The parameter a is passed by value, so the function obtains ownership of the runtime vector that it receives.  
   • The return value, here named result, is another Vec<i32>.  
   In other words, rev takes one vector of 32-bit signed integers and returns a new vector of the same type.

2. Pre-condition (requires)  
   a.len() > 0  
   – The caller must supply a vector whose length is strictly positive. An empty vector is disallowed.  
   There are no further restrictions on the actual elements, their ranges, etc.

3. Post-condition (ensures)  
   forall|k: int| 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ result[k] == a[a.len() − 1 − k]  
   • For every mathematical index k that is valid for the original vector (0 .. a.len()-1), the k-th element of result matches the element that was at the symmetric position counted from the end of a.  
   • Consequently, result is the exact reversal of a: element 0 of result was last in a, element 1 was next-to-last, …, element a.len()-1 was first.  
   • Although not spelled out, this implicitly guarantees that result has the same length as a, for otherwise the indexing result[k] would be undefined over the stated range.

4. Auxiliary items  
   The specification does not reference any helper spec functions, lemmas, or attributes. All reasoning is captured in the single universal quantifier above.

5. What an implementation must do  
   At runtime the function must construct a new Vec<i32> whose contents appear exactly in reverse order compared with the incoming vector. The pre-condition allows the implementer to assume the input is non-empty, but the algorithm should work for arbitrary positive lengths. Any implementation strategy (iterative push_back, in-place reverse via swap, use of built-ins, etc.) is acceptable as long as the verifier can prove the post-condition.

6. Subtleties  
   • The specification reasons in mathematical integers (int) when it talks about indices k, but indexing into result and a happens in executable Rust and therefore uses usize under the hood. The verifier takes care of the casts.  
   • Because a is moved into the function, its ownership is consumed, yet it may still be mentioned in ghost code such as the ensures clause; Verus allows that.  
   • No statement is made about time or memory complexity, mutability after the call, or preservation of capacity. Only functional correctness of the element order is specified.

# Final Description
<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function named rev that reverses an integer vector.

Function prototype  
fn rev(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• a: Vec<i32> — the function takes ownership of a heap-allocated vector containing 32-bit signed integers.

Output  
• result: Vec<i32> — a new vector that will hold the same elements as a but in opposite order.

Pre-condition (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0 — the input vector must contain at least one element.

Post-condition (ensures)  
For every mathematical index k with 0 ≤ k < a.len(), the following equality holds:  
    result[k] == a[a.len() - 1 - k]  
In words: element k of the returned vector equals the element that was (length-1-k) positions from the front in the original vector. Hence result is exactly the reversal of a and necessarily has the same length.

No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or special attributes are required. Any executable body that consumes the input vector and produces a vector satisfying the post-condition is acceptable and will be verified by Verus.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise only asks for a straightforward, element-wise integer division of two slices under rather generous assumptions (no zero divisors, small length, 32-bit quotients).  
To make the task more interesting we can:

• Require the function to compute *two* result vectors (quotients **and** remainders) and prove the classic division identity for every index.  
• Ask for fraction reduction, i.e. compute a *coprime* numerator/denominator pair for every component, which forces the use (and proof) of the Euclidean algorithm or a helper lemma about `gcd`.  
• Remove the “no-zero-divisor” guarantee and instead let the function return a `Result`, proving that it reports the first offending index precisely and leaves all earlier quotients correct.

All three ideas add extra reasoning steps (modular-arithmetic identities, gcd properties, conditional post-conditions), introduce less common specification patterns, and stay within typical Verus capabilities.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified function named `element_wise_division_with_remainder` in Verus-Rust that, for each position *i*, returns **both** the quotient and the (non-negative) remainder of dividing `a[i]` by `b[i]`.

Inputs  
    1. a: `&[int]` – an immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
    2. b: `&[int]` – another immutable slice of mathematical integers.

Outputs  
    • (q, r): `(Vec<int>, Vec<int>)` – two vectors of identical length.  
         • `q[i]` is the integer quotient `a[i] / b[i]` (truncated toward zero).  
         • `r[i]` is the remainder that satisfies the division identity.

Required pre-conditions  
    1. Equal lengths: `a.len() == b.len()`.  
    2. Global length bound: `a.len() < 10_000`.  
    3. Non-zero divisors: ∀ k < a.len(), `b[k] ≠ 0`.  
    4. 32-bit‐safe quotients: ∀ k, `i32::MIN ≤ a[k] / b[k] ≤ i32::MAX`.  
    5. Dividend bound: ∀ k, `|a[k]| ≤ 1_000_000 * |b[k]|` (limits the size of the remainder, forcing an extra reasoning step).

Guaranteed post-conditions  
    1. Length preservation: `q.len() == r.len() == a.len()`.  
    2. Division identity: ∀ i, `a[i] == b[i] * q[i] + r[i]`.  
    3. Proper remainder: ∀ i, `0 ≤ r[i] < |b[i]|`.  
    4. Quotient bound: ∀ i, `i32::MIN ≤ q[i] ≤ i32::MAX`.

Edge cases such as empty slices (length 0) must be handled and vacuously satisfy all clauses.

    2. b: `&[int]` – slice of **positive** mathematical integers (`b[i] > 0`).

Output  
    • result: `Vec<(int,int)>` – vector holding pairs `(p, q)`.

    2. Length bound: `a.len() ≤ 5_000`.  
    3. Magnitude constraint: ∀ k, `a[k] ≤ 10^12` ∧ `b[k] ≤ 10^12` (forces careful overflow reasoning).

Guaranteed post-conditions  
    1. Length match: `result.len() == a.len()`.  
    2. Fractional equality: ∀ i, `a[i] * result[i].1 == b[i] * result[i].0`.  
    3. Coprime pairs: ∀ i, `gcd(result[i].0, result[i].1) == 1`.  
    4. Positive, normalised denominator: ∀ i, `result[i].1 > 0`.  
    5. Size bound: ∀ i, `result[i].0 ≤ a[i]` ∧ `result[i].1 ≤ b[i]`.

No helper lemmas are pre-supplied; the implementation may define and prove its own (e.g. an iterative Euclidean algorithm) as long as the above contracts are met.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

spec fn calc_sum(n: nat) -> nat {
    n * (n + 1) / 2
}

fn sum(n: u64) -> (s: u64)
    requires
        n < 100000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX, // added check to prevent overflow
    ensures
        s == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
We have:
1. A specification-only helper `calc_sum` that, for any natural number `n` (Verus type `nat`, i.e., non-negative mathematical integer), returns the closed-form expression of the triangular number 1 + 2 + ⋯ + n, namely `n * (n + 1) / 2`.  
   • Pure, total, no pre/post clauses needed (the body is the formula itself).  
   • Result type `nat`, so the expression happens in the unbounded integer domain.

2. An executable function `sum`.
   • Signature: `fn sum(n: u64) -> (s: u64)`.
     – Input `n` is a 64-bit unsigned integer.  
     – Output binding `s` is the single returned value, also `u64`.

   • Preconditions (`requires`)
     a) `n < 100_000` – an arbitrary upper bound chosen so that the product `n*(n+1)/2` never exceeds 2⁶⁴-1.  
     b) `n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX` – an explicit overflow guard that the mathematical result still fits in a `u64`. (With the previous bound this will indeed hold; the clause is retained for clarity and to help the verifier.)

   • Postcondition (`ensures`)
     `s == n * (n + 1) / 2` – after the function returns, the produced value must equal the triangular-number formula evaluated with the same `n`.  
     The equality occurs in the `u64` domain, but because of the overflow precondition the computation is the same as the mathematical formula.

   • No further helper lemmas or spec constructs are referenced in the body; the implementer is free to return the closed-form directly, iterate and accumulate, or use any other strategy, as long as the proof shows that the result equals the expression and no runtime overflow occurs.

3. Subtleties
   • All spec arithmetic in `calc_sum` and in the `ensures`/`requires` clauses is done on mathematical integers; conversions are implicit because `n` is `u64`.  
   • The function must be executable, so if the body uses 64-bit arithmetic it has to respect Verus’s overflow-checking rules. The given preconditions guarantee that multiplying `n` and `n+1` and dividing by 2 remains within range, so a direct one-line return of the formula is permissible.  
   • The helper `calc_sum` is not strictly required to verify `sum` (the postcondition duplicates the formula), but it could be used in proofs or extensions.

# Reasoning
To raise the overall difficulty I will:
 • Increase arity (2-to-N or 3-to-4 slices) so the learner must quantify over two indices.  
 • Insert auxiliary arithmetic or memory–layout obligations (capacity, prefix-sum).  
 • Add less–common pre-conditions such as parity on data, which forces the body to *prove* facts about the elements themselves, not only their positions.  
 • Keep every new task fully self-contained, phrased in precise natural language, with no dependence on the original statement.

Three harder variants follow.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `interleave_selective`.

Signature  
    fn interleave_selective(evens: &[int], odds: &[int]) -> (r: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    evens, odds – immutable slices of unbounded mathematical integers (`int`).

Pre-conditions  
    • Equal length: evens.len() == odds.len()  
    • Parity qualification:  
        ∀ i:int (0 ≤ i < evens.len())  ⇒  evens[i] mod 2 == 0  
        ∀ i:int (0 ≤ i < odds.len())   ⇒  odds[i]  mod 2 == 1  

Output  
    r – a newly allocated vector that mixes the two slices, keeping even numbers in
        forward order and odd numbers in reverse order.

Post-conditions  
    1. Length:
           r.len() == 2 * evens.len()
    2. Layout:
           ∀ i:int (0 ≤ i < evens.len()) ⇒
               r[2*i]     == evens[i]                ∧
               r[2*i + 1] == odds[evens.len() - 1 - i]
    3. Parity preservation:
           ∀ j:int (0 ≤ j < r.len()) ⇒
               (j mod 2 == 0  ⇒  r[j] mod 2 == 0) ∧
               (j mod 2 == 1  ⇒  r[j] mod 2 == 1)

No auxiliary lemmas are required, but proving the reverse-order obligation
will demand an explicit loop invariant that tracks two independent indices.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `interleave_uniform`.

Signature  
    fn interleave_uniform(slices: &[&[int]]) -> (r: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    slices – a slice whose elements are themselves immutable slices of unbounded
             integers (`&[int]`).  Let m = slices.len().

Pre-conditions  
    • m ≥ 1  
    • All inner slices have identical length n:
         ∀ j:int (0 ≤ j < m) ⇒ slices[j].len() == n

Output  
    r – a newly allocated vector that interleaves *every* slice, cycling through
        slice 0, slice 1, …, slice m-1, then repeating, until all n rows have
        been exhausted.

Post-conditions  
    1. Length:
           r.len() == m * n
    2. Element-wise layout:
           ∀ i:int, j:int
               (0 ≤ i < n ∧ 0 ≤ j < m) ⇒
                   r[i*m + j] == slices[j][i]
    3. Minimal heap usage:
           r.capacity() == r.len()

Meeting the capacity requirement obliges the implementation to pre-reserve
exactly m × n slots (e.g. via Vec::with_capacity) and then fill them without
re-allocation, a property that must be proven inside Verus.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `interleave4_prefix`.

Signature  
    fn interleave4_prefix(a: &[int], b: &[int], c: &[int], d: &[int]) -> (r: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    a, b, c, d – immutable slices of unbounded integers (`int`).

Pre-condition  
    • Equal length: a.len() == b.len() && b.len() == c.len() && c.len() == d.len()
      (Let n denote this common length.)

Output  
    r – a newly allocated vector that interleaves elements in fixed 4-element
        blocks, in the order a, b, c, d.

Post-conditions  
    1. Length:
           r.len() == 4 * n
    2. Element-wise layout:
           ∀ i:int (0 ≤ i < n) ⇒
               r[4*i]     == a[i] ∧
               r[4*i + 1] == b[i] ∧
               r[4*i + 2] == c[i] ∧
               r[4*i + 3] == d[i]
    3. Prefix-sum preservation:
           ∀ k:int (0 ≤ k ≤ n) ⇒
               sum_{j=0}^{4*k-1} r[j] == 
               sum_{i=0}^{k-1} (a[i] + b[i] + c[i] + d[i])
           (The empty prefix case k = 0 is interpreted as 0 = 0.)
    4. No spare capacity:
           r.capacity() == r.len()

The third clause forces the implementer to maintain a running total that
co-evolves with the construction loop, demanding a non-trivial loop invariant
linking the vector’s length, its content, and the accumulated arithmetic sum.
>>>

Precondition

Interpretation

# Reasoning
The given exercise is straightforward because it simply asks the student to copy‐reverse a slice into a fresh vector.  
To make harder variants, we can
 • demand in-place mutation (no fresh vector) and stipulate O(1) extra memory,  
 • reverse only a *portion* of the slice, forcing the student to reason about three disjoint index regions,  
 • combine the reversal with some non-trivial transformation (e.g. eliminating consecutive duplicates) that changes the output length, so they must prove both a *content* property and a *length* formula.

All variants keep the spirit of “reverse” but require more intricate specifications and proofs.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally verified Verus-Rust procedure named `reverse_in_place` that reverses a mutable slice *in place* using only constant extra memory.

Signature  
fn reverse_in_place(a: &mut [char])

Inputs  
• a : mutable slice of `char`.

Outputs  
• unit (the slice itself is mutated).

Preconditions  
• a.len() ≥ 1.

Postconditions  
1. Length preservation in memory: after the call, a.len() is unchanged.  
2. Reversal property: ∀ k:nat • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒  
  old(a)[k] == a[a.len() − 1 − k] ∧ a[k] == old(a)[a.len() − 1 − k].  
   (old(a) denotes the entire slice content *before* entry.)  
3. Permutation property: the multiset of characters in `a` after the call equals the multiset in `old(a)`.  
4. Space bound: the implementation must use O(1) additional live heap cells (no auxiliary Vec or Box).

Interpretation  
On return, the original slice has been mirror-reversed in place without allocating any new dynamic buffers.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `reverse_prefix` that reverses only a prefix of a character slice.

Signature  
fn reverse_prefix(a: &[char], n: usize) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
• a : immutable slice of `char`.  
• n : length of the prefix that must be reversed.

Outputs  
• b : a `Vec<char>` of length exactly a.len().

Preconditions  
• 1 ≤ n ≤ a.len().

Postconditions  
1. Length preservation: b.len() == a.len().  
2. Reversal-of-prefix: ∀ k:nat • 0 ≤ k < n ⇒  
  b[k] == a[n − 1 − k].  
3. Suffix untouched: ∀ k:nat • n ≤ k < a.len() ⇒  
  b[k] == a[k].  
4. For all natural k, 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ the multiset of characters in `b` equals that of `a`.

Interpretation  
`b` is identical to `a` except that the first `n` characters appear in the opposite order.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `reverse_without_runs` that first removes *maximal runs* of identical consecutive characters and then returns the reverse of the remaining sequence.

Signature  
fn reverse_without_runs(a: &[char]) -> (b: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
• a : immutable, non-empty slice of `char`.

Outputs  
• b : a `Vec<char>` whose length may differ from a.len().

Helper definition  
• Define the helper function distinct_runs(a) to be the sequence obtained from `a` by keeping the first element of every maximal run of equal consecutive characters and discarding the rest.

Preconditions  
• a.len() > 0.

Postconditions  
1. Length formula: b.len() == distinct_runs(a).len().  
2. Reversal property: ∀ k:nat • 0 ≤ k < b.len() ⇒  
  b[k] == distinct_runs(a)[distinct_runs(a).len() − 1 − k].  
3. Content preservation modulo runs: the multiset of characters in `b` equals the multiset in distinct_runs(a).

Interpretation  
Take the slice, compress it by deleting all but the first character of each identical consecutive block, and finally return the reverse of that compressed sequence. The student must reason both about how compression affects indices and about the final reversal.
>>>

Design a formally-verified Verus/Rust function named `append_array_to_seq` whose purpose is to concatenate two vectors of 32-bit signed integers.

Signature  
fn append_array_to_seq(s: Vec<i32>, a: &Vec<i32>) -> (r: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• s : Vec<i32> — a vector taken by value (ownership is moved into the function).  
• a : &Vec<i32> — an immutable reference to a second vector (caller retains ownership).

Output  
• r : Vec<i32> — the vector that results from appending all elements of `a` to the end of `s`.

Specification

Pre-condition (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0 — the second vector must contain at least one element. No assumption is made about the length of `s`.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. r.len() == s.len() + a.len() — the length of the result is exactly the sum of the input lengths.  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ r[i] == s[i] — every element that was in `s` appears unchanged at the beginning of `r`.  
3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ r[s.len() + i] == a[i] — every element that was in `a` appears unchanged in `r` immediately after the elements coming from `s`.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are used. Any implementation that, given ownership of `s` and read-only access to `a`, produces a vector `r` satisfying the three post-conditions (for instance by calling `s.extend_from_slice(a)` and returning `s`) will be accepted by the verifier.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn find_min_index(a: &[int], s: usize, e: usize) -> (min_i: usize)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
        s < a.len(),
        e <= a.len(),
        e > s,
    ensures
        min_i >= s,
        min_i < e,
        forall|k: int| s as int <= k && k < e as int ==> a[min_i as int] <= a[k as int],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We examine the function signature and the contract lines to understand exactly what the function must do.

1. Name  
   • `find_min_index`

2. Inputs  
   • `a: &[int]` – an immutable slice of `int` values.  
   • `s: usize` – the inclusive lower bound of the search interval.  
   • `e: usize` – the exclusive upper bound of the search interval.

3. Output  
   • `min_i: usize` – the index where a minimum value occurs, according to the guarantees below.

4. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a) `a.len() > 0`  
      – The slice is not empty (this also implies that `usize` indices are meaningful).  

   b) `s < a.len()`  
      – The start index `s` lies inside the slice.  

   c) `e <= a.len()`  
      – The end index `e` does not extend past the slice.  

   d) `e > s`  
      – The interval `[s, e)` is non-empty; there is at least one element to examine.

   These four conditions together mean that `[s, e)` is a valid, non-empty half-open sub-range of the slice.

5. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   a) `min_i >= s` and  
   b) `min_i < e`  
      – The returned index lies inside exactly that same half-open interval.  

   c) `forall|k: int| s <= k < e ⇒ a[min_i] <= a[k]`  
      – For every index `k` inside `[s, e)` the value at `a[min_i]` is less-than-or-equal-to `a[k]`.  
      In other words, `min_i` points to a position that holds a minimum value over the specified range.  
      If several positions share that minimum value, any one of them is acceptable because the contract only requires the ≤ relation, not strict < nor uniqueness.

6. Absent clauses  
   • There are no additional `ensures` about uniqueness or about elements outside the interval.  
   • There are no helper spec functions or lemmas referenced.

7. Consequences for an implementation  
   • Because `[s, e)` is guaranteed non-empty, an implementation can safely initialise `min_i` to `s` and then scan the slice from `s+1` up to `e-1`, updating `min_i` whenever it finds a strictly smaller element (if ties are kept the same, the ≤ guarantee still holds).  
   • The values inside `a` are mathematical `int`s, so comparison uses the unbounded integer order; however, run-time code may still compile to ordinary machine integers as long as it behaves consistently with mathematical ordering.

The above fully determines the expected behaviour: given any non-empty sub-slice `[s, e)`, return some index inside it that contains a value no larger than any other value in that same sub-slice.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Create a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following specification.

Function name  
    find_min_index

Signature  
    fn find_min_index(a: &[int], s: usize, e: usize) -> (min_i: usize)

Inputs  
    • a – an immutable slice whose elements are mathematical integers (`int`).  
    • s – a starting index (inclusive) of type `usize`.  
    • e – an ending index (exclusive) of type `usize`.

Returned value  
    • min_i – the index at which a minimum element is found.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0             – the slice is not empty.  
2. s < a.len()             – the start index is within the slice.  
3. e <= a.len()            – the end index does not exceed the slice length.  
4. e > s                   – the interval [s, e) contains at least one element.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. min_i >= s              – the returned index is not before the interval.  
2. min_i < e               – the returned index is inside the interval.  
3. ∀ k:int,  s ≤ k < e ⇒ a[min_i] ≤ a[k]  
   That is, for every index k in the half-open range [s, e) the value at position min_i is less-than-or-equal-to the value at k.  
   Consequently, min_i points to a minimum value within the specified sub-slice.  
   If the minimum occurs at multiple positions, returning any one of them satisfies the contract.

No further helper functions or lemmas are required. An implementation can, for example, scan the sub-slice linearly, updating the current minimum index whenever it observes a strictly smaller element.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The learner’s original task (“just append `a` onto `s`”) is almost trivial:  
* Only one pre-condition, three straightforward post-conditions.  
* No auxiliary lemmas, no subtle arithmetic properties, and only a single linear scan of data is needed.

To raise the level of difficulty, I will create **three** stand-alone problems that each require (1) a richer specification and (2) more intricate reasoning than a direct call to `extend_from_slice`.  
The ideas used:

1. **Insertion at an arbitrary position** – forces the student to reason about three contiguous slices (`prefix`, `a`, `suffix`) and correctly glue them back together.
2. **Merging two *strictly* ascending lists while removing duplicates** – introduces ordering, set-like reasoning, and a less common “no repeats” constraint.
3. **Interleaving elements** – makes each post-index depend on two different source indices and therefore involves quantified arithmetic with `2*i` and `2*i+1`.

Each new task remains short enough to fit comfortably in a classroom/assessment setting, yet each one requires several additional verification steps compared with the original.



# Output
<<<
Design a formally-verified Verus/Rust function named `insert_slice_at` that inserts every element of the slice `a` *inside* the vector `s` at a caller-supplied index `k`.

Signature  
fn insert_slice_at(mut s: Vec<i32>, a: &Vec<i32>, k: usize) -> (r: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• s : Vec<i32> — vector whose ownership is moved into the function.  
• a : &Vec<i32> — immutable reference to the slice that will be inserted.  
• k : usize   — insertion position counted from the start of `s` (0 ≤ k ≤ s.len()).

Output  
• r : Vec<i32> — the vector obtained from `s` after inserting the full contents of `a` starting at index `k`.

Specification  

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() > 0                       — the slice to insert is non-empty.  
2. k <= s.len()                      — insertion index must be in or at the end of `s`.  

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. r.len() == s.len() + a.len()        — length grows exactly by a.len().  
2. ∀i: int • 0 ≤ i < k ⇒ r[i] == s[i] — the prefix left of `k` is unchanged.  
3. ∀i: int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ r[k + i] == a[i]  
4. ∀i: int • k ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ r[a.len() + i] == s[i]  
  (the original suffix of `s` begins right after the newly inserted slice).  

No auxiliary lemmas may be called. Any algorithm whose verified implementation satisfies these four post-conditions is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function `merge_sorted_unique` that merges two *strictly ascending* vectors of 32-bit integers, discarding duplicates so that the result is also strictly ascending.

Signature  
fn merge_sorted_unique(s: Vec<i32>, t: Vec<i32>) -> (r: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
• s : Vec<i32> — caller gives up ownership; guaranteed to be strictly increasing.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

// Specification function to calculate power
spec fn power(x: nat, y: nat) -> nat {
    x * y
}

fn pot(x: u128, y: u128) -> (r: u128)
    requires
        x >= 0,
        y >= 0,
        x < 1000000, // added a limit to prevent overflow
        y < 1000000, // added a limit to prevent overflow
        x < u128::MAX / u128::MAX, // added a limit to prevent overflow
        y < u128::MAX / u128::MAX, // added a limit to prevent overflow
    ensures
        r == power(x as nat, y as nat),
{
```

# Reasoning
The snippet contains two items:

1. A pure, ghost‐only specification function `power`.  
   • Signature: `spec fn power(x: nat, y: nat) -> nat`  
   • Behaviour: returns `x * y`.  
   • Domain: the arguments and the result live in `nat` (Verus’ mathematical type of
     non-negative unbounded integers).

2. An executable function `pot`.  
   • Signature: `fn pot(x: u128, y: u128) -> (r: u128)` – it takes two 128-bit unsigned
     integers and produces one 128-bit unsigned integer.  
   • Preconditions (requires):
       – `x >= 0`, `y >= 0` (trivially true for any `u128`, but still listed).  
       – `x < 1_000_000`, `y < 1_000_000` – the caller must supply numbers below one
         million.  
       – `x < u128::MAX / u128::MAX`, `y < u128::MAX / u128::MAX`.  Since
         `u128::MAX / u128::MAX == 1`, these two clauses force  
         `x < 1` and `y < 1`; therefore, given that `u128` is non-negative, both `x`
         and `y` can **only be 0** when the pre-condition is satisfied.
   • Post-condition (ensures):  
         `r == power(x as nat, y as nat)`  
     which, by definition of `power`, is  
         `r == (x as nat) * (y as nat)`.  
     After casting back to `u128`, this is equivalent to `r == x * y`.

Because the compound set of preconditions collapses to `x == 0 ∧ y == 0`,
the only legal invocation of `pot` is with both arguments equal to zero, and the
result must therefore be zero as well.

No additional helper lemmas, ghost state, or termination annotations are
required.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function as follows.

1. Pure specification helper  
   spec fn power(x: nat, y: nat) -> nat  
   • Returns the mathematical product x · y (simple multiplication of naturals).

2. Executable routine  
   fn pot(x: u128, y: u128) -> (r: u128)

   Inputs  
     – x: u128  
     – y: u128

   Preconditions (requires)  
     1. x ≥ 0  
     2. y ≥ 0  
     3. x < 1_000_000  
     4. y < 1_000_000  
     5. x < u128::MAX / u128::MAX   // i.e. x < 1  
     6. y < u128::MAX / u128::MAX   // i.e. y < 1  
     (Taken together, conditions 1, 5 and 6 force x = 0 and y = 0.)

   Post-condition (ensures)  
     r == power(x as nat, y as nat)  
     — equivalently, r equals the product of the two inputs, viewed as natural
       numbers and cast back to u128.

   Consequence  
     Under the legal pre-conditions the only admissible call is pot(0, 0) and the
     function must return 0.

These contracts fully determine the behaviour: any body that (provably) returns
0 when both inputs are 0, and is unreachable for all other values, satisfies the
specification.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: return any index whose element is maximal.  
To raise the difficulty, I will generate three alternative specifications that demand additional reasoning and slightly longer proofs while still being clear and self-contained.

Version 1 (tie-breaking with a secondary rule and extra output):
 • Still finds the maximum, but if several positions share that maximum, the smallest *even* index among them must be returned.  
 • In addition to the index, the function must also return the maximal value itself, so the spec now has two output components.  
 • Requires proving both “maximality” and the specific, less common tie-breaking rule.

Version 2 (simultaneous first- and second-maximums):
 • Outputs two indices, `i_max` and `i_second`, such that
      – `a[i_max]` is the greatest element,
      – `a[i_second]` is the greatest among the *strictly* smaller elements,
      – the two indices are distinct.  
 • All corner-cases (vector length exactly 2, duplicates, etc.) need careful quantification.  
 • Adds an existence precondition: `a.len() ≥ 2` and asserts that there are at least two distinct values.

Version 3 (unique maximum, plus sum-of-prefix condition):
 • Precondition states that the maximum value occurs exactly once in the vector.  
 • The function returns that unique index, and additionally proves that the sum of all elements *before* that index is ≤ the sum of all elements *after* that index.  
   This turns the problem into a two-phase reasoning task: maximality plus a non-trivial arithmetic property of the index with respect to the whole vector.

Each version adds roughly ten extra words, replaces the common “any max index” rule with a less usual requirement, and introduces more logical steps without relying on artificial time- or space-complexity constraints.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus function with the exact interface

fn find_even_minimal_max(a: &Vec<int>) -> (i: usize, max_val: int)

Specification details:

• Input  
  – a: immutable reference to a vector of mathematical integers (`int`).

• Output  
  – i: a `usize` index,  
  – max_val: the maximal value found in the vector.

• Preconditions  
  1. a.len() > 0 — the vector contains at least one element.

• Postconditions  
  1. 0 ≤ i < a.len() — the returned index is within bounds.  
  2. max_val == a[i] — `max_val` equals the element at position `i`.  
  3. ∀ k:nat • k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ max_val — `max_val` is greater than or equal to every element in `a`.  
  4. Let S = { k:nat | k < a.len() ∧ a[k] == max_val }.  
      i ∈ S ∧ (∀ k ∈ S • (k is even ⇒ i ≤ k)) ∧ (∀ k ∈ S • (i is even) ∨ (k is even) )  
      — among all indices whose element equals the maximum, the chosen index `i` is the smallest *even* one if such an index exists; otherwise it is simply the smallest index in `S`.  
  (The tie-breaking rule above is mandatory and must be formally proved.)
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus function with the exact interface

fn two_largest_indices(a: &Vec<int>) -> (i_max: usize, i_second: usize)

Specification details:

• Input  
  – a: immutable reference to a vector of mathematical integers (`int`).

• Output  
  – i_max: index of the largest element,  
  – i_second: index of the second-largest element (strictly smaller than the largest).

• Preconditions  
  1. a.len() ≥ 2 — the vector contains at least two elements.  
  2. ∃ x, y:int • x ≠ y ∧ (∃ k,l:nat • k < a.len() ∧ l < a.len() ∧ a[k]==x ∧ a[l]==y)  
      — the vector contains at least two distinct values.

• Postconditions  
  1. Distinctness: i_max ≠ i_second.  
  2. Bounds: 0 ≤ i_max < a.len() ∧ 0 ≤ i_second < a.len().  
  3. Ordering: a[i_max] > a[i_second].  
  4. Maximality: ∀ k:nat • k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i_max].  
  5. Second-maximality: ∀ k:nat • k < a.len() ∧ k ≠ i_max ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i_second].  
  6. Tie-breaking (optional): if several indices share the second-largest value, returning any one of them is acceptable.
>>>

Implement a verified Verus function with the exact interface

fn unique_max_balanced(a: &Vec<int>) -> (i: usize)

Specification details:

• Output  
  – i: the index of the unique maximal element.

• Preconditions  
  1. a.len() > 0.  
  2. ∀ k,l:nat • k < a.len() ∧ l < a.len() ∧ k ≠ l ⇒ a[k] ≠ a[l] ∨ a[k] < max(a) ∨ a[l] < max(a)  
      — the maximal value appears exactly once in `a`.

• Postconditions  
  1. 0 ≤ i < a.len().  
  2. ∀ k:nat • k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≤ a[i].  
  3. ∀ k:nat • k < a.len() ∧ k ≠ i ⇒ a[k] < a[i]  
      — strict inequality because the maximum is unique.  
  4. let prefix_sum = Σ_{k < i} a[k];  
      let suffix_sum = Σ_{k > i} a[k];  
      prefix_sum ≤ suffix_sum  
      — the sum of elements before the maximal index does not exceed the sum of those after it.  
  (The implementation must compute `i` and formally verify all four postconditions.)
>>>

    requires

The snippet contains two items: a pure specification function named `f` and an executable, verified function named `mod_func`.

1.  `spec fn f`
    • Input: `n` of type `nat` (Verus’s mathematical type for natural numbers: all non-negative integers).  
    • Output: `nat`.  
    • Body: simply returns `n * n`.  
    • Purpose: provide a ghost-level definition of “square” that can be referenced from post-conditions.

2.  `fn mod_func`
    • Input: `n: u64` (an unsigned 64-bit integer).  
    • Output: `a: u64`.  
    • Preconditions (`requires`)  
        – `n >= 0` (trivial for `u64` but stated explicitly).  
        – `n < 1000` (an arbitrary upper bound chosen so that `n * n` will not overflow a `u64` at run time).  
        – `n * n < u64::MAX` (another, more general, overflow guard).  
    • Post-condition (`ensures`)  
        – `a == f(n as nat)` – the return value `a` must be exactly the square of `n`, expressed with the helper `f` after casting `n` from `u64` to `nat`.

No other side conditions or auxiliary lemmas are involved.  Any body that calculates `n * n` (without overflow) and returns it will satisfy the specification.

# Reasoning
The original exercise only asks the learner to return a Boolean telling whether every inner vector has the same length.  
To make variants that are still recognisable but require appreciably more proof work, I introduce one or more of the following twists:

• Returning a *witness* (`Option<nat>`) instead of a bare `bool`; the post-condition must now relate the witness as well as the truth value.  
• Conjoining an *extra* structural property (e.g. “each inner vector is a palindrome”) with the original equal-length requirement, forcing the learner to reason about two independent conditions.  
• Replacing the pairwise-equality check with a less common pattern: the lengths have to form an *arithmetic progression*, so the learner must quantify over an existential “common difference” before the universal property over indices.

Each variant is still solvable with linear scans, needs no sophisticated algorithms, but demands several more verification steps and slightly deeper quantifier manipulation.

        -> (same_len: bool, witness: Option<nat>)

Outputs

    witness  – `Option<nat>` holding a candidate common length.

Pre-conditions

    • No restriction on the contents or lengths of the inner vectors.

Post-conditions  
    1. same_len  ⇔  (∀ i,j. 0 ≤ i < sequences.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < sequences.len()
                     ⇒ sequences[i].len() = sequences[j].len())  
       (i.e. `same_len` is true exactly when *every* inner vector length is identical.)  

    2. witness.is_Some() ⇔ same_len.

           ∀ i. 0 ≤ i < sequences.len() ⇒ sequences[i].len() = ℓ.  

    3. For an *empty* outer vector, the function must return  
           (same_len == true, witness == Some(0)).

       of `sequences.len()` (this disallows copying the whole input).

No auxiliary lemmas are disallowed; you may introduce helper specifications if helpful.

<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function named `all_sequences_equal_length_and_palindrome`.

Signature  
    fn all_sequences_equal_length_and_palindrome(sequences: Vec<Vec<int>>)
        -> (result: bool)

Inputs  
    sequences – a vector of integer vectors, as before.

Pre-condition  
    sequences.len() < 1000

Post-condition  
    result is *logically equivalent* to the conjunction  
        A) ∀ i,j. 0 ≤ i,j < sequences.len()
              ⇒ sequences[i].len() = sequences[j].len()  
        AND  
        B) ∀ k. 0 ≤ k < sequences.len()
              ⇒ sequences[k] is a palindrome  
                 (formally: ∀ p. 0 ≤ p < sequences[k].len()
                               ⇒ sequences[k][p] = sequences[k][sequences[k].len() − 1 − p])

    *and* each inner vector reads the same forwards and backwards.

    outer vector.

Additional notes  
• The function is polymorphic over a type parameter `T` that must satisfy the `Copy` trait, ensuring that each element can be duplicated without ownership complications.  
• There are no further restrictions on the contents or emptiness of `s`.  
• No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are needed.  
• Any algorithm that allocates a new `Vec<T>` and copies each element—while proving the above contracts—constitutes a valid solution.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Finds the longest common prefix of two strings
fn longest_common_prefix(str1: &[char], str2: &[char]) -> (prefix: Vec<char>)
    requires
        str1.len() < 1000,
        str2.len() < 1000,
    ensures
        prefix.len() <= str1.len(),
        prefix.len() <= str2.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < prefix.len() ==> prefix[i] == str1[i as int] && prefix[i] == str2[i as int],
        prefix.len() == str1.len() || prefix.len() == str2.len() || (str1[prefix.len() as int] != str2[prefix.len() as int]),
{
```

# Reasoning
• Function name & purpose  
  longest_common_prefix takes two character slices and returns the vector of characters forming their maximal common prefix.

• Inputs  
  – str1 : `&[char]` — immutable slice representing the first string.  
  – str2 : `&[char]` — immutable slice representing the second string.

• Output  
  – prefix : `Vec<char>` — a heap-allocated vector that will hold the characters that are equal at the beginning of both strings.

• Preconditions (`requires`)  
  1. `str1.len() < 1000`  
  2. `str2.len() < 1000`  
  These numeric upper bounds are arbitrary; they merely limit the domain the verifier must consider and avoid large proofs.  They do **not** influence the semantic definition of “longest common prefix”.

• Postconditions (`ensures`)  
  1. `prefix.len() <= str1.len()` – the result can never be longer than the first input.  
  2. `prefix.len() <= str2.len()` – nor longer than the second input.  
  3. `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < prefix.len() ⇒ prefix[i] == str1[i] ∧ prefix[i] == str2[i]`  
     Every index that is inside `prefix` contains exactly the same character that sits at that position in both inputs; therefore `prefix` **is** a common prefix of the two strings.  
  4. `prefix.len() == str1.len() ∨ prefix.len() == str2.len() ∨ str1[prefix.len()] != str2[prefix.len()]`  
     This disjunction captures maximality.  Either  
        • one of the strings ends exactly where the prefix stops, in which case the entire shorter string is the prefix, *or*  
        • both strings are longer than the prefix (`prefix.len()` is a valid index in each) but the very next characters differ, so the prefix cannot be extended by even one more character.  

  Together these four clauses uniquely specify “the longest common prefix”.

• Auxiliary spec items  
  None are used; the specification is completely self-contained.

• Subtleties  
  – Index arithmetic in the spec is done with mathematical integers (`int`) but cast back to Rust `usize` where necessary.  
  – Because the inputs are slices, they are not owned; returning a `Vec<char>` copies the prefix, which matches typical Rust usage.  
  – The pre-conditions impose no ordering or uniqueness constraints on the characters themselves, only size constraints.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function

    fn longest_common_prefix(str1: &[char], str2: &[char]) -> (prefix: Vec<char>)

that computes the maximal initial segment shared by two character slices.

Detailed contract:

Inputs
  • str1: immutable slice of `char` (first string).  
  • str2: immutable slice of `char` (second string).

Output
  • prefix: `Vec<char>` containing exactly the characters that both strings share from position 0 up to, but not including, the first mismatch (or until one string ends).

Pre-conditions (requires)
  1. `str1.len() < 1000`
  2. `str2.len() < 1000`
  (These bounds are only to ease verification; the algorithm must work for every pair of slices satisfying them.)

Post-conditions (ensures)
  1. `prefix.len() <= str1.len()`  
  2. `prefix.len() <= str2.len()`  
  3. `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < prefix.len() ⇒ prefix[i] == str1[i] ∧ prefix[i] == str2[i]`  
     – Every character stored in `prefix` equals the character in the same position of *both* input slices, so `prefix` is indeed a common prefix.
  4. `prefix.len() == str1.len()` **or**  
     `prefix.len() == str2.len()` **or**  
     `str1[prefix.len()] != str2[prefix.len()]`  
     – Either the prefix exhausts one of the strings, or the very next characters differ. Hence the prefix is **maximal**; it cannot be extended by even one additional character while remaining common to both strings.

There are no auxiliary spec functions or lemmas; all logical requirements are spelled out in the `ensures` list. Any body that respects the contract (for example, by iterating over the slices until a mismatch or end is reached and collecting the matching characters into a `Vec<char>`) is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original `iter_copy` specification is trivial: the implementer only has to allocate a new `Vec<T>`, copy each element, and prove a straightforward element-wise equality.  
To make the exercise more interesting while still being approachable, I introduce tasks that  

1. keep the same “copy from slice to `Vec`” flavour,  
2. add at most a handful of new English words, and  
3. require several extra proof steps (index arithmetic, ordering, set–like reasoning, or arithmetic overflow avoidance).

I propose three stand-alone variants:

• Variant 1 (“rotate”): copying while performing an in-place rotation requires index-arithmetic proofs (modulo arithmetic, non-empty slice, bound checks).  
• Variant 2 (“stable dedup”): copying while removing duplicates in first-appearance order needs reasoning about uniqueness, subset/superset relations, and order preservation.  
• Variant 3 (“exclusive prefix sum”): copying while turning each element into the sum of all previous ones involves inductive arithmetic proofs and overflow safety.

Each description below fully specifies inputs, outputs, traits, pre- and post-conditions so that a spec generator can work from the text alone.

# Output
<<<
Implement a formally-verified generic function named `rotate_left_copy` in Verus/Rust.

Signature  
    fn rotate_left_copy<T: Copy>(s: &[T], k: usize) -> (t: Vec<T>)

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. `s.len() < i32::MAX` — the slice length comfortably fits in an `i32`.  
2. `s.len() > 0` — rotation of an empty slice is undefined.  
3. `k < s.len()` — the left-rotation amount is strictly less than the slice length.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. `t.len() == s.len()` — the output vector has the same length as the input slice.  
2. `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ t[i] == s[(i + k) % s.len()]` — element `i` of `t` equals the element that was `k` places to its right in `s` (classic left rotation).  

Additional notes  
• The function is polymorphic over `T` and only requires `T: Copy`.  
• It may allocate exactly `s.len()` fresh elements; no sharing with `s` is allowed.  
• No further helpers are required, but the implementer will need to reason about modular arithmetic and array bounds to discharge the proof obligations.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified generic function named `dedup_preserve` in Verus/Rust.

Signature  
    fn dedup_preserve<T: Copy + Eq + core::hash::Hash>(s: &[T]) -> (t: Vec<T>)

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. `s.len() < i32::MAX` — the original slice fits in an `i32`.  
2. `distinct(s).len() < i32::MAX` — the number of different values appearing in `s` also fits in an `i32`.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. `t.len() == distinct(s).len()` — the result length equals the count of distinct values in `s`.  
2. `∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < t.len() ⇒ t[i] != t[j]` — all elements of `t` are pairwise different.  
3. `∀ v:T • v ∈ t ⇔ v ∈ s` — `t` contains every value that occurs in `s`, and nothing else.  
4. Stable order: `∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < t.len() ⇒ first_index(s, t[i]) < first_index(s, t[j])` — each value appears exactly once and in the same relative order as its first appearance in `s`.

Helper definitions (mathematical, not code)  
• `distinct(s)` denotes the mathematical set of values in `s`.  
• `first_index(s, v)` is the smallest index `p` such that `s[p] == v`.

Additional notes  
• Besides `Copy`, the element type must implement `Eq` and `Hash` so that concrete code can perform efficient duplicate detection.  
• The proof will involve set reasoning (membership and cardinality) and order preservation arguments.  
• No extra asymptotic complexity constraints are imposed, but an `O(n)` implementation using a ghost `HashSet` for reasoning is natural.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified function named `exclusive_prefix_sum` in Verus/Rust.

Signature  
    fn exclusive_prefix_sum(s: &[u64]) -> (t: Vec<u64>)

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. `s.len() < i32::MAX` — the slice length fits in an `i32`.  
2. `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i ≤ s.len() ⇒ sum(s[0 .. i]) ≤ u64::MAX` — every prefix sum of `s` fits in a `u64`, preventing arithmetic overflow during computation.  
   (Here `sum(s[0 .. i])` is the mathematical sum of the first `i` elements; by convention the empty sum is `0`.)

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. `t.len() == s.len()` — the output vector matches the input length.  
2. `t[0] == 0` — the first element is always zero (sum of zero previous elements).  
3. `∀ i:int • 0 < i < s.len() ⇒ t[i] == t[i-1] + s[i-1]` — each entry is obtained by adding the previous input element to the previous output entry.  
4. Equivalent global property: `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ t[i] == sum(s[0 .. i])` — `t[i]` is the sum of all elements strictly before index `i` in `s`.

Additional notes  
• Only the standard unsigned 64-bit integer type is used; no traits other than the default numeric ones are needed.  
• The implementer must prove absence of overflow using the second pre-condition and show by induction that property 4 holds for all indices.  
• A single pass over `s`, accumulating a running total, suffices for both code and proof.
>>>

Signature  
fn multiply_elements(a: Vec<i32>, b: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Contract

• The two input vectors must be the same length: a.len() == b.len().

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. result.len() == a.len() (the output vector’s length equals the inputs’ length).  
2. For every index i in the valid range 0 ≤ i < result.len(), the element at that index is the wrapping 32-bit product of the corresponding elements of the inputs:  
 result[i] == a[i].wrapping_mul(b[i]).

Goal  
Implement the function body (e.g., by zipping the two vectors and pushing the wrapping products into a new vector) and supply whatever auxiliary proofs are necessary so that the Verus verifier can confirm the above specification.

use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn pot(x: u64, y: u64) -> (r: u64)
    requires
        x < u64::MAX / u64::MAX,
        y < u64::MAX / u64::MAX,
        x * y < u64::MAX,
    ensures
        r == x * y,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The specification introduces a single executable function `pot`.

1. Function name  
   – `pot` (possibly short for “product”).

2. Arguments and their types  
   – `x: u64` – an unsigned 64-bit integer.  
   – `y: u64` – another unsigned 64-bit integer.  

3. Return value  
   – `r: u64` – the function must return a 64-bit unsigned integer.

4. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a) `x < u64::MAX / u64::MAX`  
      • `u64::MAX` is `2⁶⁴ − 1`. Integer division of it by itself yields `1`, therefore the requirement simplifies to `x < 1`.  
      • Since `x` is an unsigned integer, the only value that satisfies `x < 1` is `0`.  
   b) `y < u64::MAX / u64::MAX`  
      • Exactly the same reasoning: this forces `y` to equal `0`.  
   c) `x * y < u64::MAX`  
      • Given that both operands are forced to `0`, their product is `0`, which certainly is `< u64::MAX`.  
   In consequence, the pre-condition set restricts the function’s domain to the single input pair `(x = 0, y = 0)`.

5. Post-condition (`ensures`)  
   – `r == x * y`  
     The function must return the exact mathematical product of `x` and `y`, performed in the unbounded integer domain and then re-interpreted as `u64`.  
     Given the pre-conditions, this collapses to `r == 0`.

6. No other helper functions, ghost state, or auxiliary lemmas are referenced.

7. Practical consequences  
   – Any verified implementation may simply return `0`, because that is the only admissible result for the singleton input space.  
   – Overflow cannot occur thanks to the third pre-condition.

<<<
Implement a formally-verified Verus function called `pot` whose sole purpose is to multiply two unsigned 64-bit integers, but under an extremely restrictive domain.

Signature  
    fn pot(x: u64, y: u64) -> (r: u64)

Inputs  
    • x: u64  
    • y: u64  

Output  
    • r: u64 – expected to equal the mathematical product x × y.

Preconditions (`requires`)  
    1. x < u64::MAX / u64::MAX   // equivalent to x < 1 ⇒ x = 0  
    2. y < u64::MAX / u64::MAX   // equivalent to y < 1 ⇒ y = 0  
    3. x * y < u64::MAX          // guarantees absence of overflow (vacuously true when both are 0)

Postcondition (`ensures`)  
    r == x * y

Informally: the caller is allowed to pass only the pair (0, 0); the function must then return 0. Any body that provably meets these contracts—e.g. `r = 0`—is valid.
>>>

use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Decreases k by n
fn main_func(n: u64, k: u64) -> (k_out: u64)
    requires
        n > 0,
        k > n,
        n < u64::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        k < u64::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        k_out >= 0,
        k_out == k - n,
{
```

# Reasoning
main_func is an executable (non-spec) Verus function that simply subtracts one 64-bit unsigned integer from another and returns the result, but it is wrapped in a formally-verified contract.  

Inputs  
• n: u64 – the positive decrement amount.  
• k: u64 – the original value from which n will be subtracted.  

Preconditions (requires)  
1. n > 0 — zero is not allowed; the call must actually perform a reduction.  
2. k > n — guarantees that the subtraction is well-defined in the unsigned domain and avoids underflow.  
3. n < u64::MAX / 2 and 4. k < u64::MAX / 2 — additional “head-room” constraints that keep both operands in the lower half of the 64-bit range. These make the proof obligation k − n < u64::MAX succeed easily and also bound the result because k_out ≤ k < 2⁶³.  

Output  
• k_out: u64 – the computed difference.

Postconditions (ensures)  
1. k_out ≥ 0 (trivial for u64 but stated explicitly).  
2. k_out == k − n — the returned value must equal the mathematical subtraction of the two inputs.

There are no helper spec functions, no ghost arguments, and no decreases clause because the body will be a simple, terminating calculation.  Any implementation that, given the stated preconditions, returns k − n as a u64 will satisfy the contract.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified Verus function having the following contract:

Signature
    fn main_func(n: u64, k: u64) -> (k_out: u64)

Meaning
    main_func returns the unsigned 64-bit integer obtained by subtracting n from k.

Inputs
    n : u64 — must be strictly positive.  
    k : u64 — must be strictly greater than n.

Preconditions (requires)
    1. n > 0  
    2. k > n  
    3. n < u64::MAX / 2  
    4. k < u64::MAX / 2  
       (The last two bounds ensure that both operands lie in the lower half of the 64-bit range, making it straightforward to prove that the subtraction does not underflow or overflow.)

Output
    k_out : u64

Postconditions (ensures)
    • k_out ≥ 0   (redundant for unsigned integers but listed explicitly)  
    • k_out == k - n

No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or ghost code are required.  The body can simply compute and return k - n, and Verus will verify that the implementation satisfies the specification under the stated preconditions.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: “return the maximum of a non-empty slice.”  
To raise the difficulty we can

• demand extra information (e.g. the index of the first occurrence, the count of occurrences, the second-largest value).  
• add a “least-common” constraint (e.g. the *left-most* occurrence must be chosen).  
• require extra post-conditions that force deeper reasoning (e.g. uniqueness, minimality, relationship between two returned values).  
• occasionally impose an algorithmic shape, such as a divide-and-conquer strategy.

Below are three independent, self-contained specifications that incorporate such twists while remaining clear enough that a Verus spec generator can work directly from the English description.

# Output
<<<
Design a formally verified function in Verus-Rust named `max_with_first_index` that returns both the largest element of a non-empty slice of 64-bit unsigned integers *and* the smallest index at which that element appears.

Signature  
fn max_with_first_index(x: &[u64]) -> (idx: usize, val: u64)

Inputs  
• x – an immutable slice of length ≥ 1 containing `u64` values.

Outputs  
• idx – a `usize` representing an index inside the slice.  
• val – a `u64` equal to the maximum value found in the slice.

Pre-condition (requires)  
• x.len() > 0

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. 0 ≤ idx < x.len()                                             -- returned index is in bounds  
2. val == x[idx]                                                 -- value matches the element at that index  
3. ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ val ≥ x[j]                         -- val is at least every element (i.e. a maximum)  
4. ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < idx ⇒ x[j] < val                            -- idx is the *first* position where that maximum occurs  
5. ∃ j:int. 0 ≤ j < x.len() ∧ x[j] == val                       -- val actually occurs (redundant but explicit)

Together these guarantee that `val` is the global maximum of the slice and `idx` is the least index holding that value. No auxiliary specification functions or extra assumptions are allowed.
>>>

<<<
Design a formally verified function in Verus-Rust named `second_largest_distinct` that returns the second-largest *distinct* element of a slice of 64-bit unsigned integers.

Signature  
fn second_largest_distinct(x: &[u64]) -> (y: u64)

Inputs  
• x – an immutable slice of `u64`.

Outputs  
• y – a `u64` equal to the strictly second-largest distinct value in the slice.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
• x.len() ≥ 2                                       -- at least two elements  
• ∃ i, j : int. 0 ≤ i < j < x.len() ∧ x[i] ≠ x[j]   -- there are at least two different values

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < x.len() ⇒ x[k] ≤ y ∨ x[k] > y ∨ x[k] == y  
   (explained below)  
2. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < x.len() ⇒ x[k] ≤ max_val        -- max_val is the true maximum, see helper below  
3. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < x.len() ⇒ x[k] == y ⇒ x[k] < max_val  
4. y < max_val                                      -- y is strictly less than the maximum  
5. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < x.len() ∧ x[k] < max_val ⇒ x[k] ≤ y  
6. ∃ k:int. 0 ≤ k < x.len() ∧ x[k] == y             -- y actually occurs in the slice

Helper (ghost) definition  
• max_val ≜ the maximum element of x (uniquely characterised by the usual ∀/∃ clauses).

Condition (1) is syntactic but needed so that Verus knows every element is either below, equal to, or above y; combined with (3–5) it forces y to be the *largest element strictly below* the true maximum.  
Any implementation that respects these clauses will satisfy the specification.
>>>

<<<
Design a formally verified function in Verus-Rust named `max_divide_and_conquer` that returns the maximum element of a non-empty slice but must do so using a divide-and-conquer strategy whose depth is logarithmic in the length of the slice.

Signature  
fn max_divide_and_conquer(x: &[u64]) -> (y: u64)

Inputs  
• x – an immutable slice of `u64`, length ≥ 1.

Outputs  
• y – the largest `u64` in the slice.

Pre-condition (requires)  
• x.len() > 0

Algorithmic obligation  
• The implementation must repeatedly split the slice into two *non-empty* sub-slices of sizes differing by at most one, recurse on each sub-slice, and combine the two maxima with a single comparison.  
• The recursion depth therefore satisfies depth ≤ ⌈log₂(x.len())⌉.  
• Iterative loops that scan the entire slice are disallowed; any loop/recursion must obey the depth bound above. (The verifier may express this through an additional decreases clause on the length and an invariant on the depth counter.)

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < x.len() ⇒ y ≥ x[j]            -- y dominates every element  
2. ∃ j:int. 0 ≤ j < x.len() ∧ y == x[j]           -- y is present in the slice  
3. Recursion depth bound holds (see algorithmic obligation). This can be expressed with an “ensures” clause relating the maximal call depth ghost variable to ⌈log₂(x.len())⌉.

These conditions guarantee that y is exactly the maximum, while the extra structural requirement makes the reasoning and proof subtly more involved.
>>>
