fn reverse(xs: &Vec<int>) -> (r: Vec<int>)
    requires
        xs.len() < 1000, // added a precondition to prevent overflow
    ensures
        r.len() == xs.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < xs.len() ==> r[i] == xs[xs.len() - 1 - i],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The function `reverse`:

1. Inputs
   • `xs` – an immutable reference to a runtime `Vec<int>` (vector of mathematical, un-bounded integers).  
   • No other inputs.

2. Output  
   • `r` – an owned `Vec<int>` that will contain the reversed contents of `xs`.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `xs.len() < 1000` — the caller must guarantee that the vector’s length is strictly below one thousand.  
     This upper bound is introduced only to keep the later, executable proof/implementation simple (e.g., to avoid arithmetic overflow of an index variable); it is not otherwise essential to the logical specification.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   • `r.len() == xs.len()` – the resulting vector must have exactly the same length as the input.  
   • `∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ⇒ r[i] == xs[xs.len() − 1 − i]` – for every valid index `i`, the element found at position `i` in `r` equals the element that originally sat at the symmetrical position from the end of `xs`.  
     Concretely, index `0` of `r` holds what was last in `xs`; index `1` holds what was second-to-last; …; the final index of `r` holds what was first in `xs`.

5. Auxiliary spec functions / lemmas  
   • None are referenced; the two post-conditions fully characterise the intended behaviour.

6. What a solver needs to do  
   • Produce a body that constructs a fresh vector containing all elements of `xs` in reverse order while preserving the above contracts. Any implementation strategy—single pass appending, in-place swap on a clone, use of an iterator, etc.—is acceptable as long as it meets the pre- and post-conditions and terminates.  
   • Because the specification reasons over `int`, there are no overflow concerns inside the logic. In the executable part, the caller-provided length bound `< 1000` suffices to keep any run-time integer indices within machine range.

Postconditions (`ensures`)  
    1. r.len() == xs.len()  
       The resulting vector has exactly the same length as the input.  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < xs.len() ⇒ r[i] == xs[xs.len() − 1 − i]

No additional helper spec functions or lemmas are necessary; these two post-conditions fully capture the required behaviour. Any verified implementation that satisfies the contracts is acceptable.

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: show that `result[i] == a[i] / b[i]` under some basic length and non‐zero constraints.  
To raise the difficulty a bit, I will add extra *global* algebraic properties that require a second layer of reasoning over the whole sequence, or insert a less common requirement such as coprimality.  None of the new tasks changes the basic data types (`Seq<int>`) or the “all-ghost” nature of the code, but each one forces the learner to introduce at least one auxiliary lemma (for products, prefix sums, or gcd) or an additional quantified proof step.

I supply three harder, but still self-contained, variants:

1.  Preserve the product: besides the point-wise quotient property, the product of the quotients must equal the quotient of the products.
2.  Produce *coprime* quotients: each quotient must be coprime with its corresponding divisor; this introduces a gcd-based reasoning obligation.
3.  Enforce a prefix-sum dominance invariant: every prefix sum of the quotient sequence must not exceed the corresponding prefix sum of the dividend sequence.

Each description is detailed enough for a spec generator to write an adequate Verus specification without reference to anything outside the text.

# Output
<<<
Implement a ghost (proof) function in Verus called element_wise_divide_with_product that satisfies the following contract.

Inputs  
 • a: Seq<int> — a mathematical (ghost) sequence of integers.  
 • b: Seq<int> — another sequence of integers.

Output  
 • result: Seq<int> — the sequence obtained by dividing a element-wise by b.

Preconditions (requires)  
 1. Both sequences are non-empty: a != Seq::<int>::empty() ∧ b != Seq::<int>::empty().  
 2. They have identical length: a.len() == b.len().  
 3. No element of b is zero: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < b.len() ⇒ b[i] ≠ 0.  
 4. Every element of a is exactly divisible by the corresponding element of b (no remainder): ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] % b[i] == 0.

Postconditions (ensures)  
 1. result is non-empty: result != Seq::<int>::empty().  
 2. result.len() == a.len().  
 3. Point-wise division property: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < result.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[i] / b[i].  
 4. PRODUCT preservation: let prod(s) = ∏_{j=0}^{s.len()-1} s[j].  Then  
    prod(result) == prod(a) / prod(b).  (You may assume prod(Seq::<int>::empty()) == 1.)

No additional hidden constraints or decreases clauses are required; the function body may be entirely ghost.  The verifier must be satisfied that the four ensures clauses follow from the requires clauses.
>>>

<<<
Implement a ghost (proof) function in Verus named element_wise_divide_coprime with the following specification.

Inputs  
 • a: Seq<int> — dividend sequence, all integers.  
 • b: Seq<int> — divisor sequence, all integers.

Output  
 • q: Seq<int> — the element-wise integer quotients of a divided by b.

Preconditions (requires)  
 1. a and b are non-empty and have equal length.  
 2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < b.len() ⇒ b[i] ≠ 0.  
 3. Exact divisibility: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] % b[i] == 0.  
 4. Coprimality requirement: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ gcd(a[i] / b[i], b[i]) == 1.  
    (Here gcd(x,y) denotes the greatest common positive divisor of x and y.)

Postconditions (ensures)  
 1. q.len() == a.len() ∧ q != Seq::<int>::empty().  
 2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < q.len() ⇒ q[i] == a[i] / b[i].  
 3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < q.len() ⇒ gcd(q[i], b[i]) == 1.   (The quotients remain coprime to their divisors.)

No extra helper specs are provided; prove all three ensures clauses from the requires clauses.  The body may be entirely ghost code.
>>>

<<<
Create a ghost (proof) function in Verus called element_wise_divide_with_prefix_bound having the contract below.

Inputs

Output

Preconditions (requires)  
 1. a != empty ∧ b != empty ∧ a.len() == b.len().

Postconditions (ensures)  
 1. r.len() == a.len() ∧ r != empty.  
 2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < r.len() ⇒ r[i] == a[i] / b[i].  
 3. Prefix-sum dominance:  
    ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k ≤ r.len() ⇒  
        (∑_{j=0}^{k-1} r[j]) ≤ (∑_{j=0}^{k-1} a[j]).  
    (The sum over any prefix of the quotient sequence never exceeds the corresponding prefix sum of the dividend sequence.)

You may assume that the empty sum is 0.  The implementation can be fully ghost; prove that all ensures clauses hold from the requires clauses.

fn append_array_to_seq(s: Vec<i32>, a: &Vec<i32>) -> (r: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        r.len() == s.len() + a.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> r[i] == s[i],
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> r[s.len() + i] == a[i],
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification describes an executable function named `append_array_to_seq`.

1. Inputs  
   • `s: Vec<i32>` — an owned, heap-allocated vector of 32-bit signed integers that will be consumed by the function.  
   • `a: &Vec<i32>` — an immutable (shared) reference to another vector of 32-bit signed integers.

2. Output  
   • `r: Vec<i32>` — a newly created vector that will be returned to the caller.

3. Pre-condition (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` — the second vector must contain at least one element. There is no restriction on the length of `s`; it may be empty.

4. Post-conditions (`ensures`)  
   • `r.len() == s.len() + a.len()`  
     The resulting vector’s length is exactly the sum of the lengths of the two input vectors.  
   • `∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len()  ⇒  r[i] == s[i]`  
     Every element that was originally in `s` appears unchanged at the beginning of `r`, in the same order.  
   • `∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len()  ⇒  r[s.len() + i] == a[i]`  
     After the `s` segment, all elements of `a` are copied into `r`, again preserving their original order.

Together, these clauses say that `r` is the exact concatenation of `s` followed by `a`.  No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are referenced; the post-conditions are expressed directly with quantifiers over the indices.

Subtleties:  
• Because `s` is passed by value, its ownership is moved into the function and may be reused or dropped internally.  
• `a` is only borrowed, so the body must not mutate it.  
• The non-emptiness requirement on `a` is purely to satisfy the `requires` clause; it does not affect the logical definition of concatenation.

Any implementation that, under the single pre-condition, produces a vector equal to `s` ∥ `a` (concatenation) will satisfy the contract.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement and formally verify a Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function name  
    append_array_to_seq

Signature  
    fn append_array_to_seq(s: Vec<i32>, a: &Vec<i32>) -> (r: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
    • s — an owned `Vec<i32>`  
    • a — an immutable reference to another `Vec<i32>`

Output  
    • r — a freshly returned `Vec<i32>`

Pre-condition  
    1. a.len() > 0   // the second vector must not be empty

Post-conditions  
    1. r.len() == s.len() + a.len()  
       (the result’s length equals the sum of the two input lengths)  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ r[i] == s[i]  
       (the first `s.len()` elements of `r` are exactly the elements of `s` in the same order)  
    3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ r[s.len() + i] == a[i]  
       (the elements of `a` are appended directly after the `s` segment, preserving order)

Informally: `append_array_to_seq` must return the vector obtained by concatenating `s` followed by `a`.

All new problems still rely on elementary iteration over the slice, yet they demand more logical obligations (quantifiers over three variables instead of two, equality between several results, cardinality constraints, etc.).

    min_last_occurrence

Inputs  
    • a: &[i32] — immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.

Outputs  
    • val: i32 — the minimal value returned.  
    • idx: usize — an index into the slice.

Precondition (requires)  
    • a.len() > 0

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. 0 ≤ idx < a.len()                                   // idx is a valid position  
    2. val == a[idx]                                        // returned value equals element at idx  
    3. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ val ≤ a[i]                // val is minimal w.r.t. all elements  
    4. ∀ j:int. (0 ≤ j < a.len() ∧ a[j] == val) ⇒ j ≤ idx  // idx is the greatest index whose element is minimal

In other words, the function must pick the smallest element of the slice, but if that element occurs several times it has to return the *last* occurrence (the highest valid index) together with its value.
>>>

<<<
Function name  
    min_and_count

Inputs  
    • a: &[i32]

Outputs  
    • val: i32      — the minimal element  
    • cnt: usize    — how many times that element appears

Precondition (requires)  
    • a.len() > 0

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. cnt > 0                                                   // at least one occurrence  
    2. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ val ≤ a[i]                     // val is minimal  
    3. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ (a[i] == val) ⇔ (∃ k:int. k < cnt ∧ i == k_occ[k])  
       where k_occ is the set of indices counted by cnt          // cnt counts *exactly* the occurrences  
    4. cnt == |{ i:int | 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] == val }|        // cnt equals the cardinality of that set

Put simply, the function must return the smallest value *and* the precise number of times it occurs inside the slice.

Inputs

Output

Preconditions (requires)

       // every value in the slice is distinct.  In particular the minimum is unique.

Postconditions (ensures)

The original exercise is essentially a *structural copy* that can be discharged with a single loop or even the standard‐library `append`.  
To raise the level we can

1.   Add semantic constraints on the relationship between the two inputs and on the resulting vector (e.g. sortedness, uniqueness, alternation).
2.   Require proofs that involve *set* or *multiset* reasoning instead of mere positional equalities.
3.   Introduce time–complexity obligations or stability properties that demand an additional argument.
4.   Keep the problem self–contained so that a spec generator can work from the text alone.

Below are three harder, yet still self-contained, standalone problem statements formatted as requested.

Implement a verified function named `concat_no_overlap` in Verus-Rust that concatenates two vectors of mathematical integers **while eliminating values that appear in both inputs**.

Signature  
    fn concat_no_overlap(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
    • a: Vec<int> – ownership moved.  
    • b: Vec<int> – ownership moved.

Return value  
    • c: Vec<int> – a freshly allocated vector.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() ≤ 50 000  
    2. b.len() ≤ 50 000  
    3. Within each vector, all elements are pair-wise distinct  
       (∀ i < j < a.len(): a[i] ≠ a[j]  and  analogously for b).  

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. c contains every element of `a` **except those that also occur in `b`**.  
    2. c then contains every element of `b` **except those that also occur in `a`**.  
       Formally:  
       • ∀ k:nat  with k < a.len() and a[k] ∉ b  ⇒  ∃! i < c.len(): c[i] == a[k] ∧ i < j for every j s.t. c[j]==b[l] for any l  
       • ∀ k:nat  with k < b.len() and b[k] ∉ a  ⇒  ∃! i < c.len(): c[i] == b[k]  
    3. Relative order is preserved: for any i<j both from `a` that are kept, their indices in `c` respect i<j; same for surviving elements from `b`.  
    4. No element occurs twice in `c`.  
    5. c.len() ≤ a.len() + b.len().  

Meaning: `c` is the *stable concatenation* of `a` followed by `b` after removing all duplicate values that would appear in both sequences.

An implementation may, for instance, insert all elements of `b` into a temporary hash-set, scan `a`, skip matches, extend with non-matching `b`, and finally prove the five stated properties.

Inputs

Return value

    2. b.len() ≤ 100 000

    3. For any value v appearing in both inputs, it occurs exactly once in `c`, and its relative order with respect to neighbouring distinct values is preserved (stability).  
    4. c.len() ≤ a.len() + b.len().  
    5. The function executes in O(a.len() + b.len()) *logical* steps (a single linear pass is sufficient).

A classical “merge” proof must argue about three cases when advancing cursors and must show that all invariants imply the strict-order postcondition.

    fn zip_concat(a: Vec<int>, b: Vec<int>) -> (c: Vec<int>)

    1. a.len() + b.len() < 80 000.

       For every k such that 2·k+1 < c.len():  
         • If k < b.len()  ⇒  c[2·k+1] == b[k].  
         • Otherwise       ⇒  c[2·k+1] == a[k - b.len()].  
       (This formalises: alternate elements from `a` and `b`; when the shorter vector is exhausted, append the remainder of the longer one in place, maintaining order.)  
    3. No element is lost or duplicated: the multiset of `c` equals the multiset sum of `a` and `b`.  

The prover will need a loop invariant tracking the parity of the next insertion point as well as counts of consumed elements from each input.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task only asks the learner to show the *element-wise* comparison `a[i] > b[i]` for two equal-length slices.  
To make variants a bit harder we can

• keep the general flavour “compare two integer sequences”, yet  
• require an *extra numeric margin*, or a *lexicographic* instead of point-wise order, or a *prefix–sum* dominance (majorisation).  

Each variant obliges the implementer to

1. add one or more parameters,
2. express more involved predicates (existential witnesses, prefix sums, first-difference index, …),
3. reason about at least one additional quantifier or disjunction.

Below are three completely stand-alone, detailed natural-language problem statements.  
Each block is wrapped in `<<< … >>>` so it can be parsed independently.

# Output
<<<
You must write a Verus specification for two items.

(1) Helper *specification* function  
    Name: `dominates_with_margin`  
    Signature: `spec fn dominates_with_margin(a: Seq<int>, b: Seq<int>, m: int) -> bool`  

    Semantics:  
    ‑ The integer `m` is a non-negative “margin”.  
    ‑ The predicate is true exactly when *both* of the following hold:  
        • `a.len() == b.len()` (the two sequences have identical length);  
        • for every index `i` with `0 ≤ i < a.len()` we have `a[i] ≥ b[i] + m`.  
      In other words, every element of `a` exceeds the corresponding element of `b` by **at least** the margin `m`.

(2) Executable/verified function  
    Rust name: `dominates_with_margin_func`  
    Signature:  
        `fn dominates_with_margin_func(a: &[int], b: &[int], margin: int) -> (result: bool)`  

    Preconditions (`requires`):  
        • `a.len() == b.len()`;  
        • `margin >= 0`.  

    Postconditions (`ensures`):  
        1. `result` is logically equivalent to  
           `dominates_with_margin(a@, b@, margin)`;  
        2. if `result` is *false* then there must exist an index `j` with  
           `0 ≤ j < a.len()` such that `a@.index(j) < b@.index(j) + margin`.  
           (You do *not* have to return that index, only prove it exists.)  

Apart from these items nothing else is required.  Provide no implementation body; only the full Verus specification (function signatures, `requires`, `ensures`, and the helper predicate).
>>>

<<<
Design a Verus specification that compares two integer sequences in the **lexicographic order with tie-breaking by length**.

(1) Helper predicate  
    Identifier: `lex_greater_or_equal`  
    Type: `spec fn lex_greater_or_equal(a: Seq<int>, b: Seq<int>) -> bool`  

    Meaning: The predicate is true exactly when sequence `a` is *not smaller* than sequence `b` in the following sense:  
        • Let `k = min(a.len(), b.len())`.  
        • Scan the first `k` positions.  
          – If a position `p < k` exists where `a[p] ≠ b[p]`, then  
            `a[p] > b[p]` must hold and no earlier index differs.  
          – Otherwise all first `k` elements are identical; in that case the *longer* sequence is considered larger.  
        • The predicate is therefore equivalent to the usual lexicographic ≥ relation on integral sequences with “longer wins if prefix equal”.

(2) Verified function  
    Rust signature:  
        `fn lex_ge_func(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (ge: bool)`  

    Preconditions: *none* (the slices may have different lengths).  

    Postconditions:  
        • `ge <==> lex_greater_or_equal(a@, b@)`;  
        • `!ge` is equivalent to “sequence `b` is lexicographically greater than `a` under the same rule”.  

No additional helper lemmas are necessary, but the spec must be explicit enough that a reader could implement and verify the function later.
>>>

<<<
Formulate a specification for deciding *prefix-sum majorisation* between two integer sequences.

1. Helper spec function  
   Header: `spec fn prefix_majorises(a: Seq<int>, b: Seq<int>) -> bool`  

   Definition: The predicate is true iff  
      • `a.len() == b.len()`  and `a.len() > 0`, and  
      • for every prefix length `k` with `1 ≤ k ≤ a.len()`  
            Σ_{i=0}^{k-1} a[i] ≥ Σ_{i=0}^{k-1} b[i],  
      • and the *total* sums are equal, i.e.  
            Σ_{i=0}^{a.len()-1} a[i] == Σ_{i=0}^{b.len()-1} b[i].  

   (You may define your own pure helper like `spec fn prefix_sum(s: Seq<int>, n: int) -> int` to express the summations.)

2. Executable function  
   Rust name and type:  
       `fn prefix_majorises_func(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (maj: bool)`  

   Preconditions (`requires`):  
       `a.len() == b.len()` and `a.len() > 0`.  

   Postconditions (`ensures`):  
       • `maj <==> prefix_majorises(a@, b@)`;  
       • `!maj` implies the *existence* of a prefix length `k` (1 ≤ k ≤ a.len()) for which the required inequality fails.

Deliver the full Verus specification (helper functions, pre- and post-conditions) but no executable body.

# Reasoning (you output)
We need to explain the specification:

1. spec fn fib(n: nat) -> nat
   - Type nat means a non-negative mathematical integer.
   - Body: n * (n + 1) / 2, i.e. the nth triangular number (not the Fibonacci sequence despite the name).

2. fn compute_fib
   Signature: takes n: u64, returns ret: u64.

   Preconditions (requires):
     a. n ≥ 0 – redundant for u64 but explicitly stated.
     b. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.
        Since u64::MAX / u64::MAX == 1, this second clause forces n to be strictly less than 1, i.e. the only legal value is 0.  
        The stated reason is “to prevent overflow”; in practice it severely restricts the domain.

   Postcondition (ensures):
     ret == fib(n as nat).  
     The argument n is cast to nat (unbounded non-negative int) before being fed to the ghost helper; the returned ret must equal that result, but as a u64.

   Implementation body is omitted; any code that satisfies the spec is acceptable.

Important subtleties:
• Although the helper is named fib, the formula produces triangular numbers.
• Because of the very tight precondition, the function is only required to behave correctly for n = 0.
• All arithmetic in fib is carried out in the int domain; no overflow can occur there. Overflow in the executable function is pre-empted by the n < 1 guard.

   fn compute_fib(n: u64) -> (ret: u64)

   • Inputs  
     – n: u64

   • Outputs  
     – ret: u64

   • Preconditions (requires)

2.  require reasoning over a global slice property (monotone ordering) together with a minimality proof, or

INDEX of the *second* appearance of a value in a run-time slice.

fn second_occurrence(a: &[int], e: int) -> (n: usize)

Meaning of each part  
• a – immutable slice of mathematical integers (int).  
• e – the value being searched for.  
• n – the index returned.

    ∃ i:int, j:int. 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ∧ a[i] == e ∧ a[j] == e

3. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < n ⇒ (a[k] == e ⇒ k == i)  
   – no index strictly between i and n contains e; hence n is the second, left-to-right occurrence.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are prescribed, but you may introduce them if useful.

# Reasoning (you output)
The spec describes an executable function called `mult`.

1. Inputs  
   • `x: u64` – first unsigned 64-bit operand  
   • `y: u64` – second unsigned 64-bit operand  

2. Output  
   • `r: u64` – the product that the function returns  

3. Preconditions (requires)  
   • `x >= 0` and `y >= 0`. These are automatically satisfied for any `u64` value but are written explicitly.  
   • `x * y < u64::MAX`.  The mathematical product of the two operands (computed in the unbounded integer domain) must be strictly smaller than 2⁶⁴ − 1, the largest representable `u64`.  This guarantees that performing the multiplication at run-time will not overflow.  

4. Postcondition (ensures)  
   • `r == x * y`.  After the function returns, the value it produced must equal the mathematical product of the two inputs. Because of the precondition, that product is representable as a `u64`.  

5. Auxiliary spec functions / lemmas  
   None are referenced or needed.

6. Subtleties / edge cases  
   • If either operand is zero, the product is zero, which satisfies the `< u64::MAX` precondition automatically.  
   • The strict `<` (not `<=`) exclusion of the maximal value means `x * y` is never exactly `u64::MAX`; this avoids having to reason about “wrap-around equals MAX” situations.  
   • Implementations may simply compute `x * y` with ordinary `u64` arithmetic; the precondition ensures the operation is safe.

# Reasoning
The original specification merely checks that every occurrence of `old_char` is replaced by `new_char`, a single-pass, element-wise property.  
To increase difficulty we can:  

1. Introduce counting / quantitative properties (e.g. return number of changes) so the prover must relate *two* results simultaneously.  
2. Restrict replacement to a sub-range, forcing “frame-condition” reasoning about unchanged elements.  
3. Add an injectivity-style property that makes the prover relate sets or multisets of characters, requiring extra lemmas or quantified reasoning.  

I will supply three harder, standalone problems:

• Problem 1 (“replace_and_count”) adds a second return value that equals the exact number of characters changed and asserts that the output contains **no** remaining `old_char`.

• Problem 2 (“replace_in_range”) limits replacement to a half-open slice `[start, end)` with pre-conditions on indices; the spec must capture both changed and untouched segments.

• Problem 3 (“replace_preserve_multiset”) forces reasoning about multisets: apart from converting `old_char` occurrences to `new_char`, the multiset of characters must stay identical; additionally the function must prove that `new_char` was *not* already present in unmodified positions—preventing accidental duplication.

Each description is completely self-contained so a spec generator can work from it alone.

<<<
Design a formally-verified Verus/Rust function with the following contract.

Function signature  
fn replace_and_count(s: &Vec<char>, old_char: char, new_char: char) -> (v: Vec<char>, replaced: usize)

Inputs  
• s – immutable reference to a vector of Rust `char` values.  
• old_char – the character that must be replaced.  
• new_char – the character with which to replace.  

Outputs  
• v – a newly created `Vec<char>`.  
• replaced – the exact number of indices where the substitution took place.

Preconditions  
• None beyond type correctness; `s.len() ≥ 0` always holds.

Postconditions  
1. Length preservation: `v.len() == s.len()`.  
2. Element-wise rule: for every index `i` in `0 .. s.len()`  
   – if `s[i] == old_char` then `v[i] == new_char`,  
   – else `v[i] == s[i]`.  
3. No residual occurrences: for every index `i`, `v[i] != old_char`.  
4. Accurate counting: `replaced` equals the number of indices `i` such that `s[i] == old_char`.  
5. If `replaced == 0` then `v` is byte-for-byte identical to `s`.

No helper lemmas are forbidden; auxiliary specifications may be added as needed.

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus/Rust function as specified below.

Function signature  
fn replace_in_range(s: &Vec<char>, start: usize, end: usize, old_char: char, new_char: char) -> (v: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
• s – immutable reference to a vector of `char`.  
• start – inclusive lower bound of the replacement region.  
• end – exclusive upper bound of the replacement region.  
• old_char – character to be replaced **inside** the region `[start, end)`.  
• new_char – replacement character.

Outputs  
• v – a fresh vector returned from the function.

Preconditions  
1. 0 ≤ start ≤ end ≤ s.len().  
2. `start < end` is *not* required; an empty interval is allowed.

Postconditions  
1. Length preservation: `v.len() == s.len()`.  
2. Inside range property: for every index `i` with `start ≤ i < end`,  
   – if `s[i] == old_char` then `v[i] == new_char`,  
   – otherwise `v[i] == s[i]`.  
3. Outside range frame condition: for every index `i` with `i < start` or `i ≥ end`, we must have `v[i] == s[i]`.  
4. If `start == end` then `v` is identical to `s`.  
5. The function performs no in-place mutation; `v` must be allocated anew.

You may define helper spec functions or lemmas to express slice properties.

<<<
Define a formally-verified Verus/Rust function satisfying the following contract.

Function signature  
fn replace_preserve_multiset(s: &Vec<char>, old_char: char, new_char: char) -> (v: Vec<char>)

Inputs  
• s – immutable reference to a vector of `char`.  
• old_char – character to be replaced.  
• new_char – replacement character (*must differ from `old_char` but may equal some other element*).

Outputs

Preconditions  
1. `old_char != new_char`.  
2. The character `new_char` does not appear in `s` at any index where `s[i] != old_char`.

Postconditions  
1. Length preservation: `v.len() == s.len()`.  
2. Element transformation: for every index `i`,  
   – if `s[i] == old_char` then `v[i] == new_char`,  
   – else `v[i] == s[i]`.  
3. Multiset conservation: For every character `c` other than `old_char` and `new_char`,  
   `count(c, s) == count(c, v)`.  
4. Total-count relationship:  
   `count(new_char, v) == count(old_char, s)` and `count(old_char, v) == 0`.  
5. The function allocates a fresh vector; `v` must not alias `s`.

`count(c, xs)` is the number of indices `j` such that `xs[j] == c`.  
You may introduce helper spec functions or lemmas to reason about counts.

# Reasoning
The original task—copy a slice into a fresh vector and push one extra element—can be discharged with a single call to `Vec::from` followed by `push`, plus three very direct proof obligations.  
To make things more interesting we can:

1. Preserve and establish global order properties (strict sortedness) while inserting, which forces the student to reason about *where* the new element goes and to prove order preservation as well as element preservation.
2. Require an alternation (“interleaving”) of two distinct input slices, which introduces two‐variable indexing arithmetic (`2*i`, `2*i+1`) and length reasoning that is a little less common than a straight append.
3. Ask for a prefix‐sum scan with non-negativity and overflow constraints, which requires inductive reasoning over the slice, arithmetic on sums, and a monotonicity proof.

All three variants still keep memory-safety trivial (no aliasing or mutation of the inputs) but significantly deepen the logical part of the verification.

# Output
<<<
Create a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following contract.

Signature  
fn insert_sorted(a: &[int], b: int) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a — an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
• b — a single mathematical integer to be inserted, preserving order.

Output  
• c — a heap-allocated vector (`Vec<int>`) that contains every element of `a` together with the new element `b`, in strictly increasing order.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() + 1 < i32::MAX   
   – The resulting vector length must still fit into the runtime’s 32-bit length field.  
2. ∀ i:int, 0 ≤ i < a.len() − 1 ⇒ a[i] < a[i + 1]  
   – The input slice is already strictly increasing.  
3. (a.is_empty()) ∨ (a[0] ≤ b ≤ a[a.len() − 1] + 1)  
   – The value `b` does not violate the representable range hinted by the slice. (This avoids accidental overflow in extreme corner cases.)

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. c.len() == a.len() + 1  
   – Exactly one new element has been added.  
2. ∀ x:int • (x ≠ b ⇒ (x ∈ a) ⇔ (x ∈ c))  
   – Every element distinct from `b` appears in `c` iff it already appeared in `a`.  
3. #(i:int • 0 ≤ i < c.len() ∧ c[i] == b) == 1  
   – The element `b` occurs exactly once in the result.  
4. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < c.len() − 1 ⇒ c[i] < c[i + 1]  
   – The output vector is strictly increasing.  
5. ∀ i, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ index_of(c, a[i]) < index_of(c, a[j])  
   – The relative order of the original elements is preserved.

No auxiliary helper lemmas are provided; all necessary proofs must be inside the function body or local ghost blocks.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following contract.

Signature  
fn interleave(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a — first immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
• b — second immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).

Output  
• c — a heap-allocated vector (`Vec<int>`) whose elements alternate between `a` and `b`.  
  The interleaving is defined as follows:  
 – If i < min(a.len(), b.len()), then  
  • c[2*i]     == a[i]  
  • c[2*i + 1] == b[i]  
 – After the shorter slice runs out, the remaining elements of the longer slice are appended in their original order.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() + b.len() < i32::MAX  
   – Combined length fits the vector length field.  
2. max(a.len(), b.len()) − min(a.len(), b.len()) ≤ 1  
   – The two slices differ in length by at most one element.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. c.len() == a.len() + b.len()  
2. For every i < min(a.len(), b.len()):  
 • c[2*i]     == a[i]  
 • c[2*i + 1] == b[i]  
3. If a.len() > b.len() then ∀ j in [b.len(), a.len()) • c[b.len()*2 + (j − b.len())] == a[j]  
   Symmetrically, if b is longer the tail of `b` appears after the last paired element.  
4. ∀ e:int • (e ∈ a) ∨ (e ∈ b) ⇔ (e ∈ c)  
   – No element is dropped or duplicated beyond the specification above.

No additional helper lemmas may be assumed.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following contract.

Signature  
fn prefix_sums(a: &[int]) -> (c: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
• a — an immutable slice of *non-negative* mathematical integers (`int`).

Output  
• c — a heap-allocated vector (`Vec<int>`) where each position holds the sum of the prefix up to and including the corresponding element of `a`.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≥ 0  
   – All input numbers are non-negative.  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒  
     ∑_{j=0}^{i} a[j] < i64::MAX  
   – Every partial sum fits in a signed 64-bit integer.  
3. a.len() < i32::MAX  
   – Resulting vector length fits the runtime bound.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. c.len() == a.len()  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒  
     c[i] == ∑_{j=0}^{i} a[j]  
   – Each entry is exactly the prefix sum up to `i`.  
3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() − 1 ⇒ c[i] ≤ c[i + 1]  
   – The resulting vector is monotonically non-decreasing.  
4. c.last() == ∑_{j=0}^{a.len() − 1} a[j]  
   – The final element equals the total sum of the slice.

No helper spec functions beyond standard arithmetic on `int` should be relied upon.
>>>

easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original task is almost trivial: prove that a two-argument `min` returns one of its inputs and is no larger than either input.  
To make the exercise richer while still approachable for a learner familiar with Verus, I considered several orthogonal axes of difficulty:

• More inputs or more structure (slice/sequence instead of two scalars) adds induction or quantifier reasoning.  
• Requiring a tie-breaking rule (e.g., “leftmost minimum”) forces the student to reason about indices in addition to values.  
• Asking for *multiple* post-conditions (e.g., both the minimum and its index or multiplicity) strengthens the proof obligations.  
• Using a less common requirement such as “strictly monotonic reading of the slice outside the minimal position” or guaranteeing stability under appending transforms the typical “min” proof into a more exotic one.  
• Still, we avoid needless complexity (overflow, time limits) and keep everything within Verus’ integer arithmetic and sequence libraries.

I ended up with three stand-alone, incrementally harder problems:

1. Compute the *median* of exactly three integers.  The learner must argue about ordering relations between three, not just two, numbers.

2. Compute both the minimum value of a non-empty slice **and** how many times it appears.  This introduces quantifiers over the slice and a second returned result that must satisfy a counting property.

3. Compute the minimum value and its **leftmost** index in a slice.  Besides quantifiers, the proof must show that no earlier index holds the minimum; this is a less common tie-breaking rule compared with “any index”.

Each description is detailed enough that a spec generator can write Verus code from it alone.

<<<
Title: Median of Three Integers

You must write and verify a Verus-Rust program that provides

1. Pure specification helper

   spec fn median_of_three(a:int, b:int, c:int) -> int

   • Purpose: return the *median* (middle) value among the three given integers.  
   • Inputs: a, b, c : int (mathematical, unbounded).  
   • Preconditions: none.  
   • Definition (mathematical):
        – Let S = {a, b, c}.  
        – The median m is the unique element of S such that:
              (∀x∈S :: m ≤ x) ∨ (∀x∈S :: m ≥ x)  is false,
          but for exactly one other element x≠m we have
              (m ≤ x ∧ (∀y∈S :: y ≤ x ⇒ y = m ∨ y = x)).
        Equivalently, after sorting (a,b,c) in non-decreasing order (x₀≤x₁≤x₂) the median is x₁.

2. Executable verified function

   fn median3(a:int, b:int, c:int) -> (m:int)

   • Inputs: a, b, c : int  
   • Output: m : int  
   • Preconditions: none.  
   • Postconditions  
       1. m = a ∨ m = b ∨ m = c                (result is one of the inputs)  
       2. (a ≤ m ≤ b) ∨ (b ≤ m ≤ a) ∨          // m is between the other two  
          (a ≤ m ≤ c) ∨ (c ≤ m ≤ a) ∨  
          (b ≤ m ≤ c) ∨ (c ≤ m ≤ b)  
       3. ∃p,q:int. {p,q distinct elements of {a,b,c}} m is greater-or-equal to min(p,q) and less-or-equal to max(p,q).

   The implementation may branch arbitrarily but must satisfy the proof obligations without additional assumptions.  Overflow is impossible; integers are mathematical.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

// Specification function for max
spec fn max(x: int, y: int) -> int {
    if x >= y {
        x
    } else {
        y
    }
}

// Function to find max
fn max_func(x: int, y: int) -> (r: int)
    requires
        x >= 0,
        y >= 0,
    ensures
        r >= x && r >= y,
        r == x || r == y,
{
```

# Reasoning
Functionality overview:
• The file declares a pure, ghost‐only specification helper `spec fn max(x:int, y:int) -> int` that captures the mathematical maximum of two integers using a simple conditional (`if x ≥ y { x } else { y }`).  
• The executable function `max_func` must return the maximum of its two integer arguments subject to explicit contracts.

Inputs:
  – `x: int`, `y: int` (Verus mathematical integers, i.e. arbitrary precision).  

Preconditions (`requires`):
  – Both inputs are required to be non-negative (`x ≥ 0`, `y ≥ 0`).  No other assumptions are needed.

Output:
  – `r: int` is the single return value.

Postconditions (`ensures`):
  1. `r ≥ x ∧ r ≥ y` → the result is at least as large as each argument.  
  2. `r == x ∨ r == y` → the result must equal one of the two original arguments.  
Together these two clauses force `r` to be exactly the maximum of `x` and `y`; if `x = y`, both clauses jointly force `r` to equal that common value.

Auxiliary specification element:
  – Although not required, callers/implementers may invoke the previously defined ghost helper `max(x,y)` in proofs; the helper itself is not executed at runtime.

Implementation freedom:
  – Any body that, under the non-negativity precondition, computes either `x` or `y` such that it is the larger one (or the shared value when equal) satisfies the spec.  Because the type is Verus `int`, overflow cannot occur, so no additional arithmetic checks are necessary.

Essential subtleties to reproduce the spec:
  • Function names and exact parameter order: `max_func(x:int, y:int) → (r:int)`.  
  • Two separate `ensures` lines, the first a conjunction, the second a disjunction.  
  • The preconditions expressly restrict both arguments to `≥ 0`; without them the spec would be different.  
  • The helper `spec fn max` is placed immediately above the executable function but is not referenced inside the `ensures` clauses; it is present purely for potential use in proofs.

# Final Description (put your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Design two items in Verus Rust:

1. A ghost-only specification helper
   spec fn max(x: int, y: int) -> int
   Definition:
       if x >= y { x } else { y }
   Purpose: provide the mathematical maximum of two arbitrary-precision integers for use in proofs; it is never executed at runtime.

2. An executable function
   fn max_func(x: int, y: int) -> (r: int)

   Inputs
     • x: int
     • y: int
   Output
     • r: int – must be the larger of the two inputs (or the common value if they are equal).

   Preconditions (requires)
     1. x ≥ 0
     2. y ≥ 0

   Postconditions (ensures)
     1. r ≥ x ∧ r ≥ y
     2. r == x ∨ r == y

   These two guarantees together force r to equal either x or y and to be at least as big as both, i.e. r is exactly max(x,y).  No other conditions or helper lemmas are necessary.  Any verified body that returns whichever argument is not smaller than the other satisfies the specification.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise only asked the learner to specify and implement a very small “take-the-maximum” routine.  
To make the assignment a bit more demanding, three new standalone problems are proposed:

Problem 1 – “median_of_three”  
• More inputs (three instead of two)  
• The notion of “median” is slightly subtler than “maximum”, forcing an extra logical branch and a more delicate post-condition (“at least two values are ≤ r and at least two are ≥ r”).  
• Extra helper lemma requested (sum of inputs equals min+median+max) introduces another reasoning step.  
• One short block of intentionally wrong Rust-like code is supplied as misdirection.

Problem 2 – “clamp”  
• Adds a less common requirement: strict monotonicity with respect to its ‘x’ argument while the bounds stay fixed.  
• Post-conditions express two different properties (bounding + idempotence) and the monotonicity property must be expressed with a quantified clause.  
• Learner must also supply a ghost lemma describing reflexivity of the helper spec.  
• This forces thinking about relational properties between several (lower,upper,x) triples instead of a single call.

Problem 3 – “max_plus_min_equals_sum”  
• Still a maximum-style routine, but now it must return the *pair* (min,max).  
• Post-conditions link the two results to each other and to the original inputs; ensuring r_max + r_min = x + y is an uncommon but easy-to-overlook requirement.  
• Adds a bounded-range pre-condition (both operands must fit in a signed 32-bit range) and an explicit total-ordering requirement (inputs may be negative).  
• A faulty reference implementation (that forgets an ‘else’) is provided to raise the cognitive load.

All three problems remain reasonable for an intermediate Verus learner but require more lines of spec and a couple of additional proof obligations.

Design a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `remove_element`.

Signature  
    fn remove_element(s: &[int], k: usize) -> (v: Vec<int>)

Inputs  
  • `s` – an immutable slice containing elements of type `int` (unbounded mathematical integers).  
  • `k` – a `usize` index of the element that must be deleted.

Output  
  • `v` – a freshly allocated `Vec<int>` holding the result of the deletion.

Contract

Precondition  
    k < s.len()  
    (the index to delete is guaranteed to lie inside the slice).

Postconditions  
  1. Length:  v.len() == s.len() − 1  
     – the new vector is exactly one element shorter.  
  2. Prefix preservation:  ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < k ⇒ v[i] == s[i]  
     – every element situated before the removed index is copied unchanged.  
  3. Suffix shift:  ∀ i:int • k ≤ i < v.len() ⇒ v[i] == s[i + 1]  
     – all elements that originally followed the deleted one keep their relative order but are shifted one position left.

Informally: `v` is identical to `s` except that the element at position `k` has been excised.

No other assumptions are made about the values in the slice, and no auxiliary spec functions are required. An implementation can build `v` in any verified way (e.g., looping over the slice, splitting it into two ranges, etc.) as long as the three postconditions above are proven.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

// Function to generate a stream of natural numbers starting from n
fn up(n: u64) -> (s: Vec<u64>)
    requires
        n >= 0,
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n * n < u64::MAX, // added check to prevent overflow
    ensures
        s.len() == 2,
        s[0] == n,
        s[1] == n + 1,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The specification is extremely small, but let us break it down thoroughly so every single subtlety is understood.

1.  Function name  
    • up  

2.  Inputs  
    • n: u64.  
      – u64 is an unsigned 64-bit integer whose value domain is [0, 2⁶⁴ − 1].

3.  Output  
    • s: Vec<u64> (an owned, growable vector allocated on the heap).  

4.  Preconditions (requires)  
    a) n ≥ 0  
       – Redundant for u64, because the type system already guarantees non-negativity, but it is still part of the contract and must be copied verbatim.  
    b) n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX  
       – Since u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, this constraint means n must be strictly less than 1, i.e. n == 0. The author writes that it is a “relaxation to prevent overflow”; nevertheless, the mathematical consequence is that only the single value 0 satisfies the clause.  
    c) n * n < u64::MAX  
       – Guards against a possible overflow if n were to be squared in the body, even though that does not happen in the post-condition we see. With the previous clause restricting n to 0, this third clause is automatically satisfied.

    In practice the above three lines mean: “this function may only be called with n == 0”, but we must still list the clauses as they appear.

5.  Postconditions (ensures)  
    a) s.len() == 2  
       – the returned vector has exactly two elements.  
    b) s[0] == n  
       – the first element equals the input n.  
    c) s[1] == n + 1  
       – the second element equals n incremented by one (still in u64).

    Because of these three lines, at runtime the vector is forced to be [n, n+1].

6.  No helper spec functions, no ghost arguments, no decreases clause (the function is not recursive).

7.  Implementation freedom  
    Any executable body that, under the stated preconditions, returns a vector [n, n+1] will be accepted by the verifier. A typical implementation would allocate a Vec<u64>, push n, push n+1, and return it.

8.  Overflow considerations  
    • Creating n+1 is safe only when n < u64::MAX. The spec does not explicitly require that, but the earlier requirement n < 1 makes it trivially true.  
    • Indexing s[0] and s[1] is safe because len() == 2 is ensured.

This is everything one needs to reproduce the specification exactly.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Design and specify a formally verified Verus/Rust function called up that produces a two-element vector containing a natural number and its immediate successor.

Signature  
    fn up(n: u64) -> (s: Vec<u64>)

Inputs  
    • n: u64 — an unsigned 64-bit integer (non-negative by construction).

Output  
    • s: Vec<u64> — a heap-allocated vector of unsigned 64-bit integers.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1.  n >= 0            // redundant for u64 but must be stated.  
    2.  n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX  
        – Because u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, this effectively constrains callers to pass only n == 0.  
    3.  n * n < u64::MAX  // guarantees that squaring n would not overflow; automatically true given (2).

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1.  s.len() == 2      // the vector has exactly two elements.  
    2.  s[0] == n         // first element reproduces the input n.  
    3.  s[1] == n + 1     // second element is n plus one (no overflow possible under the pre-conditions).

There are no auxiliary spec functions, no ghost parameters, and no recursion required. Any runtime implementation that, under the stated pre-conditions, returns the vector [n, n+1] and satisfies all ensures clauses will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

5.  Postconditions (ensures)  
    a) s.len() == 2  
       – the returned vector has exactly two elements.  
    b) s[0] == n  
       – the first element equals the input n.  
    c) s[1] == n + 1  
       – the second element equals n incremented by one (still in u64).

    Because of these three lines, at runtime the vector is forced to be [n, n+1].

6.  No helper spec functions, no ghost arguments, no decreases clause (the function is not recursive).

7.  Implementation freedom  
    Any executable body that, under the stated preconditions, returns a vector [n, n+1] will be accepted by the verifier. A typical implementation would allocate a Vec<u64>, push n, push n+1, and return it.

8.  Overflow considerations  
    • Creating n+1 is safe only when n < u64::MAX. The spec does not explicitly require that, but the earlier requirement n < 1 makes it trivially true.  
    • Indexing s[0] and s[1] is safe because len() == 2 is ensured.

This is everything one needs to reproduce the specification exactly.

# Final Description
<<<
Design and specify a formally verified Verus/Rust function called up that produces a two-element vector containing a natural number and its immediate successor.

Signature  
    fn up(n: u64) -> (s: Vec<u64>)

Inputs  
    • n: u64 — an unsigned 64-bit integer (non-negative by construction).

Output  
    • s: Vec<u64> — a heap-allocated vector of unsigned 64-bit integers.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1.  n >= 0            // redundant for u64 but must be stated.  
    2.  n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX  
        – Because u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, this effectively constrains callers to pass only n == 0.  
    3.  n * n < u64::MAX  // guarantees that squaring n would not overflow; automatically true given (2).

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1.  s.len() == 2      // the vector has exactly two elements.  
    2.  s[0] == n         // first element reproduces the input n.  
    3.  s[1] == n + 1     // second element is n plus one (no overflow possible under the pre-conditions).

There are no auxiliary spec functions, no ghost parameters, and no recursion required. Any runtime implementation that, under the stated pre-conditions, returns the vector [n, n+1] and satisfies all ensures clauses will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task (“remove the element situated at a given index”) is quite short to prove:  
length bookkeeping + two index–by–index equalities.  
To make it more interesting we can:

• delete a *segment* instead of a single element – forces two indices in the
  pre-condition and a third returned value (the length of the deleted block or the
  sum of the deleted elements).  
• delete by *value* (first occurrence) – introduces a search phase and an
  existential postcondition (“there exists an index k …”).  
• add an *aggregate* constraint such as “the multiset of the remaining elements
  plus the removed one(s) equals the original multiset” or “the arithmetic sum of
  the new vector equals the old sum minus the removed element(s)”.  
• return additional information (Option, tuple), so that the proof must relate
  the two return components.  
Below are three independent, self-contained rewritten problem statements that
incorporate those ideas.

# Output
<<<
Design a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `remove_segment_and_sum`.

Signature  
    fn remove_segment_and_sum(s: &[int], start: usize, end: usize)
        -> (v: Vec<int>, removed_sum: int)

Inputs  
  • `s` – an immutable slice of arbitrary mathematical integers.  
  • `start`, `end` – indices specifying the half-open segment `[start, end)` that
    must be excised.

Outputs  
  • `v` – a freshly allocated vector containing the surviving elements, in their
    original order.  
  • `removed_sum` – the arithmetic sum of every element that has been deleted.

Contracts

Preconditions  
  1. `start ≤ end`  
  2. `end ≤ s.len()`  
     (the segment lies completely inside the slice).

Postconditions  
  1. Length    `v.len() == s.len() − (end − start)`  
  2. Prefix keep `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < start ⇒ v[i] == s[i]`  
  3. Suffix shift `∀ i:int • start ≤ i < v.len() ⇒ v[i] == s[i + (end − start)]`  
  4. Sum relation `removed_sum + v.fold_left(0, |a,b| a + b) == 
                   s.fold_left(0, |a,b| a + b)`  
     – the total numerical sum is preserved after accounting for what was
       excised.

Informally: the function cuts out the contiguous block `[start, end)`,
stitches the two outer parts back together, and reports the exact sum of the
removed integers.
>>>

<<<
Design a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `remove_first_occurrence`.

Signature  
    fn remove_first_occurrence(s: &[int], x: int)
        -> (v_opt: Option<Vec<int>>)

Inputs  
  • `s` – immutable slice of integers.  
  • `x` – the value whose first appearance, if any, must be deleted.

Output  
  • `v_opt` –  
      – `Some(v)` if `x` occurred in `s`; `v` is `s` with the *earliest*
        occurrence of `x` removed.  
      – `None` if `x` does not appear at all (no changes performed).

Contracts

Postconditions  
  1. Presence flag  
        `v_opt.is_some()  ⇔  (∃ k:int • 0 ≤ k < s.len() ∧ s[k] == x)`  
  2. Length when present 
        `v_opt == Some(v) ⇒ v.len() == s.len() − 1`  
  3. Element preservation 
        `v_opt == Some(v) ⇒`  
          (a) `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < first_k ⇒ v[i] == s[i]`  
          (b) `∀ i:int • first_k ≤ i < v.len() ⇒ v[i] == s[i+1]`  
        where `first_k` is *the* smallest index with `s[first_k] == x`.  
  4. Multiset decrement 
        `v_opt == Some(v) ⇒`  
        `multiset(v) == multiset(s) − {|x|}`  
        (exactly one copy of `x` disappears; all other counts stay equal).

Here `multiset(t)` denotes the bag of elements of the sequence `t`, and the `−`
operator removes one occurrence of the given element.

Informally: the function searches for the first `x`; if found it deletes it,
otherwise signals failure.
>>>

<<<
Design a formally verified Verus-Rust function named `dedup_sorted`.

Signature  
    fn dedup_sorted(s: &[int]) -> (v: Vec<int>)

Input  
  • `s` – an immutable slice that is *already sorted in non-decreasing order*.

Output  
  • `v` – a new vector containing the unique elements of `s`, in order.

Contracts

Precondition  
  ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≤ s[i + 1]

Postconditions  
  1. Strictly increasing result 
        ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < v.len() ⇒ v[i] < v[i + 1]  
  2. No loss nor duplication 
        multiset(v) == multiset(s) and |v| == |set(s)|  
        (every original value appears exactly once, and nothing new appears).  
  3. Order preservation 
        ∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < v.len() ⇒ v[i] was situated before v[j]
        inside `s` (index wise).

Informally: because the input is sorted, equal values form contiguous runs;
the function keeps the *first* element of each run and discards the rest,
ending up with a strictly ascending vector.
>>>

Create a formally-verified Verus function called `find` that searches a non-empty array for the first occurrence of a value.

Signature  
fn find(a: &[int], key: int) -> (i: i32)

Inputs  
  • a – immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
  • key – the integer value to look for.

Output  
  • i – a 32-bit signed integer interpreted as follows:  
      – if i ≥ 0, it is the zero-based index of the first appearance of `key`;  
      – if i < 0, the value `key` is absent from the slice (any negative sentinel is acceptable).

Preconditions  
1. a.len() > 0 (the slice is non-empty).  
2. a.len() < i32::MAX as usize (the length fits into an i32, ensuring every legal index can be returned).

Postconditions  
• Found case (i ≥ 0):  
    – 0 ≤ i < a.len() as i32  
    – a[i] == key  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < i ⇒ a[k] ≠ key  
  Together these state that `i` is a valid position containing `key`, and no earlier position does, i.e. it is the *first* occurrence.

• Not-found case (i < 0):  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ a[k] ≠ key

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;
use vstd::map::*;

verus! {

struct Reg;

fn find_first_occurrence(x: int, s: &Vec<int>) -> (r: i32)
    requires
        s.len() > 0,
        s.len() < i32::MAX,
    ensures
        r < 0 ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < s.len() ==> s[i] != x,
        r >= 0 ==> exists|i: int| 0 <= i && i < s.len() && s[i] == x,
{
```

# Reasoning
We need to describe this specification in natural language.

Inputs:
- x: int - value to search for
- s: &Vec<int> - immutable reference to a vector of mathematical integers.

Preconditions:
- Vector length strictly positive.
- Vector length less than maximum 32-bit signed integer; guarantees any valid index fits in i32.

Output:
- r: i32.

Postconditions:
- If r is negative (<0) then x does not occur anywhere in s (universal quantifier).
- If r is non-negative (≥0) then x occurs at least once in s (existential quantifier). Note: spec does not require r to equal an index of an occurrence nor the first occurrence.

Therefore function acts like "find index, return negative if not found".

Body unspecified.

No helper functions referenced. struct Reg unused.

# Final Description
<<<
Design a formally verified Verus function with the following contract:

Function signature  
fn find_first_occurrence(x: int, s: &Vec<int>) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
• x: int – the integer value we are looking for.  
• s: &Vec<int> – an immutable reference to a run-time vector that stores mathematical integers.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. s.len() > 0 – the vector is non-empty.  
2. s.len() < i32::MAX – every valid index of the vector is representable as a 32-bit signed integer; this lets us safely return an index inside an i32.

Output  
• r: i32 – the function’s single return value.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
• r < 0  ⇒  ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≠ x  
  (A negative result certifies that x does not appear anywhere in the vector.)  

• r ≥ 0 ⇒ ∃ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ s[i] = x  
  (A non-negative result guarantees that x appears at least once in the vector.)  

No other properties are imposed: the spec does not require r to be the index of the occurrence or the first occurrence, only that its sign correctly reflects absence vs. presence.

Implementation freedom  
Any executable body satisfying these contracts is acceptable (e.g. a linear scan that returns the first matching index as an i32 or –1 when no match is found).

There are no auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or ghost state involved; struct Reg is declared but not used.

>>>

The original task (“find the first occurrence of a value”) is essentially a linear search with a simple success / failure split.  
Below are three harder, yet still self-contained, variations.  Each

 • keeps the overall flavour of “search”, so the learner’s previous knowledge transfers;  
 • adds one or more constraints that force additional proof obligations (bounds involving `len-1`, sortedness, uniqueness, 2-element windows, insertion-point reasoning, etc.);  
 • remains implementable by a straightforward linear pass but now needs more intricate invariants.

Every specification is written in enough detail that a Verus‐spec generator can work from it alone.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function called `find_adjacent_pair` that locates the first index at which two consecutive elements both equal the given `key`.

Signature  
fn find_adjacent_pair(a: &[int], key: int) -> (idx: i32)

Inputs  
  • a – immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
  • key – the integer value to look for.

Output  
  • idx – a 32-bit signed integer interpreted as follows:  
      – if idx ≥ 0, it is the zero-based index of the *first* element of the left-most pair `(a[idx], a[idx+1])` that are **both** equal to `key`;  
      – if idx < 0, no such adjacent pair exists (any negative sentinel is acceptable).

Additional Helper Definition (may be implemented as a ghost function or a `spec`):  
  • is_pair_start(i) ≔ 0 ≤ i < a.len() − 1 ∧ a[i] == key ∧ a[i+1] == key.

Preconditions  
1. a.len() ≥ 2           (the slice must be long enough to hold a pair).  
2. a.len() ≤ i32::MAX as usize  (so every legal index fits into an i32).

Postconditions  
• Success case (idx ≥ 0):  
    – 0 ≤ idx ≤ (a.len() − 2) as i32  
    – is_pair_start(idx)  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < idx ⇒ ¬is_pair_start(k)  
  In words: `idx` is a valid start position of a `key,key` pair and is the earliest such position.

• Failure case (idx < 0):  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < a.len() − 1 ⇒ ¬is_pair_start(k)  
  Meaning the slice contains no two consecutive `key` values at all.

No auxiliary lemmas beyond the helper definition are required; any implementation that satisfies all pre- and postconditions is acceptable and must verify.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function called `find_or_insert_pos` that searches a **non-empty, non-decreasingly sorted** slice for a value and, using the common binary-search API convention, returns either the index of the first match or the encoded insertion position.

Signature  
fn find_or_insert_pos(sorted: &[int], key: int) -> (code: i32)

Inputs  
  • sorted – immutable slice of integers that is monotonically non-decreasing.  
  • key – the integer value to look for.

Output  
  • code – a 32-bit signed integer interpreted as follows:  
      – if code ≥ 0, it is the smallest index `i` such that `sorted[i] == key`;  
      – if code < 0, then `!code` (bit-wise NOT, equivalently `-code-1`) is the index at which `key` should be inserted to keep the slice sorted.  That index may legally equal `sorted.len()`.

Preconditions  
1. sorted.len() > 0  
2. sorted.len() < i32::MAX as usize  
3. ∀ i:nat, j:nat. 0 ≤ i < j < sorted.len() ⇒ sorted[i] ≤ sorted[j]    (the slice is sorted).

Postconditions  
• Found case (code ≥ 0):  
    – 0 ≤ code < sorted.len() as i32  
    – sorted[code] == key  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < code ⇒ sorted[k] < key  
  Together these state that `code` is the first index where `key` appears.

• Not-found case (code < 0): let ins = (!code) as usize  
    – 0 ≤ ins ≤ sorted.len()  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < ins ⇒ sorted[k] < key  
    – ∀ k:nat. ins ≤ k < sorted.len() ⇒ key < sorted[k]  
  Thus `ins` is the unique position where `key` could be inserted while preserving non-decreasing order.

No auxiliary lemmas are required; both linear and logarithmic algorithms are acceptable as long as verification succeeds.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus function called `find_first_by_pred` that returns the first index in a slice whose element satisfies an externally supplied Boolean predicate.

Signature

Inputs  
  • a – immutable slice of mathematical integers (possibly empty).  
  • pred – a pure `spec_fn` (ghost function) taking an `int` and returning `bool`.

Output  
  • idx_opt – an `Option<i32>` interpreted as follows:  
      – Some(i)  – `i` is the smallest index such that `pred(a[i])` is true;  
      – None     – no element satisfies the predicate.

Preconditions  
1. a.len() ≤ i32::MAX as usize  
2. termination: pred is pure and always terminates (implicit for `spec_fn`).  
   (No assumption is made about pred’s content; it may be arbitrary.)

Postconditions  
• Success case (idx_opt == Some(i)):  
    – 0 ≤ i < a.len() as i32  
    – pred(a[i])  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < i ⇒ !pred(a[k])  

• Failure case (idx_opt == None):  
    – ∀ k:nat. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ !pred(a[k])

A ghost helper lemma is permitted if needed to reason about `pred`, but no concrete helper structures are required.  Any implementation that satisfies the above is acceptable.

Implement a verified function in Verus/Rust called `insert_before_each`.

Inputs  
• `s: &Vec<String>` – an immutable reference to a vector of `String`.  
• `x: String` – an owned string that will be placed in front of every element of `s`.

Output  
• `v: Vec<String>` – a new vector of `String`.

Specification  
Preconditions: none (the function must work for all `s` and `x`).  

Postconditions:  
1. `v.len() == 2 * s.len()` – the resulting vector is exactly twice as long as the input.  
2. For every integer index `i` with `0 ≤ i < s.len()`, the following holds:  
      • `v[2*i]   == x` (the even slot contains a copy of `x`)  
      • `v[2*i+1] == s[i]` (the subsequent odd slot contains the ith element of `s`).  

Thus `v` is the sequence `[x, s[0], x, s[1], …, x, s[n-1]]`, maintaining the original order and alternating between `x` and the elements of `s`.  
If `s` is empty, `v` must also be empty.

No additional helper functions or lemmas are required to express this behaviour. An implementation may need to clone `x` for each insertion, but any body that satisfies the above contracts is acceptable and will verify.

The specification describes a pure “make-a-reversed-copy” routine.

1. Function name

2. Inputs

3. Output

   The function returns a vector whose contents are precisely the elements of a but in reverse order. easy_to_hard_oai:
# Reasoning
The original exercise is easy because  
 – it has no parameters except `x` and `s`,  
 – the required shape of `v` is completely regular, and  
 – only two arithmetic facts must be proved (`v.len() = 2·s.len()` and the
  two‐slot mapping).  

To raise the difficulty we can

1.  Introduce an extra natural-number parameter `k` that governs how
    many copies of `x` precede each element.  This forces the learner to
    reason about an outer and an inner index as well as a variable-width
    block, not just the fixed block size 2.

2.  Make the marker string *alternate* between two values (`x` and `y`)
    depending on the parity of the element that follows.  This replaces
    the “all markers equal” property with a less common *alternating
    pattern* property and adds a small case split to the proof.

3.  Insert the marker string not only *before* every element but also
    *after* the last one, giving a length of `2·s.len() + 1`.  Because
    the final marker has no companion element, an additional boundary
    case must now be proved.

Each variant is still linear-time and linear-space, so we do not change
the asymptotic requirements; we simply add more indices and edge cases
to verify.

# Output
<<<
Function specification: `insert_k_before_each`

Inputs  
• `s: &Vec<String>` – an immutable reference to the source vector.  
• `x: String`       – the marker string that will be replicated.  
• `k: usize`        – a strictly positive integer (k ≥ 1) that tells
  how many copies of `x` must be inserted before **every** element of
  `s`.

Output  
• `v: Vec<String>` – the newly constructed vector.

Preconditions  
1. `k >= 1`.

Postconditions  
1. `v.len() == (k + 1) * s.len()`.  
2. For every index `i` with `0 ≤ i < s.len()` and every offset
   `j` with `0 ≤ j < k`,  
      • `v[(k + 1) * i + j] == x` (the first `k` slots of each block are
        copies of the marker),  
      • `v[(k + 1) * i + k] == s[i]` (the last slot of the block is the
        original element).  

Thus `v` has the block structure  
   `[x, …, x, s[0], x, …, x, s[1], …, x, …, x, s[n-1]]`,  
where every block is exactly `k` markers followed by one source
element.  If `s` is empty, `v` is empty regardless of `k`.

No helper lemmas are mandatory, but the proof must handle both the outer
index `i` and the inner offset `j`.
>>>

<<<
Function specification: `interleave_alternating_markers`

Inputs  
• `s: &Vec<String>` – an immutable reference to the source vector.  
• `x: String`       – the marker to be placed in front of even-indexed
  elements of `s`.  
• `y: String`       – the marker to be placed in front of odd-indexed
  elements of `s`.

Output  
• `v: Vec<String>`.

Preconditions  
• none.

Postconditions  
1. `v.len() == 2 * s.len()`.  
2. For every index `i` with `0 ≤ i < s.len()`,  
      • if `i` is even then `v[2*i]   == x`,  
      • if `i` is odd  then `v[2*i]   == y`,  
      • and in all cases `v[2*i + 1] == s[i]`.  

Therefore  
   `v = [x, s[0], y, s[1], x, s[2], y, s[3], …]`  
with the markers `x` and `y` alternating strictly as the parity of `i`
changes.  An empty `s` yields an empty `v`.

The alternating condition forces the implementation and the proof to
perform a parity case-split instead of reusing one invariant for every
position.
>>>

<<<
Function specification: `surround_and_separate`

Inputs  
• `s: &Vec<String>` – an immutable reference to the source vector.  
• `tag: String`     – a delimiter string that will appear
  between every pair of consecutive elements *and* at both ends.

Output  
• `v: Vec<String>`.

Preconditions  
• none.

Postconditions  
1. `v.len() == 2 * s.len() + 1`.  
2. `v[0] == tag`                                                                           (leading delimiter).  
3. For every index `i` with `0 ≤ i < s.len()`,  
      • `v[2*i + 1] == s[i]`,                                                              (original element)  
      • `v[2*i + 2] == tag`.                                                               (trailing delimiter)  

Consequently,  
   `v = [tag, s[0], tag, s[1], …, tag, s[n-1], tag]`.  
When `s` is empty, `v` contains a single element—the `tag` itself.

Because the vector ends with an extra delimiter, the learner must
reason about an index (`2*i + 2`) that is *strictly greater* than the
largest index that refers to an element of `s`, introducing a boundary
case absent from the original exercise.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn replace(v: Vec<i32>, x: i32, y: i32) -> (v_new: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        v.len() < 100000,
    ensures
        forall|k: int| 0 <= k && k < v.len() && v[k] == x ==> v_new[k] == y,
        forall|k: int| 0 <= k && k < v.len() && v[k] != x ==> v_new[k] == v[k],
{
```

# Reasoning
We examine the signature and its contracts step-by-step.

1. Inputs and their Rust types  
   • v: Vec<i32> — an owned, heap-allocated vector of 32-bit signed integers; ownership moves into the function.  
   • x: i32 — the value that we will search for inside v.  
   • y: i32 — the value that will overwrite every position whose old value equals x.

2. Output  
   • v_new: Vec<i32> — a freshly returned vector.

3. Pre-condition (requires)  
   • v.len() < 100 000  
     The caller must guarantee that the incoming vector contains strictly fewer than 100 000 elements.  No other pre-conditions are imposed; v may be empty and x, y may be any i32.

4. Post-conditions (ensures)  
   Two universally quantified clauses describe how v_new must relate to the original vector v.  
   For every mathematical integer index k satisfying 0 ≤ k < v.len():  
   a. If the element stored at that index originally equals the target value (v[k] == x), then after the call the same index must contain the replacement value (v_new[k] == y).  
   b. If the element stored at that index originally differs from x (v[k] ≠ x), then the element at the same index must be preserved verbatim (v_new[k] == v[k]).  

   Together these clauses express that v_new is identical to v except that every occurrence of x has been changed to y.

   Note that the specification implicitly forces v_new to be at least as long as v; otherwise the expressions v_new[k] would be ill-typed for some k in the quantified range.

5. What the spec does *not* say  
   • It does not state that v_new.len() == v.len(), although any implementation that satisfies the “indexing” obligations will in practice have that property.  
   • It does not constrain positions with indices k ≥ v.len(), leaving freedom to implementors to keep, truncate, or extend beyond that range (but typical, simplest implementations will return a vector of exactly the same length).  
   • There is no constraint on the order of unchanged elements—because each index is required to keep its former value when v[k] ≠ x, their positions are automatically preserved.

6. Informal behaviour summary  
   “Return a vector equal to the input vector but with every element equal to x replaced by y, while leaving all other elements untouched.  The input length is guaranteed to be < 100 000.”

7. Implementation freedom  
   Any code that consumes v and produces v_new satisfying the two quantified post-conditions (and keeping arithmetic within the i32 domain) is acceptable: e.g. allocate a new vector and copy / replace elements, or mutate v in place and return it.

# Final Description (to be given to a solver)
<<<
Design a verified function in Verus-Rust with the following specification:

Function name  
    replace

Inputs  
    • v: Vec<i32> — an owned vector of 32-bit signed integers, length strictly less than 100 000.  
    • x: i32 — the value that should be searched for in v.  
    • y: i32 — the value that will be written wherever x is found.

Output  
    • v_new: Vec<i32> — a vector returned to the caller.

Pre-condition  
    • v.len() < 100 000 (the vector is not too large).

Post-conditions  
    For every integer index k in the valid range 0 ≤ k < v.len():  
        – If v[k] == x then v_new[k] == y.  
        – If v[k] != x then v_new[k] == v[k].

In other words, v_new must be identical to the original vector except that every occurrence of x has been replaced by y. The specification implicitly requires v_new to be at least as long as v so that each original index remains addressable.

No additional helper lemmas or spec functions are needed; the task is to implement any verified code that fulfils the contracts above.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task only requires proving two straightforward facts about a single minimum value.  
To make the exercise more challenging I keep the basic “scan the first `n` elements” flavor, yet introduce additional properties that force the student to reason about *indices*, *multiplicity*, or *ordering among several extrema*.  
None of the new variants needs complicated data-structures, but each demands a few more proof steps (e.g. quantifier reasoning over two indices, or counting occurrences) and introduces at least one uncommon constraint (earliest index, distinct minima, or correct multiplicity).  
Below are three independent, self-contained problem statements that satisfy the requested difficulty increase.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Signature  
fn min_with_index(a: &[int], n: usize) -> (idx: usize, min: int)

Meaning  
Inspect the first `n` elements of `a` (indices `0 … n-1`) and return  
• `min`, their minimum value, and  
• `idx`, the *first* position at which that minimum occurs.

Inputs  
• a: immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• n: length of the prefix to examine.

Preconditions  
1. 0 < n ≤ a.len() and n < 1000.  
2. a.len() ≤ 10_000 (added upper-slice bound so the caller cannot pass a huge array).  

Postconditions  
1. 0 ≤ idx < n.  
2. min == a[idx].  
3. ∀j: 0 ≤ j < n ⇒ a[j] ≥ min.  
4. ∀j: 0 ≤ j < idx ⇒ a[j] >  min.            // idx is the *earliest* minimum occurrence.

Any executable body that meets these contracts, including proofs of (1)–(4), is acceptable.  No additional helper specifications are forbidden; ghost code is allowed.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Signature  
fn two_smallest_distinct(a: &[int], n: usize) -> (first_min: int, second_min: int)

Meaning  
Return the two smallest *distinct* values that occur among the first `n` elements of `a`.

Inputs  
• a: immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• n: number of leading elements to inspect.

Preconditions  
1. 2 ≤ n ≤ a.len().  
2. n ≤ 1024 (explicit bound).  
3. There exist at least two different values among indices `0 … n-1` (otherwise “second smallest” is undefined).

Postconditions  
1. first_min < second_min.  
2. ∃i: 0 ≤ i < n ∧ a[i] == first_min.  
3. ∃j: 0 ≤ j < n ∧ a[j] == second_min.  
4. ∀k: 0 ≤ k < n ⇒ a[k] ≥ first_min.  
5. ∀k: 0 ≤ k < n ∧ a[k] > first_min ⇒ a[k] ≥ second_min.  // second_min is minimum among elements strictly greater than first_min.

Any implementation that satisfies the specification and supplies the necessary proofs is acceptable; ghost variables or helper lemmas may be introduced freely.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Signature  
fn min_and_count(a: &[int], n: usize) -> (min: int, count: usize)

Meaning  
Scan the first `n` elements of `a`, compute their minimum `min`, and also return `count`, the number of positions in that prefix whose value equals `min`.

Inputs  
• a: immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• n: prefix length to consider.

Preconditions  
1. 0 < n ≤ a.len().  
2. n ≤ 1_000 (identical to the original bound).  
3. The slice elements are all in the range −10⁶ … 10⁶ (uncommon but easy-to-state numeric constraint).

Postconditions  
1. ∃i: 0 ≤ i < n ∧ a[i] == min.  
2. ∀i: 0 ≤ i < n ⇒ a[i] ≥ min.  
3. count ≥ 1.  
4. count == |{ i | 0 ≤ i < n ∧ a[i] == min }|.    // precise multiplicity.  
5. count ≤ n.                                       // sanity bound.

The body may use ghost state and helper proofs; all obligations must be discharged inside the function or via locally defined lemmas.
>>>

Design and verify, in Verus-Rust, an executable function called `yarra` together with one supporting pure specification predicate.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Helper predicate (pure spec function)

spec fn reversed(arr: Seq<char>, outarr: Seq<char>) -> bool

Logical meaning: “outarr is exactly the element-wise reversal of arr”.
Formal definition (only required when len equal):
    reversed(arr, outarr)  ≡
        ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < arr.len() ⇒ outarr[k] == arr[arr.len() − 1 − k]

A `recommends` clause must assert `arr.len() == outarr.len()` so the predicate is used only when both sequences have the same length.

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Primary verified function

fn yarra(arr: &[char]) -> (outarr: Vec<char>)

Inputs
    • arr : immutable slice of `char` (UTF-32 scalar values).

Outputs
    • outarr : `Vec<char>` whose contents are the exact reversal of `arr`.

Preconditions (requires)
    1. arr.len() > 0                      // slice must be non-empty
    2. arr.len() < 1_000_000              // upper bound prevents runtime index overflow

Postconditions (ensures)
    1. outarr.len() == arr.len()          // same length as the input slice
    2. reversed(arr@, outarr@)            // when viewed as sequences, outarr is the reverse of arr

Notation: `x@` is Verus’s ghost view converting a concrete collection into an immutable sequence for specification purposes.

────────────────────────────────────────
Any implementation that constructs such a vector (for example by iterating `arr` from end to start and pushing each element into `outarr`) and satisfies the two pre-conditions will meet the contract. The helper predicate is purely logical; no runtime code is generated for it.
