# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

spec fn set_union_is_associative(a: Set<int>, b: Set<int>, c: Set<int>) -> bool {
    (a + b) + c == a + (b + c)
}

fn set_union_is_associative_func(a: Set<int>, b: Set<int>, c: Set<int>) -> (result: bool)
    ensures
        result == set_union_is_associative(a, b, c),
{
```

# Reasoning
We must describe two items:

1. A pure specification helper `set_union_is_associative`.  
   • Inputs: three mathematical sets of integers `a`, `b`, `c` (type `Set<int>`).  
   • Output: `bool`.  
   • Definition: returns the truth‐value of the equality `(a + b) + c == a + (b + c)`, where `+` denotes ordinary set union.  
   • No pre- or post-conditions because it is defined by one expression.

2. An executable/ghost function `set_union_is_associative_func`.  
   • Inputs and their types match the helper (`a`, `b`, `c : Set<int>`).  
   • Output: `result : bool`.  
   • Preconditions: none (`requires` block absent).  
   • Postcondition: `result == set_union_is_associative(a, b, c)`.  
     That is, after the function returns, its Boolean result must equal the value produced by the helper predicate for the same three sets.  
   • Because union is mathematically associative, the helper always evaluates to `true`, so any body that provably returns `true` (or shows the equality directly) will satisfy the contract.

No other lemmas, loops, or decreases clauses are involved.

# Final Description
<<<
You must write two verified items:

1.  spec fn set_union_is_associative(a: Set<int>, b: Set<int>, c: Set<int>) -> bool  
    • Purpose: express, purely in the logic, that set union is associative for the specific sets `a`, `b`, and `c`.  
    • Definition:  
          (a + b) + c == a + (b + c)  
      where `+` is the union operator on Verus sets and `==` is extensional set equality.  
    • No explicit pre- or post-conditions; the single expression fully defines the function.

2.  fn set_union_is_associative_func(a: Set<int>, b: Set<int>, c: Set<int>) -> (result: bool)  
    • Inputs: the same three `Set<int>` parameters.  
    • Output: `result : bool`.  
    • Preconditions: none.  
    • Postcondition:  
          result == set_union_is_associative(a, b, c)  
      In words, the function must return a Boolean that is exactly the truth-value of the associative-union predicate for the provided sets.  
    • Since union is always associative, the helper predicate is universally true, so the simplest correct implementation is to return `true`, but any implementation that verifies the stated postcondition is acceptable.

These details are sufficient to recreate the entire specification and devise a verified body that meets it.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is a very small‐scale verification task:  
single helper predicate, two elementary pre/post conditions, no extra
logical structure, and nothing about memory usage, element uniqueness,
in-place mutation, or interaction with additional helper predicates.
To raise the difficulty, I introduce one or more of the following in
each proposed variant:

• extra helper predicates (power-of-two, no‐duplicates, balanced‐brackets)  
• more sophisticated post-conditions (element-wise properties that are
  not implied by simple reversal)  
• in-place mutation requirements (harder than allocating a new `Vec`)  
• size restrictions that interact with arithmetic reasoning  
• slightly less common condition “length is a power of two” instead of
  a plain upper bound  

Three harder standalone variants are supplied.  Each is entirely
self-contained; a spec generator could work from the description alone.

# Output
<<<
Design and verify, in Verus-Rust, an executable **in-place** function
called `yarra_inplace` together with two supporting pure specification
predicates.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Helper predicate (pure spec function #1)

spec fn reversed(a: Seq<char>, b: Seq<char>) -> bool

Meaning — identical to the original statement:
    reversed(a, b)  ≡
        ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ b[k] == a[a.len() − 1 − k]

recommends a.len() == b.len()

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Helper predicate (pure spec function #2)

spec fn power_of_two(n: int) -> bool

Logical meaning:
    power_of_two(n)  ≡  ∃ k:int • k ≥ 0 ∧ (1 << k) == n

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Primary verified function (executing code)

fn yarra_inplace(buf: &mut [char])

Inputs
    • buf : mutable slice whose content will be reversed **in place**

Outputs
    • none — the slice itself is mutated

Preconditions (requires)
    1. buf.len() > 1                       // at least two elements
    2. power_of_two(buf.len())             // length is an exact power of two (rarer condition)
    3. buf.len() ≤ 1_048_576               // prevent arithmetic overflow

Postconditions (ensures)
    1. reversed(old(buf)@, buf@)           // after the call the slice is the reversal
    2. forall i:int, j:int •              // no element was duplicated or lost
            0 ≤ i < buf.len() ∧ 0 ≤ j < buf.len()
            ⇒ ♦(buf@[i] == old(buf)@[j])   // multiset equality

Time-complexity hint (informal): must perform O(buf.len()) swaps and
use only O(1) additional memory (stack temporaries allowed).

────────────────────────────────────────
No vector allocation is permitted; the function must reverse the slice
by swapping elements symmetrically.
>>>

<<<
Implement and verify, in Verus-Rust, a function `yarra_unique`
together with one auxiliary pure specification predicate.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Helper predicate

spec fn is_strictly_unique(s: Seq<char>) -> bool

Meaning:
    is_strictly_unique(s) ≡
        ∀ i:int, j:int • 0 ≤ i < j < s.len() ⇒ s[i] ≠ s[j]

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Primary verified function

fn yarra_unique(arr: &[char]) -> (outarr: Vec<char>)

Inputs
    • arr : immutable slice

Outputs
    • outarr : newly allocated vector

Preconditions (requires)
    1. arr.len() ≤ 262_144
    2. is_strictly_unique(arr@)            // the caller guarantees all characters are distinct

Postconditions (ensures)
    1. outarr.len() == arr.len()
    2. reversed(arr@, outarr@)             // normal reversal
    3. is_strictly_unique(outarr@)         // still unique
    4. forall ch:char •                    // set equality, no element lost
         (∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < arr.len() ∧ arr@[i] == ch)
         ⇔
         (∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < outarr.len() ∧ outarr@[j] == ch)

Additional requirement: the implementation must run in Θ(n) time and
may allocate **at most one** Vec of capacity `arr.len()`; any further
heap allocation invalidates the specification.

<<<
Develop and verify, in Verus-Rust, a function `yarra_balanced`
and two supporting predicates.

────────────────────────────────────────
1.  Predicate balanced_brackets (pure)

spec fn balanced_brackets(s: Seq<char>) -> bool

Informal meaning: when reading the sequence left-to-right, treating
‘(’ as +1 and ‘)’ as –1, the running sum never becomes negative and
ends at zero.  Formally:
    balanced_brackets(s) ≡
        (∑_{k=0}^{s.len()−1} delta(s[k]) == 0) ∧
        (∀ m:int • 0 ≤ m ≤ s.len()
             ⇒ ∑_{k=0}^{m−1} delta(s[k]) ≥ 0)
where delta(c) = 1 if c == '(' else –1 if c == ')' else 0.

recommends
    ∀ c:char • c ∈ s ⇒ (c == '(' ∨ c == ')')   // only brackets allowed

────────────────────────────────────────
2.  Predicate reversed (as in the first task)

────────────────────────────────────────
3.  Primary verified function

fn yarra_balanced(arr: &[char]) -> (outarr: Vec<char>)

Inputs
    • arr : slice that contains ONLY '(' and ')' characters

Outputs
    • outarr : vector

Preconditions
    1. arr.len() % 2 == 0                   // even length
    2. arr.len() ≥ 2
    3. balanced_brackets(arr@)             // input is already balanced

Postconditions
    1. outarr.len() == arr.len()
    2. reversed(arr@, outarr@)             // simple reversal
    3. balanced_brackets(outarr@)          // reversal remains balanced
    4. arr.len() < 1_000_000

Implementation hint: a naive reverse works, but proving that a reversal
of a balanced bracket sequence is again balanced requires a small lemma
about symmetry, which the student must supply and verify.
>>>

# Reasoning
We need to describe main function to_lower_case and helper spec functions.

is_upper_case: predicate returns true if char code between 65 and 90 inclusive (ASCII uppercase 'A'..'Z').

is_upper_lower_pair: predicate returns true if integer value of first char equals integer value of second char minus 32; i.e., second char is lowercase corresponding to first uppercase.

shift_32: returns char by adding 32 modulo 128 to code of c; thus for uppercase ASCII letters gives lowercase.

to_lower_case: input s: &Vec<char>, requires vector non-empty. Output v: Vec<char>. Postconditions: v length equals s length. For every index i in bounds: if s[i] is uppercase ASCII, then v[i] is its lowercase counterpart (s[i] corresponds to v[i] via is_upper_lower_pair). Otherwise v[i]==s[i].

No other conditions. So function returns new vector same size, converting uppercase ASCII letters to lowercase; other characters unchanged.

Important details: char to int casting; reasoning uses mathematical int; ASCII mapping difference 32.

Now craft final description.

# Reasoning
The original exercise merely asks the learner to prove an `is_min_heap` predicate and to tie the executable function to that predicate.  
To make the task more challenging we can

1. Add uniqueness, strict–ordering, or “global-minimum” constraints so that the learner must state and quantify additional properties instead of only local parent/child relations.

2. Ask for a function that returns richer information (e.g. the **first violating index**).  This forces the learner to specify an `option<int>` result and relate it to the heap property with an existential/uniqueness post-condition.

3. Introduce a small, single-step heap operation (such as **sift-down once**) that mutates the array.  This demands reasoning about permutations, frame conditions, and preservation of the heap outside the affected subtree.

Below are three stand-alone, successively harder problem descriptions that incorporate these ideas.  Each description is detailed enough for a spec generator to create Verus code from it alone.

# Output
<<<
Title: Strict-Min-Heap With Uniqueness Check

Write a Verus specification function  
    `spec fn is_strict_min_heap(a: Seq<int>) -> bool`  

that returns `true` exactly when all of the following hold for the sequence `a` of integers:

1. Every parent value is **strictly smaller** than each of its children’s values; that is,  
   • for every index `i` with `0 ≤ i < a.len()/2` the left child index `2*i + 1` exists and  
      `a[i] < a[2*i + 1]`, and  
   • if the right child index `2*i + 2` exists then `a[i] < a[2*i + 2]`.

2. All elements of `a` are **pair-wise distinct** (no duplicates anywhere in the array).

3. The root element `a[0]`, when the array is non-empty, is the **minimum of the entire array**  
   (i.e. `∀j. 0 ≤ j < a.len() ⇒ a[0] ≤ a[j]`).

Next, implement an executable function

```
fn is_strict_min_heap_array(a: &[int]) -> (ok: bool)
```

with the following contract:

requires:    `true` (no runtime pre-conditions).  
ensures:     `ok` implies `is_strict_min_heap(a@)` and  
             `!ok` implies `!is_strict_min_heap(a@)`.

The implementation may iterate through the slice or use any other algorithm, but it must be accepted by the Verus verifier under the given specification.
>>>

<<<
Title: Locate First Heap-Property Violation

Design a specification and implementation for a function that inspects an array that *may* represent a min-heap and pin-points the first index that violates the heap property.

Specification to create:

```
fn first_heap_violation(a: &[int]) -> (idx: Option<int>)
```

where `Option<int>` is interpreted as follows:

• `None`   — the array satisfies the *non-strict* min-heap property  
  (`∀i< a.len()/2. a[i] ≤ a[2*i+1]` and, when in range, `a[i] ≤ a[2*i+2]`).

• `Some(k)` — the smallest index `k` (with respect to natural ordering) such that the value at `k`
  is larger than at least one of its children.  
  Formally:

  1. `0 ≤ k < a.len()/2`.  
  2. Either `(a[2*k+1] < a[k])` or `(2*k+2 < a.len() ∧ a[2*k+2] < a[k])`.  
  3. For every `i` with `0 ≤ i < k`, the heap property is respected at `i`.

Contract you must express in Verus:

requires:    `true`.  
ensures:     `idx.is_None()` ⇔ `is_min_heap(a@)` (where `is_min_heap` is the usual non-strict version).  
             If `idx.is_Some(k)` then conditions 1–3 above hold.

You may freely define any helper `spec fn` you need (e.g. the usual `is_min_heap`).  The verified body can perform a linear scan; no asymptotic constraints are imposed.
>>>

<<<
Title: Sift-Down-Once With Localised Mutation Guarantees

You are given a slice `a` whose ghost view `a@` already satisfies the *non-strict* min-heap property.  Implement one step of the standard “sift-down” (heapify) operation starting at an arbitrary index.

Provide the following function and full Verus contracts:

```
fn sift_down_once(a: &mut [int], start: int)
```

Preconditions  
1. `0 ≤ start < a.len()` (start index is inside the array).  
2. `is_min_heap(a@)` (the entire array is currently a valid min-heap).

Operational intent  
• Among `start` and its (at most two) children that exist, swap `a[start]` with the **smallest** of those three elements *if and only if* at least one child is strictly smaller than `a[start]`.  
• No further levels are adjusted; this is exactly **one** downward move, not a loop.

Postconditions to be specified and proven  
1. The resulting array `a@` is still a *non-strict* min-heap **except possibly at the subtree rooted at the chosen child** (since only one step is taken).  Formally, all indices other than `start_child` maintain the heap property.  
2. `a@` is a **permutation** of its old value (no elements are added or lost, only swapped).  
3. All indices strictly outside `{start, left_child, right_child}` remain unchanged.  
4. If the swap was performed, then `a[start]` is now ≤ both children (when they exist); otherwise the array is identical to its initial state.

You may define any auxiliary spec-level permutation predicate or use the built-in multiset equality provided by Verus.  The verifier must accept an implementation that performs at most three comparisons and (optionally) one swap.
>>>

Implement a verified Verus-Rust function with the exact specification below.

Function signature  
fn first_e(a: &Vec<char>) -> (x: i32)

Inputs  
• a : &Vec<char> — immutable reference to a vector of characters.

Output

Preconditions (requires)  
1. a.len() ≤ 1000.  (The vector’s length is at most one thousand.)

Postconditions (ensures)  
Let P ≡ ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] == 'e'.

• If P holds (at least one `'e'` is present):
    – 0 ≤ x < a.len()   (x is a valid in-bounds index),  
    – a[x] == 'e',  
    – ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < x ⇒ a[j] ≠ 'e'  (no earlier index contains `'e'`).  

• If P does not hold (no `'e'` is present):  
    – x == ‑1.

Cast note: inside the logical predicates indices live in `int`, hence you will need
`a[i as int]`, `x as int`, etc., when mixing runtime `i32` values with quantified
integers.

No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are necessary.  Any body that provably
meets the contract is accepted (e.g., a simple linear search).  For an empty vector
the function must return ‑1 because P is false.
nl_desc_oai_full_with_reasoning:
# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn get_even(s: Vec<u64>) -> (result: Vec<u64>)
    requires
        s.len() > 0,
        s.len() < 1000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> s[i as int] < u64::MAX / 2, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        result.len() == s.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < s.len() ==> if s[i as int] % 2 == 1 { result[i as int] == s[i as int] + 1 } else { result[i as int] == s[i as int] },
```

# Reasoning
We must digest the specification:

Function get_even:
- Takes ownership of a runtime vector s of 64-bit unsigned integers.
- Pre-conditions:
  1. s is non-empty (len() > 0).
  2. Its length is strictly less than 1000; this is a pragmatic bound so that later proofs about total size/overflow are easier.
  3. Every stored value is strictly less than u64::MAX / 2.  Because, in the worst case we will add 1 to an element, and the author wants to guarantee the result still lies within the u64 range.  (u64::MAX / 2 + 1 is still < u64::MAX.)
- Output: result, another Vec<u64>.
- Post-conditions:
  1. result has exactly the same length as s.
  2. Element-wise transformation rule:
     • If position i of s is odd (s[i] % 2 == 1) then result[i] must equal s[i] + 1 (which turns the odd number into the next even number).
     • Otherwise (when s[i] is already even), result[i] must equal s[i] (unchanged).

There are no other constraints.  In particular, the spec does not insist that result be distinct from s (the vector is newly produced anyway, because s was moved).  It also does not require any particular ordering or aliasing guarantees beyond the element-wise mapping described.

From the post-conditions we can also infer—but the spec doesn’t state explicitly—that every element of result is even:
   – if s[i] was odd, we add 1, making it even;
   – if s[i] was even, we leave it alone.

A correct implementation could, for example, allocate a new vector, iterate once over the source vector, push either the original value or value+1, and finally return the new vector.  Thanks to the numeric bound (< u64::MAX/2) every addition is arithmetic-safe in both the implementation and the proof.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a formally verified function in Verus-Rust called get_even with the following complete behaviour specification.

Signature
    fn get_even(s: Vec<u64>) -> (result: Vec<u64>)

Inputs
    s : Vec<u64> — a heap-allocated vector of 64-bit unsigned integers that is moved into the function.

Output
    result : Vec<u64> — a new vector that must contain exactly one value for every element of s, in the same order.

Pre-conditions (requires)
1. s.len() > 0               — the input vector is non-empty.
2. s.len() < 1000            — its length is strictly less than 1000.
3. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒ s[i] < u64::MAX / 2  
   — every element of s is strictly below half of the maximum u64 value.
   (This numeric guard guarantees that the computation described below never overflows.)

Post-conditions (ensures)
1. result.len() == s.len()  
   — the output vector’s length equals the input length.

2. ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒
      if s[i] % 2 == 1            // original element is odd
         then result[i] == s[i] + 1   // store the next even number
         else result[i] == s[i]       // keep the element unchanged
   — element-wise mapping rule: odd numbers are incremented by one, even numbers are copied verbatim.

(As a consequence of rule 2 every element stored in result is even, but that fact need not be written explicitly in the spec.)

No other pre- or post-conditions, helper lemmas, or ghost functions are required.

Any implementation that provably satisfies these contracts—such as allocating a fresh vector and processing s once—is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is a straight-forward linear search for a single character.  
To make the challenge slightly tougher yet still approachable, I introduce
variants that add one or more of the following:

• an extra input argument forcing the implementation to respect an
  additional bound (search must *start* at some given offset);

• multiple related outputs whose relationships must be proven
  simultaneously (first and last position, or a count);

• a character *set* instead of a single literal, pushing the learner to
  employ membership reasoning rather than a simple equality test;

• stricter post-conditions (e.g. prove that the slice before the returned
  index is completely free of any character in the target set);

• a modest increase of the vector length bound and precise requirements
  about not modifying the vector.

None of the variants requires sophisticated algorithms beyond linear scanning,
but each demands more intricate specifications and proofs than the original.

# Output
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function with the exact specification below.

Function signature  
fn first_e_from(a: &Vec<char>, start: i32) -> (idx: i32)

Inputs  
• a : &Vec<char> — immutable reference to a vector of characters (read-only).  
• start : i32     — starting index at which the search for `'e'` must begin
                    (inclusive).

Output  
• idx : i32 — the index of the first occurrence of `'e'` *at or after* `start`,
              or ‑1 if no such character exists in that suffix of the vector.

Preconditions (requires)  
1. 0 ≤ start ≤ a.len()      (the `start` index is in-bounds or exactly `len`).  
2. a.len() ≤ 1500.          (vector length is at most one thousand five-hundred).  

Postconditions (ensures)  
Let P ≡ ∃ i:int • start ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] == 'e'.

• If P holds (at least one `'e'` occurs at/after `start`):
    – start ≤ idx < a.len(),  
    – a[idx] == 'e',  
    – ∀ j:int. start ≤ j < idx ⇒ a[j] ≠ 'e'  (no earlier `'e'` in the searched suffix).  

• If P does not hold (no `'e'` after `start`):  
    – idx == ‑1.

The function must not mutate `a`.
>>>

Function name  
    cube_elements

Inputs  
    a: &[int] – an immutable slice containing at least one mathematical integer (`int`).

Output

Precondition

   For every valid index, the element in the result vector equals the cube of the element at the same index in the input slice.

Notes / hints for implementation  
• Arithmetic uses Verus’ unbounded `int` type, so overflow cannot occur.  
• Any algorithm that allocates a fresh vector and fills it with the corresponding cubes will satisfy the contract; a simple loop over the indices is sufficient.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Inverts an array of integers.
fn invert_array(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < a.len() ==> result[i] == a[a.len() - 1 - i],
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
The function is named invert_array.

1. Inputs & their types  
   • a: Vec<i32> – the function takes ownership of a heap-allocated vector containing 32-bit signed integers.

2. Output & its type  
   • result: Vec<i32> – a new (or possibly the same, mutated) vector returned to the caller.

3. Preconditions (requires)  
   • a.len() > 0 – the input vector must contain at least one element. An empty vector is not allowed.

4. Postcondition (ensures)  
   • ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[a.len() − 1 − i]  
     For every valid index of the original vector, the element stored at that index in the output equals the element that appeared at the mirror position counted from the end of the input.  
     Consequently, the returned vector is the exact reversal of the input’s contents.  
     Accessing result[i] inside the postcondition implicitly obliges result to have length ≥ a.len(); otherwise `result[i]` would be ill-formed. In practice one implements result so that its length is exactly a.len().

5. Supporting spec functions / lemmas  
   • None are referenced; the single postcondition fully specifies the behaviour.

6. Edge cases  
   • When a.len() == 1 the postcondition collapses to result[0] == a[0]; any implementation must therefore return a vector identical to the singleton input.  
   • Overflow is irrelevant because the arithmetic `a.len() - 1 - i` is carried out on mathematical integers in the spec.

7. Implementation freedom  
   Any verified implementation is permitted:  
   • allocate a new vector and push elements from back to front,  
   • perform an in-place reversal and return the same vector,  
   • or copy into an auxiliary slice and then back.  
   As long as the pre- and postconditions are provably satisfied, the verifier will accept the solution.

# Final Description
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus/Rust function called invert_array that reverses the order of the elements in a vector of 32-bit signed integers.

Signature  
fn invert_array(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Detailed contract

Inputs  
• a: Vec<i32> – the function takes ownership of this vector.

Precondition (requires)  
• a.len() > 0 – the input vector must not be empty.

Output  
• result: Vec<i32> – a vector whose elements represent the input in reversed order.

Postcondition (ensures)  
∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[a.len() − 1 − i]

Interpretation: for every index i that was valid in the original vector a, the element found at that index in the output equals the element that originally sat a.len()−1−i positions from the start. Thus result is the exact mirror image of a. Because the expression result[i] appears inside the quantifier, the verifier implicitly requires result.len() ≥ a.len(); a typical implementation makes the two lengths equal.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are necessary; the single postcondition fully captures the required behaviour. Any body that consumes the input vector and produces a vector satisfying the reversal property will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

   • allocate a new vector and push elements from back to front,  
   • perform an in-place reversal and return the same vector,  
   • or copy into an auxiliary slice and then back.  
   As long as the pre- and postconditions are provably satisfied, the verifier will accept the solution.

# Final Description
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus/Rust function called invert_array that reverses the order of the elements in a vector of 32-bit signed integers.

Signature  
fn invert_array(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Detailed contract

Inputs  
• a: Vec<i32> – the function takes ownership of this vector.

Precondition (requires)  
• a.len() > 0 – the input vector must not be empty.

Output  
• result: Vec<i32> – a vector whose elements represent the input in reversed order.

Postcondition (ensures)  
∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[a.len() − 1 − i]

Interpretation: for every index i that was valid in the original vector a, the element found at that index in the output equals the element that originally sat a.len()−1−i positions from the start. Thus result is the exact mirror image of a. Because the expression result[i] appears inside the quantifier, the verifier implicitly requires result.len() ≥ a.len(); a typical implementation makes the two lengths equal.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are necessary; the single postcondition fully captures the required behaviour. Any body that consumes the input vector and produces a vector satisfying the reversal property will be accepted by the verifier.
>>>

The original task is a straightforward element-wise map (x ↦ x³) with a single, easy-to-state postcondition.  
To make the exercise slightly harder while still keeping it reasonable for somebody learning Verus, we can:

1. Introduce index manipulations (e.g., reversing the output) so that learners must reason about `a.len() - 1 - i`.
2. Add a *filtering* requirement (skip the zero or negative elements), forcing reasoning about a second indexing relation between input and output lengths.
3. Return an *aggregate* (e.g., the sum of all cubes) in addition to the vector, adding a quantified post-condition about a scalar result.

Each variant below contains about ten extra words compared with the original statement and demands at least one additional logical step.  No variant changes time/space complexity beyond the obvious `O(n)` bound.

3. sum(cubed_rev) == sum( { j:int | 0 ≤ j < a.len() } a[j] * a[j] * a[j] )

Construct a small, formally-verified refinement framework consisting of two state machines—an abstract one and a “protocol” (concrete) one—and prove that the protocol’s initial states refine the abstract specification.

Entities to introduce (all exactly as named below).

1.  Abstract state
    struct Variables { value: int }

2.  Abstract predicates
    • spec fn init(v: Variables) -> bool  
      ‑ The variable starts at 0.  
      Formula: v.value == 0

    • spec fn increment_op(v: Variables, v_prime: Variables) -> bool  
      ‑ Single abstract “increment” transition: the successor’s value is exactly one larger.  
      Formula: v_prime.value == v.value + 1

    • spec fn decrement_op(v: Variables, v_prime: Variables) -> bool  
      ‑ Abstract “decrement” transition: the successor’s value is exactly one smaller.  
      Formula: v_prime.value == v.value − 1

    • enum Step { Increment, Decrement }

    • spec fn next_step(v, v_prime, step) -> bool  
      ‑ Dispatches to increment_op or decrement_op according to the concrete Step tag.

    • spec fn next(v, v_prime) -> bool  
      ‑ A nondeterministic one-step relation: ∃ step:Step • next_step(v, v_prime, step).


3.  Protocol (“concrete”) state
    struct VariablesProtocol { value: int }

4.  Protocol predicates
    • spec fn init_protocol(v: VariablesProtocol) -> bool  
      ‑ Same initial condition as the abstract machine: v.value == 0

    • spec fn increment_op_protocol(v, v_prime) -> bool  
      ‑ NB: this deliberately does the opposite of the abstract one:  
        v_prime.value == v.value − 1

    • spec fn decrement_op_protocol(v, v_prime) -> bool  
      ‑ Also opposite:  
        v_prime.value == v.value + 1

    • enum StepProtocol { Increment, Decrement }

    • spec fn next_step_protocol(v, v_prime, step) -> bool  
      ‑ Dispatch on StepProtocol.

    • spec fn next_protocol(v, v_prime) -> bool  
      ‑ Nondeterministic successor relation for the concrete machine:
        ∃ step:StepProtocol • next_step_protocol(v, v_prime, step).


5.  Abstraction function
    spec fn abstraction(v: VariablesProtocol) -> Variables  
    ‑ Currently just copies the single field: Variables { value: v.value }


6.  Required proof obligation
    proof fn refinement_init(v: VariablesProtocol)
        requires  init_protocol(v)
        ensures   init(abstraction(v))

    The task: implement the body of refinement_init so that Verus is able to verify it.
    Since both initial predicates demand that value == 0 and abstraction is the identity on
    that field, the proof can be a one-liner (e.g., `assert(init(abstraction(v)));`) or a
    sequence of simple `assert`/`proof` statements. No loop or recursion is needed.

Summary of what must hold after you finish:

    For every protocol state v that satisfies init_protocol,
    the abstract state obtained by abstraction(v) also satisfies init.
    This formally establishes that the concrete machine’s set of initial
    states refines (is a subset of) the abstract machine’s initial states.

Apart from filling in the body of refinement_init, **no other code needs to be written**—all types, predicates and helper functions are provided exactly as specified above.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn compute_fib(n: u64) -> (f: u128)
    requires
        n >= 0,
        n < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        f == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
We must analyse exactly what the specification says.

1. Function name and signature  
   • Name: `compute_fib`  
   • Input parameter: `n` of type `u64` (64-bit unsigned integer).  
   • Return value: `f` of type `u128` (128-bit unsigned integer).

2. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a. `n >= 0` – vacuously true for `u64`, but formally present.  
   b. `n < 1_000_000` – an upper-bound that rules out very large inputs.  
   c. `n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX` – since `u64::MAX / u64::MAX` equals `1`, this constraint means `n < 1`.  
      With unsigned integers the only value satisfying `n < 1` is `n == 0`.  
   Combined, the pre-conditions force callers to supply exactly `n == 0`.

3. Postcondition (`ensures`)  
   `f == n * (n + 1) / 2`  
   – The returned 128-bit integer must equal the arithmetic expression `n·(n+1)/2` computed in the mathematical domain (no overflow in the spec).  
   Given that `n` must be `0`, this simplifies to `f == 0`.

4. Observations / subtleties  
   • Despite the function’s name (`compute_fib`), the post-condition characterises the n-th triangular number, not the Fibonacci sequence.  
   • Because the pre-conditions limit `n` to `0`, the specification ultimately requires the function to return `0` for its single admissible input.  
   • The wider return type `u128` is employed to guarantee that the expression fits, although under the current pre-conditions overflow can never happen.  
   • There are no additional helper lemmas or spec functions.

From these requirements an implementation can be trivially: assert the pre-conditions (they are automatically enforced), then return `0u128`.

# Final Description
<<<
Design a formally verified Verus-Rust function with the exact specification below.

Function name: `compute_fib`

Inputs  
    • n : u64 — a 64-bit unsigned integer.

Output  
    • f : u128 — a 128-bit unsigned integer that the function returns.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. n ≥ 0.  
    2. n < 1,000,000.  
    3. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.  
       (Because u64::MAX / u64::MAX evaluates to 1, this forces n to be strictly less than 1, i.e. n == 0.)

Post-condition (ensures)  
    f == n * (n + 1) / 2.

Taken together, the contract allows only the caller input n == 0, and then demands that the function return f == 0 (since 0·(0+1)/2 = 0).  
No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are referenced; the body may simply return 0u128.

Anyone re-implementing the spec must reproduce the same signature, the three pre-conditions enumerated above, and the single post-condition equating the returned value with n·(n+1)/2.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original exercise is little more than a one-line `assert` proof — both
initial predicates are identical and the abstraction is the identity.  To make
the assignment a bit harder I generate three **stand-alone** variants that
preserve the overall “abstract vs. protocol machine” flavour but inject extra
reasoning obligations.

Variant 1 (Parity field)  
• Adds a new boolean component recording the parity of the value.  
• The abstraction must *derive* this flag instead of copying it, so the learner
  must supply a small chain of equalities / arithmetic lemmas.

Variant 2 (Step-simulation obligation)  
• Keeps a single `value` field, but requires the proof of *one-step
  simulation* in addition to initial refinement.  
• Because the protocol increments when the abstract decrements (and vice-versa)
  the abstraction flips the sign.  The student now needs to analyse both step
  cases to exhibit a matching abstract successor.

Variant 3 (Monotone counter with bounded credit)  
• Adds an extra natural-number `credit` that is **consumed** on `Increment`
  and **gained** on `Decrement`.  
• The abstraction forgets `credit`, so the learner must show that the credit
  bound in the protocol initialises high enough to match every abstract
  initial state (straight-forward) *and* prove an invariant preserving
  `credit >= 0` over one transition — a slightly longer inductive argument.

Each statement is self-contained and phrased so that a Verus-spec generator can
work directly from the English description.

# Output
<<<
Construct a formally-verified refinement framework with two state machines—an
abstract one and a concrete “protocol” one—and prove that the protocol’s
_initial_ states refine the abstract specification.

1.  Abstract state  
    struct Variables {  
        value: int,       // current counter  
        is_even: bool     // true  ⇔  value is even  
    }

2.  Abstract predicates  
    • spec fn init(v: Variables) -> bool  
      – Starts at zero and claims its parity correctly.  
        Formula: v.value == 0  &&  v.is_even == true

    • spec fn increment_op(v, v′) -> bool  
      – Adds one and flips the parity bit.  
        v′.value == v.value + 1  
        && v′.is_even == !v.is_even

    • spec fn decrement_op(v, v′) -> bool  
      – Subtracts one and flips parity.  
        v′.value == v.value − 1  
        && v′.is_even == !v.is_even

    • enum Step { Increment, Decrement }

    • spec fn next_step(v, v′, step) -> bool  – dispatch

    • spec fn next(v, v′) -> bool             – ∃ step …

3.  Protocol state  
    struct VariablesProtocol {  
        value: int,        // identical counter  
        parity_hint: bool  // *may be wrong* in general
    }

4.  Protocol predicates  
    • spec fn init_protocol(vp) -> bool  
      – Also starts at zero, but its parity_hint field is left **unconstrained**  
        except that it must be *some* boolean.  
        Formula: vp.value == 0

    • spec fn increment_op_protocol(vp, vp′) -> bool  
        vp′.value == vp.value + 1  
        && vp′.parity_hint == vp.parity_hint   // no update

    • spec fn decrement_op_protocol(vp, vp′) -> bool  
        vp′.value == vp.value − 1  
        && vp′.parity_hint == vp.parity_hint

    • enum StepProtocol { Increment, Decrement }

    • spec fn next_step_protocol( … )

    • spec fn next_protocol( … )

5.  Abstraction function  
    spec fn abstraction(vp: VariablesProtocol) -> Variables  
        = Variables {  
              value:   vp.value,  
              is_even: vp.value % 2 == 0        // *computed*, not copied
          };

6.  Required proof obligation  
    proof fn refinement_init(vp: VariablesProtocol)  
        requires init_protocol(vp)  
        ensures  init(abstraction(vp))

    Implement the body so that Verus verifies it.  Hints: you will have to
    reason about `vp.value == 0` implying `vp.value % 2 == 0`.
>>>

<<<
Extend the two-machine framework and show *both* initial-state and one-step
refinement.

1.  Abstract state

2.  Abstract predicates

       • increment_op:      v′.value == v.value + 1

Design an abstract counter that ignores a “credit” resource tracked only in the
concrete machine, then prove initial refinement and a credit-non-negativity
invariant.

   • decrement_op_protocol(vp,vp′)

        && vp′.credit == vp.credit + 1  
   • enums and dispatchers as usual.

5. Abstraction

6. Proof obligations

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn reverse(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
    ensures
        result.len() == a.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < result.len() ==> result[i] == a[a.len() - 1 - i],
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification describes a single, executable function `reverse`.

1. Inputs  
   • `a: Vec<i32>` – ownership of a heap-allocated vector whose elements are 32-bit signed integers is moved into the function (it is *not* borrowed).

2. Output  
   • `result: Vec<i32>` – a newly produced vector of `i32` values that will be returned.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `a.len() > 0` – the caller promises that the input vector contains at least one element.  
     (The function therefore never has to cope with an empty vector.)

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   • `result.len() == a.len()` – the returned vector must have exactly the same length as the input.  
   • `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < result.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[a.len() − 1 − i]`  
     For every valid index `i`, the element stored at position `i` in the result equals the element that was stored at position `a.len()-1-i` in the original vector.  
     In other words, the order of elements is completely reversed: the last input element becomes the first output element, the second-to-last becomes the second, …, and the first input element becomes the last output element.

5. Helper specifications / lemmas  
   • None are used; the behaviour is fully captured by the two postconditions.

6. Subtleties to notice  
   • Because the function takes ownership of `a`, it may freely move, mutate, or even consume the original vector while building the result.  
   • The specification does not demand any particular algorithmic complexity; any implementation that obeys the contracts is acceptable (e.g., allocate a new vector and push elements in reverse order, or reverse the vector in place and return it).  
   • Indexing in the postcondition is expressed with mathematical integers (`int`) but must correspond to valid runtime indices (the surrounding range guard `0 ≤ i < result.len()` guarantees this).

# Final Description
<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function with the exact signature

    fn reverse(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Its formally specified behaviour is:

Inputs
• `a` – a non-empty `Vec<i32>` that is moved (taken by value) into the function.

Precondition
• `a.len() > 0` – the caller must supply at least one element.

Outputs
• `result` – a `Vec<i32>` produced by the function.

Postconditions
1. `result.len() == a.len()` – the output length equals the input length.
2. For all indices `i` in the valid range, the following equality holds:

       result[i] == a[a.len() - 1 - i]

   This means `result` contains exactly the same elements as `a` but in the opposite order; the last element of `a` becomes the first of `result`, the second-to-last becomes the second, and so on.

No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or additional requirements are involved. Any concrete implementation (e.g., allocating a new vector and copying elements backwards, or reversing in place and returning the vector) is acceptable as long as it satisfies the above contracts and can be proved correct by Verus.
>>>

     For every valid index `i`, the element stored at position `i` in the result equals the element that was stored at position `a.len()-1-i` in the original vector.  
     In other words, the order of elements is completely reversed: the last input element becomes the first output element, the second-to-last becomes the second, …, and the first input element becomes the last output element.

5. Helper specifications / lemmas  
   • None are used; the behaviour is fully captured by the two postconditions.

6. Subtleties to notice  
   • Because the function takes ownership of `a`, it may freely move, mutate, or even consume the original vector while building the result.  
   • The specification does not demand any particular algorithmic complexity; any implementation that obeys the contracts is acceptable (e.g., allocate a new vector and push elements in reverse order, or reverse the vector in place and return it).  
   • Indexing in the postcondition is expressed with mathematical integers (`int`) but must correspond to valid runtime indices (the surrounding range guard `0 ≤ i < result.len()` guarantees this).

# Final Description
<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function with the exact signature

    fn reverse(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Its formally specified behaviour is:

Inputs
• `a` – a non-empty `Vec<i32>` that is moved (taken by value) into the function.

Precondition
• `a.len() > 0` – the caller must supply at least one element.

Outputs
• `result` – a `Vec<i32>` produced by the function.

Postconditions
1. `result.len() == a.len()` – the output length equals the input length.
2. For all indices `i` in the valid range, the following equality holds:

       result[i] == a[a.len() - 1 - i]

   This means `result` contains exactly the same elements as `a` but in the opposite order; the last element of `a` becomes the first of `result`, the second-to-last becomes the second, and so on.

No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or additional requirements are involved. Any concrete implementation (e.g., allocating a new vector and copying elements backwards, or reversing in place and returning the vector) is acceptable as long as it satisfies the above contracts and can be proved correct by Verus.

• demand stronger uniqueness properties (e.g. first occurrence);  
• require reasoning about two different extremal values at once;  
• introduce quantified equalities that force the prover/user to count or compare.  

All of these raise the proof effort while still being perfectly expressible with pure ghost/spec code in Verus.  Below are four independent, self-contained problem descriptions that each satisfy the brief and are harder than the original.

Input

Output

Specification  
• Preconditions (requires)

• Postconditions (ensures)

    4. First-occurrence property: ∀ j:int. 0 ≤ j < idx ⇒ s[j] < val.  
       (So `idx` is the smallest index whose element equals the maximum.)

No additional helper specifications or ghost arguments are required.

Specification

• Postconditions

Input

Output

Specification

• Postconditions

    4. Maximality of `largest`: ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < s.len() ⇒ s[k] ≤ largest.

No external lemmas or libraries beyond the Verus standard library are necessary.

Input

Create a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    fn get_mini(a: &[int]) -> (mini: usize)

whose contract is as follows.

Inputs  
• `a`: an immutable slice (`&[int]`) containing at least one element.

Pre-condition  
• `a.len() > 0` — the slice must be non-empty.

Outputs  
• `mini`: a `usize` index.

Post-conditions  
1. Validity of the index: `0 ≤ mini < a.len()`.  
2. Minimality: for every index `x` with `0 ≤ x < a.len()`, the element at `mini` is no larger than the element at `x`:
      ∀ x:int • 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ a[mini] ≤ a[x].

      ∀ x:int • 0 ≤ x < mini ⇒ a[mini] < a[x].

Together, clauses 2 and 3 mean that `mini` is the smallest (left-most) index whose value equals the overall minimum of the slice.

No helper spec functions or additional assumptions are required; a straightforward linear search implementation will satisfy the contract.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn compute_fib(n: u64) -> (b: u128)
    requires
        n >= 0,
        n < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n * (n + 1) / 2 < u128::MAX as u64, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < u128::MAX as u64, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        b == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
The snippet defines an executable function `compute_fib` (the name is misleading; it actually computes the *triangular* number of `n`, i.e. `1 + … + n`).  
Inputs and outputs
• Input `n` is an unsigned 64-bit integer (`u64`).  
• Output `b` is an unsigned 128-bit integer (`u128`).  

Pre-conditions (`requires`)
1. `n >= 0` – vacuous because `u64` is already non-negative.  
2. `n < 1_000_000` – caps the argument to below one million.  
3. `n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX` – since `u64::MAX / u64::MAX == 1`, this forces `n < 1`, i.e. `n` must be **0**.  
4. `n*(n+1)/2 < u128::MAX as u64` – guarantees the result fits into a 128-bit value when cast back to `u64`.  
5. `n < u128::MAX as u64` – always true for `u64`, but restated for overflow safety.

Because of condition 3, the only legal runtime input is `n == 0`.  

Post-condition (`ensures`)
`b == n*(n+1)/2` – the function must return exactly the triangular number of `n`. For the admissible domain this simplifies to `b == 0`.

There are no extra helper spec functions, no decreases clauses, no loop annotations.

# Final Description (put between tags)
<<<
Specification summary for the function `compute_fib`

Purpose  
Return the arithmetic series sum `1 + 2 + … + n`, expressed by the closed-form formula `n*(n+1)/2`. (Despite its name, it is *not* the Fibonacci number.)

Signature  
fn compute_fib(n: u64) -> (b: u128)

Contract  
Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. n ≥ 0.  
2. n < 1 000 000.  
3. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.  Because `u64::MAX / u64::MAX == 1`, this further restricts the domain to n = 0.  
4. (n*(n+1))/2 < u128::MAX as u64.  
5. n < u128::MAX as u64.  

Post-condition (ensures)  
b == n*(n + 1) / 2.

Observations  
• The combination of the above pre-conditions means the only admissible runtime value is n = 0, hence the function is effectively required to return 0.  
• No auxiliary spec functions or ghost state are involved; the behavioural description is fully captured by the single ensures clause.  
• Any verified implementation is acceptable provided it respects the contract—e.g. it may simply return 0, or compute the formula generically (with overflow-safe widening to u128).

A re-implementation from scratch must therefore create an executable Verus function with the exact signature and contract shown above, ensuring that, whenever the caller supplies an `n` satisfying all five requires clauses, the result `b` equals `n*(n+1)/2`.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is a classic “arg-min” specification that can be discharged with a simple linear scan.  
To toughen it, we can:  
• add secondary outputs that must be correlated with the first output;  
• change the tie-breaking rule to an unusual one (e.g. “right-most” or “median-most” rather than “left-most”);  
• require the function to exploit extra structure in the input (e.g. the slice is a *rotated* strictly-increasing array) so that a logarithmic algorithm is expected;  
• ask for additional quantified guarantees such as counting the number of minima.  

Below are three harder, stand-alone specifications that follow those ideas.  
Each is written so a spec generator can translate it directly into Verus contracts.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    fn leftmost_min_with_count(a: &[int]) -> (mini: usize, multiplicity: nat)

whose contract is as follows.

Inputs  
• `a`: an immutable slice (`&[int]`) that contains at least one element.

Pre-condition  
• `a.len() > 0` — the slice must be non-empty.

Outputs  
• `mini`: a `usize` index.  
• `multiplicity`: a natural number (`nat`) representing how many times the overall minimum value occurs in `a`.

Post-conditions  
1. Index validity: `0 ≤ mini < a.len()`.  
2. Minimality: ∀ x:int • 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ a[mini] ≤ a[x].  
3. Left-most tie-breaking: ∀ x:int • 0 ≤ x < mini ⇒ a[mini] < a[x].  
4. Correct multiplicity:  
   (a) `multiplicity > 0`.  
   (b) multiplicity = |{ x:int | 0 ≤ x < a.len() ∧ a[x] == a[mini] }|.  
5. Consistency: if multiplicity == 1 then mini is the unique index satisfying a[mini] = min(a).  

No helper spec functions or extra assumptions are required; a linear scan implementation that simultaneously tracks the running minimum and its count is sufficient.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    fn rightmost_abs_min(a: &[int]) -> (mini: usize)

whose contract is as follows.

Inputs  
• `a`: an immutable slice (`&[int]`) containing at least one element.

Pre-condition  
• `a.len() > 0`.

Outputs  
• `mini`: a `usize` index.

Post-conditions  
1. Index validity: `0 ≤ mini < a.len()`.  
2. Absolute minimality: ∀ x:int • 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ |a[mini]| ≤ |a[x]|.  
3. Right-most tie-breaking: ∀ x:int • mini < x < a.len() ⇒ |a[mini]| < |a[x]|.  
   (That is, if several elements share the same smallest absolute value, `mini` is the greatest index among them.)

Implementations must examine every element in `a` (a linear scan is acceptable); no helper proof functions are required.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally verified Verus-Rust function

    fn pivot_of_rotated_sorted(a: &[int]) -> (pivot: usize)

whose contract is as follows.

Inputs  
• `a`: an immutable slice (`&[int]`) that contains at least one element and is known to be a *rotation* of a strictly increasing sequence without duplicates.  
  Formally, there exists some index `k` (0 ≤ k < a.len()) such that  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() − 1 ⇒ a[(k + i) % a.len()] < a[(k + i + 1) % a.len()].

Pre-conditions  
• `a.len() > 0`.  
• The “rotation of strictly increasing” property stated above holds.

Outputs  
• `pivot`: a `usize` index, the place where the rotation occurs (i.e. the index of the overall minimum element).

Post-conditions  
1. Validity: `0 ≤ pivot < a.len()`.  
2. Pivot minimality: ∀ x:int • 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ a[pivot] ≤ a[x].  
3. Uniqueness: ∀ x:int • 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ a[pivot] = a[x] ⇒ x = pivot.  
4. Adjacent ordering:  
   (a) If a.len() > 1 then a[pivot] < a[(pivot + 1) % a.len()].  
   (b) If a.len() > 1 then a[(pivot + a.len() - 1) % a.len()] < a[(pivot + 0) % a.len()].  
5. Logarithmic progress hint: an implementation that performs at most ⌈log₂(a.len())⌉ + 1 comparisons is expected (binary search on rotation point), though this is a non-binding hint and need not be formally proved.

No duplicates exist by assumption; verifying the binary-search style algorithm will therefore satisfy all obligations.
>>>

Inputs

Output

   Passing by value means the body may freely mutate or consume the vector.  

2. Output & type  
   • result: Vec<i32> – another vector, returned by value.

3. Preconditions (requires)  
   • a.len() > 0 – the incoming vector must be non-empty.  
   • a.len() < i32::MAX as usize – the length must be strictly less than 2 147 483 647 to avoid any later cast or arithmetic overflow involving the length.

   • ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[i] + 1 – for every valid index, the element in the output is precisely the corresponding input element plus one.

   Note: the spec reasons over mathematical integers (int) inside the quantifiers; the overflow guard in the precondition guarantees the corresponding i32 arithmetic in the implementation is safe.

5. Behavioural summary

   Every element is at most i32::MAX-1, ensuring that adding one will still fit in the i32 range and hence no overflow can occur at run time.

Postconditions (ensures)

2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[i] + 1  
   For each valid index, the element in the output equals the corresponding input element increased by one. Order is preserved.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are necessary. Any implementation strategy (in-place mutation, allocation of a new vector, iteration with a loop or iterator combinator, etc.) is acceptable, provided it can be proven to satisfy the above contracts and executes without i32 overflow.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is a very small “linear search or equivalent” specification:  
• no stability or minimal–index requirement,  
• no guarantees when several occurrences exist,  
• no description of what the negative code means beyond “not found”, and  
• no structural property (like sortedness) that could be exploited for a faster algorithm.

To raise the difficulty only “a bit”, we can ask for stronger behavioural guarantees that force extra logical reasoning but are still feasible for an intermediate Verus learner.  Three independent ways to do so are:

1 Minimal‐index search: in addition to correctness, the implementation must prove that the returned index is the *first* occurrence.  This adds a universally-quantified minimality clause.

2 Binary-search-style contract on a *strictly increasing* slice.  
   – The slice must be proven sorted, which learners must express formally.  
   – The result must mimic Java’s `Arrays.binarySearch`: on failure a negative value encodes an *insertion point*, which itself has to satisfy two boundary inequalities.  
   This introduces a less common requirement (insertion-point semantics) and a considerably richer postcondition.

3 Range search: return both the first and last index of the sought value, or `None` if absent.  
   The post-conditions must establish three separate facts at once (all positions in the range equal *x*, everything outside different from *x*, and l ≤ h).  
   This obliges the student to craft two minimality/maximality proofs instead of one.

Each specification below is entirely self-contained, detailed enough for a spec generator, and roughly 10–25 words longer than the original.

# Output
<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus-Rust function named `buscar_primero` (“search first”) with the following exact behaviour.

Inputs
• `a: &[int]` — immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• `x: int`     — value to locate.

Output
• `r: i32` — 32-bit signed integer result.

Preconditions
• Slice length fits in `i32`: `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize`.

Postconditions
1. If the function returns a negative number (`r < 0`) then *x* is absent:  
   ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ x.
2. If the function returns a non-negative index within bounds (`0 ≤ r < a.len()`) then  
   (a) `a[r] == x`, and  
   (b) `r` is the *smallest* such index: ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < r ⇒ a[i] ≠ x.

Any algorithm satisfying these contracts is acceptable; a linear scan returning the first match or −1 is typical.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus-Rust function named `buscar_ordenado` (“search ordered”) with strict binary-search semantics.

Inputs
• `a: &[int]` — slice whose elements are *strictly increasing*: ∀ i,j • 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ⇒ a[i] < a[j].  
• `x: int`     — value to locate.

Output
• `r: i32`.

Preconditions
• `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize`.  
• The strict-order property above must hold (caller responsibility).

Postconditions
1. If `0 ≤ r < a.len()` then `a[r] == x`.  
2. If `r < 0` then *x* is absent and the *insertion-point* encoded by  
   `ip := -(r + 1)` satisfies  
   • 0 ≤ ip ≤ a.len(),  
   • (ip < a.len() ⇒ a[ip] > x),  
   • (ip > 0     ⇒ a[ip − 1] < x).

Thus the return value exactly matches Java’s `Arrays.binarySearch`.  Any correct algorithm (e.g., proven binary search) is valid.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus-Rust function named `buscar_rango` (“search range”) that returns the whole contiguous block of occurrences of a value.

Inputs
• `a: &[int]` — immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• `x: int`     — value to locate.

Output
• `res: Option<(i32, i32)>` — either `None` or `Some((l,h))` where *l* and *h* are 32-bit indices.

Preconditions
• `a.len() < i32::MAX as usize`.

Postconditions
1. If `res == None` then ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ x.  
2. If `res == Some((l,h))` then  
   (a) 0 ≤ l ≤ h < a.len(),  
   (b) ∀ i:int • l ≤ i ≤ h ⇒ a[i] == x,  
   (c) ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < l ∨ h < i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ x.

Any algorithm that produces such a result, e.g., two linear passes or a pair of binary searches on a sorted block, is acceptable as long as it fulfils the contracts.
>>>

Function signature  
fn find(a: &[int], key: int) -> (index: i32)

Inputs  
• a – immutable slice of mathematical integers (`&[int]`), required to be non-empty.  
• key – the integer value to search for.

Output

Preconditions

Postconditions

2. Correctness when found:  
   index ≠ −1  ⇒  
      • a[index as int] == key (the element at the returned position is the key) and  
      • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < index ⇒ a[i] ≠ key (no earlier position contains the key).  
   Together these clauses specify that the function returns the *first* occurrence of key.  
3. Correctness when not found:  
   index == −1 ⇒ ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ key.  
   Hence ‑1 is returned exactly when the key does not appear anywhere in the slice.

No additional helper specifications or lemmas are required.

Semantically, `find` performs a linear search over `a`, returning the smallest index whose element equals `key`, or −1 if the key is absent. All arithmetic in the specification is done in the mathematical `int` domain, while the concrete index returned must be representable as an `i32`, guaranteed by the preconditions.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn binary_search(int_seq: &[i32], key: i32) -> (r: i32)
    requires
        int_seq.len() > 0,
        int_seq.len() < i32::MAX as usize,
        forall|i: int, j: int| 0 <= i && i <= j && j < int_seq.len() as int ==> int_seq[i] <= int_seq[j],
    ensures
        r >= 0 ==> r < int_seq.len() as i32 && int_seq[r as int] == key,
        r < 0 ==> forall|i: int| 0 <= i && i < int_seq.len() as int ==> int_seq[i] != key,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We’re asked to turn this Verus specification into a very explicit English-language problem statement.

Inputs  
• int_seq: an immutable slice (&[i32]) whose length is strictly positive and strictly smaller than i32::MAX; this bound guarantees that any valid index fits in a signed 32-bit integer.  
• key: the 32-bit value we are looking for in the slice.

Preconditions  
1. int_seq.len() > 0 — the slice is non-empty.  
2. int_seq.len() < i32::MAX — so every index fits into the i32 type that the function returns.  
3. The slice is sorted in non-decreasing order: for every pair of indices i, j with 0 ≤ i ≤ j < len, we have int_seq[i] ≤ int_seq[j].  Equality is permitted, so duplicate values may appear.

Output  
• r: i32.

Postconditions  
• If r ≥ 0, then it denotes a valid index of the slice (0 ≤ r < len) and int_seq[r] equals key.  
• If r < 0, then key does not occur anywhere in the slice (∀ i: 0 ≤ i < len ⇒ int_seq[i] ≠ key).

Thus r is an index when the key is present and any negative number when it is absent.  There is no restriction on which negative number or, in the case of duplicates, which matching index is returned—only the boolean “found?” information and the correctness of an index when found.

No auxiliary spec functions or lemmas are referenced; everything is captured directly in the requires/ensures clauses.

Implementation freedom: an actual binary search algorithm is natural, but any verified implementation that satisfies the contract is acceptable.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Implement a formally-verified function in Verus called `binary_search`.

Signature  
fn binary_search(int_seq: &[i32], key: i32) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
1. int_seq — an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.  
2. key — the value to look up in the slice.

Required properties of the input (preconditions)  
• The slice is not empty: int_seq.len() > 0.  
• The slice length is strictly less than i32::MAX so that every valid index fits in an i32.  
• The slice is sorted in non-decreasing order: for every pair of indices i and j with 0 ≤ i ≤ j < len, element i is ≤ element j.  (Duplicates are therefore allowed.)

Returned value  
r: i32.

Meaning of the returned value (postconditions)  
• If r ≥ 0  
    – 0 ≤ r < int_seq.len() (r is a valid index)  
    – int_seq[r] == key (the element at that index equals the search key)  
• If r < 0  
    – the key does not occur anywhere in the slice: for all i in 0 .. len-1, int_seq[i] ≠ key.

In other words, the function must return a non-negative index where the key is stored if the key is present, and a negative number otherwise.  Any negative number is acceptable when the key is absent, and when duplicates exist any matching index may be returned.

No additional helper functions or ghost state are necessary—only the contract above must hold.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is a straight-forward linear search that merely proves “first occurrence or –1”.  
To make the assignment more challenging we can:

• Require the caller to supply an extra logical parameter (e.g. the ordinal n of the desired occurrence), forcing the implementation to maintain an additional counter and reason about it.  
• Change the data-structure assumption (e.g. the slice is sorted) and ask for a lower-bound search.  This replaces the linear-search proof by a logarithmic one and introduces reasoning about ordering.  
• Restrict the search to a sub-slice described by two indices.  This obliges the student to juggle several index domains simultaneously and prove that the result is located inside the contractual window.

Each re-formulation keeps the spirit of “find”, but adds new constraints, uses less common post-conditions, and needs more verification steps (e.g. reasoning about counters, ordering, or window bounds).


# Output
<<<
Implement a formally-verified function in Verus-Rust with the following full specification.

Function signature  
fn find_nth(a: &[int], key: int, n: nat) -> (index: i32)

Inputs  
• a – immutable slice of mathematical integers (`&[int]`), required to be non-empty.  
• key – the integer value to search for.  
• n – a natural number (0-based) telling which occurrence of `key` to return  
  (0 ⇒ first, 1 ⇒ second, …).

Preconditions  
1. a.len() > 0.  
2. a.len() ≤ i32::MAX as usize.  
3. n ≤ i32::MAX as nat.     // allows casting the ordinal to i32 if desired.

Postconditions  
1. Range guarantee: −1 ≤ index < a.len() as i32.  
2. Correctness when found:  
      index ≠ −1 ⇒  
        • a[index as int] == key,  
        • (# { i:int | 0 ≤ i ≤ index ∧ a[i] == key }) == n + 1,   // exactly the (n+1)st hit
        • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < index ∧ a[i] == key ⇒
            (# { j:int | 0 ≤ j ≤ i ∧ a[j] == key }) ≤ n.          // no earlier extra hit
   In words: the result is the (n)-th occurrence (0-based).  
3. Correctness when not found:  
      index == −1 ⇒  
        (# { i:int | 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] == key }) ≤ n.         // fewer than n+1 matches exist

Semantics  
The function performs a linear scan of `a`, counting how many times `key` has appeared.
It returns the index of the (n)-th hit if it exists, otherwise −1.
All arithmetic is in mathematical `int`; the concrete result fits in `i32` by the range guarantee.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified function in Verus-Rust with the following full specification.

Function signature  
fn lower_bound(a: &[int], key: int) -> (index: i32)

Inputs  
• a – immutable slice of mathematical integers sorted in non-decreasing order.  
• key – the probe value.

Preconditions  
1. a.len() ≤ i32::MAX as usize.          // the slice may be empty.  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ a[i + 1].  // sortedness

Postconditions  
1. 0 ≤ index ≤ a.len() as i32.            // note inclusive upper bound  
2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < index ⇒ a[i] < key.  // all earlier elements are strictly smaller  
3. index < a.len() as i32 ⇒ a[index as int] ≥ key.   // first element ≥ key, if any

Semantics  
The function computes the classic “lower bound”: the smallest index whose element is ≥ `key`,
or `a.len()` (cast to `i32`) if every element is < `key`.
Although the most common implementation uses binary search (O(log n)),
the spec does NOT prescribe an algorithm; it only states the logical behaviour.
>>>

<<<
Implement a formally-verified function in Verus-Rust with the following full specification.

Function signature  
fn find_in_window(a: &[int], key: int, start: nat, window_len: nat) -> (index: i32)

Inputs  
• a – immutable slice of mathematical integers.  
• key – the value to search for.  
• start – starting position of the search window, counted from the beginning of `a`.  
• window_len – number of elements in the window.

Preconditions  
1. start + window_len ≤ a.len().         // the window lies wholly inside the slice  
2. a.len() ≤ i32::MAX as usize.          // entire slice fits into `i32`  
3. window_len > 0.                       // non-empty window

Postconditions  
1. Range guarantee: −1 ≤ index < window_len as i32.  
2. Correctness when found:  
      index ≠ −1 ⇒  
        • a[(start + index as nat) as int] == key,  
        • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < index ⇒ a[(start + i as nat) as int] ≠ key.  // first hit inside window
3. Correctness when not found:  
      index == −1 ⇒ ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < window_len ⇒
                       a[(start + i as nat) as int] ≠ key.

Semantics  
The function linearly scans only the sub-slice a[start .. start + window_len),
returning the first occurrence of `key` relative to that window, or −1 if absent.
The absolute position inside `a` is start + index (when index ≥ 0).
>>>

// Function to add two numbers
fn add(x: u64, y: u64) -> (result: u64)
    requires
        x >= 0,
        y >= 0,
        x + y < u64::MAX,
    ensures

1. Function name and purpose

2. Inputs and their types  
   - `x: u64` — an unsigned 64-bit integer.  
   - `y: u64` — another unsigned 64-bit integer.

3. Output and its type  
   - `result: u64` — the single return value of the function.

4. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   - `x >= 0` and `y >= 0`. These are tautological for `u64`, but they are explicitly stated in the spec, so they must appear in the re-implementation.  
   - `x + y < u64::MAX`. This guarantees that the mathematical addition of `x` and `y` stays strictly below the maximum representable `u64` value (2⁶⁴ − 1). Practically, it rules out overflow, making it safe to perform the addition in 64-bit arithmetic inside the body.

6. No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, or ghost state are referenced.  
7. No additional attributes (e.g., decreases, loop isolation) are present or needed.

The body can be a one-line `x + y` expression; any implementation that satisfies the above contracts is valid.

# Reasoning
The original task is a very plain linear‐search spec:  
• no ordering assumption on the vector,  
• any occurrence may be returned, and  
• the negative result can be any negative number.

To raise the difficulty I introduce stricter functional guarantees that force more delicate logical arguments and invariants while still keeping the overall problem self-contained:

1. Require the returned index to be *the first* (minimal) occurrence.  
   – This forces a loop invariant about every earlier element ≠ x.  

2. Assume the vector is *strictly ascending* and ask for a Java/Go-style
   “negative insertion point” when the value is absent.  
   – This replaces an everyday sentinel (–1) with the less common
     –insertion_idx – 1 rule, and it implicitly demands reasoning
     about where the element would fit.  

3. Ask the routine to compute *two results* at once:  
   (a) whether x was found, and  
   (b) the *total number* of occurrences of x.  
   – Now the loop must maintain two pieces of information and
     additionally prove a counting property.

Each variant stands alone and contains enough detail for a spec generator.

# Output

Signature  
    fn buscar_primero(a: &Vec<i32>, x: i32) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
    • `a`: immutable reference to a vector of 32-bit signed integers.  
    • `x`: the value to locate.

Outputs  
    • `r`: index of the first occurrence, or –1 when absent.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() > 0  
    2. a.len() ≤ 1_000_000

Postconditions (ensures) – exactly one of the two following shapes must hold:

Found case (first occurrence)  
    • 0 ≤ r < a.len()  
    • a[r] == x  
    • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < r ⇒ a[i] ≠ x     // minimality guarantee

Not-found sentinel  
    • r == -1  
    • ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≠ x

No auxiliary ghost functions or lemmas are needed, but your loop
invariants must be strong enough to establish the “first occurrence”
property.
>>>

<<<
Create a formally-verified Verus/Rust function named `buscar_ordenado`.

Signature  
    fn buscar_ordenado(a: &Vec<i32>, x: i32) -> (r: i32)

Inputs  
    • `a`: immutable reference to a vector of 32-bit signed integers,
      sorted in strict ascending order (each element is smaller than the next).  
    • `x`: the value to locate.

Outputs  
    • `r`: either the index where `x` is stored or a negative value
      encoding the insertion position.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() < 1_000_000         // zero-length allowed this time  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i+1 < a.len() ⇒ a[i] < a[i+1]    // strict order

Postconditions (ensures) – exactly one shape must hold:

Found case  
    • 0 ≤ r < a.len()  
    • a[r] == x

Not-found case using “negative insertion point”  
    • r == -(ins + 1) where  
          ins = |{ i:int | 0 ≤ i ≤ a.len() ∧
                           (∀ j:int • 0 ≤ j < i ⇒ a[j] < x) ∧
                           (∀ j:int • i ≤ j < a.len() ⇒ x < a[j]) }|  // unique index
      (verus tip: define `ins` inside an `ensures` clause with `exists` or `let`)

Intuitively, `ins` is the index where `x` could be inserted while
preserving the order; returning –(ins + 1) mimics Java’s binary-search

The original function merely returns a number that is at least every common divisor; the student can satisfy the post-condition by returning `max(a,b)` or even a constant.  
To make the task non-trivial we can

1. Force *exact* equality with a mathematically defined expression rather than a loose lower bound.  
2. Ask for less usual objects such as prime divisors, k-th power sums, or simultaneously returning several values.  
3. Add extra logical post-conditions (e.g. behaviour when `gcd(a,b)=1`, monotone properties, equivalence with a well-known arithmetic identity), so more proof steps are required.  
4. Keep the data-range restrictions so that an implementation based on explicit iteration is still feasible.

Below are three harder, standalone specifications. Each description contains all the information needed to generate a Verus spec from scratch (name, arguments, types, pre- and post-conditions, helper predicates).

<<<

    fn sum_of_common_prime_divisors(a: u64, b: u64) -> (sum: u64)

Purpose  
Return the exact sum of all **prime** numbers that divide both `a` and `b`.

Auxiliary predicate to define in the spec  
spec fn is_prime(p: nat) -> bool  
    // true iff p ≥ 2 and has no divisors other than 1 and itself.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
• a ≥ 2 ∧ b ≥ 2.  
• a ≤ 1_000_000 ∧ b ≤ 1_000_000   // bound keeps simple loops feasible.  
• result of the sum fits into `u64` (this is implied by the numeric bound but may be added explicitly).

Post-conditions (ensures)  
• sum ≥ 0.  
• For every natural d:  
     if  is_prime(d) ∧ d divides a ∧ d divides b   
     then d is included in the sum exactly once.  
  (Formally: ∀d: nat. is_prime(d) ∧ a % d == 0 ∧ b % d == 0 ⇒  
                       ∃k: nat. k = d ∧ k ≤ sum  AND  
                       The multiset of such k’s sums to `sum`.)  
• If gcd(a,b) = 1 the sum must be 0.  
• The returned value equals the mathematical Σ of all common prime divisors, not merely a lower or upper bound.

Hints for the student (not part of the spec)  
You will need to (1) implement a primality test usable in proof, (2) prove that no composite number contributes to the sum, and (3) argue that every common prime divisor is counted exactly once.

Pre-conditions

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Proof function to calculate the Fibonacci sequence
fn fib(n: u64) -> (x: u128)
    requires
        n < 100000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        x == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
The supplied specification is for a single, *pure* (side-effect-free) function called `fib`.

1.  Inputs  
    • `n`: a 64-bit unsigned integer (`u64`).  

2.  Output  
    • `x`: a 128-bit unsigned integer (`u128`) returned as a named tuple component.

3.  Preconditions (`requires`)  
    a. `n < 100000` – the caller must supply a value strictly less than one hundred thousand.  
    b. `n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX` – since `u64::MAX / u64::MAX` equals `1`, this actually tightens the previous bound to `n < 1`.  Therefore the only admissible concrete value is `n = 0`.  (The comment claims these bounds are “relaxations” to avoid overflow, but in reality the second bound is extremely restrictive; nevertheless, it is part of the formal contract and must be honoured.)

4.  Postcondition (`ensures`)  
    `x == n * (n + 1) / 2` – the function’s return value must equal the standard *triangular-number* formula for the input `n`.  
    Although the doc-comment mentions the Fibonacci sequence, the logical contract unmistakably describes triangular numbers, not Fibonacci.  Verification will therefore enforce the triangular formula.

5.  Observations / subtleties  
    • Because of the second pre-condition, the only permitted caller input is `0`.  Consequently, the only permissible output is `0` as well (`0 * 1 / 2 = 0`).  A correct body may nonetheless implement the general formula; the verifier will automatically prove it correct for the restricted domain.  
    • The computation in the postcondition is carried out in the mathematical integer domain (`int` inside Verus specs), so there is no risk of overflow there.  At run time, the programmer must ensure any intermediate arithmetic fits into `u128`; with the current pre-conditions that is trivial.

6.  Implementation guidance  
    A minimal compliant implementation could simply `return 0` given the effective domain limitation, but a more general body calculating `(n as u128) * ((n + 1) as u128) / 2` will also verify, and is usually preferable.

# Final Description
<<<
Create a Verus-verified Rust function with the exact specification below.

Function signature  
    fn fib(n: u64) -> (x: u128)

Inputs  
    • n – a 64-bit unsigned integer supplied by the caller.

Outputs  
    • x – a 128-bit unsigned integer returned to the caller.

Pre-conditions (`requires`)  
    1. n < 100 000  
    2. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX   // because u64::MAX / u64::MAX == 1, this effectively means n = 0.

Post-condition (`ensures`)  
    x == n * (n + 1) / 2  
In words: the function must return the triangular number associated with n (½·n·(n+1)).

Notes for the implementer  
    • The pre-conditions restrict callers to the single allowed value n = 0; nevertheless, you may implement the full triangular-number formula.  
    • Any implementation that the Verus verifier can prove satisfies the above contract is acceptable.  Converting n to u128 before multiplication will avoid potential runtime overflow.  
    • The doc-comment mentions Fibonacci, but the specification is explicitly about triangular numbers; the implementation must follow the spec, not the comment.

A fully verified body can be as small as:
```
fn fib(n: u64) -> (x: u128)
    requires n < 100000, n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX,
    ensures x == n * (n + 1) / 2
{
    (n as u128) * ((n + 1) as u128) / 2
}
```
This satisfies all contractual obligations and will be accepted by the Verus verifier.
>>>

# Reasoning
We want to keep the original spirit (linear scan over a `Vec<char>`) but make the proof slightly more intricate.  
Typical ways to do that are

• Make the **sought‐for element depend on its neighbourhood** (e.g. the character right before it must satisfy something).  
• Make the **property depend on a prefix aggregate** (parity / count up to the chosen index).  
• Make the **choice break ties** in a non-trivial way (longest run, earliest longest run, etc.).  

All proposals below:
– keep the single immutable input `a: &Vec<char>`;  
– keep the same easy length bound so every index fits in `i32`;  
– still return one `i32`, but the post-conditions are now noticeably richer, forcing more reasoning in both implementation and verification.

Signature

Inputs

Pre-condition (requires)

        • –1 when no such pattern occurs.

   (Meaning: `c` is the *first* index whose preceding element is a space and whose own element is ‘e’.)  
3. If c == –1 then  
       ∀x:int. 1 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ ¬(a[x] == 'e' ∧ a[x-1] == ' ').  
   (No space-followed ‘e’ exists anywhere.)

The body may be any algorithm whose asymptotic time is O(a.len()).

Definitions

Output
