              a[i] == 'e'  
              and the **number of lowercase vowels in the prefix a[0..=i] is even**, or  
        • –1 when no such index exists.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. ‑1 ≤ c < a.len() as i32.  
2. If 0 ≤ c < a.len() then  
       a[c] == 'e' ∧  
       (|{ j | 0 ≤ j ≤ c ∧ a[j] ∈ vowels }| mod 2 == 0) ∧  
       ∀x:int. 0 ≤ x < c ⇒  
           ¬(a[x] == 'e' ∧ (|{ j | 0 ≤ j ≤ x ∧ a[j] ∈ vowels }| mod 2 == 0)).  
   (So `c` is the first ‘e’ whose prefix has an even vowel count.)  
3. If c == –1 then  
       ∀x:int. 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒  
           ¬(a[x] == 'e' ∧ (|{ j | 0 ≤ j ≤ x ∧ a[j] ∈ vowels }| mod 2 == 0)).

An O(n) single-pass implementation is acceptable; no auxiliary storage beyond a few counters is required.

<<<
Design and verify a function in Verus/Rust named `first_of_longest_e_run`.

Signature  
    fn first_of_longest_e_run(a: &Vec<char>) -> (c: i32)

Inputs  
    a – immutable reference to a vector of Rust `char`.

Pre-condition (requires)  
    a.len() < 1_000_000

Output  
    c – a 32-bit signed integer that is either  
        • the first index of the **earliest longest contiguous block** of one or more consecutive ‘e’ characters, or  
        • –1 when the vector contains no ‘e’ at all.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. ‑1 ≤ c < a.len() as i32.  
2. If 0 ≤ c < a.len() then  
       a[c] == 'e' ∧  
       let L = { ℓ:int | ∃s:int. 0 ≤ s < a.len() ∧  
                             ∀k:int. 0 ≤ k < ℓ ⇒ s+k < a.len() ∧ a[s+k] == 'e' ∧  
                             (s+ℓ == a.len() ∨ a[s+ℓ] ≠ 'e') }  
       in  
          ℓ_max = max L ∧                     // length of the longest run of 'e's  
          ∃s:int. s == c ∧                    // our run starts at c  
                   (∀k:int. 0 ≤ k < ℓ_max ⇒ a[s+k] == 'e') ∧  
                   (s+ℓ_max == a.len() ∨ a[s+ℓ_max] ≠ 'e') ∧  
                   ∀t:int. t < s ⇒            // tie-breaking: earliest such run  
                        ¬(∀k:int. 0 ≤ k < ℓ_max ⇒ t+k < a.len() ∧ a[t+k] == 'e' ∧
                                                  (t+ℓ_max == a.len() ∨ a[t+ℓ_max] ≠ 'e')).  
3. If c == –1 then  
       ∀x:int. 0 ≤ x < a.len() ⇒ a[x] ≠ 'e'.

The reference solution must still run in O(a.len()) time and O(1) extra space, yet a proof must analyse two passes’ worth of reasoning: one to compute maximum run length, one to pick the earliest such run.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn bijectivity_implies_equal_cardinality(set_a: Vec<u64>, set_b: Vec<u64>) -> (equal: bool)
    requires
        set_a.len() > 0,
        set_a.len() == set_b.len(),
        set_a.len() < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        equal == true,
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification is extremely simple:

1. Function name and intention  
   - bijectivity_implies_equal_cardinality — the name evokes set theory, but the spec itself does not attempt to model any mapping between the two collections.  The only thing it demands is that, once the pre-conditions hold, the function must return true.

2. Inputs  
   - set_a: Vec<u64> (passed by value, not by reference)  
   - set_b: Vec<u64> (also passed by value)  
   Although the identifiers suggest mathematical sets, nothing in the spec enforces uniqueness of the stored elements; the vectors may contain duplicates.  The body is free to drop or move the vectors; it only has to observe their lengths to satisfy the pre-conditions if it wants, but it is not required to read the data at all.

3. Output  
   - equal: bool — the single return variable.

4. Preconditions (requires)  
   - set_a.len() > 0 — the first vector must be non-empty.  
   - set_a.len() == set_b.len() — both vectors must have exactly the same length.  
   - set_a.len() < 1_000_000 — an upper bound introduced merely to rule out oversized inputs and eliminate any risk of arithmetic overflow when working with the lengths inside the body (should you do so).

   These three conjuncts together describe the admissible inputs.  If any of them is violated, the function’s behaviour is undefined from the verifier’s point of view.

5. Postcondition (ensures)  
   - equal == true — under the pre-conditions the function must always return the Boolean value true.  No further relation between the output and the contents or lengths of the vectors is required.

6. Consequences for an implementation  
   - Because the ensures clause fixes the result to true, the executable body can be as small as `true` (converted to the named return variable) or something equally trivial.  
   - The vectors are owned by the function, but since they are never mentioned in the post-condition, they can be dropped immediately.  
   - No auxiliary spec functions, lemmas, ghost variables, or arithmetic reasoning are necessary.

# Final Description
<<<
Create a verified Verus-Rust function with the following specification:

Signature  
    fn bijectivity_implies_equal_cardinality(set_a: Vec<u64>, set_b: Vec<u64>) -> (equal: bool)

Inputs  
    • set_a — an owned vector of 64-bit unsigned integers.  
    • set_b — another owned vector of 64-bit unsigned integers.  

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. set_a.len() > 0  (the first vector must not be empty)  
    2. set_a.len() == set_b.len()  (the two vectors must have identical length)  
    3. set_a.len() < 1_000_000  (upper bound to avoid overflow; any value strictly below one million is allowed)

Output  
    • equal — a Boolean value.

Post-condition (ensures)  
    equal == true  
In other words, provided the three pre-conditions hold, the function is obligated to return the constant value true.  No other relationship between the vectors and the result is imposed.

Implementation guidance  
Because the spec fixes the return value, a minimal correct body is simply `true` (or `let equal = true; equal`).  The vectors can be ignored or immediately dropped; they are not referenced in the ensures clause.  No ghost code, helper specifications, or proofs are necessary beyond satisfying the stated contracts.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is a straightforward element-wise absolute-value conversion that can be discharged with a single `while` loop and a trivial invariant.  
To raise the difficulty, we can introduce specifications that

• require auxiliary reasoning such as prefix-sums, ordering constraints, or index bookkeeping;  
• involve multiple quantified properties instead of one;  
• replace the “absolute value” theme with a related but less common operation (e.g. prefix sums of absolute values or producing a permutation that is sorted by absolute value);  
• keep all requirements *executable* so that learners must furnish both proof and code.

Below are three harder, self-contained tasks that satisfy those goals.

# Output
<<<
You must create two items in Verus-Rust.

1. A pure specification helper:

    spec fn abs(a: int) -> u64

    Definition:
        if a < 0 { (-a) as u64 } else { a as u64 }

2. A verified, executable function:

    fn prefix_abs_sums(a: &[i32]) -> (b: Vec<u64>)

    Inputs  
        • a – an immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers.

    Output  
        • b – a heap-allocated vector of 64-bit unsigned integers.

    Preconditions (requires)  
        • true.

    Postconditions (ensures)  
        1. Length preservation: b.len() == a.len()  
        2. Prefix-sum definition:  
               ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len()
                   ⇒ b[i] ==
                      (∑ j:int • 0 ≤ j ≤ i ⇒ abs(a[j] as int))
        3. Monotonic growth:  
               ∀ i:int • 1 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ b[i-1] ≤ b[i]
        4. Non-overflow guarantee:  
               ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ b[i] ≤ u64::MAX

The function must compute, for each index i, the sum of the absolute values of elements a[0] … a[i] and store it in b[i]; proofs must establish all four properties.

Design and verify a function that performs a classic linear search over a runtime slice, returning the position of the first occurrence of a given value or the length of the slice if the value is absent.

1. Helper specification function
   –  name: array_to_seq  
   –  signature: `open spec fn array_to_seq(a: &[int]) -> Seq<int>`  
   –  purpose: present the contents of the concrete slice `a` as an immutable mathematical sequence `Seq<int>`.  
   –  definition: `array_to_seq(a) == a@.map(|_index, n:int| n)` (simply copies each element).  
   –  trusted: marked `open` so it can be used in triggers.

2. Primary executable function
   –  name: `linear_search`  
   –  signature: `pub fn linear_search(a: &[int], e: int) -> (n: usize)`  

3. Inputs
   • `a`: an immutable runtime slice whose elements have type `int`.  
   • `e`: the integer value being searched for.

4. Output
   • `n`: a `usize` index that locates the first occurrence of `e` in `a`; if `e` does not appear, `n` equals `a.len()` (the customary “not-found” sentinel).

5. Preconditions (requires)
   • `a.len() > 0` — the slice must be non-empty (simplifies reasoning about the first element; an implementation is free to relax this in the future).

6. Postconditions (ensures)
   a)  Index range:
        `0 ≤ n ≤ a.len()`  (expressed as `0 <= n as int && n as int <= a.len()` in Verus).  
        The result is never negative and never exceeds the length.

   b)  Correctness of the returned position:
        `n == a.len()` OR `array_to_seq(a)[n] == e`.  
        In words, either `n` is exactly the length of the slice (meaning “not found”), or the element stored at position `n` equals the sought value.

   c)  Minimality / first occurrence:
        `∀ i • 0 ≤ i < n ⇒ array_to_seq(a)[i] ≠ e`.  
        No index strictly smaller than `n` holds the searched value; thus, if `e` is present, `n` is its very first appearance.

These three clauses together fully characterise a conventional linear search that stops at the first match and otherwise scans the whole slice.

7. Implementation freedom
Any executable body that provably respects the contracts is acceptable, e.g., a simple loop that compares each element with `e`. The helper `array_to_seq` and the quantified post-conditions give enough information for automated verification.

With this description a developer can recreate the exact Verus specification from scratch.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

spec fn c(n: nat) -> nat {
    n * (n + 1) / 2
}

fn calc_c(n: u64) -> (res: u64)
    requires
        n >= 0,
        n < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        res == c(n as nat),
{
```

# Reasoning
We need to write a problem statement describing:

1. A specification-only function `c` that takes a natural number `n` and returns the triangular number n*(n+1)/2. Its purpose is purely logical; it will never be executed.

2. An executable, verified function `calc_c`.
   * Input: one 64-bit unsigned integer `n`.
   * Output: one 64-bit unsigned integer `res`.
   * Preconditions:
        - `n >= 0`.  (Redundant for `u64` but written explicitly.)
        - `n < 1_000_000`.  A pragmatic upper bound that helps prove the arithmetic is safe.
        - `n*(n+1)/2 < u64::MAX`.  Guarantees that the mathematical triangular number fits into `u64`, so the implementation can compute it with no overflow.
   * Post-condition: `res` must equal `c(n as nat)`.  `n as nat` casts the runtime value to Verus’s logical natural-number domain, then the helper `c` is applied.  Thus after the call, `res` is exactly the triangular number of `n`.

We must emphasise that:
• `c` works in the mathematical `nat` domain (unbounded).
• Arithmetic in the spec is exact; the executable code must adhere to the overflow-free range promised by the `requires` clauses.
• There are no other helpers, ghost variables, or decreasing clauses.

# Final Description
<<<
Create two items in Verus-Rust.

1. Helper specification function

spec fn c(n: nat) -> nat

Purpose: given a non-negative integer n, return the n-th triangular number, defined as n · (n + 1) ⁄ 2.  This function is purely logical, runs only in verification, and uses mathematical naturals (`nat`).

Exact definition:
    c(n) = n * (n + 1) / 2

2. Verified executable function

fn calc_c(n: u64) -> (res: u64)

Inputs
    n : u64 — any 64-bit unsigned integer satisfying the preconditions below.

Outputs
    res : u64 — the computed triangular number of n.

Preconditions (requires)
    1. n >= 0                         // explicit although true for all u64

# Reasoning
The original exercise is rather short-lived: a single loop, three simple post-conditions, and only one integer result.  To raise the bar I introduce new functional goals that force the student to reason about  
• several different return values,  
• universal and existential quantifiers that interact,  
• side constraints on the data (sortedness, positivity, disjoint indices, …), and  
• in two variants, logarithmic-time behaviour (binary search) which typically demands an auxiliary loop invariant over a variable-size search interval.

For each harder variant I add a small helper `open spec fn`, so that the task remains self-contained and the prospective spec-generator has all mathematical building blocks it needs.

I provide three completely standalone problem statements, each enclosed in its own `<<< … >>>` block, ready for feeding into an automatic Verus spec generator.


# Output
<<<
Design and verify a function that, in a **sorted (non-decreasing)** slice, returns the index of the *first* element that is **strictly greater** than a given key.  If no such element exists the function must return the length of the slice.

1. Helper specification function  
   – name: `array_to_seq`  
   – signature: `open spec fn array_to_seq(a: &[int]) -> Seq<int>`  
   – purpose: presents the contents of the concrete slice `a` as an immutable mathematical sequence.  
   – definition: `array_to_seq(a) == a@.map(|_i, n:int| n)`.

2. Primary executable function  
   – name: `upper_bound`  
   – signature: `pub fn upper_bound(a: &[int], key: int) -> (idx: usize)`

3. Inputs  
   • `a`: immutable runtime slice of `int`s, *sorted in non-decreasing order*.  
   • `key`: an integer value.

4. Output  
   • `idx`: the smallest index such that `array_to_seq(a)[idx] > key`; if every element is `≤ key`, `idx` equals `a.len()`.

5. Preconditions (requires)  
   • `// sortedness`  
     `forall |i:int, j:int| 0 <= i < j < a.len() ==> array_to_seq(a)[i] <= array_to_seq(a)[j]`.

6. Postconditions (ensures)  
   a) Index range: `0 <= idx as int && idx as int <= a.len()`.  
   b) If `idx < a.len()` then the returned element indeed exceeds the key: `array_to_seq(a)[idx] > key`.  
   c) Minimality: for all `i` with `0 <= i < idx`, `array_to_seq(a)[i] <= key`.  
   d) If *every* element is ≤ `key` then `idx == a.len()`.  Written equivalently:  
      `idx == a.len() <==> forall |i:int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> array_to_seq(a)[i] <= key`.

The implementation is expected to run in `O(log n)` time (a classical binary search), though any body that the verifier can prove to satisfy the contract is acceptable.
>>>

<<<
Create and verify a function that simultaneously returns (1) the first position of a target value in a slice and (2) *how many times* that value occurs.

1. Helper specification function  
   – name: `array_to_seq`  
   – signature: `open spec fn array_to_seq(a: &[int]) -> Seq<int>`  
   – definition: `array_to_seq(a) == a@.map(|_i, n:int| n)`.

2. Primary executable function  
   – name: `first_and_count`  
   – signature: `pub fn first_and_count(a: &[int], target: int) -> (first: usize, count: usize)`

3. Inputs  
   • `a`: immutable slice of `int`s (may be empty).  
   • `target`: the integer to look for.

4. Outputs  
   • `first`: the index of the *first* occurrence of `target`, or `a.len()` if absent.  
   • `count`: the total number of indices `i` with `array_to_seq(a)[i] == target`.

5. Preconditions  
   (none)

6. Postconditions  
   a) Index bounds: `0 <= first as int && first as int <= a.len()`.  
   b) Count bounds: `count as int >= 0 && count as int <= a.len()`.  
   c) Correctness of `first`:  
      `first == a.len() || array_to_seq(a)[first] == target`.  
   d) Minimality of `first`:  
      `forall |i:int| 0 <= i < first ==> array_to_seq(a)[i] != target`.  
   e) Correctness of `count`:  
      `count as int == (sum |i:int| 0 <= i < a.len() && array_to_seq(a)[i] == target)`.  
      (The summation denotes counting via comprehension.)  
   f) Relationship between the two results:  
      `count == 0 <==> first == a.len()`;  
      if `count > 0` then it is guaranteed that `first < a.len()`.

The student must devise a proof that relates the loop which scans the slice exactly once to the two coupled postconditions, making the exercise significantly more intricate than a plain linear search.
>>>

<<<
Specify and verify a function that scans a slice of **non-negative** integers and returns the *smallest* index whose prefix-sum is **strictly greater** than a given threshold.  If even the complete slice sums to `≤ threshold`, the function must return the slice length.

1. Helper specification functions  
   • name: `array_to_seq`  
     signature: `open spec fn array_to_seq(a: &[int]) -> Seq<int>`  
     definition: `array_to_seq(a) == a@.map(|_i, n:int| n)`.  

   • name: `prefix_sum`  
     signature: `open spec fn prefix_sum(s: Seq<int>, k: int) -> int`  
     definition: `prefix_sum(s, k) == (sum |i:int| 0 <= i < k ==> s[i])`.  
     (Returns 0 when `k ≤ 0`.)

2. Primary executable function  
   – name: `first_prefix_exceeding`  
   – signature: `pub fn first_prefix_exceeding(a: &[int], threshold: int) -> (idx: usize)`

3. Inputs  
   • `a`: immutable slice with `forall |i:int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> array_to_seq(a)[i] >= 0`.  
   • `threshold`: an arbitrary integer (may be negative).

4. Output  
   • `idx`: the smallest index such that `prefix_sum(array_to_seq(a), idx + 1) > threshold`; if no such index exists, `idx == a.len()`.

5. Preconditions  
   • `threshold < int::MAX`  (avoids overflow when adding non-negative elements).

6. Postconditions  
   a) Range: `0 <= idx as int && idx as int <= a.len()`.  
   b) If `idx < a.len()` then  
        `prefix_sum(array_to_seq(a), idx + 1) > threshold`.  
   c) Minimality:  
        `forall |j:int| 0 <= j < idx ==> prefix_sum(array_to_seq(a), j + 1) <= threshold`.  
   d) If the entire sum remains below or equal to the threshold then `idx == a.len()`.

Because reasoning about an incrementally maintained running sum is trickier than checking single elements, the learner must craft a loop invariant that relates the current index, the running total, and the quantified post-conditions – a distinct step-up in difficulty over the original task.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
#![allow(unused_imports)]
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn compute_fact(n: u64) -> (f: u64)
    requires
        n >= 0,
        n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX, // added check to prevent overflow
    ensures
        f == n * (n + 1) / 2,
{
```

# Reasoning
The specification is for a single, executable Verus/Rust function named `compute_fact`.  
Below is a line-by-line explanation of everything that appears in the contract so that a reader could recreate it from scratch.

1.  Function signature  
    • `fn compute_fact(n: u64) -> (f: u64)`  
      – The function is public inside the current module (no visibility modifier is given).  
      – It takes one run-time argument, `n`, whose concrete type is `u64` (an unsigned, 64-bit machine integer).  
      – It returns a single value, bound to the out-variable name `f`, also of type `u64`.

2.  Preconditions (`requires`)  
    These are logical conditions that must hold on entry; the implementation may assume them.  
    a) `n >= 0`  
       • Redundant for `u64` (every `u64` is non-negative), but written explicitly.  
    b) `n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX`  
       • `u64::MAX` is the compile-time constant `2⁶⁴ − 1`.  
       • Integer division of that constant by itself yields the constant `1`, so the clause is equivalent to `n < 1`.  
       • Together with unsignedness this restricts the domain to exactly one admissible run-time value: `n == 0`.  
    c) `n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX`  
       • Expressed in the `u64` arithmetic domain.  
       • Guarantees the triangular number `n(n+1)/2` does not overflow the `u64` range when it is computed in the implementation body.

3.  Post-condition (`ensures`)  
    `f == n * (n + 1) / 2`  
    • After the function returns, the ghost view of the return variable `f` must be exactly the triangular number of `n`.  
    • Because both sides are `u64`, the equation has to hold in normal modular arithmetic, but the precondition guarantees no wrap-around can happen.

4.  Ghost helpers / other spec artifacts  
    • None are used—all reasoning is done directly in the contract.

5.  Behavioral summary  
    If `n` satisfies the three pre-conditions, the function must return the unsigned 64-bit integer equal to `n(n+1)/2`.  
    Given the second precondition (`n < 1`), the only concrete input that meets the contract is `n = 0`, and thus the only admissible output is `f = 0`.  Nevertheless, the specification is written in general algebraic form and could be relaxed later by deleting or changing the second `requires` clause.

6.  Implementation freedom  
    • The body can be as small as `return n * (n + 1) / 2;` but any provably equivalent code is allowed.  
    • The verifier will statically confirm that the multiplication followed by the addition and division stays inside bounds, courtesy of the third pre-condition.


# Final Description (put your answer between the special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Design a verified function in Verus named `compute_fact` having the following complete contract:

Function signature  
    fn compute_fact(n: u64) -> (f: u64)

Arguments  
    n : u64 – an unsigned 64-bit integer supplied by the caller.

Return value  
    f : u64 – the function’s single numeric result.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. n ≥ 0.  
       – Included explicitly even though every u64 already satisfies it.  
    2. n < u64::MAX / u64::MAX.  
       – Since u64::MAX / u64::MAX equals 1, this is equivalent to n < 1, i.e. the only legal concrete input is n == 0.  
       – The clause is present to keep later arithmetic well-defined and overflow-free.  
    3. n * (n + 1) / 2 < u64::MAX.  
       – Ensures that computing the triangular number for n does not overflow the 64-bit range.

Post-condition (ensures)  
    f == n * (n + 1) / 2  
    – Upon return, the value stored in `f` must equal the triangular number of n (the sum of the first n natural numbers).

No auxiliary specification functions or lemmas are needed; all reasoning is contained in the contract itself.

In summary: when the caller provides a u64 argument n that meets the three requires-clauses (effectively n == 0), the function must return, as a u64, the exact value n(n+1)/2.  Any implementation that the verifier can prove satisfies these constraints is acceptable.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: copy the first element of every inner vector into a new vector.  
To make the assignment more challenging while still expressible as a Verus-Rust spec, I add requirements that force the student to:

1. Reason about *relations among different inner vectors* (e.g. distinctness or monotonicity of the extracted elements).
2. Maintain one or more *derived values* (e.g. prefix sums) that depend on *all* preceding elements, not only the current one.
3. Handle an *extra parameter* (e.g. an index `k`) whose legality must be verified against *every* inner vector.

Each new problem is self-contained, includes complete inputs/outputs, precise `requires`/`ensures`, and is only ~10–20 words longer than the original.  No body implementation details are leaked; only the specification is described.  This should demand deeper reasoning, quantified properties, and inductive proofs from the learner.

# Reasoning
The original task (“return the maximum element in a non-empty slice of `int`s”) is straightforward: a single loop, one invariant, two postconditions.  
To raise the level of difficulty I will:

1. Require additional results (index, count, or second-max) so that the proof must relate *several* returned values.
2. Introduce “tie-breaking” rules (e.g., **left-most** index of the max) that force quantifier reasoning about *which* occurrence is chosen.
3. Demand properties about the relationship between returned values (e.g., `second_max < max`) or about the *absence* of larger values anywhere else.
4. Keep the element type `int` and slices so that memory-safety issues stay irrelevant; complexity remains linear.

Below are three harder, stand-alone specifications written in natural language detailed enough for a Verus spec generator.

# Output
<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function signature  
    fn max_with_leftmost_index_and_count(a: &[int]) -> (max: int, idx: nat, occ: nat)

Inputs  
    • a — immutable slice of unbounded `int`s whose length is strictly positive.

Outputs  
    • max — the largest element in the slice.  
    • idx — the smallest index at which that largest element occurs.  
    • occ — the total number of times `max` occurs in the slice.

Precondition  
    • a.len() > 0.

Postconditions  
    1. 0 ≤ idx < a.len() ∧ max == a[idx]  
       (Returned index indeed points at a copy of `max`.)  
    2. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ max ≥ a[k]  
       (`max` is greater than or equal to every element.)  
    3. ∀ k:int. 0 ≤ k < idx ⇒ a[k] < max  
       (Left-most tie-breaking: there is **no earlier** element equal to `max`.)  
    4. occ == |{ k | 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ a[k] == max }|  
       (Returned count equals the exact number of occurrences.)  
    5. occ ≥ 1  
       (At least one occurrence exists by definition.)

No helper lemmas are provided; the implementation may iterate once while maintaining `(current_max, first_idx, occurrences)` invariants.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function signature  
    fn two_distinct_largest(a: &[int]) -> (max: int, second_max: int)

Inputs  
    • a — immutable slice of `int`s of length at least two.

Outputs  
    • max — the greatest element of the slice.  
    • second_max — the greatest element that is **strictly smaller** than `max`.

Preconditions  
    1. a.len() ≥ 2.  
    2. ∃ i j. 0 ≤ i < j < a.len() ∧ a[i] ≠ a[j]  
       (The slice is **not** uniform; at least two distinct values exist.)

Postconditions  
    1. ∃ k. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ max == a[k].  
    2. ∃ k. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ second_max == a[k].  
    3. max > second_max.  
    4. ∀ k. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ⇒ max ≥ a[k] ≥ second_max  
       (All elements lie between `max` and `second_max` inclusive.)  
    5. ∀ k. 0 ≤ k < a.len() ∧ a[k] < max ⇒ a[k] ≤ second_max  
       (`second_max` is the greatest value strictly below `max`.)

An implementation using a single pass that keeps both largest and runner-up values satisfies these specifications; careful reasoning is required to prove Postconditions 4–5.
>>>

<<<
Design and verify a Verus-Rust function with the following contract.

Function signature  
    fn max_in_matrix(m: &[&[int]]) -> (max: int, row: nat, col: nat)

Inputs  
    • m — slice of row slices; each inner slice is non-empty and all rows have identical length (rectangular matrix).

Outputs  
    • max — the largest integer in the entire matrix.  
    • row — the row index where `max` first appears in row-major order.  
    • col — the corresponding column index.

Preconditions  
    1. m.len() > 0.  
    2. ∀ r:nat. r < m.len() ⇒ m[r].len() > 0  
       (Every row is non-empty.)  
    3. ∀ r1 r2:nat. r1 < m.len() ∧ r2 < m.len() ⇒ m[r1].len() == m[r2].len()  
       (All rows have the same length.)

Postconditions  
    1. 0 ≤ row < m.len() ∧ 0 ≤ col < m[0].len().  
    2. max == m[row][col].  
    3. ∀ r c. 0 ≤ r < m.len() ∧ 0 ≤ c < m[0].len() ⇒ max ≥ m[r][c].  
    4. ∀ r c. (r, c) comes **earlier** than (row, col) in row-major order  
       ⇒ m[r][c] < max  
       (The reported coordinates are the earliest occurrence of the maximal value.)

Row-major ordering means `(r1, c1)` is earlier than `(r2, c2)` iff `r1 < r2 ∨ (r1 == r2 ∧ c1 < c2)`.

No auxiliary lemmas are supplied; the proof must simultaneously track maximum value and its earliest 2-D position through nested iteration.
>>>

```rust
use builtin::*;
use builtin_macros::*;
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn increment_array(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
        a.len() < 1000,
        forall|i: int| a[i as int] + 1 >= i32::MIN && a[i as int] + 1 <= i32::MAX,
    ensures
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> result[i as int] == a[i as int] + 1,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We need to digest exactly what the specification says so we can rewrite it unambiguously in prose.

1. Function name and kind
   • increment_array is an executable (non-ghost) Verus function.

2. Inputs
   • a: Vec<i32> – an owned, heap-allocated vector whose elements are 32-bit signed integers.  
     The vector is moved into the function; the caller relinquishes ownership.

3. Output
   • result: Vec<i32> – a fresh vector of 32-bit signed integers produced by the function and returned to the caller.

4. Preconditions (requires)
   1. a.len() > 0  
      The incoming vector must be non-empty.
   2. a.len() < 1000  
      Its length is strictly less than 1000.  (So 1 ≤ len ≤ 999.)
   3. ∀ i:int. a[i] + 1 is still inside the i32 range  
      Concretely, for every logical index i (implicitly meant to be within bounds),  
      i32::MIN ≤ a[i] + 1 ≤ i32::MAX.  
      This guarantees that incrementing any element by one cannot overflow a 32-bit signed integer.  
      (Because Verus’s integer reasoning is unbounded, we need this assumption so that the eventual executable addition in Rust is also safe.)

5. Postcondition (ensures)
   • ∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[i] + 1  
     After the call, at every position i that originally existed in a, the element stored in the same position of result equals the original element incremented by one.

   Note that the spec does not explicitly state `result.len() == a.len()`, but that fact follows from the point-wise equality over the whole valid index range.  An implementation will need to return a vector of the same length; otherwise it could not satisfy the quantified equality.

6. Auxiliary items
   • No helper spec functions, lemmas, or decreases clauses are involved.

7. Informal interpretation
   Given a non-empty vector shorter than one thousand elements, whose values can be safely incremented, return a new vector of the same size where every element is exactly one greater than the corresponding element of the input.

8. Obligations for an implementation
   • Must construct a vector of identical length.  
   • Each entry must be `old_value + 1`.  
   • Must avoid run-time overflow (already ensured by the precondition).  
   • Any algorithm that satisfies the contract is acceptable (e.g., allocate a fresh vector and push values, or mutate `a` in place and return it).

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Write a formally-verified Verus/Rust function called `increment_array`.

Signature  
    fn increment_array(a: Vec<i32>) -> (result: Vec<i32>)

Inputs  
  • a — an owned `Vec<i32>` (heap vector of 32-bit signed integers).

Outputs  
  • result — a new `Vec<i32>` that will be returned to the caller.

Preconditions (requires)  
 1. `a.len() > 0`  // the incoming vector is non-empty.  
 2. `a.len() < 1000` // its length is strictly less than 1000.  
 3. `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ i32::MIN ≤ a[i] + 1 ≤ i32::MAX`  
    Every element can be safely incremented by one without leaving the legal
    32-bit signed range; this rules out overflow in the executable code.

Postcondition (ensures)  
  • `∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ result[i] == a[i] + 1`  
    For every valid index of the original vector, the corresponding element of
    the returned vector equals the original element plus one.  
    Consequently, `result.len() == a.len()` as well.

Behavioural summary  
Given any non-empty vector shorter than one thousand elements, return a new
vector of the same length where each entry is exactly one greater than the
entry at the same position in the input.  No auxiliary specification functions
or lemmas are required; a straightforward loop that allocates a fresh vector or
mutates the input in place (then returns it) will satisfy the contract as long
as it preserves the point-wise `+1` relationship and respects the overflow
guarantee.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task only asks the learner to specify “the length of the longest common prefix of two slices.”  
That can be proved with three very direct conditions.  
To make the exercise more challenging I designed three *stand-alone* alternative specs, each of which introduces at least one of the following:

• an additional output value that has to stay consistent with the main result,  
• a less common arithmetic property (e.g. parity, divisibility) that must be tracked in the spec,  
• extra conditional reasoning (different behaviours in mutually-exclusive cases), or  
• the need to relate **three** quantities instead of two.

Each problem description below is worded so that a spec-generator could translate it into a Verus‐style function signature together with `requires`/`ensures` clauses only from the English text.

# Output
<<<
Title: longest_even_divisible_prefix

Write the complete Verus specification (signature, `requires`, `ensures`) for a total function

    fn longest_even_divisible_prefix(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (len: usize)

that returns the length `len` of the longest common prefix of `a` and `b` **whose elements are all even and divisible by 4**.

Informal semantics that must be captured by your spec:

1. A *candidate prefix* is a length `k` such that  
   0 ≤ k ≤ min(a.len(), b.len()) and  
   for every index `j < k` we have a[j] == b[j] and a[j] mod 4 == 0.
2. `len` must be the greatest such `k`.
3. Standard upper-bound condition: `len ≤ a.len()` and `len ≤ b.len()`.
4. For every `j < len` we have a[j] == b[j] and a[j] mod 4 == 0.
5. If `len < a.len()` and `len < b.len()` then either a[len] ≠ b[len] **or** a[len] is not divisible by 4; in both cases the prefix cannot be extended.

No additional pre-conditions are allowed: the function must work for arbitrary slices (possibly empty).  Remember to keep the arithmetic in the spec at mathematical-`int` level, not at machine-`i32` level.
>>>

<<<
Title: first_mismatch_with_prefix_sum

Design a Verus specification for

    fn first_mismatch_with_prefix_sum(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (idx: usize, sum: int)

Task description:

• `idx` is the *smallest* index at which the two slices differ.  
• `sum` is the mathematical sum of all elements **of `a`** strictly before `idx`.

Formal obligations that must appear in the spec:

1. Bounds: idx ≤ a.len() and idx ≤ b.len().  
2. Agreement before the index: ∀ j < idx . a[j] == b[j].  
3. If idx < a.len() and idx < b.len() then a[idx] ≠ b[idx] (meaning a mismatch was found).  
4. If idx == a.len() or idx == b.len() then all elements up to `min(a.len(), b.len())` are equal, and the shorter slice ends exactly at `idx`.  
5. Definition of `sum`: `sum == Σ_{j=0}^{idx-1} a[j]`.  (For idx = 0 the sum is 0.)  
6. If idx == 0 then `sum == 0` by the above rule.

No pre-conditions.  You may use a builtin or helper lemma to define the summation, but its relationship to `sum` must be enforced by `ensures`.
>>>

<<<
Title: longest_strict_increasing_common_prefix

Create the specification for

    fn longest_strict_increasing_common_prefix(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (k: usize)

Behaviour to encode:

   a. ℓ ≤ a.len() and ℓ ≤ b.len();  
   b. the two slices are equal up to (but not including) ℓ;  
   c. and, when ℓ ≥ 2, the sequence a[0], …, a[ℓ-1] is *strictly increasing* (each next value is greater than the previous one).  (For ℓ ≤ 1 this part is vacuously true.)
2. `k` is the **maximum** valid ℓ.
3. Post-conditions must state:
   • upper-bound property as usual,  
   • equality of the two slices for all indices `< k`,  
   • strict-increasing witness for the prefix in `a` (and therefore also in `b`),  
   • if k < a.len() and k < b.len(), then either a[k] ≠ b[k] **or** the strict-increase would be violated at index k (i.e. a[k] ≤ a[k-1]).

No particular pre-conditions are allowed.  Use mathematical ordering on `int`, not machine overflow semantics.

```rust
        forall|k: int| 0 <= k && k < v.len() ==> v[i as int] >= v[k as int],
```

# Reasoning
The Verus item `mmaximum1` is an *executable* (non-`spec`) function whose purpose is to locate a maximum element in an immutable slice of signed machine integers (`int` in Verus/Rust terminology).

1. Inputs  
   • `v: &[int]` — a shared (read-only) slice of `int` values.  
   Slices carry both a data pointer and a runtime length; they borrow existing storage instead of owning it.

2. Output  
   • `i: usize` — a zero-based index into the same slice.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `v.len() > 0` — the slice must be non-empty so that a maximum element exists.  
   There are no other requirements: elements may be negative, positive, duplicated, etc.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   a) `0 <= i && i < v.len()` – the returned index is guaranteed to be in-bounds.  
   b) `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v.len() ⇒ v[i] ≥ v[k]` – the element at position `i` is *not smaller* than any other element in the slice.  
      In other words, `v[i]` is a maximum value of the collection (ties permitted).  
      Because quantification is written over mathematical `int`, both `i` and the bound variable `k` are cast to `int` when used as subscripts.

5. Uniqueness / tie-breaking  
   The spec does **not** require `i` to be the *first* or *only* index whose value is maximal; any index whose value attains the maximum is acceptable.

6. Implementation freedom  
   Inside the function the programmer may iterate, use recursion, or call library helpers, as long as the verification tool can prove the above contract. Overflow is no issue because the computations in the ensures clause occur in unbounded `int`; the executable body, however, must respect machine limits if it performs arithmetic beyond comparisons.

# Final Description
<<<
Design a verified function named `mmaximum1` that, given a non-empty slice of `int`, returns the position of a maximum element.

Signature  
    fn mmaximum1(v: &[int]) -> (i: usize)

Contract  
• Requires  
    – `v.len() > 0` (the slice is non-empty).  

• Ensures  
    1. Index validity: `0 ≤ i < v.len()`.  
    2. Maximality: `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v.len() ⇒ v[i] ≥ v[k]`.  
       That is, the value stored at index `i` is at least as large as every other value in the slice (ties allowed).

Behavioural summary  
`mmaximum1` locates any occurrence of the largest element in the slice and returns its index. The function never mutates the slice, may choose any admissible index when duplicates exist, and is obligated only to satisfy the contract above. No additional helper functions or lemmas are mandated.
>>>

The Verus item `mmaximum1` is an *executable* (non-`spec`) function whose purpose is to locate a maximum element in an immutable slice of signed machine integers (`int` in Verus/Rust terminology).

1. Inputs  
   • `v: &[int]` — a shared (read-only) slice of `int` values.  
   Slices carry both a data pointer and a runtime length; they borrow existing storage instead of owning it.

2. Output  
   • `i: usize` — a zero-based index into the same slice.

3. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   • `v.len() > 0` — the slice must be non-empty so that a maximum element exists.  
   There are no other requirements: elements may be negative, positive, duplicated, etc.

4. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   a) `0 <= i && i < v.len()` – the returned index is guaranteed to be in-bounds.  
   b) `∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v.len() ⇒ v[i] ≥ v[k]` – the element at position `i` is *not smaller* than any other element in the slice.  
      In other words, `v[i]` is a maximum value of the collection (ties permitted).  
      Because quantification is written over mathematical `int`, both `i` and the bound variable `k` are cast to `int` when used as subscripts.

5. Uniqueness / tie-breaking  
   The spec does **not** require `i` to be the *first* or *only* index whose value is maximal; any index whose value attains the maximum is acceptable.

6. Implementation freedom  
   Inside the function the programmer may iterate, use recursion, or call library helpers, as long as the verification tool can prove the above contract. Overflow is no issue because the computations in the ensures clause occur in unbounded `int`; the executable body, however, must respect machine limits if it performs arithmetic beyond comparisons.

increasing integer slices into a single strictly increasing vector without duplicates.

Signature

Inputs  
 • `a` – immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers, already **strictly** increasing.  
 • `b` – another immutable slice of 32-bit signed integers, also **strictly** increasing.

Output

Formal requirements

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() + b.len() < i32::MAX as usize  
2. Strict monotonicity of both inputs  
        ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < a.len() ⇒ a[i] < a[i+1]  
        ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < b.len() ⇒ b[i] < b[i+1]

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. Strict monotonicity of the result  
        ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i + 1 < c.len() ⇒ c[i] < c[i+1]
2. Set equality (union without duplication)  
        ∀ v:int • (∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < c.len() ∧ c[i] == v)
                 ⇔ (∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] == v)
                    ∨ (∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < b.len() ∧ b[j] == v)
3. Length matches the cardinality of the union  
        c.len() == |{ v | ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ a[i] == v } ∪
                       { v | ∃ j:int • 0 ≤ j < b.len() ∧ b[j] == v }|

No auxiliary spec functions beyond those needed to express sets or multisets are forbidden;

Signature

Inputs

   Either slice may be shorter than the other.

Output  
 • `c` – an owned `Vec<i32>` whose first element is `a[0]`, the second is `b[0]`,
   the third is `a[1]`, the fourth is `b[1]`, and so on; once one slice is exhausted,
   the remainder of the longer slice appears unchanged at the end.

Formal requirements

Pre-condition (requires)  
    a.len() + b.len() < i32::MAX as usize

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. Length preservation

2. Alternating prefix

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

/// Function to calculate the quotient using division
fn int_div_quotient(a: u64, b: u64) -> (q: u64)
    requires
        a >= 0,
        b > 0,
        a < u64::MAX / b,
    ensures
        q == a / b,
{
```

# Reasoning
The verification contract is extremely small but still has a few points worth spelling out:

1. Inputs  
   • a: u64 – the dividend, an unsigned 64-bit integer.  
   • b: u64 – the divisor, also unsigned 64-bit.

2. Output  
   • q: u64 – the function’s sole return value, intended to be the quotient of the Euclidean integer division a / b carried out in the normal Rust sense for unsigned integers.

3. Preconditions (requires)  
   • a ≥ 0 – vacuously true for u64, but the spec lists it explicitly.  
   • b > 0 – the divisor must be strictly positive so that the division is defined.  
   • a < u64::MAX / b – an extra guard preventing potential overflow if an implementation later multiplies q by b or performs some other arithmetic; from the spec’s perspective it does not influence the meaning of the post-condition but it must be preserved.

4. Postcondition (ensures)  
   q == a / b – on return the result must equal Rust’s built-in integer division of a by b (i.e. the floor of the real quotient, because everything is unsigned and division is exact when a is a multiple of b).

5. Auxiliary spec items  
   None are referenced; the function is entirely characterised by the single post-condition.

Anyone re-implementing this function must therefore accept two u64s satisfying the pre-requirements and return the proper division quotient, proving that the returned value matches the built-in / operator.

# Final Description
<<<
Write a formally verified Verus/Rust function with the following contract:

Signature  
fn int_div_quotient(a: u64, b: u64) -> (q: u64)

Inputs  
• a – an unsigned 64-bit integer serving as the dividend.  
• b – an unsigned 64-bit integer serving as the divisor.

Output  
• q – an unsigned 64-bit integer that will be the quotient of a divided by b.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a ≥ 0 (trivially true for u64, but it is part of the contract).  
2. b > 0 the divisor must be strictly positive; division by zero is disallowed.  
3. a < u64::MAX / b a safety bound that can be used to avoid intermediate overflow in the implementation.

Post-condition (ensures)  
q == a / b  
—in other words, the value returned by the function must equal the result of Rust’s built-in unsigned integer division of a by b (the floor of the real quotient).

No helper specification functions, lemmas, or additional annotations are required. The implementer may compute the quotient in any verified way (calling the / operator directly, performing repeated subtraction, using bit-shifts, etc.) as long as the above contract is met and proven.
>>>

# Reasoning
The original task is straightforward: prove that the result of a point-wise addition of two matrices satisfies four simple shape- and value-based properties.  To increase the difficulty we can:

• Introduce an extra structure (e.g., an accompanying “checksum” vector) whose relationship to the matrix must also be preserved.  
• Require the learner to reason about two different operations at once (e.g., a *broadcast* addition combined with a row checksum).  
• Add universally-quantified properties that refer to derived values (row/column sums) in addition to the element-wise relation.  
• Keep everything proof-only and ghost-only so that the task is still about specification rather than executable code.

Below are three harder, self-contained specifications that follow those ideas.

# Output
<<<
Create a pure, proof-only Verus function named `broadcast_add_with_row_checksums` that *broadcast-adds* a column vector to every row of an integer matrix and simultaneously updates a “row checksum” vector.

Signature  
    proof fn broadcast_add_with_row_checksums(
        m: Seq<Seq<int>>,    // original matrix
        v: Seq<int>,         // column vector being broadcast
    ) -> (
        result_matrix: Seq<Seq<int>>,
        new_row_checksums: Seq<int>
    )

Inputs  
    m – a non-empty outer sequence whose elements are non-empty sequences of `int` (“rows”).  
    v – a non-empty sequence of `int` (“column vector”).

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. m is non-empty.  
2. Every row of m is non-empty and all rows have the *same* length.  
3. v.len() == m[0].len() (the vector length equals the number of columns).  

Auxiliary definition (informal)  
• For any row r: Seq<int>, define row_sum(r) = Σ_{j}<r.len()> r[j].

Outputs  
    result_matrix – the matrix obtained by adding v element-wise to each row of m.  
    new_row_checksums – a sequence containing the row sums of result_matrix.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. result_matrix.len() == m.len().  
2. For every valid row index i: result_matrix[i].len() == m[i].len().  
3. For all indices (i, j) within bounds:  
       result_matrix[i][j] == m[i][j] + v[j].  
4. new_row_checksums.len() == m.len().  
5. For every row index i:  
       new_row_checksums[i] == Σ_{j}<m[i].len()> result_matrix[i][j].  
6. For every row index i:  
       new_row_checksums[i] == row_sum(m[i]) + row_sum(v).  
   (Notice row_sum(v) is the same constant added to every row checksum.)

No additional helper lemmas are mandatory, but any ghost definitions required to express or prove the above properties may be provided.
>>>

<<<
Design a pure Verus proof function named `matrix_add_and_column_totals` that performs element-wise addition of two conforming integer matrices *and* returns a vector containing, for every column, the sum of that entire column in the resulting matrix.

Signature  
    proof fn matrix_add_and_column_totals(
        a: Seq<Seq<int>>,
        b: Seq<Seq<int>>
    ) -> (
        c: Seq<Seq<int>>,
        col_totals: Seq<int>
    )

Inputs  
    a, b – non-empty outer sequences of equal length; each inner sequence (row) is non-empty.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. a.len() == b.len().  
2. For all valid i: a[i].len() == b[i].len() > 0.  
3. All rows of a (and hence of b) have *identical* length (i.e., the matrix is rectangular).

Outputs  
    c – matrix representing the element-wise sum of a and b.  
    col_totals – a sequence containing, for each column j, Σ_{i}<a.len()> c[i][j].

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. c.len() == a.len().  
2. For every i: c[i].len() == a[i].len().  
3. ∀ i, j within bounds: c[i][j] == a[i][j] + b[i][j].  
4. col_totals.len() == a[0].len() (the number of columns).  
5. ∀ j within bounds:  
       col_totals[j] == Σ_{i}<a.len()> c[i][j].  
6. (Derived fact) ∀ j: col_totals[j] == Σ_{i}<a.len()> a[i][j] + Σ_{i}<b.len()> b[i][j].

No execution-time efficiency constraints are imposed, but the proof must justify every post-condition.
>>>

<<<
Write a pure Verus proof function called `tensor3_addition_preserve_depth_sums` that performs component-wise addition of two 3-dimensional integer tensors represented as `Seq<Seq<Seq<int>>>` and additionally returns a “depth-sum” sequence where each element is the total of an entire depth slice (i.e., a fixed outer-most index).

Signature

        x: Seq<Seq<Seq<int>>>,
        y: Seq<Seq<Seq<int>>>
    ) -> (
        z: Seq<Seq<Seq<int>>>,
        depth_sums: Seq<int>
    )

Inputs

Pre-conditions (requires)  
1. x.len() == y.len() > 0.  
2. For all depth indices d: x[d].len() == y[d].len() > 0.  
3. For all depth indices d and row indices r: x[d][r].len() == y[d][r].len() > 0.  

Outputs  
    z – tensor obtained by adding corresponding elements of x and y.  
    depth_sums – sequence where depth_sums[d] = Σ_{r,j}<shape> z[d][r][j].

Post-conditions (ensures)  
1. z has exactly the same 3-level shape as x and y.  
2. ∀ d, r, j within bounds: z[d][r][j] == x[d][r][j] + y[d][r][j].  
3. depth_sums.len() == x.len().  
4. ∀ d within bounds:  
       depth_sums[d] == Σ_{r,j}<shape> z[d][r][j].  
5. ∀ d: depth_sums[d] == Σ_{r,j}<shape> x[d][r][j] + Σ_{r,j}<shape> y[d][r][j].  
   (The sum for each depth slice equals the sum of the slices being added.)

Ghost helper functions to define multi-index summations are permitted.
>>>

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn get_even(a: Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)
    requires
        a.len() < 1000000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < a.len() ==> a[i] < u32::MAX - 1,
    ensures
        result.len() == a.len(),
        forall|i: int| 0 <= i < result.len() ==> result[i] % 2 == 0,
{
```

# Reasoning (you output)
We must describe the contract of `get_even` in natural language so that someone can reconstruct it precisely:

1. Function name & signature  
   - `fn get_even(a: Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)`  
     • The function takes ownership of an input vector `a` of 32-bit unsigned integers.  
     • It returns (moves out) another vector, labelled `result`, also of `u32`.

2. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a. `a.len() < 1_000_000`  
      – The caller is only allowed to pass vectors containing strictly fewer than one million elements.  
   b. `∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] < u32::MAX – 1`  
      – Every element already stored in `a` must be at most `u32::MAX − 2`.  
      – Together these two assumptions usually guarantee that any “+1” or “+2” arithmetic an implementation might perform on each element will never overflow 32-bit unsigned arithmetic.

3. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   a. `result.len() == a.len()`  
      – The output vector must have exactly the same length as the input vector.  
   b. `∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < result.len() ⇒ result[i] % 2 == 0`  
      – Every element placed in `result` must be an even number (its remainder modulo 2 is zero).

4. Missing obligations  
   Notice that the spec does *not* impose any relationship between the elements of the input and the elements of the output besides the length: the implementer is free to construct any even numbers they like (for example, all zeros, or “round each input up to the next even value”, etc.) as long as the two postconditions are satisfied.

5. Subtleties  
   • The vector is passed by value, not by reference. The caller loses ownership of `a`; the function may destroy or ignore it.  
   • All arithmetic in the specification is logical `int` arithmetic, but inside the body an implementation must still be careful about `u32` overflow. The value-range precondition makes this straightforward.  
   • The `len() < 1_000_000` bound is unrelated to functional correctness; it is there only to simplify reasoning about potential resource or arithmetic limits.

# Final Description (you output, putting your answer between special tags so it can be parsed)
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

Signature  
    fn get_even(a: Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)

Inputs  
    • a : Vec<u32> — the function takes ownership of a heap-allocated vector whose elements are 32-bit unsigned integers.

Preconditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() < 1_000_000  
       The vector must contain strictly fewer than one million elements.  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] < u32::MAX − 1  
       Every individual element is at most `4294967294` (`u32::MAX − 1`).  
       This guarantees that adding 1 or 2 to any element cannot overflow a `u32`.

Outputs  
    • result : Vec<u32> — another vector of 32-bit unsigned integers produced by the function.

Postconditions (ensures)  
    1. result.len() == a.len()  
       The returned vector must have exactly the same length as the input vector.  
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < result.len() ⇒ result[i] mod 2 = 0  
       Every element in the returned vector must be an even number; its remainder when divided by 2 is zero.

No other relationships between `a` and `result` are required. Any implementation that, for every legal call, produces a vector of the same length in which all values are even, satisfies the specification.
>>>

We must describe the contract of `get_even` in natural language so that someone can reconstruct it precisely:

1. Function name & signature  
   - `fn get_even(a: Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)`  
     • The function takes ownership of an input vector `a` of 32-bit unsigned integers.  
     • It returns (moves out) another vector, labelled `result`, also of `u32`.

2. Preconditions (`requires`)  
   a. `a.len() < 1_000_000`  
      – The caller is only allowed to pass vectors containing strictly fewer than one million elements.  
   b. `∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] < u32::MAX – 1`  
      – Every element already stored in `a` must be at most `u32::MAX − 2`.  
      – Together these two assumptions usually guarantee that any “+1” or “+2” arithmetic an implementation might perform on each element will never overflow 32-bit unsigned arithmetic.

3. Postconditions (`ensures`)  
   a. `result.len() == a.len()`  
      – The output vector must have exactly the same length as the input vector.  
   b. `∀ i:int. 0 ≤ i < result.len() ⇒ result[i] % 2 == 0`  
      – Every element placed in `result` must be an even number (its remainder modulo 2 is zero).

4. Missing obligations  
   Notice that the spec does *not* impose any relationship between the elements of the input and the elements of the output besides the length: the implementer is free to construct any even numbers they like (for example, all zeros, or “round each input up to the next even value”, etc.) as long as the two postconditions are satisfied.

5. Subtleties  
   • The vector is passed by value, not by reference. The caller loses ownership of `a`; the function may destroy or ignore it.  
   • All arithmetic in the specification is logical `int` arithmetic, but inside the body an implementation must still be careful about `u32` overflow. The value-range precondition makes this straightforward.  
   • The `len() < 1_000_000` bound is unrelated to functional correctness; it is there only to simplify reasoning about potential resource or arithmetic limits.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified Verus-Rust function with the following contract:

Signature  
    fn get_even(a: Vec<u32>) -> (result: Vec<u32>)

Inputs  
    • a : Vec<u32> — the function takes ownership of a heap-allocated vector whose elements are 32-bit unsigned integers.

Outputs

Postconditions (ensures)

    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < result.len() ⇒ result[i] mod 2 = 0  
       Every element in the returned vector must be an even number; its remainder when divided by 2 is zero.

No other relationships between `a` and `result` are required. Any implementation that, for every legal call, produces a vector of the same length in which all values are even, satisfies the specification.

# Reasoning
The original task is fairly straightforward: replace three specific punctuation
characters by a colon while preserving length.  To raise the difficulty we can

• Widen the character set that must be recognised, forcing an explicit helper
  spec function with more cases.

• Ask the function to compute a *second* result (e.g. the number or positions
  of the replacements), so the post-conditions must simultaneously describe two
  related outputs.

• Introduce global properties about the whole returned vector, such as “no two
  consecutive colons” or “every maximal run of punctuation collapses to a
  single colon”, which requires reasoning about sequences rather than single
  elements.

• Require the caller to supply (and the callee to use) a non-trivial
  pre-condition such as “there is at least one replaceable character”, adding an
  existential quantifier on the input.

Below are three independent, self-contained problem statements incorporating
those ideas.  Each gives enough detail for a spec generator to create the
necessary Verus specifications.

# Output
<<<
Create a verified Verus/Rust function named replace_with_colon_and_count
together with one helper specification function.

Helper specification function
    spec fn is_space_comma_dot_tab(c: char) -> bool
        • Returns true exactly when c is one of the four characters
          space ' ', comma ',', period '.', or horizontal tab '\t'.
        • Pure logic; used only inside specifications.

Primary function
    fn replace_with_colon_and_count(s: &Vec<char>) -> (v: Vec<char>, replaced: nat)

Inputs
    – s: an immutable reference to a non-empty Vec<char> that contains
      at least one character c such that is_space_comma_dot_tab(c) is true.

Outputs
    – v: a fresh Vec<char>.
    – replaced: a natural number (usize) equal to the number of characters
      that were replaced.

Preconditions
    1. s.len() > 0
    2. ∃ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ is_space_comma_dot_tab(s[i])

Postconditions
    1. v.len() == s.len()
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒
           if is_space_comma_dot_tab(s[i]) then v[i] == ':'  else v[i] == s[i]
    3. replaced ==
           | { i:int | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ is_space_comma_dot_tab(s[i]) } |
       (i.e. “replaced” is exactly the count of modified entries)
    4. replaced > 0                                                  // follows from (2) and the pre-condition
>>>

<<<
Create a verified Verus/Rust function named collapse_punctuation_to_colon
together with one helper specification function.

Helper specification function
    spec fn is_ascii_punctuation(c: char) -> bool
        • Returns true exactly when c is one of the characters
          space ' ', ',', '.', ';', ':', '!', or '?'.
        • Pure logic; used only inside specifications.

Primary function
    fn collapse_punctuation_to_colon(s: &Vec<char>) -> (v: Vec<char>)

Input
    – s: an immutable reference to a (possibly empty) Vec<char>.

Output
    – v: a fresh Vec<char>.

Intuitive behaviour
    Scan the input from left to right.  Whenever you encounter a *maximal*
    contiguous run of characters for which is_ascii_punctuation is true,
    emit exactly one ':' into the output; otherwise copy the character
    unchanged.  The ordering of all non-punctuation characters must be
    preserved.

Formal specification

Precondition
    • none, other than standard memory safety.

Postconditions
    1. v.len() ≥ 0                                   // v is a valid vector
    2. v.len() ≤ s.len()                             // collapsing never enlarges
    3. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < v.len()-1 ⇒
           !(v[i] == ':' ∧ v[i+1] == ':')            // no two consecutive colons
    4. For every index j in 0 ≤ j < s.len():
           if ¬is_ascii_punctuation(s[j]) then
               ∃ k:int • 0 ≤ k < v.len() ∧ v[k] == s[j] ∧
               (k is the count of non-punctuation positions ≤ j)
           else
               /* characters inside a punctuation run never appear verbatim */
    5. For every index k in 0 ≤ k < v.len():
           if v[k] == ':' then
               /* k corresponds to some (unique) maximal punctuation block
                  in s whose first element’s position equals
                  the number of non-punctuation chars before that block */
           else
               ¬is_ascii_punctuation(v[k])           // copied characters are non-punctuation
>>>

<<<
Create a verified Verus/Rust function named replace_and_record_indices
together with one helper specification function.

Helper specification function
    spec fn is_whitespace_or_dot(c: char) -> bool
        • Returns true exactly when c is either a space ' ',
          a newline '\n', a carriage return '\r', or a period '.'.
        • Pure logic; used only inside specifications.

Primary function
    fn replace_and_record_indices(s: &Vec<char>) -> (v: Vec<char>, idxs: Vec<usize>)

Input
    – s: an immutable reference to a Vec<char>.  The vector may be empty.

Outputs
    – v:  a fresh Vec<char> whose length is identical to s.len().
    – idxs: a fresh Vec<usize> containing the *ascending* list of indices
            where a replacement took place.

Preconditions
    • none (other than ordinary memory safety).

Postconditions
    1. v.len() == s.len()
    2. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ⇒
           if is_whitespace_or_dot(s[i]) then v[i] == ':'  else v[i] == s[i]
    3. idxs.is_sorted() ∧ (∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < idxs.len()-1 ⇒ idxs[k] < idxs[k+1])
    4. ∀ k:int • 0 ≤ k < idxs.len() ⇒
           idxs[k] < s.len() ∧ is_whitespace_or_dot(s[idxs[k]]) ∧ v[idxs[k]] == ':'
    5. ∀ i:int • 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ is_whitespace_or_dot(s[i]) ⇒
           i ∈ idxs
    6. idxs.len() ==
           | { i:int | 0 ≤ i < s.len() ∧ is_whitespace_or_dot(s[i]) } |
>>

nl_desc_oai:
Design a verified Verus/Rust function named `leq` that compares two slices of mathematical integers in a lexicographic “less-or-equal” sense.

Signature  
    fn leq(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (result: bool)

Inputs  
    • `a` – immutable reference to a slice of `int`.  
    • `b` – immutable reference to a (not shorter) slice of `int`.

Pre-condition (requires)  
    • `a.len() <= b.len()` – the first slice must not be longer than the second.

Output  
    • `result` – a boolean indicating whether `a` is lexicographically ≤ `b`.

Post-condition (ensures)  
`result` is true exactly when ONE of the following holds:

    1. Component-wise ≤ :   
       ∀ i ∈ [0, a.len()) • a[i] ≤ b[i].

    2. Strict lexicographic advantage:   
       ∃ index i (0 ≤ i < a.len()) such that  
           – all earlier positions are equal:  ∀ j < i • a[j] == b[j], and  
           – at that first differing position, a[i] < b[i].

Formally:
    result  ⇔  (a.len() ≤ b.len() ∧ ∀ i. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] ≤ b[i])
               ∨
               (∃ i. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ∧ i < b.len() ∧
                      (∀ j. 0 ≤ j < i ⇒ a[j] == b[j]) ∧ a[i] < b[i])

Observations / guidance for implementation
    • Because the contract reasons in `int`, no overflow checks are needed.  
    • If the two slices are identical, clause 1 holds (all elements equal, hence ≤) and `result` must be true.  
    • If a first mismatch exists, `result` is true precisely when that mismatch favours `a` (its element is smaller).  
    • Later elements after the first mismatch are irrelevant to the outcome.

Any body that satisfies these contracts—e.g., a single pass that stops at the first unequal pair—will be accepted by the verifier.

# Reasoning
The original task only asks for a Boolean “≤” comparison between two slices; it can be solved by a single loop and one post-condition.  
To raise the difficulty we can

• ask the implementation to return auxiliary information (e.g. the first index at which the slices differ), which forces the spec writer to talk about *both* the Boolean result **and** the index and to relate the two.  
• keep the original `a.len() ≤ b.len()` pre-condition so the overall structure is familiar, yet add a rarely-used but precise requirement such as “the index must be −1 if and only if the slices are identical”.  
• add more quantified reasoning steps (e.g. stating that every earlier element is equal up to `diff`), which must appear in `ensures`.  

Below are three harder, but still self-contained, variations.  Each is written so that a spec generator could translate it directly into Verus code.

# Output
<<<
Design a verified Verus/Rust function named `leq_with_first_diff` that, in addition to the usual lexicographic ≤ result, also reports the first position at which the two slices differ.

Signature  
    fn leq_with_first_diff(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (result: bool, diff: int)

Inputs  
    • `a` – immutable reference to a slice of mathematical integers.  
    • `b` – immutable reference to a (not shorter) slice of mathematical integers.

Pre-conditions (requires)  
    1. a.len() ≤ b.len()                – the first slice must not be longer.  
    2. 0 ≤ a.len()                      – lengths are natural numbers (implicit in Verus).

Outputs  
    • `result` – true exactly when `a` is lexicographically ≤ `b`.  
    • `diff`   – the least index at which the two slices differ, or −1 if they are identical.

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. diff == −1  ⇔  (a.len() == b.len() ∧ ∀i. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == b[i])  
       (i.e. −1 signals perfect equality of every compared element **and** equal length).  
    2. 0 ≤ diff < a.len()  ⇒  ∀j. 0 ≤ j < diff ⇒ a[j] == b[j]             – all earlier positions match.  
    3. 0 ≤ diff < a.len()  ⇒  a[diff] ≠ b[diff]                           – diff really differs.  
    4. result is true exactly when  
           (diff == −1 ∧ a.len() ≤ b.len())           // identical slices
           ∨  
           (0 ≤ diff < a.len() ∧ a[diff] < b[diff])   // first mismatch favours a
       Formally:  
         result ==
           ((diff == −1 ∧ a.len() ≤ b.len()) ∨
            (0 ≤ diff < a.len() ∧ a[diff] < b[diff])).
Observations  
    • If diff == −1 the two slices are equal in length and content, hence result is true.  
    • The implementation may compute diff while scanning and stop at the first unequal pair.
>>>

<<<
Create a verified Verus/Rust function called `lex_ordering` that performs a *total* lexicographic comparison between two integer slices and returns the classic three-way ordering.

Signature  
    fn lex_ordering(a: &[int], b: &[int]) -> (ord: i8)

Meaning of `ord`  
    • ord == −1  ⇒  a is lexicographically smaller than b.  
    • ord ==  0  ⇒  a and b are identical (same length and same elements).  
    • ord ==  1  ⇒  a is lexicographically greater than b.

Inputs  
    • `a`, `b` – immutable slices of mathematical integers of arbitrary (possibly different) length.

Pre-condition  
    None (even the lengths may differ arbitrarily).

Post-conditions  
    1. ord ∈ {−1, 0, 1}.  
    2. ord == 0  ⇔  (a.len() == b.len() ∧ ∀i. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == b[i]).  
    3. ord == −1 ⇔  
          (∃k. 0 ≤ k < min(a.len(), b.len()) ∧
                (∀j. 0 ≤ j < k ⇒ a[j] == b[j]) ∧
                a[k] < b[k])  
          ∨  
          (a.len() < b.len() ∧ ∀j. 0 ≤ j < a.len() ⇒ a[j] == b[j])  
       (strictly less either by a smaller element at first mismatch, or by being a proper equal prefix).  
    4. ord == 1  is the exact logical negation of clauses 2 and 3.

Additional hint  
    • Implementations may use a single pass over the shorter length, then break ties by length.
>>>

<<<
Implement a verified Verus/Rust function `lexicographic_min3` that returns, among three integer slices, the one that is lexicographically minimal (according to the usual total lexicographic order that first compares elements, then length).

Signature  
    fn lexicographic_min3<'a>(
        x: &'a [int],
        y: &'a [int],
        z: &'a [int]
    ) -> (min: &'a [int])

Post-conditions (ensures)  
    1. min == x ∨ min == y ∨ min == z.                         – result is one of the inputs.  
    2. min ≤_lex x ∧ min ≤_lex y ∧ min ≤_lex z, where ≤_lex is:  
            (a) a is a prefix of b                 →  a ≤_lex b  
            (b) Otherwise, let i be the first index with a[i] ≠ b[i]; then a ≤_lex b ⇔ a[i] < b[i].  
    3. If two or three slices are pair-wise equal and minimal, any of them may be returned.

Helper predicate (for specification only)  
    prefix(a,b) ≝ (a.len() ≤ b.len() ∧ ∀i. 0 ≤ i < a.len() ⇒ a[i] == b[i]).

Notes for implementers  
    • The verifier must be convinced that the chosen slice indeed satisfies the comparison against *both* other candidates, so at least two lexicographic comparisons are necessary.  
    • No constraints on lengths; they may differ arbitrarily and be empty.  
    • Because the function only *returns a reference* and never copies elements, its space complexity is O(1).
>>>

Task: Specify and implement a verified function called `find`.

Signature  
fn find(blood: &[int], key: int) -> (index: i32)

Inputs  
• `blood`: an immutable slice of mathematical integers (`int`).  
• `key`: the integer value to search for.

Output  
• `index`: a 32-bit signed integer.

Pre-conditions  
1. The slice is non-empty: `blood.len() > 0`.  
2. Its length does not exceed the maximum positive `i32` value: `blood.len() <= i32::MAX`.  
   (This guarantees that any legitimate position can be expressed in the 32-bit return type.)

Post-conditions  
Return value semantics are divided by sign:

• If the function returns a non-negative value (0 ≤ index), it must be a valid position inside the slice and the element found there equals the sought value:  
 0 ≤ index < blood.len()  and  blood[index] == key.

• If the function returns a negative value (index < 0), it is a witness that the key is absent:  
 ∀ k ∈ [0, blood.len()) · blood[k] ≠ key.

No restriction is placed on which occurrence is reported when the key appears more than once, nor on the exact negative value chosen when the key is missing.

With these contracts, any verified implementation—typically a linear scan through the slice—is acceptable.

# Spec code (given to you)
```rust
use vstd::prelude::*;

verus! {

fn rotate_right(a: Vec<i32>) -> (a_rotated: Vec<i32>)
    requires
        a.len() > 0,
        a.len() < 1000, // added relaxation to prevent overflow
    ensures
        forall|i: int| 1 <= i && i < a_rotated.len() ==> a_rotated[i] == a[(i - 1) as int],
        a_rotated[0] == a[a_rotated.len() - 1],
{
```

# Reasoning

1. Function name  
   rotate_right

2. Inputs  
   a: Vec<i32> – ownership of a heap-allocated vector containing 32-bit signed integers.  
   (Because the parameter is taken by value, the caller relinquishes ownership; the function may consume or reuse the memory.)

3. Output  
   a_rotated: Vec<i32> – a fresh vector returned to the caller.

4. Preconditions (requires)  
   • a.len() > 0 : the input vector must be non-empty.  
   • a.len() < 1000 : the length must be < 1000.  (This bound is not logically required for the rotation itself; it simply avoids any risk of arithmetic overflow inside the proof.)  
   No other assumptions about the element values are made.

5. Postconditions (ensures)  
   • ∀ i:int. 1 ≤ i < a_rotated.len() ⇒ a_rotated[i] == a[i-1]  
     Meaning: for every index i except 0, the element stored at position i in the result equals the element that was at position i − 1 in the original vector.  
   • a_rotated[0] == a[a_rotated.len() − 1]  
     The first element of the result equals the last element of the input.

   Together these two clauses fully characterise a right-rotation by one position.  
   Notice that the specification implicitly forces  
        a_rotated.len() == a.len()  
   because both sides of the equalities are only well-typed when the lengths match.

6. Absence of additional spec helpers  
   No ghost functions, lemmas, or decreases clauses are involved.

7. Implementation intuition  
   Any verified implementation may:  
   • allocate a new vector, push elements in the required order, and return it; or  
   • move the last element to the front in-place.  
   All that matters is proving the two postconditions above under the stated preconditions.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified Rust-Verus function with the following contract.

Signature  
    fn rotate_right(a: Vec<i32>) -> (a_rotated: Vec<i32>)

Parameters  
    a – a non-empty Vec<i32>; ownership is transferred to the function.

Return value  
    a_rotated – a vector that is the result of rotating a one position to the right.

Preconditions  
1. a.len() > 0        (the input vector must contain at least one element)  
2. a.len() < 1000     (upper-length bound, included only to sidestep any proof-time overflow issues)

Postconditions  
1. For every integer index i with 1 ≤ i < a_rotated.len(),  
       a_rotated[i] == a[i − 1]  
2. The first element of the result equals the last element of the original vector:  
       a_rotated[0] == a[a_rotated.len() − 1]

These two guarantees mean that a_rotated is exactly the vector obtained by shifting every element of a one slot to the right, wrapping the former last element around to the front.  The lengths of the two vectors are necessarily identical.

No auxiliary spec functions are required.  Any body that satisfies the contract—e.g., by cloning into a new vector or by performing an in-place swap—is acceptable.
>>>

   because both sides of the equalities are only well-typed when the lengths match.

6. Absence of additional spec helpers  
   No ghost functions, lemmas, or decreases clauses are involved.

7. Implementation intuition  
   Any verified implementation may:  
   • allocate a new vector, push elements in the required order, and return it; or  
   • move the last element to the front in-place.  
   All that matters is proving the two postconditions above under the stated preconditions.

# Final Description
<<<
Implement a verified Rust-Verus function with the following contract.

Signature  
    fn rotate_right(a: Vec<i32>) -> (a_rotated: Vec<i32>)

Parameters  
    a – a non-empty Vec<i32>; ownership is transferred to the function.

Return value  
    a_rotated – a vector that is the result of rotating a one position to the right.

Preconditions  
1. a.len() > 0        (the input vector must contain at least one element)  
2. a.len() < 1000     (upper-length bound, included only to sidestep any proof-time overflow issues)

Postconditions  
1. For every integer index i with 1 ≤ i < a_rotated.len(),  
       a_rotated[i] == a[i − 1]  
2. The first element of the result equals the last element of the original vector:  
       a_rotated[0] == a[a_rotated.len() − 1]

These two guarantees mean that a_rotated is exactly the vector obtained by shifting every element of a one slot to the right, wrapping the former last element around to the front.  The lengths of the two vectors are necessarily identical.

No auxiliary spec functions are required.  Any body that satisfies the contract—e.g., by cloning into a new vector or by performing an in-place swap—is acceptable.
>>>
